Controls:
Ensure AWS AppSync caching is configured to encrypt data at rest if being utilized.
AWS config rule:
cscoe-config-appsync-graphql-api-cache-encryption-at-rest-compliance
Context:
Cache is fully managed by AppSync, as anything in the service you don’t need to worry about the undifferentiated heavy lifting of maintaining and operating infrastructure. You can also chose to encrypt the cache in transit and at rest (for data saved to disk from memory during swap operations).

cscoe-config-appsync-graphql-api-waf-compliance
Context:
AWS WAF can be used to protect AppSync API from common web exploits, such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks. These could affect API availability and performance, compromise security, or consume excessive resources. For example, you can create rules to allow or block requests from specified IP address ranges, requests from CIDR blocks, requests that originate from a specific country or region, requests that contain malicious SQL code, or requests that contain malicious script.
Since PG&E uses the Silverline SaaS WAF offering, AWS WAF's primary function in this configuration would be to ensure the source IP of requests bound to AppSync is that of Silverline.  Silverline's IP Set which should be configured for WAF is 107.162.0.0/16.

122691216.title: Sentinel: AWS AutoScaling analysis
122691216.plain_text:
References:
EC2 Auto Scaling JIRA Epic:
CLOUDCOE-4350
Sentinel Implementation Notes:
S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
Control is implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Context:
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling conforms to the AWS shared responsibility model, which includes regulations and guidelines for data protection. AWS is responsible for protecting the global infrastructure that runs all of the AWS services. AWS maintains control over data hosted on this infrastructure, including the security configuration controls for handling customer content and personal data. AWS customers and APN Partners, acting either as data controllers or data processors, are responsible for any personal data that they put in the AWS Cloud.
Additional Notes:
Control to ensure the proper configuration of EBS Volumes is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
2
Control is implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Context:
You can configure your Auto Scaling group to encrypt Amazon EBS volume data stored in the cloud with AWS Key Management Service customer master keys (CMK). Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling supports AWS managed and customer managed CMKs to encrypt your data. Note that the KmsKeyId option to specify a customer managed CMK is not available when you use a launch configuration. Use a launch template instead.
Additional Notes:
Control to ensure the proper configuration of EBS Volumes is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Controls to ensure a CMK is used in Auto Scaling launch configurations are covered in item 8, below.
3
Not Applicable
Context:
As a managed service, Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling is protected by the AWS global network security procedures that are described in the Amazon Web Services: Overview of security processes whitepaper. You use AWS published API calls to access Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling through the network. Clients must support Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.0 or later. We recommend TLS 1.2 or later.
Additional Notes:
While TLS for the Auto Scaling API is enforced, the version of TLS cannot be configured.
TLS to the to auto-scaling group is dependent on the configuration of the individual EC2 instances.
4
Not Applicable
Not Applicable
- User access to AWS services is being granted by IAM policies attached to IAM roles. In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution, PingFederate, which enforces multi-factor authentication where applicable. The EC2 Autoscaling service does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.
5
Controls to ensure a CMK is used in Auto Scaling launch configurations are covered in item #8
Context:
Amazon EC2
Autoscaling
integrates with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) for server side encryption. Rotation of the customer managed key used for encryption will be handled by the Key Management Service (KMS).
Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure the Key Management Service (KMS) is configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
Controls to ensure a CMK is used in Auto Scaling launch configurations are covered in item 8, below.
6
Control will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail
.
Context:
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling is integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service using Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling. CloudTrail captures all API calls for Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling as events. The calls captured include calls from the Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling console and code calls to the Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling API.
Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure CloudTrail is enabled and configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail
.

128320694.title: Sentinel: AWS Backup analysis
128320694.plain_text:
References:
AWS Backup JIRA Epic:
CLOUDCOE-4751
Sentinel Implementation Notes:
S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
covered in item#2
Context:
The way to configure encryption differs depending on the resource type. Certain resource types support the ability to encrypt your backups using a separate encryption key from the key used to encrypt the source resource. This capability adds another layer of protection for your backups.
The controls for Encryption at Rest for Backup-managed services are including in the following SAF Documents:
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic File System - EFS
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS EC2
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS DynamoDB
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Storage Gateway - File Gateway
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Lustre
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Windows
Additional Notes:
Currently only EFS supports independent encryption via AWS Backup Vaults
2
CLOUDCOE-4752
ensure-aws-backup-vaults-using-customer-managed-kms-key
Developed
Controls:
Ensure AWS Backup Vaults use PG&E-managed CMKs
AWS Config Detection Rule:
cscoe-config-backup-vault-customer-cmk-compliance
Context:
The AWS KMS encryption master key is used to protect your backups in this backup vault. By default, AWS Backup creates a master key with the alias
aws/backup
for you. You can choose that key or choose any other key in your account.
SAF Controls for Encryption Keys for services whose backups are managed by AWS are defined in the following documents:
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic File System - EFS
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS EC2
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS DynamoDB
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Storage Gateway - File Gateway
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Lustre
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Windows
Additional Notes:
In AWS Backup, a
backup vault
is a container that you organize your backups in. You can use backup vaults to set the AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) encryption key that is used to encrypt backups in the backup vault and to control access to the backups in the backup vault. If you require different encryption keys or access policies for different groups of backups, you can optionally create multiple backup vaults. Otherwise, you can have all your backups organized in the default backup vault.
3
Not Applicable
Context:
AWS Backup encrypts data in transit between the service and other integrated AWS services. All data that passes between Backup and integrated services is encrypted using Transport layer Security (TLS).
Additional Notes:
The version of TLS used is not configurable and will require the consuming application to enforce in their request.
4
Not Applicable
Not Applicable

5
managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
.
Context:
AWS Backup's integrated data services integrate with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) for server side encryption. Rotation of the AWS managed key used for encryption will be handled by the Key Management Service (KMS).
AWS KMS automatically rotates AWS managed CMKs every three years (1095 days).
Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure the Key Management Service (KMS) is configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
.
6
Not Applicable
Context:
AWS Backup is integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service in AWS Backup. CloudTrail captures all API calls for AWS Backup as events. The calls captured include calls from the AWS Backup console and code calls to the AWS Backup API operations. If you create a trail, you can enable continuous delivery of CloudTrail events to an Amazon S3 bucket, including events for AWS Backup. If you don't configure a trail, you can still view the most recent events in the CloudTrail console in
Event history
. Using the information collected by CloudTrail, you can determine the request that was made to AWS Backup, the IP address from which the request was made, who made the request, when it was made, and additional details.

115343538.title: Sentinel: AWS Certificate Manager analysis
115343538.plain_text:
References:
AWS Certification Manager JIRA Epic:
CLOUDCOE-3457
Sentinel Policy Implementation Notes:
S.No
Jira story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
Non configurable
Context:
When you request a public certificate, AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) generates a public/private key pair. For imported certificates, you generate the key pair. The public key becomes part of the certificate. ACM stores the certificate and its corresponding private key, and uses AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) to help protect the private key.
An AWS managed key is used to encrypt the data. This is a non-configurable option.
2
Not Applicable
Not Applicable
- Only AWS managed KMS keys can be used to encrypt the ACM private key.
3
Non configurable
Context:
As a managed service, this service is protected by the AWS global network security procedures that are described in the Amazon Web Services: Overview of Security Processes whitepaper. This forces all communication to this service to use TLS 1.0 or later. This is non-configurable.
4
Not Applicable
Not Applicable
- User access to AWS services is being granted by IAM policies attached to IAM roles. In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution, PingFederate, which enforces multi-factor authentication where applicable. The Certificate Manager service does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.
5
Non configurable (Attribute not found in terraform to import certificates)
Controls:
Ensure certificates with auto-renewal enabled are renewed successfully
Ensure all imported certificates are rotated every year
Ensure all imported certificates issued by PGE has a lifespan of 2 years
Ensure all imported certificates not issued by PGE has a lifespan of 1 year
Context:
AWS managed keys are used to encrypt data for AWS Certificate Manager. AWS managed keys are automatically rotated every 3 years by AWS.
Prisma detection policies:
AWS ACM Certificate has auto-renewal enabled but is not renewing successfully
AWS ACM Imported Certificate is not rotating once a year
AWS ACM imported certificate does not have the correct lifespan
AWS ACM Certificate is either Expired or is no longer a valid certificate
6
Compliance covered by Cloud Trail
Context:
To guard against SSL/TLS certificates that are issued by mistake or by a compromised CA, some browsers require that public certificates issued for your domain be recorded in a certificate transparency log. The domain name is recorded. The private key is not. Certificates that are not logged typically generate an error in the browser.
Before the Amazon CA issues a publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificate for your domain, it submits the certificate to at least two certificate transparency log servers. These servers add the certificate to their public databases and return a signed certificate timestamp (SCT) to the Amazon CA. The CA then embeds the SCT in the certificate, signs the certificate, and issues it to you. The timestamps are included with other X.509 extensions.
AWS Certificate Manager is integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service in ACM. CloudTrail is enabled by default on your AWS account. CloudTrail captures API calls for ACM as events, including calls from the ACM console and code calls to the ACM API operations.

ensure-codebuild-reportgroups-encryption-disabled-set-to-false
Developed
Controls:

Ensure CodeBuild Report Groups have encryptionDisabled set to False
AWS Config Rules
cscoe-config-codebuild-encrypted-report-group-compliance
cscoe-config-codebuild-
artifacts-unencrypted
-compliance
Context:
Build artifacts, such as a cache, logs, exported raw test report data files, and build results, are encrypted by default using customer master keys (CMKs) for Amazon S3 that are managed by the AWS Key Management Service. If you do not want to use these CMKs, you must create and configure a customer-managed CMK.
You can store the identifier of the AWS KMS key that CodeBuild uses to encrypt the build output artifact in the CODEBUILD_KMS_KEY_ID environment variable.
The Amazon Elastic Block Store volumes of your build fleet are encrypted by default using CMKs managed by AWS.

Controls:

Ensure VPC endpoint is deployed in private subnet
Context:
Typically, AWS CodeBuild cannot access resources in a VPC. To enable access, you must provide additional VPC-specific configuration information in your CodeBuild project configuration. This includes the VPC ID, the VPC subnet IDs, and the VPC security group IDs.
You can improve the security of your builds by configuring AWS CodeBuild to use an interface VPC endpoint. Interface endpoints are powered by PrivateLink, a technology that you can use to privately access Amazon EC2 and CodeBuild by using private IP addresses. PrivateLink restricts all network traffic between your managed instances, CodeBuild, and Amazon EC2 to the Amazon network. (Managed instances don't have access to the internet.)
Prisma Policies:
AWS CodeBuild VPC Endpoint is not deployed in private subnet
AWS VPC endpoint for CodeBuild has private DNS enabled
AWS Config Rules:
cscoe-config-codebuild-vpc-exists-compliance

privilegedMode` Enables running the Docker daemon inside a Docker container and should be set to true only if the build project is used to build Docker images. Otherwise, a build that attempts to interact with the Docker daemon fails. Setting privilegedMode is permissible at PG&E since CodeBuild is approved to build Docker containers.
IAM policies are used to assign permissions to IAM users, groups, and roles. It is up to the business to decide the minimum required permission needed
You must specify privilegedMode with a value of true only if you plan to use this build project to build Docker images, and the build environment image you specified is not provided by CodeBuild with Docker support. Otherwise, all associated builds that attempt to interact with the Docker daemon fail. You must also start the Docker daemon so that your builds can interact with it. One way to do this is to initialize the Docker daemon in the install phase of your buildspec file by running the following build commands. Do not run these commands if you specified a build environment image provided by CodeBuild with Docker support.

CodePipeline uses GitHub OAuth tokens and personal access tokens to access your GitHub repositories and retrieve the latest changes. There are two ways to configure authentication in GitHub:
AWS creates a default AWS managed OAuth token when you use the console to create or update pipelines.
You can create and manage your own customer-generated personal access tokens. You need personal access tokens when you use the CLI, SDK, or AWS CloudFormation to create or update your pipeline

Developed

Ensure Amazon DocumentDB cluster snapshots have server side encryption enabled.
AWS Config Rule Detection:

cscoe-config-docdb-snapshot-server-side-encryption-compliance

using-customer-managed-kms-key

2&3 require dynamic data

Ensure Amazon DocumentDB instances are encrypted using a customer managed KMS key.

are encrypted using a customer managed KMS key.
AWS Config Rule Detection:
cscoe-config-docdb-cluster-customer-kms-key-compliance
cscoe-config-docdb-instance-check-encryption-key-compliance
cscoe-config-docdb-snapshot-check-encryption-key-compliance

Dynamic data is required
Controls:
Ensure Amazon DocumentDB parameter group associated with the cluster has the 'TLS' parameter set to 'enabled'.
AWS Config Rule Detection:
cscoe-config-documentdb-cluster-parameter-group-tls-compliance
Context:
When TLS is enabled, Amazon DocumentDB enforces TLS >= 1.0 so it will be up to the consuming application to establish connections using TLS 1.2 or later.

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: Key Management Service
.
Context:
Amazon DocumentDB integrates with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) for server side encryption. Rotation of the customer managed key used for encryption will be handled by the Key Management Service (KMS).
Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure the Key Management Service (KMS) is configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: Key Management Service
.
6
CLOUDCOE-4878
aws/documentdb/ensure-documentdb-audit-logging-enabled
1.Development
2. Dynamic data is required
Controls:
Ensure the Amazon DocumentDB cluster is configured to publish Audit logs to Cloudwatch
Ensure there is a subscription on all log groups starting with "/aws/docdb" to send logs to a central location
AWS Config Rule Detection:
cscoe-config-docdb-cluster-audit-logs-compliance
cscoe-config-docdb-cluster-profiler-logs-compliance
cscoe-config-docdb-centralized-logs-compliance
Context:
When auditing is enabled, Amazon DocumentDB records Data Definition Language (DDL), authentication, authorization, and user management events to Amazon CloudWatch Logs. When auditing is enabled, Amazon DocumentDB exports your cluster’s auditing records (JSON documents) to Amazon CloudWatch Logs. You can use Amazon CloudWatch Logs to analyze, monitor, and archive your Amazon DocumentDB auditing events.
A new log group will be automatically created for the DocumentDB cluster under the following prefix, in which
cluster-name
represents the DB cluster name, and
log_type
represents the log type:
/aws/docdb/
cluster-name
/
log_type
7
CLOUDCOE-4788
ensure_documentdb_retention_backup_period_is_atleast_15_days
Development
Controls:
Ensure automated backups are enabled on the Amazon DocumentDB Cluster with the retention period of at least 15 days.
AWS Config Rule Detection:
cscoe-config-docdb-cluster-backup-retention-compliance
Context:
Amazon DocumentDB backs up your cluster volume automatically and retains restore data for the length of the
backup retention period.
DocumentDB backups are continuous and incremental so you can quickly restore to any point within the backup retention period. You can specify a backup retention period, from 1 to 35 days, when you create or modify a DB cluster.
8
Not Applicable
Context:
User access to AWS services is being granted by IAM policies attached to IAM roles. In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution. Multi-factor authentication is also enforced where applicable by the SSO solution. Identities are being populated by Active Directory. Access for a user can be terminated by removing that user from the security group granting access or by disabling the AD user. This process is currently configured outside of AWS and there is no service level control needed.
Authentication to an Amazon DocumentDB database is done via standard MongoDB tools and drivers with Salted Challenge Response Authentication Mechanism (SCRAM), the default authentication mechanism for MongoDB.  DocumentDB does NOT offer a database authentication mechanism that federates with IAM or Active Directory.

122688664.plain_text:
References:
AWS DynamoDB JIRA Epic:

Sentinel Policy Implementation Notes:
S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
CLOUDCOE-3950
ensure-dynamodb-table-is-encrypted-with-cmek
Developed
Control:
Ensure DynamoDB table is encrypted.
Context:
DynamoDB encryption at rest provides an additional layer of data protection by securing your data in an encrypted table—including its primary key, local and global secondary indexes, streams, global tables, backups, and DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) clusters whenever the data is stored in durable media.
AWS Config Rule Name:
cscoe-config-dynamodb-table-encryption-enabled-compliance
2
CLOUDCOE-3938
ensure-dynamodb-table-is-encrypted-with-cmek
Developed
Control:
Ensure KMS key used to encrypt DynamoDB table is a PG&E managed key (aka customer managed key).
Context:
DynamoDB has added support to enable you to switch from AWS-owned CMKs to customer-managed CMKs managed by Amazon Key Management Service (KMS), without having to implement any code or application changes to encrypt your data.
Prisma Cloud Detection Policy:
AWS DynamoDB encrypted using AWS owned CMK instead of AWS managed CMK
3
non-configurable
Context:
DynamoDB can only be accessed via the AWS API. As a managed service, this service is protected by the AWS global network security procedures that are described in the Amazon Web Services: Overview of Security Processes whitepaper. This forces all communication to this service to use TLS 1.0 or later. This is non-configurable.
Additional Notes:
This controls ensures TLS communication but does not verify the version of TLS used. The TLS version cannot be set as a DynamoDB configuration and must be set at the client calling the DynamoDB service
4

Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure the Key Management Service (KMS) is configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
.
6
Compliance managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail
.
Context:
DynamoDB is integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service in DynamoDB. CloudTrail captures all API calls for DynamoDB as events. The calls captured include calls from the DynamoDB console and code calls to the DynamoDB API operations.
Additional Notes:
Logging for DynamoDB will be implement via CloudTrail. The controls to ensure CloudTrail is enabled and configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail
.
7
CLOUDCOE-3939
ensure-point-in-time-recovery-is-enabled

Ensure point in time recovery (PITR) is enabled for all DynamoDB tables:
Context:
Point-in-time recovery helps protect your DynamoDB tables from accidental write or delete operations. With point-in-time recovery, you don't have to worry about creating, maintaining, or scheduling on-demand backups.

cscoe-config-dynamodb-pitr-enabled-compliance

control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
Context:
Amazon EBS encryption is an encryption solution for your EBS volumes and snapshots. It uses AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) customer master keys (CMK).
The data on NVMe instance store volumes is encrypted using an XTS-AES-256 cipher implemented on a hardware module on the instance. The encryption keys are generated using the hardware module and are unique to each NVMe instance storage device. All encryption keys are destroyed when the instance is stopped or terminated and cannot be recovered. You cannot disable this encryption and you cannot provide your own encryption key.
Additional Information:
This control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
2
control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store

3
Shared(Not Applicable)
Shared responsibility

LOB is responsible for ensuring applications that are hosted on EC2 instances have encryption in transit throughout the application using TLS v1.2 or greater. Encryption in transit to and from an EC2 instance can be managed by AWS if the following requirements are met:
The instances use the following instance types: C5a, C5ad, C5n, G4, I3en, M5dn, M5n, P3dn, R5dn, and R5n.
The instances are in the same Region.
The instances are in the same VPC or peered VPCs, and the traffic does not pass through a virtual network device, such as a load balancer or a transit gateway.

The EC2 services does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.
Direct access to the EC2 instance is not supported and all access must use SSM Session Manager which requires AWS IAM access.
5
managed by the

Amazon EC2 integrates with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) for server side encryption. Rotation of the customer managed key used for encryption will be handled by the Key Management Service (KMS).

Amazon EC2 and Amazon EBS are integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service in Amazon EC2 and Amazon EBS. CloudTrail captures all API calls for Amazon EC2 and Amazon EBS as events, including calls from the console and from code calls to the APIs.
Logging from within the EC2 instance is not covered by CloudTrail.

Developed
Controls:
Ensure Redis cluster is encrypted at rest
Context:
Ensure that your AWS ElastiCache Redis clusters are encrypted in order to meet security and compliance requirements (keep Personally Identifiable Information safe). Data encryption helps prevent unauthorized users from reading sensitive data available on your Redis clusters and their associated cache storage systems. This includes data saved to persistent media, known as data at-rest.

Context:
Ensure that your Redis clusters are using KMS CMK customer-managed keys instead of AWS managed-keys (default keys used by the Redis service when there are no customer keys defined) in order to have more granular control over your data-at-rest encryption/decryption process.

To help keep your data secure, Amazon ElastiCache and Amazon EC2 provide mechanisms to guard against unauthorized access of your data on the server. By providing in-transit encryption capability, ElastiCache gives you a tool you can use to help protect your data when it is moving from one location to another. For example, you might move data from a primary node to a read replica node within a replication group, or between your replication group and your application

Developed
Controls:
Ensure automated backups have a retention policy of at least 15 days
Context:
For any Redis cluster, you can enable automatic backups. When automatic backups are enabled, ElastiCache creates a backup of the cluster on a daily basis. Automatic backups can help guard against data loss. In the event of a failure, you can create a new cluster, restoring your data from the most recent backup. The result is a warm-started cluster, preloaded with your data and ready for use.
Prisma Cloud Detection Policy:
AWS ElastiCache Redis cluster retention policy less than 15 days

Ensure that your Redis clusters have the Auto Minor Version Upgrade feature enabled in order to receive automatically minor engine upgrades. CLOUDCOE-3782
ensure-at-rest-and-transit-encryption-enabled-for-redis
Developed
Controls:
Ensure Redis Cluster has encryption in-transit enabled (OOTB Prisma: AWS ElastiCache Redis cluster with in-transit encryption disabled)
Context:
To help keep your data secure, Amazon ElastiCache and Amazon EC2 provide mechanisms to guard against unauthorized access of your data on the server. By providing in-transit encryption capability, ElastiCache gives you a tool you can use to help protect your data when it is moving from one location to another. For example, you might move data from a primary node to a read replica node within a replication group, or between your replication group and your application.
In-transit encryption is optional and can only be enabled on Redis replication groups when they are created. You enable in-transit encryption on a replication group by setting the parameter TransitEncryptionEnabled to true (CLI: --transit-encryption-enabled) when you create the replication group. You can do this whether you are creating the replication group using the AWS Management Console, the AWS CLI, or the ElastiCache API. If you enable in-transit encryption, you must also provide a value for CacheSubnetGroup.
Prisma Cloud Detection Policy:
AWS ElastiCache Redis cluster with in-transit encryption not enabled

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
.
EBS volumes are encrypted using a data key derived from the Customer Master key.  The data key itself will not be rotated unless it's suspected to be compromised, at that point a new data key should be created and the volumes needs to be re-encrypted with the newly derived data key.

CloudTrail is required on all accounts and captures events of all enabled services within an account by default.

AWS automatically encrypts at rest using Amazon S3 server-side encryption (ECR is backed by S3)
Amazon ECR stores images in Amazon S3 buckets that Amazon ECR manages. By default, Amazon ECR uses server-side encryption with Amazon S3-managed encryption keys which encrypts your data at rest using an AES-256 encryption algorithm. This does not require any action on your part and is offered at no additional charge.

Context:
For more control over the encryption for your Amazon ECR repositories, you can use server-side encryption with customer master keys (CMKs) stored in AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS). When you use AWS KMS to encrypt your data, you can either use the default AWS-managed CMK, which is managed by Amazon ECR, or specify your own CMK (referred to as a customer-managed CMK).
Note: S3 encryption is managed by AWS for ECR

Amazon ECR lifecycle policies enable you to specify the lifecycle management of images in a repository. A lifecycle policy is a set of one or more rules, where each rule defines an action for Amazon ECR. The actions apply to images that contain tags prefixed with the given strings. This allows the automation of cleaning up unused images, for example expiring images based on age or count. You should expect that after creating a lifecycle policy the affected images are expired within 24 hours.
See:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECR/latest/userguide/LifecyclePolicies.html
https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/clean-up-your-container-images-with-amazon-ecr-lifecycle-policies/
Regarding backups, implement cross-regional backups for critical images based on the needs of the business

com.amazonaws.
region
.ecr.dkr
This endpoint is used for the Docker Registry APIs. Docker client commands such as push and pull use this endpoint. When you create the com.amazonaws.region.ecr.dkr endpoint, you must enable a private DNS hostname. To do this, ensure that the Enable Private DNS Name option is selected in the VPC console when you create the VPC endpoint.
com.amazonaws.
region
.ecr.api
This endpoint is used for calls to the Amazon ECR API. API actions such as DescribeImages and CreateRepositories go to this endpoint.
The specified
region
represents the Region identifier for an AWS Region supported by Amazon ECR, such as us-east-2 for the US East (Ohio) Region.

Amazon ECS tasks using Fargate require the awsvpc network mode, which provides each task with an elastic network interface (ENI) and a primary private IP address. When you run a task or create a service with this network mode, you must specify one or more subnets to attach the network interface to and one or more security groups to apply to the network interface. Because each task gets its own ENI, you can also take advantage of other Amazon EC2 networking features like VPC Flow Logs so that you can monitor traffic to and from your tasks.
PG&E VPCs are configured to use PAN firewalls and F5 for network threat assessment.
A VPC endpoint should be deployed in each VPC to allow for communication to the ECS API without going over the internet.

CLOUDCOE-3372
ensure-efs-is-encrypted
Developed
Controls:
Ensure EFS file system is encrypted
Context:
Ensure that your Amazon EFS file systems are encrypted in order to meet security and compliance requirements. Your data is transparently encrypted while being written and transparently decrypted while being read from your file system, therefore the encryption process does not require any additional action from you or your application.
Prisma Policy:
AWS Elastic File System (EFS) with encryption for data at rest is disabled
2
CLOUDCOE-3375
ensure-efs-is-encrypted-using-customer-managed-keys
Developed
Controls:
Ensure EFS file systems is encrypted using PG&E Managed KMS keys
Context:
Ensure that your Amazon EFS file systems are encrypted using KMS CMK customer-managed keys instead of AWS managed-keys (default keys used by the EFS service when there are no customer keys defined) in order to have more granular control over your data-at-rest encryption/decryption process
Prisma Policy:
AWS Elastic File System (EFS) not encrypted using Customer Managed Key
3
CLOUDCOE-3373
ensure-to-block-efs-non-tls-traffic
Developed
Controls:
Ensure a EFS file system policy exists that blocks non-TLS traffic
Context:
This policy was created using the policy wizard that is part of the EFS console. You can use the wizard to add this policy to a particular EFS file system. The policy listed above should not be copied as it specific to a particular EFS file system used for development.
Enabling encryption of data in transit for your Amazon EFS file system is done by enabling Transport Layer Security (TLS) when you mount your file system using the Amazon EFS mount helper.
When encryption of data in transit is declared as a mount option for your Amazon EFS file system, the mount helper initializes a client stunnel process. Stunnel is an open source multipurpose network relay. The client stunnel process listens on a local port for inbound traffic, and the mount helper redirects Network File System (NFS) client traffic to this local port. The mount helper uses TLS version 1.2 to communicate with your file system.
AWS Config detection rule:
cscoe-config-efs-tls-compliance
4

The EFS services does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.

127107831.title: Sentinel: AWS Elastic Kubernetes Service analysis
127107831.plain_text:
References:
EKS Epic:
CLOUDCOE-4582
&
CLOUDCOE-4347
Sentinel Implementation Notes:
S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
taken care in item#2
Controls:
Ensure AWS KMS is used with envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets
Context:
Envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets allows you to encrypt your secrets with a unique data encryption key (DEK). The DEK is then encrypted using a key encryption key (KEK) from AWS KMS which can be automatically rotated on a recurring schedule. With the KMS plugin for Kubernetes, all Kubernetes secrets are stored in etcd in ciphertext instead of plain text and can only be decrypted by the Kubernetes API server.
Prisma Cloud detection policies:
AWS EKS cluster is not using Secrets encryption with KMS
Additional Notes:
Controls to check for the encryption of the EBS volumes attached to EKS worker nodes will be covered by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Controls for how to configure storageClasses in Kubernetes will be covered as part of the Kubernetes Platform SAF document.
Docker is designed to be stateless.
Encourage AWS storage options, see individual SAF2.0 criteria:
S3
,
EFS
,
EBS,
MySQL
,
Oracle
,
Postgres
,
SQLServer
,
DynamoDB,
DocumentDB
,
Neptune
,
Redshift
...
Ensure encrypted storage for these options...
Host-based persistence shared among containers
Host-based persistence per container
Distributed filesystems like NFS
Docker Volumes
Additional Kubernetes Best Practices:
Ensure fsx storageclass specifies a KMS key
Ensure the ebs storageclass has encryption set to true
Ensure secrets are mounted as volume mounts instead of environment variables

See
Cloud Security KMS Standard.
See
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
.
3
Not configurable
Context:
The Kubernetes control API is only accessible via HTTPS on port 443. This is not configurable.
This ensures TLS communication but does not verify the version of TLS used. The TLS version cannot be set as an EKS configuration and must be set at the client calling the EKS control API.
Additional Notes:
Encryption in transit within the EKS platform will depend on the Container Network Interface (CNI) being used along with the service mesh being implemented.
Controls for services that integrate with EKS such as ECR, ALB, ELB, and RDS will be defined as part of the SAF documentation for the respective service.
Additional Kubernetes Best Practices:
Ensure EFS persistent volumes have the TLS parameter listed under mountOptions
Ensure pod to pod communication is encrypted using TLS
Ensure node to node communication is encrypted using TLS
Ensure all traffic entering the EKS cluster is using TLS

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
.
Context:
Amazon EKS integrates with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) used for envelope encryption. Rotation of the customer managed key used for encryption will be handled by the Key Management Service (KMS).
Additional Notes
:
The controls to ensure the Key Management Service (KMS) is configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
.
Kubernetes will
rotate some certificates automatically
(notably, the kubelet client and server certs) by creating new CSRs as its existing credentials expire.
EKS includes a fully managed control plane for the Kubernetes cluster. This will ensure encryption of data and rotation of keys for data in the control plane such as etcd.
6
CLOUDCOE-4613
eks-ensure-controlPlane-logging-enabled
Developed
Dynamic data from account is required
Control:
Ensure Kubernetes control plane logging is enabled
Ensure there is a subscription on all log groups starting with "/aws/eks" to send logs to a central location
Context:
Amazon EKS control plane logging provides audit and diagnostic logs directly from the Amazon EKS control plane to CloudWatch Logs in your account. These logs make it easy for you to secure and run your clusters. You can select the exact log types you need, and logs are sent as log streams to a group for each Amazon EKS cluster in CloudWatch.
Prisma Cloud detection policies...
AWS EKS control plane logging disabled
Additional Notes:
Pod level and service level logging within the Kubernetes environment will be dependent on the tool stack being used (EFK, Splunk, CloudWatch Container Insights, etc.)
Control plane log retention is a business decision.
Application logs (container level logs) are not captured as part of EKS logging.
Keeping K8s logs inside the cluster is a security threat in case of compromise. Security-sensitive logs should be transported outside the cluster to prevent tampering in the event of a breach.

This ensures TLS communication but does not verify the version of TLS used. The TLS version cannot be set as an EKS configuration and must be set at the client calling the EKS control API.
Additional Notes:
Encryption in transit within the EKS platform will depend on the Container Network Interface (CNI) being used along with the service mesh being implemented.
Controls for services that integrate with EKS such as ECR, ALB, and RDS will be defined as part of the SAF documentation for the respective service.
Kubernetes container and service network endpoints are configured by default with a cluster-internal IP thus they are unreachable.
Kubernetes containers are to be exposed to PGE private subnet(s) as Kubernetes Services.  A Service configuration may only be of ServiceType: ClusterIP or LoadBalancer thus rendering the container/service as either unreachable or only reachable via a LoadBalancer.
Use EKS Managed NodeGroups to provision the compute capacity of your EKS Cluster. Each NodeGroup is allocated both an AutoScalingGroup and ElasticLoadBalancer.
Practice defense-in-depth with SecurityGroups by permitting only TLS-based protocols.
By default, Kubernetes API Server exposes only TLS endpoint.

334368464.title: TFC Agents V1 workspace CVE / Instance Update
334368464.plain_text:
Git Repo:
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tf-agents
Workspace:
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/workspaces/tfc-agent-compute
Prerequisite
Join Terraform Cloud organization "
pgetech
".
Terraform Cloud onboard
2. You need to be
added to a team
that has access to the workspace.
Teams are managed under
Settings > Teams
.
The team must have at least
"Write"
access to the workspace to run
apply
.
If you need access. Reach out internally to CCOE or post question to
terraform chat in teams
Overview
This document outlines the process for managing Terraform Cloud (TFC) agent EC2 instances that are dynamically updated with a new Amazon Machine Image (AMI) ID. The AMI ID is retrieved from AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Parameter Store, and the Auto Scaling Group (ASG) is updated accordingly. Upon AMI update, the associated TFC workspace is automatically re-run to reflect the changes.
Background.
MRAD is the current team that has not migrated their workspaces to V2. Currently the TFC agents are deployed in shared services prod which the team leverages to deploy IaC (Infrastructure as Code). The Agents are installed on Ec2 instances and leverage the Golden AMI via a launch template which is deprecated. From time to time CVE's will crop up and the underlining image must be patched. Thankfully the agents are deployed via Iac and the underlining instance image is fetched dynamically from ssm parameter store via the data block in terraform. Please review the git repo to see the configuration. Thus to effectively patch ensure the ssm parameter value is updated (review golden ami release schedule) and rerun the workspace.
🔁 Workflow Summary
AMI ID is updated
in the SSM Parameter Store.
Terraform detects the change
via a
data
block referencing the SSM parameter.
ASG launch template
is updated with the new AMI ID.
ASG triggers instance refresh
, replacing old EC2 instances.
TFC workspace is re-run
to apply any additional configuration or validation.
To update the instances, simply rerun the workspace to which will fetch the Golden AMI dynamically via a the terraform data block. This will replace and update the following ASG (Auto Scaling Group) and 1 by 1 replace the existing instances effectively patching the instances themselves.

228036670.title: Prisma Cloud - Run Task
228036670.plain_text:
Integrating Prisma Cloud with Terraform Cloud
Prisma Cloud can scan Terraform configuration files at different run stages like pre-plan, post-plan, and apply.
It checks for IaC misconfigurations that may result in a finding if provisioned to cloud.
Failures can be set to either advisory or blocking mode:
In Terraform Cloud, policies can be set to advisory or mandatory. Advisory is only a warning, but mandatory can block the run depending on the severity level.
In Prisma Cloud, severity levels are info, low, medium, high and critical. Hard failures can be configured for high and critical levels, which will stop the run.
Integrate Prisma Cloud with Terraform Cloud (Run Tasks) to scan workspaces in Terraform Cloud. The integration enables Prisma Cloud to use policies and regulate runs in Terraform Cloud. Through the integration, Prisma Cloud manages run-related information of a workspace and communicates the status of the run (either pass or fail) accessible on the Prisma Cloud console.

354856382.title: Terraform-Managed TFC Infrastructure - Unified Design Solution
354856382.plain_text:
Overview: Terraform on Terraform
Core Concept
Self-Service Infrastructure
: Uses Terraform to manage Terraform Cloud (TFC) resources programmatically
Automation-First
: Eliminates manual workspace creation and permission management
Scalable Architecture
: Supports hundreds of workspaces through declarative configuration
Architecture: Two-Layer Design
Layer 1: Foundational Infrastructure
Projects → Teams → Project Access
Components
projects
Logical grouping containers for workspaces
Namespace isolation for different applications/teams
Foundation for permission inheritance
teams
AD group integration via SSO
Organization-level permissions (workspace admin, policy management)
Reusable security principals across projects
Project-Level Access
High-level team-to-project assignments
Read-only access for AD groups (e.g., viewers, auditors)
Establishes baseline permissions before workspace creation
Layer 2: Workspace Infrastructure
Workspaces → Variables → Run Tasks → Team Access
Components
Workspaces
Individual workspace provisioning
VCS integration (GitHub repository connection)
Terraform version pinning and working directory specification
Variable Management
Terraform variables (application-specific)
Environment variables (credentials, settings)
Auto-injected metadata (workspace ID, cloud provider accounts)
Security & Compliance
Policy set attachments (Sentinel policies)
Run task integration (Prisma Cloud, cost estimation)
Drift detection and auto-destroy scheduling
Workspace-Level Access
Granular AD group permissions per workspace
Read-Only
: View runs and state

: Execute runs, manage variables, write state
Dynamic Provisioning Model
Data-Driven Workflow
YAML/JSON Definitions → Orchestration → Terraform Modules → TFC Resources

Complete Workflow: End-to-End
Step 1: Infrastructure Foundation

tfe/
├── modules/

Summary: Unified Design Principles
Declarative
: Infrastructure defined as code
Modular
: Reusable components with clear boundaries
Automated

Secure

If no
working_directory
is specified, the root of your repo will be used. Ref:
Terraform Cloud - Working Directories
Ensure the repo and branch specified exist in the remote GitHub repo, and is not just a local repo and branch.
Section 1 summary
Became a member of the pge-tfc-workspace-developers team.
Cloned the pge-tfc-workspaces repository to your local machine.
Added a block of YAML code that declaratively defines your new workspace.
Move on to section 2 to validate your updates, and generate the required JSON files.
Section 2: Validate your updates and generate JSON file(s)
Now you need to run a script that validates your new workspace code. It ensures all the required fields are there, with valid values, that your repo and branch exist, as well as other things. This script is referred to as
orchestration.py
and is located in the
scripts
folder of the
pge-tfc-workspaces
repository.
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tfc-workspaces/blob/main/scripts/orchestration.py
Prerequisites:
You must have a personal GitHub PAT created with appropriate permissions and have it set to the $GH_TOKEN environment variable in your shell. Ref:
CCOE Wiki: Creating a Github PAT
You must have added the
CCoE-Pipeline
GitHub user to your repository with
Admin
privileges. This can be done in your repository settings.
Open your terminal, and navigate to the root of your local
pge-tfc-workspaces
repository.
Run one of the following (option
a
is preferred if you are only adding a single workspace, option
b
will validate every single workspace in the entire file, leading to a much longer output):
To validate a single workspace:
./scripts/orchestration.py
--path path/to/file.yaml
-n workspace_name
To validate multiple workspaces:
./scripts/orchestration.py
--path path/to/file.yaml
-n workspace_name -n workspace_name_2
To validate all workspaces in the given file:
./scripts/orchestration.py
--path path/to/file.yaml
-a
Windows users only:
You may need to prepend the above command with "python" or "python3"
.
PowerShell users only:
To set your GitHub PAT as the appropriately named environment variable:
$Env:GH_TOKEN='ghp_XXX'
After a few seconds, you'll see output similar to the following, assuming a successful run (troubleshooting steps at the end if needed):
Commit your files: If you now run a
git status
you'll see your YAML file you edited, as well as 1 or more JSON files that were automatically edited by this script. Commit all these files and push to the remote repo. Do NOT edit JSON files directly. Do NOT run orchestration then edit any file without re-running orchestration.
Create a PR: Ensure you follow the rules specified in
prlint.json
. Failure to follow that may result in delayed workspace creation.
Make a post in
Terraform PR Requests
requesting review of your PR.
Include a clickable, working hyperlink to your PR. Not just a plain-text URL.
Wait for all the PR checks to complete before requesting review of your PR. Also ensure that no resources are being destroyed (unless you're intentionally removing something)
Once someone has reviewed, approved, and merged your PR, it can take a few minutes to create the workspace in Terraform Cloud. If the workspace has not been provisioned after 1hr, please contact the CCOE TF team or the person who approved your PR for assistance.
Once the workspace shows up in
Terraform Cloud
, you may start using your new workspace.
Section 3: Deleting your workspace
Workspace deletion follows the same flow, except you are deleting the workspace block you added, and passing a different flag to
orchestration.py
:
Delete your workspace block
Run:
./scripts/orchestration.py --path path/to/file.yaml
--
remove
You should see output similar to:
your-workspace namespace has been removed
Follow the rest of the steps from above.
Troubleshooting
This will update your PR with the latest changes.
Examples
These are actual workspace definitions pulled from various files, and demonstrations of the appropriate command to run given that workspace.
Note: All these examples are run from the root of the repo. This is not necessarily required. You can run the script from anywhere in the repo, you just need to make sure your
--path
is correct. Use tab auto-complete to ensure a valid path.
Example 1: Validating a single AWS workspace
Workspace Definition
Running the script
Example 2: Validating multiple workspaces at once

292033023.title: Terraform Basics
292033023.plain_text:
Terraform recap
Terraform is an infrastructure as code tool to create resources, PG&E has
requirement
that all cloud resources are created using IaC (Infrastructure as Code).
Terraform workflow is as follows
Write – Design and Develop infrastructure configuration files
Init – Initialize directory containing terraform configuration files
Plan – Review the changes to your infrastructure
Apply – Provision your infrastructure
Terraform remembers infrastructure provisioned by updating a state file
Terraform opensource
Typical users’ exposure to terraform
Open source terraform client
Plan/apply is executed from users’ desktop
State is stored locally
Workspaces are not mandatory
Terraform Cloud at PG&E
PG&E CCoE supported infrastructure as code
Version controlled using GitHub
Plan/apply is executed from terraform cloud
State is managed in terraform cloud
Workspaces are mandatory

319719814.title: Terraform Module PRs - Release Notes Pipeline
319719814.plain_text:
What is it?
It is a CodePipeline/Lambda script that parses release notes as entered by TF module developers and sends the updates to MS Teams. It also checks for certain criteria that must be met in the PR and the updates made .
Why?
The release notes Lambda has two purposes:
To update users of module development and let them know when there are updates (and what those updates are)
To enhance the standards of TF module developers.
How does it work?
On PR merge*, a CodepPipeline pipeline is started via GitHub webhook. This pipeline invokes a Lambda function that retrieves the newly merged PR from GitHub, parses out the "Release Notes" section entered by the developer, runs checks against those release notes as well as the files that were updated. Assuming all is well, a "Successful" Adaptive Card JSON payload is sent to an MS Teams channel with the validated release notes. If the validation and checks fail, a "Failure" Adaptive Card JSON payload is sent to an MS Teams channel. In both cases, links to the PRs are included.
*
eventually this should be a pre-merge hook
Developer pushes and merges a TF module update PR
GitHub sends message to Codepipeline via webhook notifying of the PR merge
Codepipeline invokes the Lambda
Lambda parses release notes and runs checks on the PR, and sends results to MS Teams
MS Teams replies with HTTP status code of success or failure
Lambda reports results to Codepipeline job.
What checks does it perform?
Ensures only 1 module was updated by checking what files were updated in the PR
Ensures the module name and version in the release notes matches what module was actually updated and its actual version in the module.info
Ensures that if a module was updated, release notes were submitted (there are times where there will be PRs in that repo where someone isn't actually updating a module. Updates to this script for instance)
Ensures the inverse of the above bullet point, that if there are release notes, that a module was actually updated (we don't want erroneous updates being sent to Teams)
Other notes
It interacts with two different Github API endpoints:
https://api.github.com/search/issues
https://api.github.com/repos
The first is used for finding and retrieving the PR itself. The second is for retrieving the PR files.
The Lambda uses raw GitHub API queries instead of something like PyGithub, due to PyGithub's cryptography dependency not playing well with Lambda at the time of creation. It was trying to invoke that module multiple times for some reason. Its possible it was a temporary issue and it may be worth revisiting in the future.
Account:
514712703977
Codepipeline ARN:
arn:aws:codepipeline:us-west-2:514712703977:tf-module-release-notes
Lambda ARN:
arn:aws:lambda:us-west-2:514712703977:function:tf-release-notes
Github Repo:
Uses the pge-terraform-modules repo, exists in the "scripts" subfolder.
TFC Workspace:
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/workspaces/tf-release-notes-pipeline
- Working directory is the "terraform" subfolder. Therefore if updating the Lambda code, it requires a manual run. In both cases, a manual apply is required.

360437661.title: Terraform Module CI Validation Pipeline
360437661.plain_text:
<work in progress>
Here is the initial Jira story I wrote up before splitting it up into smaller chunks (at least 2 stories: plan, implenentation):
Summary
As a Cloud Platform Engineer, I want to implement a Terraform CI validation pipeline that runs automatically for newly submitted or updated Terraform modules.
Description
Create a CI-based validation pipeline that executes on pull request creation or updates for Terraform modules. The pipeline will enforce formatting, linting, documentation, and baseline quality requirements before merge. This will be used as a preliminary review of the update and does not replace human review as it will be impossible to account for nuance and every possibility of “issues” that could arise.
Scope includes (mostly a list of ideas that we COULD include):
terraform fmt -check
terraform init -backend=false -upgrade=false
tflint
Terraform documentation validation (e.g., generated README exists and is up to date)
Ensure TF tests exist and are up-to-date
Validate directory and file structure conformance
root contains
main.tf
,
variables.tf
,
outputs.tf
,
module.info
there is an example subfolder, and tests subfolder (modules subfolder may not always be a thing - perhaps generate a warning if missing?)
Additional static analysis or standards checks as required, some ideas:
Module interface quality checks
variables must have descriptions, explicit types, avoid
any
(we have tons of any unfortunately - would need to start off as a warning to encourage correction - the type should be defined, especially on complex objects and maps where we use
any
a lot right now)
outputs must have descriptions
??
These can possibly be accomplished with various tools including tflint custom rules.
This validation will avoid security scanning (via something like Checkov or tfsec) as that is already handled by the repo’s Wiz integrations.
Result:
The pipeline runs automatically on PRs
Checks are enforced as required status checks before merge
Ensures consistent quality and standards across Terraform modules
Buyer:
@Balaji Venkataraman
Seller:
@Eric Barnard

148668531.title: RDS Aurora PostgreSQL
148668531.plain_text:
The Terraform RDS Aurora Postgresql submodule can be found
here
in the PGETech Terraform private registry.
Limitations
At this time of writing, the RDS modules are still undergoing testing and should not be used to deploy Production grade DB instances.  While most SAF compliance is built into this module, we are still working out the issues surrounding the implementation of the Secrets Manager Rotation.
Terraform module which creates an RDS Aurora Postgresql Database.
Pre-Requisites:
1. IAM Role rds-enhanced-monitoring-role exists within your account (should be automatically deployed to PG&E accounts)
2. RDS VPC Endpoint exists for the account.  If not, you can deploy one in the account as shown with the rds_vpc_endpoint example module (rds/examples/rds_vpc_endpoint)
Notes:
1. At this time, the secrets manager is not implemented to store the master credentials.  User will need to do so manually and ensure that the secret is rotated before 90 days. All other Saf           Compliance is implemented.
2. This module will also create various SSM Parameters to store data pertaining to the DB Cluster and it's instances.
3. This module includes logic to pass in private subnets and vpc(/vpc/privatesubnet1/id, /vpc/privatesubnet2/id, /vpc/privatesubnet3/id, /vpc/id).  Be sure that the account in which you are using has adequate IP space for the subnets stored in Parameter Store.
4. Serverless v1 clusters do not support managed master user password, Aurora Serverless v2 is supported for MySQL 8.0 onwards & PostgreSQL 13 onwards.
5. if the DataClassification is not internal or public, passing null KMS will result in error, hence create the KMS key using the module in the code, its a 2 step process
a. first comment all the code except for kms module, run terraform apply, this create the KMS key and stores in state file.
b. uncomment remaining code, replace null for kms for all the arguments with the module created value.
In your Terraform project, you'll want to use the configuration block provided in the terraform module registry in your
main.tf
file.  You can use the rds_aurora_postgresql example module as a guide in your development, but be aware that the example module is using a relative path for the module source.  You will want to replace that section with the source and version attributes shown in the code block below.
This module is designed to deploy resources with the same identifying name.
Dependencies for RDS Aurora PostgreSQL
The dependences used for RDS Aurora PostgreSQL are main_security_group (0.0.7), ssm_parameter (0.0.8), validate-pge-tags (0.0.5), ws (0.0.5).
These dependencies are collectively contribute to the setup, management and security of an RDS Aurora PostgreSQL instance on AWS using Terraform.
main_security_group
which defines the network access rules for this module and also used to controls inbound and outbound traffic, ensuring proper security for database.
ssm_parameter
is used for storing configuration parameters, secrets, and other sensitive information securely and also used to manage
database collection strings and API keys.
validate-pge-tags
are metadata labels which helps to organize and manage resources associated with Aurora PostgreSQL instances.
ws
refers to a workspace where configuration, scripts and other relevant files as stored and also help to maintain a structured approach to managing resources.

148668537.plain_text:

here

2. RDS VPC Endpoint exists for the account.  If not, you can deploy one in the account as shown with the rds_vpc_endpoint example module (rds/examples/rds_vpc_endpoint)
Notes:
1. At this time, this module is not fully SAF 2.0 Compliant.  Compliance will be implemented at a later date.
2. This module will also create various SSM Parameters to store data pertaining to the DB Cluster and it's instances.
3. This module includes logic to pass in private subnets and vpc(/vpc/privatesubnet1/id, /vpc/privatesubnet2/id,
/vpc/privatesubnet3/id, /vpc/id).  Be sure that the account in which you are using has adequate IP space for the subnets stored in Parameter Store.
4. Serverless v1 clusters do not support managed master user password, Aurora Serverless v2 is supported for MySQL 8.0 onwards & PostgreSQL 13 onwards.
5. if the DataClassification is not internal or public, passing null KMS will result in error, hence create the KMS key using the module in the code, its a 2 step process
a. first comment all the code except for kms module, run terraform apply, this create the KMS key and stores in state file.
b. uncomment remaining code, replace null for kms for all the arguments with the module created value.
In your Terraform project, you'll want to use the configuration block provided in the terraform module registry in your
main.tf
file.  You can use the rds_aurora_mysql example module as a guide in your development, but be aware that the example module is using a relative path for the module source.  You will want to replace that section with the source and version attributes shown in the code block below.
This module is designed to deploy resources with the same identifying name.
module "rds_aurora-mysql" { source = "app.terraform.io/pgetech/rds/aws//modules/aurora-mysql" version = "0.0.11" # insert required variables here }
Dependencies for RDS Aurora MYSQL
The dependences used for RDS Aurora MYSQL are main_security_group (0.0.7), ssm_parameter (0.0.8), validate-pge-tags (0.0.5), ws (0.0.5).
These dependencies are used for security enhance and enables automation in managing Aurora MYSQL.
`
main_security_group
is used to control the inbound and outbound traffic in MYSQL instances and it will act as virtual firewalls.
ssm_parameter
is used for storing configuration parameters, secrets, and other sensitive information securely and also used to manage
database collection strings and API keys.
validate-pge-tags
are metadata labels which helps to organize and manage resources associated with Aurora MYSQL  instances.
ws
refers to a workspace where configuration, scripts and other relevant files as stored.

148668527.title: RDS Oracle
148668527.plain_text:
The Terraform RDS Oracle submodule can be found
here
in the PGETech Terraform private registry.  Please refer to the
example
rds_oracle module on how to configure your oracle module.  The example is deployed with an Amazon RDS Oracle Database 19c.
Limitations
At this time of writing, the RDS modules are still undergoing testing and should not be used to deploy Production grade DB instances.  While most SAF compliance is built into this module, we are still sorting out issues that began after the implementation of the Secrets Manager Rotation.  While the secrets manager rotation executes successfully in our example Oracle module, the configurations necessary for the rotation have caused the kerberos enablement to fail.  Additionally, the use of Kerberos for authentication is still in its beginning phases.
Terraform module which creates SAF2.0 Oracle RDS Database.
Pre-Requisites:
1. Directory Service and it's domain are shared within your account.  Your security group egress cidr should reflect the VPC of the Managed AD account.
2. IAM Role rds-directoryservice-kerberos-access-role exists within your account (should be automatically deployed to PG&E accounts).If not available work with respective team in PG&E.
3. S3 integration requires an s3 bucket has been previously created and the arn passed in this module as a variable
4. S3 key must exist/ uploaded to S3 bucket before running the module
5. To have automated replication, multi region KMS must be created before running the module
6. RDS VPC Endpoint exists for the account.  If not, you can deploy one in the account as shown with the rds_vpc_endpoint example module (rds/examples/rds_vpc_endpoint)
7. if the DataClassification is not internal or public, passing null KMS will result in error, hence create the KMS key using the module in the code, its a 2 step process
a. first comment all the code except for kms module, run terraform apply, this create the KMS key and stores in state file.
b. uncomment remaining code, replace null for kms for all the arguments with the module created value.
Notes
If the password you pass in does not satisfy the minimum length as per SAF compliance which is min 16 chars, a validation is put in place to meet the requirement, Password will be stored in secrets manager.
If argument manage_master_user_password is set to "true", then user provided password will be disregarded, and AWS creates random password and creates a secret manager and stores this password, rotation is enabled and default is 7 days.
In your Terraform project, you'll want to use the configuration block provided in the terraform module registry in your main.tf file.  Be sure to configure your module with a valid AWS Directory Service domain ID and with the IAM Role 'rds-directoryservice-kerberos-access-role'.  Both should be shared within your aws account.  You should also ensure that a RDS VPC endpoint exists in the account.  You can use the rds_oracle example module as a guide in your development, but be aware that the example module is using a relative path for the module source.  You will want to replace that section with the source and version attributes shown in the code block below.
To implement the Secrets Manager rotation, you'll need to provide a secrets manager rotation function
template
(provided by AWS) and the necessary libraries for your lambda layer.   Please note that the function templates/libraries require a runtime of Python 3.7, and it is recommended that you use an Amazon Linux 2 AMI to download the necessary libraries.  Please see the attached document for directions on installing the necessary libraries for the cx_Oracle import.  Be sure to ensure that the Secrets Manager and RDS vpc endpoints have been deployed in your account with DNS enabled.  In order for the Secrets Manager rotation to work, both vpc endpoints should include the same security group, or a security group with the same permissions as that of the RDS security group as shown in the example module.  The security group in the example module allows traffic on the necessary RDS ports and port 443 for the vpc cidrs.
Dependencies for RDS Oracle
The dependencies for Submodule Oracle are aws_iam_role (0.0.8), lambda_layer (0.0.15), main_security_group (0.0.7), rotation_lambda_function (0.0.15),
rotation_lambda_iam_role (0.0.8), secrets_manager (0.0.8), validate-pge-tags (0.0.5), ws (0.0.5).
These dependencies provide secure credential management, automated rotation, network security.
aws_iam_role
is likely used to grant necessary AWS services and resources interacting with Oracle. It also provides permissions for Lambda functions, Secrets Manager and other services.
lambda_layer
is used to share code and dependencies across multiple Lambda Functions. This lambda layer contains functions and libraries used by lambda functions associated with oracle databases.
main_security_group
is used to define the network access rules for Oracle related resources, it controls the inbound and outbound traffic to oracle instances, ensuring proper network security.
rotation_lambda_function
is responsible for the credential rotation and other automated tasks related to oracle database management, this is triggered periodically to update credentials securely.
rotation_lambda_iam_role
which provides the necessary permissions to the Lambda function to interact with Oracle RDS instance, secrets manager, and other required AWS services
secrets_manager
is used to securely store and manage sensitive information, such as oracle database credentials.
validate-pge-tags
is used for validating tags associated with AWS resources, Tags can be used for organization management ensuring that resources related to Oracle.
ws
is workspace or environment that represent a designated space for organizing configurations, scripts, or files specific to Oracle and is used to maintain clean and organized structure.
Design Requirements for RDS Oracle
https://wiki.comp.pge.com/display/CCE/AWS+RDS+Module+for+Oracle+-+Findings+and+Requirements+for+Design

300754986.title: RDS start stop
300754986.plain_text:
Problem description:
In AWS RDS, a database instance that has been stopped will automatically start after 7 days, and this behavior is by design.
Unfortunately, there's no direct way to disable the automatic restart feature entirely, as it's an AWS-imposed limit to prevent the instance from remaining in a stopped state indefinitely.
Workaround implementation:
you can implement a workaround by automating the process of stopping the instance again after it auto-starts. Here’s how you can prevent it from staying up by setting up automation to stop the instance:
Use AWS Lambda with a CloudWatch Rule
You can create a scheduled AWS Lambda function that checks if the RDS instance has started, and if so, automatically stops it.
Terraform submodule is developed to create the necessary AWS resources to automate the rds start and stop as per the enduser requirements.
please note: when creating the RDS cluster/instance , make to sure to add tags autostart = "yes" and autostart ="no" because lambda functions retrieves the tags from the rds instances and based on its status the action to "start" or "stop" will be performed.
Below is the link for pge private registry for this module.
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/registry/modules/private/pgetech/rds/aws/0.0.19/submodules/rds-start-stop
Usage of this module is shown in the examples for the same.
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/registry/modules/private/pgetech/rds/aws/0.0.19/examples/rds_start_stop

148668529.title: RDS SQLServer
148668529.plain_text:
The Terraform RDS SQLServer submodule can be found
here
in the PGETech Terraform private registry.
