70321798.title: DynamoDB of AMPS data
70321798.plain_text:
Overview
The on-premise AMPS database contains detail information of all applications in PG&E.  We want to leverage the AMPS data when tagging the AWS resources.  In the Shared Services Production account, there is a scheduled job to run a Lambda function every night to download data from the on-premise AMPS database into the DynamoDB table amps_master.  Then, the same Lambda function will transform the AMPS data to update another DynamoDB table amps_tags which contains only applications deployed in AWS.  The amps_tags table has attributes mapped to the AWS tagging key naming convention.  This table will be referenced by the automated tagging Lambda function which will be covered in another wiki page.
Lambda function: load-amps-master
This function is scheduled to run every night.  It connects to the on-premise AMPS database to refresh and update the following two DynamoDB tables.  Its CloudWatch log groups is /aws/lambda/load-amps-master .
DynamoDB table: amps_master
Primary key: APP_ID
This table is a copy of the on-premise AMPS database and has the attributes named as in the on-premise AMPS.  Only a subset of items in the tables are related to the AWS application.  This table will be dropped and re-created every night to refresh the AMPS data.
DynamoDB table: amps_tags
Primary key: AppID
This table contains items in the amps_master table where the APP_ID exists in the AWS_Accounts table.  Its attributes are also named according to the tagging standard.
Git Repo
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-amps-tags

50888444.title: AWS Encryption Strategies
50888444.plain_text:
Overview
The wiki documents AWS's best practices for encryption strategies, so that data is securely encrypted at rest and while in-transit.
This Wiki outlines:
What are the encryption types at rest and while in-transit for EBS, S3, and RDS?
AWS Key Management Service (KMS) and best practices.
Symbols
Description
Caution
AWS ProServe Recommended
Important
See section below
Encryption of Data at Rest and In-Transit
Encrypting data at rest is vital for regulatory compliance to ensure that sensitive data saved on disks is not readable by any user or application without a valid key. Below outline the AWS services that provide data encryption at rest with important items to consider.
Encryption data-in-transit protect confidentiality by using Transport Layer Security (TLS). TLS is used with every AWS API to project for data upload and download. It is also used for configuration changes. AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) or on-premise Certificate Authority solutions can be used to handle the complexity of creating and managing public TLS certificates for your AWS based websites and applications.
Amazon EBS Encryption
Amazon EBS encryption offers a simple encryption solution for your EBS volumes without the need to build, maintain, and secure your key management infrastructure.
When you create an encrypted EBS volume and attach it to a supported instance type, the following types of data are encrypted:
Data at rest inside the volume.
All data are moving between the volume and the instance.
All snapshots created from the volume.
All volumes created from those snapshots
EBS encrypts your volume with a data key using the industry-standard
AES-256
algorithm.
Amazon EBS encryption uses AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) customer master keys (CMKs) when creating encrypted volumes and any snapshots created from them.
A unique AWS-managed CMK is created for you automatically in each region where you store AWS assets.
There is
no
direct way to encrypt an existing unencrypted volume or to remove encryption from an encrypted volume. However, you can migrate data between encrypted and unencrypted volumes. You can also apply for a new encryption status while copying a snapshot. You cannot remove encryption from an encrypted snapshot.
Encryption is supported by all EBS volume types
General Purpose SSD [gp2]
Provisioned IOPS SSD [io1]
Throughput Optimized HDD [st1]
Cold HDD [sc1]
Magnetic [standard])
Supported Instance Types
Amazon EBS encryption is available on the instance types listed below.
General purpose: A1, M3, M4, M5, M5d, T2, and T3
Compute-optimized: C3, C4, C5, C5d, and C5n
Memory-optimized: cr1.8xlarge, R3, R4, R5, R5d, X1, X1e, and z1d
Storage optimized: D2, h1.2xlarge, h1.4xlarge, I2, and I3
Accelerated computing: F1, G2, G3, P2, and P3
Bare metal: i3.metal, u-6tb1.metal, u-9tb1.metal, and u-12tb1.metal
S3 Encryption
Data protection refers to protecting data while in-transit (as it travels to and from Amazon S3) and at rest (while it is stored on disks in Amazon S3 data centers). You can protect data in transit by using SSL or by using client-side encryption.
You have the following options for protecting data at rest in Amazon S3.
Use Server-Side Encryption
You request Amazon S3 to encrypt your object before saving it on disks in its data centers and decrypt it when you download the objects.
Server-side encryption is about data encryption at rest; Amazon S3 encrypts your data at the object level as it writes it to disks in its data centers and decrypts it for you when you access it.
As long as you authenticate your request and you have access permissions, there is no difference in the way you access encrypted or unencrypted objects.
You can't apply different types of server-side encryption to the same object simultaneously. You have three mutually exclusive options depending on how you choose to manage the encryption keys:
Server-Side Encryption with Amazon S3-Managed Keys (SSE-S3)
Each object is encrypted with a unique key employing strong multi-factor encryption.
As an additional safeguard, it encrypts the key itself with a master key that it regularly rotates.
Amazon S3 server-side encryption uses one of the strongest block ciphers available, 256-bit Advanced Encryption Standard (AES-256), to encrypt your data.
Server-Side Encryption with AWS KMS-Managed Keys (SSE-KMS)
Similar to SSE-S3, but with some additional benefits along with some additional charges for using this service.
There are separate permissions for the use of an envelope key (that is, a key that protects your data's encryption key) that provides added protection against unauthorized access of your objects in S3.
SSE-KMS also provides you with an audit trail of when your key was used and by whom.
You have the option to create and manage encryption keys yourself or use a default key that is unique to you, the service you're using, and the region you're working in. Protecting Data Using Server-Side Encryption with AWS KMS–
Managed Keys (SSE-KMS).
Server-Side Encryption with Customer-Provided Keys (SSE-C)
You manage the encryption keys,
and Amazon S3 manages the encryption, as it writes to disks, and decryption when you access your objects. For more information, see Protecting Data Using Server-Side Encryption with Customer-Provided Encryption Keys (SSE-C).
Use Client-Side Encryption
Client-side encryption is the act of encrypting data before sending it to Amazon S3.
To enable client-side encryption, you have the following options:
Use an AWS KMS-managed customer master key
Use a client-side master key
The following AWS SDKs support client-side encryption:
AWS SDK for .NET
AWS SDK for Go
AWS SDK for Java
AWS SDK for PHP
AWS SDK for Ruby
RDS Data at Rest Encryption
Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) builds on Amazon EBS encryption to provide full disk encryption for database volumes.
When you create an encrypted database instance with Amazon RDS, Amazon RDS creates an encrypted EBS volume on your behalf to store the database.
Data stored at rest on the volume, database snapshots, automated backups, and read replicas are all encrypted under the KMS CMK that you specified when you created the database instance.
Similar to Amazon EBS, you can set up an AWS Lambda function to monitor for the creation of new RDS instances via the reateDBInstance API call via CloudTrail. Within the CreateDBInstance event, ensure that KmsKeyId parameter is set to the expected CMK.
Amazon RDS encrypted DB instances provide an additional layer of data protection by securing your data from unauthorized access to the underlying storage.
You can use Amazon RDS encryption to increase data protection of your applications deployed in the cloud and to fulfill compliance requirements for data-at-rest encryption.
Amazon RDS also supports encrypting an Oracle or SQL Server DB instance with Transparent Data Encryption (TDE)
You can use SSL from your application to encrypt a connection to a DB instance running MySQL, MariaDB, SQL Server, Oracle, or PostgreSQL.
AWS Key Management Service (KMS)
AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) is a service that combines secure, highly available hardware and software to provide a key management system scaled for the cloud.
Protecting Data Using Server-Side Encryption with AWS KMS–Managed Keys (SSE-KMS)
AWS KMS uses customer master keys (CMKs) to encrypt your Amazon S3 objects.
The first time you add an SSE-KMS–encrypted object to a bucket in a region, a default CMK is created for you automatically.
This key is used for SSE-KMS encryption unless you select a CMK that you created separately using AWS Key Management Service.
Creating your own CMK gives you more flexibility, including the ability to create, rotate, disable, and define access controls, and to audit the encryption keys used to protect your data.
If you are uploading or accessing objects encrypted by SSE-KMS, you need to use AWS Signature Version 4 for added security.
The highlights of SSE-KMS are:
You can choose to create and manage encryption keys yourself, or you can choose to use your default service key uniquely generated on a customer by service by region level.
The ETag in the response is not the MD5 of the object data.
The data keys used to encrypt your data are also encrypted and stored alongside the data they protect.
Auditable master keys can be created, rotated, and disabled from the AWS KMS console.
The security controls in AWS KMS can help you meet encryption-related compliance requirements.
KMS Best Practices
The AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF) whitepaper guides coordinating the
different parts of organizations that are moving to cloud computing.
Identity and Access Management
Enables you to create multiple access control mechanisms and manage the permissions for each.
Key Policies
Key policies are the primary way to control access to CMKs in AWS KMS. Each CMK has a key policy attached to it that defines permissions on the use and management of the key.
It's also possible to constrain a CMK so that it can only be used by specific AWS services through the use of the kms:
ViaService conditional statement within the CMK key policy
Key policies specify a resource, action, effect, principal, and conditions to grant access to CMKs. Key policies allow you to push more granular permissions to CMKs to enforce least privilege.
Remember that explicit deny policies take precedence over implicitly deny policies. When you use NotPrincipal in the same policy statement as "Effect: Deny," the permissions specified in the policy statement are explicitly denied to all principals except for the ones specified. A top-level KMS policy can explicitly deny access to virtually all KMS operations except for the roles that need them. This technique helps prevent unauthorized users from granting themselves KMS access.
Cross Account Sharing of Keys
A delegation of permissions to a CMK within AWS KMS can occur when you include the root principal of a trusted account within the CMK key policy.
The trusted account then can further delegate these permissions to IAM users and roles within their account using IAM policies.
While this approach may simplify the management of the key policy, it also relies on the trusted accounts to ensure that the delegated permissions are correctly managed.
The other approach would be to explicitly manage permissions to all authorized users using only the KMS key policy, which, in turn, could make the key policy complex and less manageable. Regardless of the approach you take, the specific trust should be broken out on a per key basis to ensure that you adhere to the least privilege model.
Encryption Context
In addition to limiting permission to the AWS KMS APIs, AWS KMS also gives you the ability to add layer of authentication for your KMS API calls utilizing encryption context. The encryption context is a key-value pair of additional data that you want to be associated with AWS KMS-protected information.
Detective Controls
Provides you the capability for native logging and visibility into the service.
CMK Auditing
AWS KMS is integrated with CloudTrail. To audit the usage of your keys in AWS KMS, you should enable CloudTrail logging in your AWS account. This ensures that all KMS API calls made on keys in your AWS account are automatically logged.
CMK Use Validation
In addition to capturing audit data associated with key management and use, you should ensure that the data you are reviewing aligns with your established best practices and policies.
One method is to use AWS Config rules. By using AWS Config rules, you can ensure that the configuration of many of the AWS services is set up appropriately. For example, with EBS volumes you can use the AWS Config rule ENCRYPTED_VOLUMES to validate that attached EBS volumes are encrypted.
Infrastructure Security
Provides you with the capability to shape your security controls to fit your requirements.
AWS-managed CMKs
AWS-managed and customer-managed. An AWS-managed CMK is created when you choose to enable server-side encryption of an AWS resource under the AWS-managed CMK for that service for the first time (e.g., SSE-KMS).
AWS-managed CMK is unique to your AWS account and the Region in which it's used.
Data Protection
Provides you with the capability for maintaining visibility and control over data.
Enforcing Data at Rest Encryption within AWS Services
There should be a requirement that the encryption of all data that meets a specific classification. Depending on the specific service, you can enforce data encryption policies through preventative or detective controls. For some services like Amazon S3, a policy can prevent
storing unencrypted data. For other services, the most efficient mechanism is to monitor the creation of storage resources and check whether encryption is enabled appropriately. In the event that unencrypted storage is created, you have some possible responses ranging
from deleting the storage resource to notifying an administrator.
Common AWS KMS Use Cases
Encrypting PCI Data Using AWS KMS
Secret Management Using AWS KMS and Amazon S3
Encrypting Lambda Environment Variables
Encrypting Data within Systems Manager Parameter Store
Data at Rest Encryption with Amazon S3
Data at Rest Encryption with Amazon EBS
Data at Rest Encryption with Amazon RDS
Incident Response
Provides you with the capability to respond to, manage, reduce harm, and restore operations during and after an incident.
Security Automation of AWS KMS
During your monitoring of your CMKs, if a specific action is detected, an AWS Lambda function
could be configured to disable the CMK or perform any other incident response actions as
dictated by your local security policies. Without human intervention, a potential exposure
could be cut off in minutes by leveraging the automation tools inside AWS.
Deleting and Disabling CMKs
While deleting CMKs is possible it has significant ramifications to an organization.
You should first consider whether it's sufficient to set the CMK state to disabled on keys that you no longer intend to use. This will prevent all future use of the CMK.
Deleting a key must be very carefully thought out. Data can't be decrypted if the
corresponding CMK has been deleted. Moreover, once a CMK is deleted, it's gone forever.
AWS has no means to recover a deleted CMK once it's finally deleted. Just as with other critical
operations in AWS, you should apply a policy that requires MFA for CMK deletion.
References
1
Amazon EBS Encryption
+
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/EBSEncryption.html+
2
AWS re:invent 2017: Best Practices for Implementing AWS Key Management Service (SID330)
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X1eZjXQ55ec
3
AWS Key Management Service Best Practices
https://d1.awsstatic.com/whitepapers/aws-kms-best-practices.pdf

51055244.title: AWS Secrets Manager
51055244.plain_text:
Overview
To define and document the secrets management strategy so that you can enable the rotation of secrets securely without the need for code deployments.
Document Deliverable:
What is the benefit of the service or feature?
How Secrets Manager offers built-in integration for AWS services.
Recommendation for future needs.
AWS Secrets Manager is an AWS service that makes it easier for you to manage secrets.
Secrets
Database credentials
Passwords
3rd-party API keys
Arbitrary text.
You can store and control access to these secrets centrally by using the Secrets Manager console, the Secrets Manager command line interface (CLI), or the Secrets Manager API and SDKs.
Symbols
Description
Caution
AWS ProServe Recommended
Important
Currently Implemented
Features
Programmatically Retrieve Encrypted Secrets
Programmatically Retrieve Encrypted Secrets Values at Runtime Instead of Storing Them
Improve your security posture by removing hard-coded credentials from your app's source code, and by not storing credentials in or with the app itself.
Replace stored credentials with a runtime call to the Secrets Manager web service, so you can retrieve the credentials dynamically when you need them.
Store Any Secret
Can store text up to 4096 bytes in length in the encrypted secret data portion of a secret.
This typically includes the connection details of the database or service. can include:
Server name
IP address
Port number
User name and password that are used to sign in to the service.
The protected text doesn't include:
The secret name and description
The rotation or expiration settings
The ARN of the AWS KMS customer master key (CMK) that's associated with the secret
Any AWS tags that you might attach
Encrypt Your Secret Data
Automatically Rotate Your Secrets
Databases with Fully Configured and Ready-to-Use Rotation Support
When you choose to enable rotation, the following Amazon Relational Database Service (Amazon RDS) databases are fully supported with AWS written and tested Lambda rotation function templates, and full configuration of the rotation process:
Amazon Aurora
MySQL
PostgreSQL
Oracle
MariaDB
Microsoft SQL
Control Who Can Access Secrets
Access to AWS Secrets Manager requires AWS credentials. Those credentials must have permissions to access the AWS resources that you want to access, such as your Secrets Manager secrets.
(IAM) permission policies to your users, groups, and roles that grant or deny access to specific secrets, and restrict what they can do with those secrets.
the permissions capabilities of AWS and IAM permission policies, you can tightly control which users (or services) have access to your secrets. You can specify which API, CLI, and console operations the user can perform on the authorized secrets.
IAM policy language, you can choose to limit the user to only a subset of your secrets—or even to one individual secret—by using tags as filters. You can also restrict a user to specific versions of a secret by using staging labels as filters.
You can also determine who can manage which secrets. You can determine who can update or modify the secrets and their associated metadata. If you have admin (all, sometimes called
/
meaning "all actions on all resources") permissions to the AWS Secrets Manager service, you can delegate access to Secrets Manager tasks by granting permissions to others.
You can attach permission policies to your users, groups, and roles, and specify the secrets that the attached identities can access. These are called "
identity-based policies
".
Alternatively, you can attach a permission policy directly to the secret and specify who can access it. This is called a "
resource-based policy
". Either way, those policies specify what actions each principal can perform on which secrets.
Using Resource-based Policies for Secrets Manager
You can attach a resource-based policy directly to the secret to grant permissions that specify who is allowed to read or modify the secret and its versions. Unlike an identity-based policy (which automatically applies to the user, group, or role that it's attached to), a resource-based policy attached to a secret uses the Principal element to identify who the policy applies to.
The Principal element can include users and roles from the same account as the secret or principals from other accounts.
Controlling Which Principals Can Access a Secret - resource-based policy attaches directly to a secret, the Resource automatically and implicitly becomes the secret that the policy is attached to. You now get to specify the Principal element. The Principal element enables you to specify which IAM users, groups, roles, accounts, or AWS service principals can access this secret, and which actions they can perform on this secret.
Grant Read-Only Access to a Role -A application that's running on an Amazon EC2 instance needs access to a database to perform its required tasks. The application must retrieve the database credentials from Secrets Manager. To make a request to Secrets Manager, you must have AWS credentials with permissions to perform the request. The recommended way to achieve this is to create an IAM role that's attached to the EC2 instance profile.
Best Practices
Protect Additional Sensitive Information
A secret often includes several pieces of information beyond the user name and password. Depending on the database, service, or website, you can choose to include additional sensitive data. This data can include password hints, or question-and-answer pairs that you can use to recover your password if you forgot it.
Make sure that any information that might be used to gain access to the credentials in the secret is protected as securely as the credentials themselves. Don't store such information in the Description or any other non-encrypted part of the secret.
Instead, store all such sensitive information as part of the encrypted secret value (in either the SecretString or SecretBinary field). You can store up to 4096 characters in the secret. In the SecretString field, the text usually takes the form of JSON key-value string pairs
Improve Performance by Using the AWS provided Client-side Caching Components
To use your secrets most efficiently, you should not simply retrieve the secret value from Secrets Manager every time you need to use its credentials. Instead, use a Secrets Manager client component that knows how to cache your secrets and update them only when required because of rotation. AWS has created such client components for you and made them available as open-source.
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/manage_retrieve-secret.html#use-client-side-caching-components
Mitigate the Risks of Logging and Debugging Your Lambda Function
When you create a custom Lambda rotation function to support your Secrets Manager secret, be careful about including debugging or logging statements in your function. These statements can cause information in your function to be written to CloudWatch.
Ensure that this logging of information to CloudWatch doesn't include any of the sensitive data that's stored in the encrypted secret value.
Mitigate the Risks of Using the AWS CLI to Store Your Secrets
When you use the AWS Command Line Interface (AWS CLI) to invoke AWS operations, you enter those commands in a command shell.
Many of these command shells include functionality that's designed to increase productivity. But this functionality could be used to compromise your secrets.
For example, in most shells, you can use the up-arrow key to see the last command that was entered. This command history feature could be exploited by anyone who walks up to your unsecured session. Also, other utilities that work in the background might have access to your command parameters.
Cross-Account Access – Should I Specify a User/Role or the Account
When you want to use a resource-based policy that's attached to a secret to grant access to an IAM principal in a different AWS account.
Specify only the other account ID – In the Principal element of the statement, you specify the Amazon Resource Name (ARN) of the "foreign" account's root. This enables the administrator of the foreign account to delegate access to roles in the foreign account. The administrator must then assign IAM permission policies to the role or roles that must be able to access the secret.
As a best practice, we recommend that you specify only the account in the secret-based policy, for the following reasons:
In both cases, you're trusting the administrator of the other account. In the first case, you trust the administrator to ensure that only authorized individuals have access to the IAM user, or can assume the specified role. This is essentially the same level of trust as specifying only the account ID. You trust that account and its administrator. When you specify only the account ID, you give the administrator the flexibility to manage their users as they see fit.
When you specify an exact role, it's internally converted to a "principal ID" that's unique to that role. If you delete the role and recreate it with the same name, it gets a new principal ID. This means that the new role doesn't automatically get access to the resource. This functionality is for security reasons, but it means that an accidental delete and restore can result in "broken" access.
When you grant permissions to only the account root, that set of permissions then becomes the limit of what the administrator of that account can delegate to their users and roles. The administrator can't grant a permission to the resource that you didn't grant to the account first.
If you choose to grant cross-account access directly to the secret without using a role, then the secret must be encrypted by using a custom AWS KMS customer master key (CMK). A principal from a different account must be granted permission to both the secret and the custom AWS KMS CMK.
Tag Your Secrets
Secrets Manager lets you tag your secrets. tag your secrets, be sure to follow these guidelines:
Use a standardized naming scheme across all your resources. Remember that tags are case sensitive.
Security/access control – You can grant or deny access to a secret by checking the tags that are attached to the secret.
Never store sensitive information for a secret in a tag.
Monitor the Use of Your AWS Secrets Manager Secrets
As a best practice, you should monitor your secrets to make sure that usage of your secrets and any changes to them are logged. This helps you to ensure that any unexpected usage or change can be investigated, and unwanted changes can be rolled back.
AWS Secrets Manager currently supports two AWS services that enable you to monitor your organization and the activity that happens within it.
Logging AWS Secrets Manager API Calls with AWS CloudTrail
All Secrets Manager actions are logged by CloudTrail and are documented in the AWS Secrets Manager API Reference.
calls to the CreateSecret, GetSecretValue and RotateSecret sections generate entries in the CloudTrail log files.
Amazon CloudWatch Events
Trigger alerts when administrator-specified operations occur in an organization.
Warned when a secret is deleted or when a secret is rotated.
If anyone tries to use a secret version while it's in its waiting period to be deleted.
Currently implemented uses of Secrets Manager
For the Contact Center 2020 project, AWS Secrets Manager is being used as follows:
The team is storing Database credentials in Secrets Manager.
This secret is encrypted with a KMS encryption key
Access to the specific secret is controlled by fine-grained IAM policy
Access to the encryption key is controlled by fine-grained IAM policy
Access to / Retrieval of the secret is audited and logged in CloudTrail
References
1
AWS Secrets Manager Best Practices
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/best-practices.html
2
Monitor the Use of Your AWS Secrets Manager Secrets
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager/latest/userguide/monitoring.html
3

49957991.title: Secrets Management
49957991.plain_text:
Secrets Management Overview
At its most fundamental first order principles, secrets management seems like a fairly intractable problem.  How can we securely generate, store, and propagate secrets - whether they be passwords, API keys, or encryption keys –
and
be able to
programmatically
retrieve them, without human intervention, across a diverse set of endpoints and consumers?  Doesn't that imply that there is somehow a 'key for the key' - and, if so – how do we secure that?
There are a variety of approaches to address these goals - and some of the leaders in this space,
today
, include:
Hashicorp
's
Vault
:  Both open source and commercial versions, supports multiple storage backends, auth mechanisms, and integrations (such as with AWS for credential management).  This product is currently the most feature rich product in the space.
KeyWhiz
by Square:  Open source key management tool supporting TLS certificates and keys, GPG keys, API tokens and database credentials
AWS
Secrets Manager
:  Cloud-native, managed service from AWS.  Integrated into AWS ecosystem, and available from anywhere (e.g. on-prem, other cloud providers)
While Hashicorp's Vault is currently the most feature-rich solution - it definitely comes with a corresponding learning curve, as well as ongoing operational implications (e.g. servers to manage, patches to monitor and apply, security vulnerability scanning, etc).  For this reason, the current architectural recommendation is to use AWS Secrets Manager, as discussed in greater detail below.
AWS Secrets Manager
AWS Secrets Manager provides an easy-to-use, low-cost solution which requires zero infrastructure footprint.  It also integrates with and leverages AWS
Key Management Service
(KMS) for fine-grained use of encryption keys to secure and restrict access to secrets.  The service is relatively new, launched in April of 2018, and most certainly still has some rough edges and room to grow.  In its current form, Secrets Manager provides:
Pay-as-you-go, zero footprint service with no upfront costs, .40 cent per secret per month plus .05 cents per 10,000 API calls (retrieval, update, etc)
Fully managed service requires no additional running instances, infrastructure to manage, security patches to apply
Secure Secrets Storage:  Encrypts secrets at rest using KMS, and in transit using TLS
Automatic Secret Rotation Without Disruption
Programmatic Retrieval of Secrets
Audit and Monitoring of Secrets Usage
CC2020 Usage of Secrets Manager:
For the Contact Center 2020 project, AWS Secrets Manager is being used as follows:
The team is storing Database credentials in Secrets Manager.
This secret is encrypted with a KMS encryption key
Access to the specific secret is controlled by fine-grained IAM policy
Access to the encryption key is controlled by fine-grained IAM policy
Access to / Retrieval of the the secret is audited and logged in CloudTrail

85590477.title: AWS CCOE Asset Tagging Automation Solution - ACLs
85590477.plain_text:
High-Level AWS Architecture Diagram
Purpose
Automatically populates the tags from the AMPS database and ensures that they are up to date for AWS Service Linked Roles, IAM Roles that have the prefix AWSServiceRoleFor*.
How it Works
AWS Service Linked Roles are typically automatically created by AWS as you use new services within your account. If these roles are automatically created by AWS, you won't have access in QA/Prod to add the tags to the roles since you don't have write access.
The config rule will evaluate IAM Roles with the prefix of AWSServiceRoleFor* and will add the mandatory tags based on the AppID of the account.
Resources Included in Tagging Automation Solution
IAM Roles with the prefix AWSServiceRoleFor*
Resources Not Included in Tagging Automation Solution
Any other IAM Roles that do not begin with AWSServiceRoleFor*
All other AWS Resources
Advantages
The config rule is automatically deployed to accounts
The tags are kept up to date with the AMPS DB
QA/Prod don't have write access so this solution remediates the Service Linked Roles for you
Disadvantages
If you create an AWS Service Linked Role, this automation does not differentiate between one you created and one AWS created
The remediation would fix all AWS Service Linked Roles
Mandatory Tags AWS Service Linked Roles Config Rule
You can view the compliance of the AWS Service Linked Roles from the Config console.
https://us-west-2.console.aws.amazon.com/config/home?region=us-west-2#/rules/details?configRuleName=mandatory-tags-aws-service-linked-roles
Compliance Evaluation
When ever you use a new service and AWS automatically creates a service linked role, the compliance evaluation for the mandatory tags config will run.
NonCompliant
In the Config console, you can view why an AWS Service Linked Role is Noncompliant by hovering over the little info icon next to the red Noncompliant.
Note: you might not see any resources in Noncompliant as they don't stay too long in this state.
Invalid AppID
Ensure that your AppID tag is in the format APP-#### and is a valid App ID within the AMPS Database.
Missing Tags
The IAM tags is missing mandatory tags. See
for the mandatory tags.
Erroneous Tag
The particular tag does not match what's in the AMPS Database.
Compliant
You can also view the compliant Stacks by changing the Compliance Status to Compliant.
Resource Details
If you click on a resource, you can view the resource details such as the tag values and the last time the remediation ran.
Configuration Timeline
As you make changes the the CloudFormation Stack, you can view the configuration timeline and see what changed.
Remediation
The remediation will use the AppID associated with the Account and retrieve the other values for tags mentioned in
from the AMPS Database associated with the AppID.
In the Config console, you will see the compliant resources.

85590414.title: AWS CCOE Asset Tagging Automation Solution - ENIs
85590414.plain_text:
High-Level AWS Architecture Diagram
Purpose
Automatically populates the tags from the AMPS database and ensures that they are up to date for ENIs.
How it Works

Resources Included in Tagging Automation Solution
All ENIs
Resources Not Included in Tagging Automation Solution

Advantages

The tags are kept up to date with the AMPS DB
QA/Prod don't have write access so this solution remediates the ENIs for you
Disadvantages
Mandatory Tags ENIs Config Rule
You can view the compliance of the ENIs from the Config console.
https://us-west-2.console.aws.amazon.com/config/home?region=us-west-2#/rules/details?configRuleName=mandatory-tags-enis
Compliance Evaluation
When ever you create a new resource inside a VPC and AWS automatically creates a service linked role, the compliance evaluation for the mandatory tags config will run.
NonCompliant
In the Config console, you can view why an ENI is Noncompliant by hovering over the little info icon next to the red Noncompliant.
Note: you might not see any resources in Noncompliant as they don't stay too long in this state.
Invalid AppID
Ensure that your AppID tag is in the format APP-#### and is a valid App ID within the AMPS Database.
Missing Tags
The IAM tags is missing mandatory tags. See
for the mandatory tags.
Erroneous Tag
The particular tag does not match what's in the AMPS Database.
Compliant
You can also view the compliant Stacks by changing the Compliance Status to Compliant.
Resource Details
If you click on a resource, you can view the resource details such as the tag values and the last time the remediation ran.
Configuration Timeline
As you make changes the the CloudFormation Stack, you can view the configuration timeline and see what changed.
Remediation
The remediation will use the AppID associated with the Account and retrieve the other values for tags mentioned in
from the AMPS Database associated with the AppID. The remediation will occur on Configuration Changes and happen automatically.
In the Config console, you will see the compliant resources.

High-Level AWS Architecture Diagram
Purpose
Automatically populates the tags from the AMPS database and ensures that they are up to date for AWS Service Linked Roles, IAM Roles that have the prefix AWSServiceRoleFor*.
How it Works
AWS Service Linked Roles are typically automatically created by AWS as you use new services within your account. If these roles are automatically created by AWS, you won't have access in QA/Prod to add the tags to the roles since you don't have write access.
The config rule will evaluate IAM Roles with the prefix of AWSServiceRoleFor* and will add the mandatory tags based on the AppID of the account.

The remediation would fix all AWS Service Linked Roles

This page describes the Cloud Security COE Service Adoption Framework. The Cloud Security COE team will continue enhancing criteria that should be met for enabling any cloud service.  This list has evolved and represents a 'point-in-time' listing of required and desired
characteristics
or

of the cloud services being reviewed, defined, and ratified for use.
Services that have not completed the full cycle to ratified, are not to be used in qa/production unless agreed upon with Cloud Security COE Leadership.

Start Service Adoption Evaluation for an AWS service

277382022.title: KSPM (Kubernetes Security Posture Management)
277382022.plain_text:
What is KSPM?
Kubernetes Security Posture Management (KSPM) is critical to Kubernetes container security and ensures compliance across K8s clusters. It is similar to Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) and deals with the automation of various security processes.
KSPM scans for Kubernetes misconfigurations, detects vulnerabilities, and assesses and categorizes threats. It integrates with CI/CD pipelines throughout the Software Development Lifecycle (SDLC).
SecOps
can enforce shift-left security by using Kubernetes Security Posture Management.
What distinguishes KSPM from CSPM?
Both CSPM and KSPM are essential for establishing a strong security foundation in modern cloud environments. KSPM is dedicated to securing Kubernetes containers and clusters, whereas CSPM evaluates and secures the entire cloud infrastructure.
KSPM provides comprehensive analytics and context-based risk prioritization, revealing hidden vulnerabilities within Kubernetes environments. It enhances the threat remediation process, identifies misconfigurations, and tackles common security challenges before they escalate. KSPM also ensures continuous compliance and keeps policy rules current. On the other hand, CSPM addresses different types of risks in cloud-native environments, continuously monitoring cloud IAM policies and cloud networking configurations.
Why is KSPM Important?
The significance of KSPM lies in several key areas:
Catching Human Errors and Oversights
KSPM tools can verify security configurations governing Kubernetes resources. Since baseline configurations are not inherently secure and human errors are possible, KSPM identifies and rectifies these issues, effectively preventing data breaches.
Cluster Management
As organizations grow, their Kubernetes environments scale accordingly. With technological advancements, configurations that were once secure may become vulnerable. For instance, Kubernetes version 1.25 announced the discontinuation of pod security policy enforcement. KSPM tools can alert users about deprecated features and suggest alternatives like Kubernetes security contexts or custom admissions controllers to mitigate emerging security gaps.
Validating Third-Party Configurations
Kubernetes ecosystems often pull container images from public Docker Hub registries and apply deployment files from GitHub. KSPM tools scan these third-party resources, detect critical security issues, and provide security teams with effective threat mitigation strategies.
Enforcing Kubernetes Compliance
KSPM tools evaluate configurations and use policy engines to identify risks associated with non-compliance. Users can write custom policies and access Kubernetes in ways that ensure data storage adheres to industry compliance standards such as GDPR, ISO 27001, and HIPAA.
KSPM involves monitoring, assessing, and ensuring the security and compliance of Kubernetes environments, especially given the increasing complexity and scale of Kubernetes deployments.
Enable RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
:
RBAC restricts access based on roles and permissions.
Avoid granting admin roles to everyone; follow the principle of least privilege.
Define permissions at the resource and action levels.
Use Proper Namespaces
:
Organize your workloads into namespaces.
Namespace isolation helps manage resources and access control.
Verify Container Images
:
Use trusted, verified container images.
Regularly scan images for vulnerabilities.
Implement Runtime Container Forensics
:
Monitor containers during runtime.
Detect and investigate suspicious activities.
Continuously Upgrade
:
Keep Kubernetes and components up to date.
Regularly apply security patches.
Proper Logging
:
Enable logging for all components.
Centralize logs for analysis and auditing.
Practice Isolation
:
Isolate sensitive workloads.
Use network policies to control communication.
Turn on Auditing
:
Enable Kubernetes audit logs.
Track API server requests and changes.
Apply CIS Benchmarks
:
Follow the Center for Internet Security (CIS) Kubernetes benchmarks.
These provide security guidelines and best practices.
PCI DSS, HIPAA, and NIST require organizations to follow CIS Benchmarks

345425496.title: IAM SVC Account Pipeline
345425496.plain_text:
📦 Repository
Use the
aws-iam-svcaccts
GitHub repository to manage all service account configurations.
Each application (e.g., CSCOE, MRAD) should have its own directory within this repo.
Under each application, create subdirectories for each AWS account that needs service accounts to be provisioned with AWS Account name
AWS Account Named directory works as working directory for workspace setup in next step
📁 Directory Structure
aws-iam-svcaccts/
└── applications/
├── CSCOE/
│   └── SecureByDesign-Test
|__ SVC-AXXXX-DB-User.tf
|__ terraform.auto.tfvars
🧭 Workspace Naming Convention
Create a Terraform Cloud workspace per AWS account using the format:
SVC-IAM-{TEAM}-{AccountName}
Examples:
SVC-IAM-CSCOE-SecureByDesign-Test
SVC-IAM-MRAD-
ACCOUNTNAME
Workspace Rules:
Auto-apply must be
disabled
.
Only Cyber Security team has write/apply permissions.
🔐 Service Account Guidelines
✅ Naming Format
Service account names must follow:
SVC-AXXXX-<custom-name>
XXXX
= 4-digit number
This format is validated in Terraform variables

310983323.title: Cloud Service Adoption Framework 3.0
310983323.plain_text:
The Service Adoption Framework (SAF) is a comprehensive set of controls designed to ensure secure and compliant adoption of cloud services across both Microsoft Azure and Amazon Web Services (AWS) platforms. These controls are based on industry best practices, including the NIST Cybersecurity Framework, CIS, Mitree, and are tailored to meet PG&E's specific requirements. The SAF controls provide a unified approach to security and compliance, regardless of the chosen cloud provider.
NEW FOR 3.0!!! MULTICLOUD SAF
Introducing our latest multicloud initiative, designed to enhance security and compliance across both AWS and Azure environments. This initiative includes:
Azure Guardrails through Azure Policy
: Implementing robust guardrails to ensure compliance and security within Azure.
MSFT Baseline Work
: Establishing a strong security baseline for Azure resources.
Onboarding Wiz to both AWS and Azure
: Integrating Wiz
https://app.wiz.io/
for comprehensive security monitoring and management across both cloud platforms.
This page and section describes the PG&E Cloud Service Adoption Framework that was recently amended with additional controls.
Overview
Key Principles
The SAF controls are built on several key principles:
Security by Design
: Incorporating security measures from the outset of service adoption and throughout the lifecycle.
Automation
: Leveraging automation for configuration, deployment, and management to reduce human error and ensure consistency.
Least Privilege
: Ensuring that users and systems have only the minimum necessary access rights.
Encryption Everywhere
: Protecting data at rest and in transit using strong encryption methods.
Continuous Monitoring
: Implementing robust logging and monitoring solutions to detect and respond to security events.
Compliance Integration
: Aligning with regulatory requirements and industry standards.
Separation of Duties
: Segregating environments and access to maintain security and reduce risk.
The list of SAF controls used are now categorized into common controls to make it uncomplicated and effortless to implement it to the cloud services. The List of SAF controls used are given below in the document.
Control Type Categories:
Encryption Configuration Types:
Data at Rest
Data in Transit
Key Management
Authentication
Logging and Monitoring Configuration Types:
Centralized Logging
Alerting and Monitoring
Regulatory and Data Protection Configuration Types:
Data Lifecycle Management
Compliance Standards
Auditing
Third-Party Management
Identity and Access Management Configuration Types:
Access Control
Authentication and Authorization
User Lifecycle Management
Secrets Management
Credential Management
System Management Configuration Types:
Baseline Images
Automation
Asset Tagging
Environment Management
OS Security
Network Security Configuration Types:
Threat Assessment
Network Segmentation
Traffic Control
Communication Security
Infrastructure Security Configuration Types:
Infrastructure as Code
Policy as Code
SAF 3.0 Analysis:
Control Type
Category
Description
Severity
Encryption and Key Management
Security
Implement encryption for data at rest and in transit. Use managed keys for sensitive data. Ensure proper key management and rotation.
Access Control and Identity Management
Security
Implement strong authentication (e.g., MFA). Apply least privilege principle. Regularly review and audit access.
Network Security
Security
Secure all network communications. Implement proper network segmentation. Control inbound and outbound traffic.
Logging and Monitoring
Operations
Centralize logging. Implement comprehensive monitoring. Ensure proper log retention and protection.
System Management Configuration
Compliance
Identify and adhere to relevant regulatory requirements. Implement Tagging standards. Conduct regular audits.
Regulatory Compliance and Data Protection
Data Management
Implement data classification, retention, and backup policies. Ensure proper data handling throughout its lifecycle.
Infrastructure Security
Operations
Use Terraform or CloudFormation and implement policy as code using respective cloud provider's policy services (AWS Config Rules, Azure Policy)
Example SAF 3.0 for AWS lambda:
Control Type
Category
AWS Lambda Controls
Encryption and Key Management
Security
Lambda function environment variables should be encrypted at rest with a KMS customer master key
(If the tag is DataClassification is Confidential or Restricted)
Access Control and Identity Management
Security
Lambda function should not use deprecated runtime
Lambda function URL Auth type should not be configured to NONE
Lambda function should not be publicly accessible
Lambda function should not allow public actions via API Gateway
Lambda function should have a unique IAM Role
Lambda function should not be associated with a privileged role
Lambda Layer Version IAM policy should not be overly permissive
Lambda function permissions delegated to AWS services should be limited by SourceArn or SourceAccount
Network Security
Network
Lambda Function should be configured inside a VPC
Lambda function in a VPC should operate in multiple Availability Zones
Logging and Monitoring
Operations
Lambda function X-Ray tracing should be enabled
System Management Configuration
Compliance
Implement tagging strategy for Lambda functions
tags
Lambda Layer should not be attached from an unrecognized AWS account
Lambda function should have a dead letter queue
Lambda function should be configured with a function-level concurrent execution limit
Regulatory Compliance and Data Protection
Data Management
Infrastructure Security
Operations
Lambda function Access Keys should not be hardcoded
The above table controls are referred from Wiz cloud configuration rules.

Federated Cloud Identity is a collection of services to allow leveraging PG&E identities in cloud applications.  It provides a secure, auditable, and scalable identity management solution. 49962567.title: Transit VPC (Network Security)
49962567.plain_text:
List of Lifecycle Stage
Description
WAF, Application Delivery Controllers, Firewalls, Data Loss Prevention, Intrusion Detection and Prevention, DDoS Protection
CCOE Team
Network Protection Services /
List of Cloud COE Teams
Teams Channel
dynFocus-Transit
Projects
JIRA Labels
ccoe-transit
List of JIRA Labels
Product Owner
/
Overview
The purpose of PG&E's transit VPC is to provide a communication path from on-premise infrastructure to PG&E AWS Cloud customers.
A
transit VPC
is a common strategy for connecting multiple, geographically dispersed Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs) and remote networks to create a global network
transit
center. A
transit VPC
simplifies network management and minimizes the number of connections required to connect multiple VPCs and remote networks.
PG&E currently has six
Transit VPC Network Designs
:
Current interim design
Traffic flow from LOB to PG&E
Traffic flow from PG&E to LOB services (DNS, NTP, etc.)
Traffic flow from internet to LOB
Traffic flow from PG&E to access LOB services
Traffic flow from LOB to internet
Features and Benefits
Security: there is now more granularity control traffic between LOBs
Visibility: there is now a more clear representation of traffic–how it flows and an better odds of identifying and stopping malware or breaches
HA / Failover capability across 2 AWS Availability Zones (A&B)
Getting Started
Transit VPC is part of the AWS Landing Zone so no action needs to be taken to get started with this product.
FAQs
Who maintains the Transit VPC system?
Can I install my server inside the Transit VPC system?
Can I route my internet traffic back to PG&E and out through PG&E's Fairfield/Rancho internet egress?
Do I need to have a IPSEC tunnel to both availability zones?
What is the timing to complete an
Firewall Exception Request
(FER)
once it has been submitted?
My FER was completed but I still can't gain access to it. What could be the cause?
Why aren't the flow lines straight in the network diagrams?
Why is the current design an
interim
design?
Related Information and Resources
Service Level Agreements
TBA
Support and Assistance
To get support or assistance, go to:
ChatOps:
dynFocus-Transit
Assumptions:
You have access to MS-Teams
Expectations on response:
Normal questions: 1 business day response from COE, not monitored by NPS support staff.
Expectations: Support is available during normal business hours.
URGENT/BROKEN:
Escalate to the ENOC or
Cloud COE Incident Triage for PG&E Clouds

330699611.title: F5 XC
330699611.plain_text:
F5 Distributed Cloud Services are SaaS-based security, networking, and application management services that enable customers to deploy, secure, and operate their applications in a cloud-native environment wherever needed–data center, multi-cloud, or the network or enterprise edge.
Web App Security & Performance Guide
Objective
Secure your web application and enhance its performance using F5® Distributed Cloud Console and F5 Distributed Cloud Mesh.
Components
1. Load Balancer
Create an HTTP load balancer with automatic certificate management.
Secure the load balancer with a JavaScript challenge and a web application firewall (WAF).
2. Origin Pools
Configure origin pools to manage backend servers.
Set up health checks to ensure the availability of backend servers.
3. Service Policy
Define service policies to control traffic behavior.
Apply rate limiting, caching, and other traffic management techniques.
4. IP Sets
Create IP sets to group IP addresses for security policies.
Use IP sets to allow or block traffic from specific IP ranges.
5. Alerts
Configure alerts to monitor the performance and security of your application.
Set up notifications for critical events and thresholds.
Steps
1. Create Primary Zone
Configure your domain to use F5 Distributed Cloud DNS.
Set up resource record sets and optionally enable DNSSEC.
2. Load Balancer
Create an HTTP load balancer with automatic certificate management.
Secure the load balancer with a JavaScript challenge and a web application firewall (WAF).
3. Secure App
Apply a JavaScript challenge to prevent automated attacks.
Configure the WAF for additional security.
4. Performance and Security Monitoring
Monitor the performance and security of your application through dashboards and traffic analysis.

99424074.title: Flexera Software License Scanning
99424074.plain_text:
Additional capability was added to PG&E AWS environment that allows for on premise Flexera software to connect to AWS and scan EC2 instances for software in use for both Windows and Linux.
Flexera has api access key configured for AWS IAM user (SVC-A2939-All-Flexera) in PG&E master account (739846873405). The IAM user in the master account can connect to any PG&E AWS account that has the role defined in the
IAM user policy
sts:AssumeRole. The access key and secret should be
rotated every 90 days
by the account owner per access key policy.  The role that is in the account where the IAM user will assume role should be least privileged, an example of a least privileged role used for scanning can be found
here
.
If an additional account should be scanned, then a pull request should be opened in GitHub repo
aws-lz-aws
. A new least privileged role should be created in the account to be scanned using the template of the existing least privileged role. The IAM user should have that least privileged role added to the sts:AssumeRole policy like the other role arns for accounts already being scanned.
Alternatively, a stackset can be used to create stacks in many accounts to create the least privileged role, but the template should still be used to not over provision the role. The role arns need to be added to the
IAM user policy
sts:AssumeRole.
A trust will also be added if stackset approach is used.

330694930.title: Identity Center
330694930.plain_text:
Identity Center (IDC) is a centralized service that allows users to access AWS accounts and SSO applications.
Users request membership through these groups in MEA. The synchronization process happens as follows:
MEA
synchronizes with
EntraID
.
EntraID
provisions the access to
AWS Identity Center (IDC)
via
SCIM
as both users and groups, including the association of users and groups.
How to Access AWS Accounts Using Identity Center
Console Access
To access AWS through the IAM Identity Center:
Open the AWS IAM Identity Center
: Go to
https://pge.awsapps.com/start
.
⚠️ If you do not see any permission sets after signing in, your AWS roles may not be configured.
Please reach out in the Teams channel to request your roles be added:
Cloud Product - AWS Identity Center
Select Your Account Tile
: This will automatically assume your AWS account and permission set.
Set Your AWS Region
: Ensure you set your region to
us-west-2
in the account settings under the gear icon. This setting must be changed manually for each account, as there is no global setting in IAM Identity Center.
Enabling Multi-Account Support
To enhance your experience with the Identity Center, we recommend turning on multi-account support. This feature allows you to have multiple sessions running simultaneously. It also displays the active account in the top right-hand corner, making it easy to identify which account you're currently using at a glance. This is particularly useful for managing multiple accounts efficiently.
Configuring Default Region
One minor annoyance you might encounter is that the region always defaults to us-east-1, and you need to configure this setting for each account individually. To change the default region:
Click on the gear icon in the top right-hand corner to access the settings.
Hit the edit button next to the region setting.
Change the default from "Last Used" to your preferred region, such as us-west-2.
CLI Access
Instructions on how to access AWS using CLI through the IAM Identity Center under Access Keys.
Support
Reach out to
Cloud COE | Cloud Product - AWS Identity Center | Microsoft Teams
Administrators Managing Identity Center
Repository Information
For administrators who need to manage roles and permissions:
Repository
: The
aws-idc-policy-sets
repository manages AWS IAM Identity Center permission sets and account assignments.
Access
: Roles are created and maintained by the CCOE team. To request a new role or make changes, follow the CONTRIBUTING guidelines in the repository.
Link
:
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-idc-policy-sets
Role Management
Instructions for administrators on how to manage roles and permissions.
Troubleshooting
Common issues and solutions related to role deployment.

342302844.plain_text:

triage follows with on-demand support from SME if requested at developer discretion
once fixes are made to all Medium and higher findings (low and informational are optional and encouraged) re-scan takes place
developers can work with SME for exception handling, false positives, or findings that do not apply such as only in build chain and not in deployed code
SLA  for the tools are to be determined based on departmental needs, platform, risk level, public-facing, UDN/ODN, etc.
Black Duck Service SLA's:
What are the support SLAs for cases opened with Black Duck?
Our SLA for first response on cases submitted is based on priority. You can expect a response within 1 day for P1/P2, 2 days for P3, and 3 days for P4 (business days).
NB: all repos in pgetech are scanned on one branch on a weekly basis enabling on-demand findings

338467917.title: Sonrai Security
338467917.plain_text:
🛡️ Sonrai Permissions Firewall - Zero Standing Privileges for AWS
🎯 Executive Summary
Sonrai's Permissions Firewall transforms how we manage AWS access by eliminating standing privileges and enabling true Zero Trust security. Instead of users having permanent elevated permissions, access is granted just-in-time through intelligent approval workflows managed by account owners who understand their business context.
🚀 Core Value Proposition
🧩 How It Works: Two Key Components
1️⃣ Permissions Firewall (Prevention)
The foundation that blocks unauthorized access and triggers approval workflows:
Real-time Enforcement:
Instantly blocks sensitive actions across all AWS services
Smart Detection:
Identifies when users attempt actions beyond their baseline permissions
Automatic Triggering:
Seamlessly initiates approval workflows when needed
Policy-Driven:
Configurable rules based on service sensitivity and user roles
2️⃣ Permissions on Demand (PoD) (Approval)
The intelligent workflow that grants temporary access through business-context approvals:
Delegated Authority:
Account owners approve requests for their domains
Business Context:
Approvers understand the users and workloads they're protecting
Time-Bounded:
All access automatically expires (default 4 hours)
Multi-Channel:

342295942.title: Source Code Management (SCM) Best Practices for Security
342295942.plain_text:
Various security tools will scan code based on different integration points:
Wiz can scans based on repository default branch and then based on specific Pull Requests
BlackDuck-Coverity can scan default branch and then additional specified branches.
Sonarqube offers this insight:
Community Edition doesn't support the analysis of multiple branches, so you can only analyze your main branch. With
Developer Edition
, you can analyze multiple branches and pull requests.
consider the following guidelines:
Organize Workflows
: Ensure that team members can work independently without disrupting each other.
Prevent Conflicts
: Reduce the chances of code conflicts during integration by keeping branches
short-lived
.
Improve Code Quality
: Make it easier to review and test changes before merging.
Document and communicate practices on a per-project basis
Common  types of branches that are present in for different purposes:
Master:
Used for product release
Develop:
Used for ongoing development
Feature Branches:
Created from the develop branch to work on specific features.
Release
Branches:
Created from the develop branch to prepare for production releases and bug fixes
Fix
Branches:
Created from the master branch to address urgent issues directly in production.It helps in addressing discovered bugs smoothly, allowing developers to continue their work on the develop branch while the issue is resolved.
The Master and Develop branches are the main branches that remain throughout the journey of the software. The other branches are supporting branches and are short-lived that serving specific purposes.

51060117.title: AWS GuardDuty Setup with CloudFormation Templates
51060117.plain_text:
GuardDuty Set-up
Overview
The GuardDuty wiki contains:
How to enable GuardDuty manually?
CloudFormation templates that configure PGE's AWS environment for GuardDuty.
The CloudFormation templates that configure PGE's AWS environment for GuardDuty Master in the CyberSecurity Account. The GuardDuty Master/Setup Detector CFN templates are working (see landing-zone repo files). Objective is to Deploy CFN template in the Account, the SIOC generates findings that appear in QRadar.
Symbols
Description
Caution
AWS ProServe Recommended
Important
Currently Implemented
Manually
Turn GuardDuty "On" in a member account
Use the following procedure to enable GuardDuty.
The IAM identity (user, role, group) that you use to enable GuardDuty must have the required permissions. To grant the permissions required to enable GuardDuty, attach the following policy to an IAM user, group, or role:
When you open the GuardDuty console for the first time, choose
Get Started
, and then choose
Enable GuardDuty
.
Note the following about enabling GuardDuty:
GuardDuty is assigned a service-linked role called
AWSServiceRoleForAmazonGuardDuty
.
This service-linked role allows GuardDuty to retrieve metadata for the EC2 instances in your AWS environment that are involved in potentially suspicious activity. It also allows GuardDuty to include the retrieved EC2 instance metadata in the findings that GuardDuty generates about potentially suspicious activity. To view the details of
AWSServiceRoleForAmazonGuardDuty
,
On the
Welcome to GuardDuty
page, choose
View service role permissions
.
After you enable GuardDuty, it immediately begins pulling and analyzing independent streams of data from AWS CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to generate security findings.
Because GuardDuty only consumes this data for purposes of determining if there are any findings, GuardDuty doesn't manage AWS CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs for you or make their events and logs available to you.
If you have enabled these services independent of GuardDuty, you will continue to have the option to configure the settings of these data sources through their respective consoles or APIs
It is highly recommended that you enable GuardDuty in all supported AWS regions. This allows GuardDuty to generate findings about unauthorized or unusual activity even in regions that you are not actively using. If GuardDuty is not enabled in all supported regions, its ability to detect activity that involves global services is reduced.
Note: You can disable GuardDuty at any time to stop it from processing and analyzing AWS CloudTrail events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs
Pricing
When you enable GuardDuty for the first time, your AWS account is automatically enrolled in a 30-day GuardDuty free trial.
The details include your current position on the free trial timeline and the estimated daily cost for using GuardDuty after your free trial ends. The table below gives the price after the 30 days.
Table 1: GuardDuty Pricing
VPC Flow Log and DNS Log Analysis
First 500 GB / month
$1.00 per GB
Next 2000 GB / month
$0.50 per GB
Over 2500 GB / month
$0.25 per GB
AWS CloudTrail Event Analysis
Per 1,000,000 events / month
$4.00 per 1,000,000 events
This estimate is based on the logs that GuardDuty processes and analyzes daily during the free trial.
This estimated daily cost does NOT project charges for using GuardDuty in all of the AWS accounts and regions where you enabled GuardDuty.
The daily cost estimate is based only on the GuardDuty usage in the AWS account and the AWS region to which you're currently signed in.
Designating Master and Member Accounts Through GuardDuty Console
In GuardDuty, your account is designated a master account when you add another AWS account to be associated with your account or when another account accepts your invitation to become a member account.
If your account is a non-master account, you can accept an invitation from another account. When you accept, your account becomes a member account.
Use the following procedures to add an account, invite an account, or accept an invitation from another account.
Step 1 - Add an account
In the navigation pane, under Settings, choose Accounts.
Choose Add accounts.
On the Add member accounts page, under Enter accounts, type the AWS account ID and email address of the account that you want to add. Then choose Add account.
The email address that you specify in this step MUST be identical to the email address associated with the AWS account that you want to add as GuardDuty member account.
When you are finished adding accounts, choose Done. The added accounts appear in a list on the Accounts page. Each added account in this list has an Invite link in the Status column.
Step 2 - Invite an account
Open the GuardDuty console at
https://console.aws.amazon.com/guardduty
.
In the navigation pane, under Settings, choose Accounts.
For the account that you want to invite to enable GuardDuty, choose the Invite link that appears in the Status column of the added accounts list.
In the Invitation to GuardDuty dialog box, type an invitation message (optional), and then choose Send notification. The value in the Status column for the invited account changes to Pending.
Step 3 - Accept an invitation
Open the GuardDuty console at
https://console.aws.amazon.com/guardduty
.
Do one of the following:
If you don't have GuardDuty enabled, on the Enable GuardDuty page, choose Enable GuardDuty. Then use the Accept widget and the Accept invitation button to accept the membership invitation.
Important
You must enable GuardDuty before you can accept a membership invitation.
If you already have GuardDuty enabled, use the Accept widget and the Accept invitation button to accept the membership invitation.
After you accept the invitation, your account becomes a GuardDuty member account. The account whose user sent the invitation becomes the GuardDuty master account. The master account user can see that the value in the Status column for your member account changes to Monitored. The master account user can now view and manage GuardDuty findings for your member account.
CloudFormation Template
The following sections defines how to do the above tasks with a CloudFormation Script. These are located in PG&E's Githib account. The location is defined below with a quick description of it purpose
aws-landing-zone./pge-scripts/01-all-accounts/01-guardduty-setup-detector.yaml
Turns GuardDuty "On" in a member account
aws-landing-zone/pge-scripts/03-cybersecurity-account/03-guardduty-add-member.yaml
Initiates invite to master from member
aws-landing-zone/pge-scripts/01-all-accounts/01-guardduty-add-master.yaml
Accepts invite from master by member
References
1
Managing AWS Accounts in Amazon GuardDuty
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_accounts.html
2
3

51151867.title: EC2 Forensic Isolation Environment
51151867.plain_text:
Overview
The wiki documents will outline the:
Develop CloudFormation templates for EC2 forensics / isolation environment
Develop isolation automation for forensics environment
Plan the next version with updated AWS services
Purpose: The Forensics Automation Stack contains the resources and automation required to isolate a compromised EC2 instance from the network, image it, and re-deploy the image into a special VPC for forensic analysis.
Symbols
Description
Caution
AWS ProServe Recommended
Important
See section below
PGE Forensics Automation Stack
As illustrated in Figure 1: Forensics AWS Environment, the diagram displays an architectural view on how the account is laid out. Within a single VPC, three security groups (SG) contain a single EC2 instance. In the Tools SG there is the Sift workstation. This is the workstation that will have installed the required applications to perform a foresting analysis on the compromised EC2 instance. In the isolation SG it will contain the EC2 instance of the comprised EC2 instanced that was copied from another PG&E account. The Bastion SG contains the Bastion host that would give the Forensic investigator the access to SSH into the SIFT workstation from an Internet gateway.
Figure 1: Forensics AWS Environment
Stacks Defined
The five scripts below explain how the Forensic infrastructure is created and how a function is executed to copy a comprised EC2 instance and spin it up in a isolation VPC/SG. The function will alert the necessary party and shut down the comprised instance.
Payload Account stack
00-all-accounts/
00-forensics-tools.yaml
Deploys 2 resources (IAM role and security group) used for instance isolation and associated automation for the Lambda "isolater-function .
Need to be deploy to all accounts.
Resources include:
Isolation Security Group
- Creates a forensic isolation security group
Collector Role
- Creates a role called
forensics-collector-role
Roles gives allow permissions to EC2

DescribeImages
CreateTags
DescribeInstances
StopInstances
Parameters include:
Forensics Account Id
- Account ID for the Forensics Account
Output includes:
None
Infrastructure Stack
05-forensics-account/
05-forensics-stack.yaml
Build the remainder of the forensics automation stack.
!worddav3280d2c9a55c1b77570e625de12b6753.png|height=52,width=52!Deploy in the forensics account.

Forensics

creates the isolation route table.

Associates the subnet to the route table.

Isolation Security Group
– Creates the solation security group
Isolation Security Group Egress Rule

– creates environmental variables for the Lamdba function (isolater-function) to pick up. The variables include: SNS ARN, Tag Name, Security group ID, Subnet ID, Region, SSH Key name and Sift workstation ID.
Timeout session timer is set to 900 although it should be done in a couple minutes
Isolater Function Role
– creates a role called "forensics-collector-role" to invoke the Lambda function called "isolater-function in all accounts. Enables the permissions to grab forensics isolater python function from the S3 bucket.

https://github.com/pgetech/aws-cfn-vpcendpoints/

Lambda Function to Isolate EC2 Instance
05-forensics-account/lambda/
isolater-function.zip
***Requirement- need role for both SSM and SIFT
The deployment package for the lambda function "isolater.py". You will need to copy the zip file to S3 and provide location to
05-forensics-stack.yaml
as a parameter.
Lambda script, run from your laptop using an Admin role tied to the forensics account. Must provide AWS account number and instance id of the compromised host.
assume_role(AccountId):
apply_tags(InstanceId, Credentials)
apply_security_group(InstanceId, Credentials)
create_image(InstanceId, Credentials)
create_image(InstanceId, Credentials)
check_image_status(ImageId, Credentials)
launch_instance(ImageId, InstanceType, InstanceId)
send_sns(ImageId, InstanceId, NewInstanceId, TimeStamp):
stop_instance(InstanceId, Credentials)
start_sift_workststion
Events
Starts the Sift Workstation
Pulls the 2 values to run (AccountID and Instand ID)
Assume role to pull credentials for the forensic collector role
Apply Tags to the compromised instance. Tag name not defined yet.
Checks instance type to make sure it is relaunched as same type.
Creates the image.
Captures ImageID name for SNS message
Stops the compromised instance when above when steps 5,6,7 are complete.
Error handle and Logs
Flow logs go to the Forensics Log Group called "forensics-vpc-flow-logs"
If you perform these tasks more than once on the same comprised image, you will receive an error saying "InvalidAMIName.Duplicate". To resolve this error, you can delete the old image, or it perform a iteration of a version number on the name. ( e.g., name + 1)
The should not be an issue since you should not do this task twice. Only for testing.
Next Version Steps
Update to Services:
AWS Systems Manager
Systems Manager – Run Command
AWS Inspector
AWS Security Hub
Requirements:
Separate forensics account with isolation VPC configured
EC2 Instances will need to be running Systems Manager and Inspector
Both accounts running Security Hub
Lambda function(s) in Cybersecurity account
Forensics role in workload account
Step 0:
Amazon Inspector reports an issue to the local Security Hub
Issue is sent to master Security Hub in Forensics account
Lambda function running in Forensics account polls Security Hub for new issues
Figure 3: Step 0
Step 1:
When new issue if discovered in Security Hub, the Lambda function launches a container to handle the isolation automation steps
The container process assumes the Forensics role in the target workload account and applies an "isolation" security group to the instance, preventing traffic in and out
Figure 4: Step 1
Step 2:
Using the Systems Manager Run Command, we use the LiME tool to take a memory dump (Linux)
Memory dump is saved to s3 bucket in Forensics account
Figure 5: Step 2
Step 3:
Using the Forensics role in the target workload account, we'll take a snapshot of the affected instance (Snapshots are always saved to S3)
Figure 6: Step 3
Step 4:
We'll then convert the Snapshot to an AMI and adjust it's permission to share it with the Forensics account
Figure 7: Step 4
Step 5:
The AMI will be launched into the payload subnet of the Isolation VPC within the Forensics account
Figure 8: Step 5
Step 6:
The original affected instance will be tagged and stopped
The tools machines in the Isolation VPC will be started
SIOC will be notified (not pictured)
Notes
Will opt instances in via tagging, ie Isolation = True
Not appropriate for single points of failure
Need a memory dump solution for Windows boxes
Need to know what tools to include in the Forensics VPC
