51153797.title: Forensic Operational Guide
51153797.plain_text:
Overview
The wiki will walk the Forensic Investigator on how to trigger the Lambda function to isolate a compromised EC2 instance from the payload accounts, image it, and re-deploy the image into a special Account/VPC for forensic analysis. Once the image is recreated in the Forensic Account Session Manager will be used to access the SIFT workstation.
The wiki will define the SIFT workstation template used and how the AMI will be updated and be shared to the Forensic Account/VPC. This will be an iterative process as we discover more efficient avenues to keep the Sift workstation up to date with application updates and patches.
Symbols
Description
Caution
AWS ProServe Recommended
Important
Currently Implemented
Operational Process (Initial Version)
Figure 1 : Operational Process workflow
Once the isolater-function lambda is triggered using the AWS Management console, the target host is tagged as 'Compromised' and a special security group is applied preventing inbound and outbound network traffic, while leaving the host running to gather evidence.
Once isolation is achieved, the host is imaged, and the image is copied to the forensics account. A new Instance from the compromised image is launched into the isolation subnet of the forensics VPC, and a SIFT Workstation is started.
SNS notification goes out to the SIOC letting them know the Instance is ready for analysis.
Finally, the compromised instance will be tagged and stopped.
Lamdba Execution
Log into AWS console
Type in Lambda in search box
Go to Lambda > Functions
Double click the isolater-function (Figure 2: Lambda Console)
Figure 3: Lambda Console
5. Go to the Test Button
6. Enter in text boxes, as illustrated below.
7. Go to Monitoring tab and select "View logs CloudWatch"
AWS Systems Manager Session Manager
Session Manager is a fully managed AWS Systems Manager capability that lets you manage your Amazon EC2 instances through an interactive one-click browser-based shell or through the AWS CLI.
Session Manager provides secure and auditable instance management without the need to open inbound ports, maintain bastion hosts, or manage SSH keys.
Before using Session Manager, make sure your environment meets the following requirements.
Session Manager Prerequisites
Requirement
Description
Supported Operating Systems
AWS Session Manager supports the following operating system versions:
Note
Session Manager supports Amazon EC2 instances, as well as servers or virtual machines (VMs) in your hybrid environment that use the
advanced-instances
tier. For more information about advanced instances, see
Using the Advanced-Instances Tier
.
Linux
Session Manager supports all the versions of Linux that are supported for AWS Systems Manager as a whole. For information, see
Systems Manager Prerequisites
.
Windows
Session Manager supports Windows Server 2008 R2 through Windows Server 2016.
Note
Microsoft Windows Server 2016 Nano is not supported.
SSM Agent
SSM Agent version 2.3.68.0 or later must be installed on the instances you want to connect to through sessions.
Note
When a version of SSM Agent that supports Session Manager starts on an instance, it creates a user account with root or administrator privileges called ssm-user. Sessions are launched using the credentials of this user account. For information about restricting administrative control for this account, see
Step 6: (Optional) Disable or Enable ssm-user Account Administrative Permissions
.
To install or update SSM Agent, see
Installing and Configuring SSM Agent
.
AWS CLI
(Optional) If you use the AWS CLI to start your sessions (instead of using the AWS Systems Manager console), version 1.16.12 or later of the CLI must be installed on your local machine.
You can call
aws --version
to check the version.
If you need to install or upgrade the CLI, see
Installing the AWS Command Line Interface
in the AWS Command Line Interface User Guide.
In addition, to use the CLI to manage your instances with Session Manager, you must first install the Session Manager plugin on your local machine. For information, see
(Optional) Install the Session Manager Plugin for the AWS CLI
.
IAM ROLE
Role that enables the instance to use the SSM agent for Session manager
Starting a Session (Console)
You can use the AWS Systems Manager console to start a session with an instance in your account.
To start a session (console)
Open the AWS Systems Manager console at
https://console.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/
.
In the navigation pane, choose
Session Manager
.
or
If the AWS Systems Manager home page opens first, choose the menu icon (
) to open the navigation pane, and then choose
Session Manager
in the navigation pane.
3. Choose
Start session
.
4. For
Target instances
, choose the option button to the left of the instance you want to connect to.
If an instance you want to connect to is not in the list, or is listed but an error message reports, "The instance you selected is not configured to use Session Manager," see
Instance Not Available or Not Configured for Session Manager
for    troubleshooting     steps.
5. Choose
Start session
.
After the connection is made, you can run bash commands (Linux) or PowerShell commands (Windows) as you would through any other connection type.
Starting a Session (AWS CLI)
To start a session using the AWS CLI, run the following command:
Note
To use the AWS CLI to run session commands, the Session Manager plugin must also be installed on your local machine. For information, see
(Optional) Install the Session Manager Plugin for the AWS CLI
.
aws ssm start-session --target
instance-id
instance-id
represents of the ID of an instance configured for use with AWS Systems Manager and its Session Manager capability.
For information about other options you can use with the
start-session
command, see
start-session
in the AWS Systems Manager section of the AWS CLI Command Reference.
Sift Instance AMI
Amazon Linux EC2 Instances ( Ubuntu Server 16.04 LTS )will be used as the SIFT workstation image.
Provision a Forensic Workstation
To manually provision a SIFT Workstation on an AWS EC2 Instance, perform
the following steps:
Log into the incident-response account
Launch an EC2 instance using the console. Use the latest Ubuntu AMI in the AWS Marketplace, for example:
Ubuntu Server 16.04 LTS (HVM), SSD Volume Type - ami-996372fd"
Choose a t2.large or larger instance type.
Click "next" as needed to get to the "Configure Security Group" form.
Create a new "Incident Response" security group and configure it such that the only ingress rule is to allow SSH from the external IP address by selecting the "My IP" option as shown in Figure 2, below. The egress rulemay be left as the default "allow all" so that the system can install software.
4. Choose an existing SSH key or select the option to that the AWS console create a new one.
Once the instance launches, SSH to it with the appropriate key.
On the Ubuntu EC2 instance, run the following command:
wget
https://github.com/sans-dfir/siftcli/
releases/download/v1.5.1/sift-cli-linux
The version will change over time, so update the path as necessary to
ensure the command pulls down the latest version.
Move and rename the SIFT Install tool:
sudo mv sift-cli-linux /usr/local/bin/sift
2. Set the proper permissions and run it:
s
udo chmod 755 /usr/local/bin/sift
sudo sift install
The SIFT installation process may take up to 30 minutes and it is highly
recommended that incident handlers provision a new system for each case. One way to
streamline this is to make an AMI of the SIFT Workstation before using it (Olsen, 2014).
This allows future SIFT instances to be launched from the AMI. However, the instance
may still need to be updated.
To update a SIFT Workstation, run the following commands:
apt-get update
apt-get upgrade
sift update
shutdown -r now #Reboot
Mount An EBS Volume To EC2 Linux
In this tutorial, we will teach you how to  attach and mount an EBS volume to ec2 linux instances. Follow the steps given below carefully for the setup.
Step 1:
Head over to EC2 –> Volumes and create a new volume of your preferred size and type.
Step 2:
Select the created volume, right click and select the “attach volume” option.
Step 3:
Select the instance from the instance text box as shown below.
Step 4:
Now, login to your ec2 instance and list the available disks using the following command.
lsblk
The above command will list the disk you attached to your instance.
Step 5:
Check if the volume has any data using the following command.
sudo
file
-
s
/
dev
/
xvdf
If the above command output shows “/dev/xvdf: data”, it means your volume is empty.
Step 6:
Create a directory of your choice to mount our new ext4 volume. I am using the name “newvolume”
sudo
mkdir
/
newvolume
Step 8:
Mount the volume to “newvolume” directory using the following command.
sudo
mount
/
dev
/
xvdf
/
newvolume
/
Step 9: cd into newvolume directory and check the disk space for confirming the volume mount.
cd
/
newvolume
df
-
h
.
The above command would show the free space in the newvolume directory.
To unmount the volume, you have to use the following command.
umount
/
dev
/
xvdf
References
1
Session Manager Prereq
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/systems-manager/latest/userguide/session-manager-prerequisites.html
2
Digital Forensic Analysis of Amazon Linux EC2 Instances
https://www.sans.org/reading-room/whitepapers/cloud/paper/38235
3
How To Attach And Mount An EBS Volume To EC2 Linux Instance
https://devopscube.com/mount-ebs-volume-ec2-instance/

51061170.title: Incident Response Runbooks for Amazon Guard Duty
51061170.plain_text:
Overview
The objective of this document is to develop three security run-books to detect and respond to the critical security threats within AWS Guard Duty so that the SIOC can be knowledgeable of each threat and how to respond in the most time efficient manner. The three security run-books will provide the methodology for a solid baseline for many similar threats.
The document will walk the security analyst with:
How to gather the necessary data to troubleshoot.
Steps to troubleshoot the detection
Urgency Category
Escalation Path
NIST Cybersecurity Framework in the AWS Cloud
The Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) is a risk-based, outcome-focused framework. It helps you establish a foundational set of security activities organized around five functions. Identify, Protects, Detect, Respond, Recover. The functions help you improve the security, risk management, and resilience of your organization. Within these functions, the document focuses on the detect and respond portion of the framework. Under the detective controls, it defines how AWS GuardDuty is the ideal tool for threat detection.
GuardDuty Descriptions
How GuardDuty works?
Amazon GuardDuty is a continuous security monitoring service that analyzes and processes the following data sources:
VPC Flow Logs
VPC Flow Logs capture information about the IP traffic going to and from Amazon EC2 network interfaces in your VPC.
When you enable GuardDuty, it immediately starts analyzing your VPC Flow Logs data.
It consumes VPC Flow Log events directly from the VPC Flow Logs feature through an independent and duplicative stream of flow logs. This process does not affect any existing flow log configurations that you might have.
GuardDuty doesn't manage your flow logs or make them accessible in your account. To manage access and retention of your flow logs, you must configure the VPC Flow Logs feature.
There is no additional charge for GuardDuty access to flow logs. However, enabling flow logs for retention or use in your account falls under existing pricing
AWS CloudTrail event logs
Currently, GuardDuty only analyzes CloudTrail management events. If you have CloudTrail configured to log data events, there will be a difference between GuardDuty analysis based on CloudTrail data and the logs that CloudTrail itself is delivering.
DNS logs
If you use AWS DNS resolvers for your EC2 instances (the default setting), then GuardDuty can access and process your request and response DNS logs through the internal AWS DNS resolvers.
If you are using a 3rd party DNS resolver or if you set up your DNS resolvers, then GuardDuty cannot access and process data from this data source.
It uses threat intelligence feeds, such as lists of malicious IPs and domains, and machine learning to identify unexpected and potentially unauthorized and malicious activity within your AWS environment.
The logs from these data sources are stored in the Amazon S3 buckets.
Threat Detections
1.
Reconnaissance - Activity suggesting reconnaissance by an attacker includes:
unusual API activity
Intra-VPC port scanning
unusual patterns of failed login requests*
unblocked port probing from a known bad IP
2. Instance compromise -
Activity indicating an instance compromise includes:
Amazon EC2 credentials used by an external IP address*
outbound denial of service activity
unusually high volume of network traffic
unusual network protocols
outbound instance communication with a known malicious IP
data exfiltration using DNS*
cryptocurrency mining
malware using domain generation algorithms (DGA
3. Account compromise
-
Common patterns indicative of account compromise include:
API calls from unusual geolocation or anonymizing proxy
attempts to disable AWS CloudTrail logging
unusual instance or infrastructure launches
infrastructure deployments in an unusual region
API calls from known malicious IP addresses
Threat Purpose
Threat purpose describes the primary purpose of a threat or a potential attack.
Threat purpose can have the following values:
Backdoor
- this value indicates that the attack has compromised an AWS resource and is capable of contacting it's home command and control server to receive further instructions for malicious activity.
Behavior
- this value indicates that GuardDuty is detecting activity or activity patterns that are different from the established baseline for a particular AWS resource.
Cryptocurrency
- this value indicates that GuardDuty is detecting software that is associated with cryptocurrencies (for example, Bitcoin).
Pentest
- Sometimes owners of AWS resources or their authorized representatives intentionally run tests against AWS applications to find vulnerabilities, like open security groups or access keys that are overly permissive. These pen tests are done in an attempt to identify and lock down vulnerable resources before they are discovered by attackers. However, some of the tools used by authorized pen testers are freely available, and therefore can be used by unauthorized users or attackers to run probing tests. Although GuardDuty can't identify the true purpose behind such activity, the
Pentest
value indicates that GuardDuty is detecting such activity and that it is similar to the activity generated by known pen testing tools. Therefore, it can be a potential attack.
Persistence
- this value indicates that a principal in your AWS environment is exhibiting behavior that is different from the established baseline. For example, this principal has no prior history of updating network configuration settings or updating policies or permissions attached to AWS users or resources.
Policy
- this value indicates that your AWS account is exhibiting behavior that goes against recommended security best practices.
Recon
- this value indicates that a reconnaissance attack is underway, scoping out vulnerabilities in your AWS environment by probing ports, listing users, database tables, and so on.
resource consumption
- this value indicates that a principal in your AWS environment is exhibiting behavior that is different from the established baseline. For example, this principal has no prior history of launching EC2 instances.
Stealth
- this value indicates that an attack is actively trying to hide its actions and its tracks. For example, an attack might use an anonymizing proxy server, making it virtually impossible to gauge the true nature of the activity.
Trojan
- this value indicates that an attack is using Trojan programs that silently carry out malicious activity. Sometimes this software takes on the appearance of a legitimate program. Sometimes users accidentally run this software. Other times this software might run automatically by exploiting a vulnerability.
UnauthorizedAccess
- this value indicates that GuardDuty is detecting suspicious activity or a suspicious activity pattern by an unauthorized individual.
ResourceTypeAffected
- describes which AWS resource is identified in this finding as the potential target of an attack. In this release of GuardDuty, only EC2 instances and principals (and their credentials) can be identified as affected resources in GuardDuty findings.
ThreatFamilyName
- describes the overall threat or potential malicious activity that GuardDuty is detecting. For example, a value of
NetworkPortUnusual
indicates that an EC2 instance identified in the GuardDuty finding has no prior history of communications on a particular remote port that also is identified in the finding.
ThreatFamilyVariant
- describes the specific variant of the
ThreatFamily
that GuardDuty is detecting. Attackers often slightly modify the functionality of the attack, thus creating new variants.
Artifact
- describes a specific resource that is owned by a tool that is used in the attack. For example,
DNS
in the finding type
CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B!DNS
indicates that an EC2 instance is communicating with a known Bitcoin-related domain.
Locating and Analyzing GuardDuty Findings
Choose a specific finding to view its details. A details pane appears where you can view the following information:
A finding's summary section that includes the following information:
Finding type
– a concise yet readable description of the potential security issue. For more information, see
GuardDuty Finding Types Format
.
Severity
– a finding's assigned severity level of either High, Medium, or Low. For more information, see
Severity Levels for GuardDuty Findings
.
Region
– the AWS region in which the finding was generated.
Note:
For more information about supported regions, see
Amazon GuardDuty Supported Regions
Count
– the number of times GuardDuty generated the finding after you enabled GuardDuty in your AWS account.
Account
– location of centralized Guard Duty
Account ID
– the ID of the AWS account in which the activity took place that prompted GuardDuty to generate this finding.
Resource ID
– the ID of the AWS resource against which the activity took place that prompted GuardDuty to generate this finding.
Threat list name
- the name of the threat list that includes the IP address or the domain name involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Last seen
– the time at which the activity took place that prompted GuardDuty to generate this finding.
Note:
Findings' time stamps in the GuardDuty console appear in your local time zone, while JSON exports and CLI outputs display timestamps in UTC.
A finding's
Resource affected
section that includes the following information:
Resource role
– a value that usually is set to
Target
because the affected resource can be a potential target of an attack.
Resource type
– the type of the affected resource. This value is either
AccessKey
or
Instance
. Currently, supported finding types highlight potentially malicious activity against either EC2 instances or AWS credentials. For more information, see
Remediating Security Issues Discovered by GuardDuty
.
Instance ID
– the ID of the EC2 instance involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Port
– the port number for the connection used during the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Access key ID
– access key ID of the user engaged in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Principal ID
– the principal ID of the user engaged in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
User type
– the type of user engaged in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding. For more information, see
CloudTrail
userIdentity element
.
User name
– The name of the user engaged in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
A finding's
Action
section that can include the following information:
Action type
– the finding activity type. This value can be one of the following: NETWORK_CONNECTION, AWS_API_CALL, PORT_PROBE, or DNS_REQUEST. NETWORK_CONNECTION indicates that network traffic was exchanged between the identified EC2 instance and the remote host. AWS_API_CALL indicates that an AWS API was invoked. DNS_REQUEST indicates that the identified EC2 instance queried a domain name. PORT_PROBE indicates that a remote host probed the identified EC2 instance on multiple open ports.
API
– the name of the API operation that was invoked and thus prompted GuardDuty to generate this finding.
Note :
These operations can also include non-API events captured by CloudTrail. For more information, see
Non-API Events Captured by CloudTrail
.
Service name
– the name of the AWS service (GuardDuty) that generated the finding.
Connection direction
– the network connection direction observed in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding. The values can be INBOUND, OUTBOUND, and UNKNOWN. INBOUND indicates that a remote host initiated a connection to a local port on the identified EC2 instance in your account. OUTBOUND indicates that the identified EC2 instance initiated a connection to a remote host. UNKNOWN indicates that GuardDuty could not determine the direction of the connection.
Protocol
– the network connection protocol observed in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
A finding's
Actor
section that can include the following information:
Location
– location information of the IP address involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Organization
– ISP organization information of the IP address involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
IP address
– the IP address involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Port
– the port number involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Domain
– the domain involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
A finding's
Additional information
section that can include the following information:
Threat list name
– the name of the threat list that includes the IP address or the domain name involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Sample
– indicates whether this is a sample finding.
Unusual
– activity details that were not observed historically. These can include an unusual (previously not observed) user, or location, or time.
Unusual protocol
– the network connection protocol involved in the activity that prompted GuardDuty to generate the finding.
Stories and/or Phases
Example Runbooks
Thing to consider when designing a security run books to detect and respond to critical security threats.
Attribution – catching or at least knowing who caused the incident.
Review if a 3
rd
party will need to control the information surrounding the incident.
Review if your enterprise wants to do forensics.
What timed procedures are being run.
Review the ground rules that you have found and build these as your guardrails.
Example Template
Problem description
[Your Enterprise Here] is under a [ Attack Type]
[Attack Description]
Data to gather for troubleshooting
[Evaluation of current data]
Steps to troubleshoot and fix
Log into AWS
Action …..
Correct issue ……
Move to forensic environment
Urgency Category
[ Critical, Moderate, Informational]
Escalation Path
Unable to fix, escalate to these personnel or group in the following order:
Someone – Email and phone number
Someone else (backup) - Email and phone number
Group - Email Distribution List or Teams Group
CISO
Example 1 – Unauthorized Access
Unable to fix, escalate to these personnel or group in the following order:
Someone – Email and phone number
Someone else (backup) - Email and phone number
Group - Email Distribution List or Teams Group
CISO
Three Critical Security Runbooks
1) Temporary Amazon EC2 Credentials Used by An External IP Address
Problem description:
PG&E is under a Unauthorized Access attack
UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/InstanceCredentialExfiltration
AWS Description
Credentials that were created exclusively for an EC2 instance through an instance launch role are being used from an external IP address. This finding informs you of attempts to run AWS API operations from a host outside of EC2, using temporary AWS credentials that were created on an EC2 instance in your AWS account. Your EC2 instance might be compromised, and the temporary credentials from this instance might have been exfiltrated to a remote host outside of AWS.
Data to gather for troubleshooting:
Account ID - Current state is being parsed in QRadar
Resolve the number to an account name - Current state is in SIOC wiki. Future state will be parsed in QRadar
LANID/AD Credentials – Current state is not available . Future state Syslog going to QRadar
Assumed Role - Current state is being parsed in QRadar
Identify external IP – Current state is using tcputils tool and QRadar.
Instance ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
Subnet ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
VPC ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
Connectivity to other services or AWS endpoints ( e.g., S3) – current state VPC Flowlogs. Future state Redlock dashboard.
Look for brute force attach that proceeded this event – current state obtains information from RT. Future state Redlock dashboard and QRadar history.
Steps to troubleshoot detection:
Engage on-call Incident Responder
Notify your supervisor (phone only)
Escalate RT ticket to L3 Incident Response Analysis (IR)
IR will notify application owner
If applicable, IR will run the automated instance quarantine
IR will validate with application owner if the new instance created is working as expected
IR or MR - Reset compromised credentials
Urgency Category:
Critical
Escalation Path:
Unable to fix, escalate to these personnel or group in the following order:
Notify your supervisor (phone)
Notify manager (phone)
Notify Senior manager (phone)
Notify Director (phone)
2) Data Exfiltration Using DNS
Test - DNS Exfiltration
A common exfiltration technique is to tunnel data out over DNS to a fake domain. It's an effective technique because most hosts have outbound DNS ports open. This test wont exfiltrate any data, but it will generate enough unusual DNS activity to trigger the detection.
Problem description:
PG&E is under a Trojan horse attack
Trojan:EC2/DNSDataExfiltration
EC2 instance is attempting to query domain names that resemble exfiltrated data. This finding informs you that there is an EC2 instance in your AWS environment with malware that uses DNS queries for outbound data transfers. The result is the exfiltration of data. Your EC2 instance might be compromised.
Data to gather for troubleshooting:
Account ID - Current state is being parsed in QRadar
Resolve the number to an account name - Current state is in SIOC wiki. Future state will be parsed in QRadar
Identify external IP/DNS – Current state is using tcputils tool and QRadar.
Instance ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
Subnet ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
VPC ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
Role ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
Investigate the observed traffic (IP, port…) - current state VPC Flow logs. Future state Redlock dashboard and QRadar event history.
Steps to troubleshoot and fix:
IR and MR will analyze VPC Flow logs and session logs (local and remote paths)
Engage on-call Incident Responder
Notify your supervisor (phone only)
Escalate RT ticket to L3 Incident Response Analysis (IR)
IR will notify application owner
If applicable, IR will run the automated instance quarantine
IR will validate with the application owner if the new instance created is working as expected
IR or MR - Reset compromised credentials
Urgency Category:
Critical
Escalation Path:
Unable to fix, escalate to these personnel or group in the following order:
Notify your supervisor (phone)
Notify manager (phone
Notify Senior manager (phone)
Notify Director (phone)
3) Unusual Patterns Of Failed Login Requests with RDP
Test - RDP Brute Force with Password List
This simulates an RDP brute force attack on the internal RDP port of the windows server that we installed in the environment. It uses a list of common passwords that can be found on the web. This test will trigger a detection but will fail to get into the target.
Problem description:
PG&E is under a Brute Force Attack
UnauthorizedAccess:EC2/RDPBruteForce
This finding informs you that an EC2 instance in your AWS environment was involved in a brute force attack aimed at obtaining passwords to RDP services on Windows-based systems. This can indicate unauthorized access to your AWS resources.
Attempted brute force attack using RDP ports targeting a Windows host in AWS environment
Data to gather for troubleshooting:
Account ID - Current state is being parsed in QRadar
Resolve the number to an account name - Current state is in SIOC wiki. Future state will be parsed in QRadar
Assumed Role – Future state will be parsed in QRadar
Analyze VPC Flow logs and session logs (local and remote paths with Source IPs) - Current state is AWS console. Future state will be parsed in QRadar
Instance ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
Subnet ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
VPC ID - current state is being parsed in QRadar
Investigate the observed traffic (IP, port…) - current state VPC Flow logs. Future state Redlock dashboard.
Connectivity to other services or AWS endpoints ( e.g., S3) – current state VPC Flow logs. Future state Redlock dashboard.
Check if the brute force was followed by a UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/InstanceCredentialExfiltration event -
current state obtains information from RT. Future state Redlock dashboard and QRadar Event history.
Confirm with the user that this activity was expected (Email and Phone)
Check with Active Directory if password was recently changed or expired.
Reset user's password
Steps to troubleshoot and fix:
Engage on-call Incident Responder
Notify your supervisor (phone only)
Escalate RT ticket to L3 Incident Response Analysis (IR)
IR will notify application owner
If applicable, IR will run the automated instance quarantine
IR- Validate the new instance created is working as expected
IR or MR - Reset compromised credentials
Urgency Category:
Critical
Escalation Path:
Unable to fix, escalate to these personnel or group in the following order:
Notify your supervisor (phone)
Notify manager (phone
Notify Senior manager (phone)
Notify Director (phone)
Glossary of terms
Amazon GuardDuty Terminology and Concepts
Account
A standard Amazon Web Services (AWS) account that contains your AWS resources. You can sign in to AWS with your account and enable GuardDuty.
You can also invite other accounts to enable GuardDuty and become associated with your AWS account in GuardDuty.
If your invitations are accepted, your account is designated as the master GuardDuty account, and the added accounts become your member accounts.
You can then view and manage those accounts' GuardDuty findings on their behalf.
Users of the master account can configure GuardDuty as well as view and manage GuardDuty findings for their own account and all of their member accounts. You can have up to 1000 member accounts in GuardDuty.
Users of member accounts can configure GuardDuty as well as view and manage GuardDuty findings in their account (either through the GuardDuty management console or GuardDuty API). Users of member accounts can't view or manage findings in other members' accounts.
An AWS account can't be a GuardDuty master and member account at the same time.
An AWS account can accept only one membership invitation. Accepting a membership invitation is optional.
Auto-archive
Auto-archive rules allow you to create very specific combinations of attributes to suppress findings.
For example, you can define a rule through the GuardDuty filter to auto-archive Recon:EC2/Portscan from only those instances in a specific VPC, running a specific AMI, or with a specific EC2 tag. This rule would result in port scan findings being automatically archived from the instances that meet the criteria. However, it still allows alerting if GuardDuty detects those instances conducting other malicious activity, such as crypto-currency mining. Auto-archive rules defined in the GuardDuty master account apply to the GuardDuty member accounts. GuardDuty member accounts can't modify auto-archive rules. With auto-archive rules, GuardDuty still generates all findings. Auto-archive rules provide suppression of findings while maintaining a complete and immutable history of all activity.
Data source
The origin or location of a set of data. To detect unauthorized and unexpected activity in your AWS environment, GuardDuty analyzes and processes data from AWS CloudTrail event logs, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs.
Finding
A potential security issue discovered by GuardDuty.
Trusted IP list
A list of whitelisted IP addresses for highly secure communication with your AWS environment.
GuardDuty does not generate findings based on trusted IP lists. Threat list A list of known malicious IP addresses. GuardDuty generates findings based on threat lists.
References
1
AWS re:Invent 2018: Supercharge GuardDuty w/ Partners: Threat & Response at Scale GPSTEC304
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DygpSx1e3Dg
2
Amazon GuardDuty Findings
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/guardduty/latest/ug/guardduty_findings.html
3
Threat Response Scenarios Using Amazon GuardDuty - AWS Online Tech Talks
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LChSrke5U2o

292035948.title: Running Peirates to test Kubernetes and GuardDuty
292035948.plain_text:
Running Peirates
If you just want the peirates binary to start attacking things, grab the latest release from the
releases page
.
Peirates as a Container Image
You can find a useful
alpine-peirates container image on Docker Hub
, with a version number tag that tracks the Peirates version.
For example, for
alpine-peirates:1.1.16
, which contains peirates version
1.1.16
, run:
docker pull bustakube/alpine-peirates:1.1.16
Building Peirates
However, if you want to build from source, read on!
Get peirates
go get -v "github.com/inguardians/peirates"
Get libary sources if you haven't already (Warning: this will take almost a gig of space because it needs the whole kubernetes repository)
go get -v "k8s.io/kubectl/pkg/cmd" "github.com/aws/aws-sdk-go"
Build the executable
cd $GOPATH/github.com/inguardians/peirates/scripts
./build.sh
This will generate an executable file named

192350229.title: Prisma Findings Requiring Discussion
192350229.plain_text:
Summary
There are a few categories of tickets currently being created and assigned which
appear
to be 'false positives' - that is the the condition of the actual resource doesn't seem to match the summary description of the finding.  In some cases this is 'making busy work' for the team, while at it's worst - remediation may actually
break
existing functionality.
Findings
AWS Default Security Group does not restrict all traffic
This finding references the out-of-the-box, not-created-by-us
default
Security Group that it automatically created by AWS with every account.  By my estimation / understanding this SG
does
'restrict all traffic'.
Additional relevant details:
This Security Group
cannot be deleted
The single Inbound rule is 'Self-Referential' - meaning it
only
allows traffic to/from
itself
Additional Prisma/Brinqa configurations generate tickets if this default Security Group is
used
Based on these details, it feels like we're creating work for our future selves - and work that doesn't seem to positively influence our security posture.
AWS SQS queue encryption using default KMS key instead of CMKs
This finding seems to be triggering off the 'default' term - but something's not right.  I
believe
the spirit of this control is to
not use
the default (SSE-SQS) encryption leveraging the AWS-provided and managed key.  We're
not
- as seen below - but we
are
using a service-scoped KMS CMK (e.g. /alias/sqs).  The finding reads "...
instead
of CMKs" – but we are
clearly
using a CMK.
Additional relevant details:
A CMK
is
in use.  It's the 'default' CMK for the service - but a CMK, and that's what the control is alluding to, right?  We're NOT using the AWS-provided-and-managed key:
(though I think in
most
cases - we probably should be?)
My concern with
any
'remediation' (e.g. - selecting a
different
CMK than the current, service-level CMK) is that we will
break something
.  Whatever new key we identify / select / create / use –-
every
process or service that interacts with these queues will need to have permission to use the key.  Kinesis Firehose?  Maybe.  Lamda consumers?  Probably.  QRadar configuration that pulls items from the queues?  Yup.  So – while I understand how we
could
remediate the finding, by provisioning
yet another
(identical but different) CMK – I'm worried about what we'll break, and why we're doing it.  If we were remediation by flipping it to SSE-SQS - I might agree that the benefit is worth the effort.

198902675.plain_text:
URL
New Entra Portal:

Azure Portal:

Tenants
PGE - login with PROD credentials for read only; login using Prod Admin ID (Incognito window) for write access
PGEDev - login with
lab.pge.com
Admin ID (Incognito Window)
To get a regular (non-Admin ID) in PGEDev, r
equest
PGEDev AzureAD Guest Account in MEA.
For Admin access:  Simon needs to assign the application administrator and application developer role in each tenant.  If missing, you may not be able to "create your own application" under the New Application screen (option will be greyed out).  Creating a new App Registration will also fail.
PROD Tenant MEA Role:  Apr-A2262-Prod-AzureAD_Applicationadministrator
Configuration Notes
Choose Azure Active Directory from top left menu
Choose Enterprise Applications for SAML
Choose App Registrations for OAuth
Per A8CO on 11/22/2022 Teams Chat:  CRQs for PROD only, example:  CRQ000000129243
Azure/Entra uses the Office365 SAML SP Connection in PingFederate.
Enterprise Applications
Properties tab
Name
Enabled for users to sign-in - must be checked for SSO
User Access URL - IdP-initiated URL, includes Application ID and Tenant ID
Assignment required - set to no to allow all AD users; or set to yes for authorization
NOTES field - please enter the following details in the Notes field of the Properties page for every Enterprise Application:
AMPS ID
Contacts (Email addresses, comma separated)
Your own Corp ID (the SSO Admin who created the Enterprise Application)
Users and Groups tab
Use to configure users and/or groups to be used for authorization
AD users and groups are synced automatically in PROD AD Tenant.  Users and groups are not synced to PGEDev Tenant at this time.
Single Sign On tab
Basic SAML - Identifier (Entity ID):  SP Entity ID
Basic SAML - Reply URL:  ACS URL
Basic SAML - Sign On URL/Relay State/Logout URL - optional, not used much.  Sign On URL would be the App URL, not exactly sure how it is used?
Attributes & Claims - Required claim = SAML Subject, leave Namespace blank? Can do transformations or just include attribute.
Attributes & Claims - Additional Claims - Add additional claims as needed.  May not have all the same attributes and transformations available.
SAML Certificates - each app has its own certificate by default, good for three years.  Need to update the email notification field to send to
azuresso@pge.com
and App team.  May need to set up a self-service portal for these updates.  Sanjay is working on this.
SAML Certificates - you can download the certificate and IdP metadata from here.  Verification certificates is where the SP certificate would be stored for validating the AuthNRequest.
Logs
Logs can be found under Activity -> Sign-in logs.  Will appear 5-15 minutes after activity.  No other logs exist.
Notes
Conditional Access - two primary policies are:  MFA for all apps and Device Compliance (must either be managed by AD or complaint; some browsers do not pass Compliance info like Firefox).  Technically, Azure goes through every policy, not an ordered policy like PF.  Not sure I 100% understand this, but in sign-in logs, you can see what policies were applied and whether they passed or failed, etc.
Guest Accounts are used for cross-tenant logins.  Guest Accounts don’t count towards our license.  Used to allow PROD AD credentials to be used for PGEDev tenant.  But, not all attributes are passed, possibly just userprincipalname, so have to do some trimming to get samAccountName.

:  Remedy DEV

For testing access, open the app in regular browser and it should seamlessly log user to the app.  Do not use Incognito or Private browsing window.

pge.com
.
If user gets prompted to enter ID on Ping login page, enter just your Prod ID (LANID).
If prompted for password (either from Ping or MSFT), enter Prod AD password.
For regular users trying to access non-prod app -
Request "AzureAD PGEDev Guest Account" in MEA.
If app has a group membership requirement, request group membership through MEA.
Once above 2 requests are complete, Access app in regular browser and it should seamlessly log user the app.
If user gets prompted to enter ID on MSFT login page, enter prod id - <LANID>@
pge.com
.
If user gets prompted to enter ID on Ping login page, enter just your Prod ID (LANID).

300748604.title: PSS and PSA
300748604.plain_text:
PSA enforces the policies outlined in PSS, and the PSS policies define a set of Pod security profiles. In the diagram below, we outline how PSA and PSS work together, with Pods and Namespaces, to define Pod security profiles and apply admission control based on those profiles. As seen in the diagram below, the PSA enforcement modes and PSS policies are defined as labels in the target Namespaces.
Below is an example of PSA and PSS Namespace configurations that can be used for testing. Note that we did not include the optional PSA mode-version label. We used the cluster-wide setting, latest, configured by default. By uncommenting the desired labels, below, you can enable the PSA modes and PSS profiles you need for your respective Namespace.
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
name: psa-pss-test-ns
labels:
pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: privileged
pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: privileged
pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: privileged
pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: baseline
pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: baseline
pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: baseline
pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted
pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted
Source:
https://www.eksworkshop.com/docs/security/pod-security-standards/

300750797.title: Wiz Findings
300750797.plain_text:
Deployed new cluster for POC
Visualization and Reporting:
Dashboards
: Visual representations of cluster components, security metrics, and compliance status.
Reports
: Detailed reports on the security posture and compliance of the Kubernetes environment
The user interface is intuitive and user-friendly, allowing security teams to navigate and operate the tool without extensive training.
The tool provides actionable insights and recommendations that are clear and easy to understand.
Security Coverage
The tool demonstrates the ability to identify at least 95% of known security misconfigurations and vulnerabilities in our
cloud environment. It supports all cloud services currently in use by our organization.
102 policy checks under Kubernetes
Node and Pod Security
:
Node Restrictions
: Implementation of NodeRestriction to prevent unauthorized modifications.
Pod Security Policies
: Enforcement of policies to control pod behavior and access.
Network Security
:
Network Policies
: Implementation and effectiveness of network policies to control traffic between pods.
Ingress/Egress Controls
: Measures to secure ingress and egress traffic.
Compliance Reporting
The tool is able to generate compliance reports that align with industry standards and regulatory requirements
relevant to our organization. Reports are customizable and exportable in formats such as CSV, PDF, or via API.
Alerting and Notifications:
The tool provides real-time alerts for critical security events and have the capability to escalate issues according to
predefined rules. Notification settings are configurable to meet the needs of different stakeholders
Attack simulation on EKS cluster:
The Wiz Runtime Sensor collects runtime events on Kubernetes-cluster workloads and translates them to detections.
Use this benign attack scenario to generate detection events simulating events that will be triggered in case of a similar attack.
https://docs.wiz.io/wiz-docs/docs/sensor-simulation
Cloud events during the attack simulation
https://pge-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/p/s9gl/EfYrWKI-nchDrD6TvIv_itsBg0PyipknMzQb4tzm1IF-6A?e=RKdLAA
Deployment of Wiz Addons for Threat detection and Monitoring
:
Wiz has made it very easy to
deploy
the sensors, brokers, admission controller and audit log controller by providing helm
chart which can be deployed using Terraform.
Status of addons in UI
Offerings after Add-on deployment and issues detected
Code :
Support and Documentation
The vendor provides comprehensive documentation, including a knowledge base and FAQs.
Support response times met the service level agreements (SLAs) specified in the vendor contract.
PRISMA CLOUD VS WIZ
Prisma Cloud
Visibility and Monitoring
Provides visibility of clusters but only after installing agents
Policies and Compliance
Prisma cloud covers 16 policies under EKS and Kubernetes including custom policies
Covers all the industry standard
Remediation
Offers remediation for identified issues and vulnerabilities but with less comprehensive steps
Vulnerabilities
Vulnerabilities are shown under each pod and need to navigate through the diagram under the cluster to view issues
After navigating though the vulnerabilities tab we need to navigate through each severity and fix accordingly
Advantage
:
Twistlock agent which can be deployed using Helm can be enough to detect Vulnerabilities and issues which no extra charge.
Disadvantage:
*No dedicated Kubernetes cluster section to view details of the cluster in UI. It is generalized under containers.
* Doesn't detect EKS cluster issues or Vulnerabilities without Twistlock agent. UI for Vulnerabilities makes it hard to navigate through each issue and no precise remediation steps
Wiz
Visibility and Monitoring
Provides visibility of clusters without installing agents
Policies and Compliance
Wiz covers 11 cloud configurations rules and 769 threat detection rules under Kubernetes. PSS and PSA are also checked
Remediation
Offers guidance for remediation and best practices for securing Kubernetes environments using Ask AI feature to generate step by step instructions for resolution
Vulnerabilities
Focus more on High and critical alerts under vulnerabilities
Each namespace or pod can be dissected and analyze using Forensics and issues tab to check the impacted resources
Disadvantage
:
* Need Connector deployed to EKS cluster with service account to view details like issues, secrets, data, and Kubernetes section.
* Too many details and tabs under each cluster which is hard to keep track of issues, vulnerabilities and events.
* Extra cost for Runtime sensors for each EKS cluster
Advantage
:
Wiz Runtime Sensor, Connector and Admission controller are all provided with simple helm chart which can track runtime time issue with precise.

292035168.title: Setup
292035168.plain_text:
For developing an application and deploying that application to Mulesoft requires the following setup
Download
studio
after providing minimal information
Unzip the download and complete installation. Launch it by selecting a directory for workspace
Go to settings
Add 'External identity' as 'pacific-gas-and-electric-6' by going to Authentication → Add –> Configure
It should then take you through SSO, if it doesn't do that, like it did to me... changeto default browser by going to Settings → General → Web browser and select 'naive' @ Settings → AnypointStudio→ Browser
and select 'Built in' at Settings → Anypoint Studio → Browser
You should then be able to see the SSO Sign-in page when you go to Settings → Anypoint Studio → Authentication and try to click on Add → Configure as like you did last time
Possible Errors
Some users might observe errors like below. If so, follow the steps as follows.
There was a problem with the SSL handshake, please check your certificate installation.
javax.net.ssl.SSLHandshakeException: PKIX path building failed: sun.security.provider.certpath.SunCertPathBuilderException: unable to find valid certification path to requested target
Delete the secure storage configuration
Add the proxy settings to your Anypoint Studio.
Apply the changes.
Perform the steps as listed above in
Add Authentication to your Anypoint Studio
Use "Google Chrome" Browsers on PGE Windows Virtual Machine for proper Authentication. Was reported for Authentication issue with Edge Browsers.

330703633.title: AWS Services
330703633.plain_text:
Overview
This page provides a comprehensive index of all AWS service categories that have defined policies in this repository. These policies help ensure that AWS resources are configured according to best practices for security, compliance, and operational excellence.
The AWS Cloud Config Policies project maintains configuration policies for services across multiple AWS categories, providing standardized guidance for cloud resource governance.
These are the service categories which contain services with defined policies:
Category
Description
AI/ML
Machine learning and artificial intelligence services for building intelligent applications
Analytics
Services for processing, analyzing, and deriving insights from large volumes of data
Application Integration
Services that connect applications, data, and services across your organization
Compute
Services that provide processing power to run applications and workloads in the cloud
Containers
Services for deploying, managing, and scaling containerized applications
Database
Purpose-built database engines to support different data models and application requirements
Management & Governance
Tools to help manage and govern AWS resources at scale
Migration & Transfer
Tools to help migrate existing applications, data, and workloads to AWS
Networking
Services for connecting applications and resources within AWS and to the outside world
Security
Services to help secure cloud resources, manage access, and protect data
Storage
Scalable, durable, and secure storage solutions for various use cases
Categories Without Defined Policies
The following AWS service categories do not currently have defined policies in this repository:
Business Applications
Cloud Financial Management
Developer Tools
Frontend Web & Mobile
IoT
Media
Policy Coverage
Each service category overview page lists only the AWS services that have defined policies in this repository. The policies include:
Security best practices
Compliance requirements
Operational recommendations
Cost optimization guidance
Performance efficiency standards
Using This Resource
To find policies for specific AWS services:
1. Navigate to the relevant service category from the table above
2. Review the services listed on the category overview page
3. Access the detailed policy documents in the corresponding services directory
References
AWS Services
AWS Documentation
AWS Well-Architected Framework

350690037.title: SAF Control Matrix - Azure Governance
350690037.plain_text:
Control Type: Encryption and Key Management
Category: Security
Description: Implement encryption for data at rest and in transit, use managed keys for sensitive data and ensure proper key management and rotation.
Mapping Detail: Policies enforcing encryption at rest and in transit; Key Vault policies for key rotation.
Gap Identified: Limited visibility into key usage and rotation
Recommendation: Integrate with Azure Key Vault monitoring and use Wiz for enhanced visibility
Control Type: Access Control and Identity Management
Category: Security
Description: To implement strong authentication, apply least privilege principle, and regularly review and audit access.
Mapping Detail: Use built-in roles and custom roles to enforce least privilege, Conditional Access policies for MFA.
Recommendation: Automate access reviews using Azure AD Identity Governance and Wiz alerts
Control Type: Network Security
Category: Security
Description: Securing all network communication, implementing proper network segmentation, and control inbound and outbound traffic.
Mapping Detail: Policies for NSG rules, VNet peering restrictions, and firewall configurations.
Recommendation: Use Terraform for centralized policy management
Control Type: Logging and Monitoring
Category: Operations
Description: Use of centralized logging, implementing comprehensive monitoring and ensure proper log retention and protection.
Mapping Detail: Custom and native policies to enforce diagnostic settings and log retention, and integration with Log Analytics.
Gap Identified: Limited enforcement of log protection
Recommendation: Use Defender for Cloud and Wiz to monitor log integrity
https://www.wiz.io/academy/file-integrity-monitoring-fim
Control Type: System Management Configuration
Category: Compliance
Description: Implement Tagging standards and also conduct regular audits while adhering to relevant regulatory requirements.
Mapping Detail: Policies enforcing tagging standards and audit configurations.
Recommendation: Automate audits using Azure Monitor and Wiz compliance dashboards
Control Type: Regulatory Compliance and Data Protection
Category: Data Management
Description: Implement PG&E data classification, retention, and backup policies and ensure proper data handling throughout its lifecycle.
Mapping Detail: Policies for backup configuration and data classification tags, and retention rules.
Recommendation: Use Azure Backup and Wiz for lifecycle tracking
Control Type: Infrastructure Security
Category: Operations
Description: Use Terraform or CloudFormation and implement policy as code using respective cloud provider's policy services (AWS Config Rules, Azure Policy)
Mapping Detail: Policy as code via Azure Policy definitions embedded in Terraform templates.
Recommendation: Use CI/CD pipelines with policy checks and Wiz for drift detection
Control Mappings
Control Type
Category
Severity
Azure Governance Tool
Encryption and Key Management
Security
High

Access Control and Identity Management
Security
High
RBAC

Security

Azure Policy

Azure Policy

Data Management

Azure Policy
Infrastructure Security
Operations

197657764.title: Entra Application Proxy
197657764.plain_text:
***   v0.1 Work in Progress   ***
Microsoft Teams Channel:
Azure AD Application Proxy
secures applications by providing clients a secure public endpoint with advanced security and Conditional Access capabilities.  Given that the Cloud Security Center of Excellence (CSCOE) can protect solutions on-premise and in the AWS Cloud,  a
fter a single sign-on to Azure AD, users can access both cloud and on-premises applications through an external URL or an internal application portal.
For example, AADAP can provide remote access and single sign-on to Remote Desktop, SharePoint, Teams, Tableau, Qlik, and line of business (LOB) applications.
Advantages
Azure Application Proxy is provides secure access to your on-premise web applications without the need to open any inbound ports on the firewall.
AADAP may be integrated with Multi Factor authentication like DUO or MS authenticator and Conditional Access Policies to harden the access paths.
Learn More
Remote access to on-premises apps - Azure AD Application Proxy - Microsoft Entra | Microsoft Learn
Table of Contents
High-Level Architecture

168199217.title: Threat Models
168199217.plain_text:
Threat modeling
is a process by which potential threats, such as
structural vulnerabilities
or the absence of appropriate safeguards, can be identified and enumerated, and countermeasures prioritized.
[1]
The purpose of threat modeling is to provide defenders with a systematic analysis of what controls or defenses need to be included, given the nature of the system, the probable attacker's profile, the most likely attack vectors, and the assets most desired by an attacker.
Here is an example of
STRIDE Threat modeling
...
Threat Model
Threat-1
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Authenticate, Spoof User Identity, Spoof AWS SFTP Service Endpoint, Spoof Notification(s), Spoof Email Sender, Spoof GeoLocation, CRUD User
Threat agent or source
External threats
include but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Goals
Reliable Identity
Trustworthy Authentication
Accurate Access Management
Exploit details
A spoofing attack is a situation in which a person or program successfully identifies as another by falsifying data, to gain an illegitimate advantage.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
SPOOFING
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches as threat responses
SFTP encrypts authentication
being transferred over the SSH data stream.
Require clients store private-key(s) in a secure vault
.  This option may protect the theft of private-key(s) should the 3rd party system be compromised.
Use IP blacklisting and whitelisting.
It may be necessary to deploy SFTP Server in private VPC to block access then manage a white-list of our approved 3rd parties ip addresses. This can be implemented with AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) and/or VPC hosted endpoints and VPC SecurityGroups.
Issue certificates to external users, 3rd parties.
This option will ensure that anyone accessing your system has a PGE-security certificate to verify who they are. This can be implemented with MTLS protocol and AWS Certificate Manager service.
SFTP doesn’t stop the unauthorized transfer of data to third parties.
This can lead to non-compliant disclosures of data, which may break regulatory rules on confidentiality and privacy.
Threat-2
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Tamper with Data-In-Transit, Tamper with File(s)Data-at-Rest, Tamper with Credentials? Tamper with Data-In-Use, Tamper with Notifications, Tamper with Logs, Encrypt by DataClassification?  Crack Encryption(s) AES, KMS, TLS?
Threat agent or source
Those working inside an organization
may be a significant cause of elevated privileges.
External threats
include but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Goals
Data Integrity
Notification Integrity
Protect by Data Classification = Restricted, Confidential, Internal, Public
Exploit details
Those working inside an organization
may be a significant cause of tampering.
External threats
include but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
TAMPERING
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Unique Customer Managed Key(s) per Workflow Instance; Managed by AWS KMS; used by AWS S3.
SFTP encrypts everything being transferred over the SSH data stream; from the authentication of the users to the actual files being transferred, if any part of the data is intercepted, it will be unreadable because of the encryption.
Configure SSH with AES-256 cipher-algorithms
SSH uses a hashing algorithm, usually SHA-2, to determine data integrity.
AWS Hardened SFTP Service
Use Digital Signatures (MD5) for Files & Notifications.
Threat-3
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Report on Authentication(s), Report on Workflow Transaction(s), Report by User-Identity, Transaction-Identity, Workflow-Instance, Sign File-Transfer, Sign Notification
Threat agent or source
Both legitimate users and criminal perpetrators.
Goals
The inability to deny responsibility for performing a specific act.
Assurance that the sender of information is provided with proof of delivery and the recipient is provided with proof of the sender’s identity, so neither can later deny having processed the information.
Protection against an individual falsely denying having performed a particular action. Provides the capability to determine whether a given individual took a particular action such as creating information, sending a message, approving information, and receiving a message.
Exploit details
Nonrepudiation is achieved through cryptography, like digital signatures,
and includes other services for authentication, auditing and logging. In online transactions, digital signatures ensure that a party cannot later deny sending information or deny the authenticity of its signature.
SFTP does not centralize audits, logs or documentation.
Most compliance frameworks require some documentation to demonstrate compliance. SFTP can include audit logs, but without a centralized SFTP server documenting access across multiple systems can make documentation hard and raise red flags for assessors. Likewise, documentation must also adhere to privacy laws, which becomes exponentially more difficult over multiple SFTP servers.
Include documentation and auditing.
Document things like compliance and file access. Monitor file access as well as document things like user consent and other requests.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
REPUDIATION
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Use digital signature(s) to support a determination of whether a message was actually signed by a given entity.
To mitigate the risk of people repudiating their own signatures, the standard approach is to involve a trusted third party.
Use CCOE's Centralized Logging: AWS CloudTrail & CloudWatch Logs
Each Workflow Instance has an identity; searchable in CloudTrail logs.
Each Transaction has an identity; searchable/traceable in CloudTrail logs.
Each File has identity; searchable/traceable in CloudTrail logs.
CloudTrail logs searchable by User identity.
Threat-4
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Assign File PG&E Data Classification, Configure Users, Folders and Roles per Workflow Instance
Threat agent or source
Those working inside an organization
are a significant cause of data breaches.
External threats
include but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Goals
Trust & Privacy of Data, i.e. Personally Identifiable Information
Data Loss Prevention
Enforcement of Data Classification Policies
Reasonable Storage Costs
Exploit details
This may also be known as
data privacy
or
data breach
.  Data privacy is challenging since it attempts to use data while protecting an individual's privacy preferences and personally identifiable information.  A data breach is a security violation, in which sensitive, protected or confidential data is copied, transmitted, viewed, stolen or used by an individual unauthorized to do so.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
INFORMATION DISCLOSURE
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Use folder security for internal access.
Access controls can be a pain to set up because somebody has to do it manually on individual folders. Business users typically don’t have the skills or permission to do this, so organizations often resort to these users writing help desk tickets for IT to undertake access management tasks.
Implement trust boundaries,
aka multi-tenant isolation, using AWS S3 Bucket Policy + S3-Labels (aka Folders) + KMS Key Policy.
Practice the principle of least privilege
when configuring AWS DataTransferUser + ssh-key + IAM RBAC.
Require clients store private-key(s) and signatures in a secure vault
.  This option may protect the theft of private-key(s) should the 3rd party system be compromised.
Use CCOEs
to label artifacts to facilitate identification, protection and notification.
Use PGE
to identify sensitivity of data.
Business defined data storage policies; use S3 Storage Class Analysis.
Threat-5
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Connect, Authenticate, Send, Receive
Threat agent or source
Criminal perpetrators of DoS attacks often target sites or services hosted on high-profile web servers such as banks or credit card payment gateways. Revenge, blackmail and hacktivism can motivate these attacks.
Goals
Prevent the
crash or flooding of services.
Minimize application downtime and latency.
Scrub bad traffic at specific layers.
Exploit details
A
denial-of-service attack (DoS attack)
is a cyber-attack in which the perpetrator seeks to make a machine or network resource unavailable to its intended users by temporarily or indefinitely disrupting services of a host connected to a network. Denial of service is typically accomplished by flooding the targeted machine or resource with superfluous requests in an attempt to overload systems and prevent some or all legitimate requests from being fulfilled. In a
distributed denial-of-service attack (DDoS attack)
, the incoming traffic flooding the victim originates from many different sources.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
DENIAL OF SERVICE
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Protect the AWS Application Load Balancers and SFTP servers behind a firewall
, use AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) with ELBs in Transit account; target auto-scaling-groups within the private VPC/subnets of partner accounts.
Protect service endpoints with security group(s) attached to VPCs, subnets.
Maximize application availability and responsiveness with managed DDoS protection.  Configure AWS Shield.
Use AWS Shield to configure static thresholds to protect services.
To integrate logging with PGE's SIOC so they
can detect and mitigate attacks, use CCOE's Centralized Logging infrastructure.
Threat-6
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
CRUD Workflow(s), CRUD User(s), Spoof ssh-Session, Guess Administrator Password
Threat agent or source
Those working inside an organization
are a significant cause of elevated privileges.
External threats
include but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Goals
Improve administrative efficiency with a self-service kiosk for CRUD'ing users.
User-Privilege oversight and governance.
Prevent Horizontal privilege escalation between tenants.
Prevent Vertical privilege escalation.
Exploit details
Privilege escalation
is the act of exploiting a
bug
, a design flaw, or a configuration oversight in an
operating system
or
software application
to gain elevated access to
resources
that are normally protected from an application or
user
. The result is that an application with more
privileges
than intended.
Vertical privilege escalation
, also known as
privilege elevation
, where a lower privilege user or application accesses functions or content reserved for higher privilege users or applications.
Horizontal privilege escalation
, where a normal user accesses functions or content reserved for other normal users.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
ELEVATION OF PRIVILEGE
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Configure S3, KMS & IAM to isolate / encapsulate Workflow Instances (aka Tenants)
.
Configure IAM-Policies to separate Administrator and User-Roles
.
Require clients store private-key(s) and signatures in a secure vault
.  This option may protect the theft of private-key(s) should the 3rd party system be compromised.
Implement a self-service kiosk for administrative tasks; use PGE LanID, SSO, MEA workflow to authorize kiosk access.
Practice the principle of least privilege
when configuring AWS DataTransferUser + ssh-key + IAM RBAC.

Vocabulary
Term
Definition
SAST
Static application security testing
(SAST) is used to secure software by reviewing the source code of the software to identify sources of vulnerabilities. SAST tools focus on the code content of the application to identify potential security vulnerabilities in their software and architecture.
DAST
A
dynamic application security testing
(DAST) is a non functional testing process where one can assess an application using certain techniques and the end result of such testing process covers security weaknesses and vulnerabilities present in an application.
IAST
SCA
Software Composition Analysis
(SCA) is the process of identifying the components that comprise a given piece of software.

49961477.title: AWS Access Control Strategy
49961477.plain_text:
Related:
AWS Access Control Strategy Overview
Purpose
Expectations
Constraints
Purpose
Used to grant access to users to AWS services.  Guiding principles include a balance of:
Maximum flexibility / productivity through relatively unconstrained access in lower envioronments
Enforced Least Privilege in critical environments / production workloads
Consistent Roles (Audit, DevOps, CloudAdmin, SecurityAdmin, etc) across all accounts
Generally - we want to empower users (be they developers, administrators, operators or auditors) to carry out their responsibilities with minimal friction, as well as to leverage automation and cloud-native services to reduce time-to-market, increase availability, and loosely-couple services.
Expectations
MyElectronicAccess
(aka Oracle Identity Manager) is used to manage access by providing a mechanism to request access to specific Active Directory security groups - which in turn enables users to perform SAML Federation into AWS (Web Console, CLI and APIs) with permissions based on the corresponding role.
AMPS IDs are used to manage the association of Applications to specific AWS resources which support or enable the service.  Where AMPS IDs exist - they should be associated with resources using AWS Tags, as described in the
Tagging Guideline
.

49961467.title: AWS IAM Roles
49961467.plain_text:
Related:
AWS IAM Role Strategy
The strategy for IAM Roles in PG&E's AWS environment is to have a single, consistent set of roles provisioned across all accounts. This strategy decreases complexity and the attempts to maximize flexibility / developer productivity while still retaining a posture of Least Privilege in critical (read: Production) accounts. Each role must be unique in purpose.  The specific policies (permissions) associated with a given role in a particular account may be tailored, if required, to meet objectives.  However, roles should only be provisioned, centrally, via CloudFormation StackSet or AWS Organization, to retain visibility and auditability.
AWS Permissions Guiding Principles
Everyone is acting with PG&E's best interest in mind:
No one
is actively trying to reduce or lower PG&E's Security Posture
Solution needs to account for / address
risks
of Account Compromise, Insider Threat.
Absolute
security - if it impairs / prevents delivery of business value - is
not
in the best interest of PG&E.
Principles of
Least Privilege
,
Segregation of Duties
, and
Containing Blast Radius
are relevant - but not as
academic
concepts which don't consider the delivery of work
In order to deliver, secure and manage the commercial cloud - CCOE staff requires
full access
to the tools and services which enable this delivery
Target state of
Everything as Code
for production environments:  e.g. only "break glass" access for human intervention.  All Production environments should be deployed via automation.
Single-Account Federation, Cross-Account AssumeRole Pattern
In lieu of configuring multiple, direct federation relationships between PingFederate and each AWS Account - we are exploring an approach by which we centralize the SAML Federation relationship to a single account (the top of the AWS Organizations hierarchy - aka the "Master Account") - and from there, leverage Cross-Account AssumeRole policies to allow users to assume the required roles in the various management and workload accounts.  This configuration is examined and demonstrated in the following tutorial:
Delegate Access Across AWS Accounts Using IAM Roles
- and implemented in depth in this AWS Answer covering
Multi-Account Security Strategy
and
Cross-Account Manager
solution deployments.  The solution delivered by the provided CloudFormation templates adds some interesting value-adds around creating a static web-page hosted in S3 containing all the various "AssumeRole" links.  Not part of our MVP - but definitely something to add to the backlog.
AWS IAM Roles
This table describes the global IAM Roles used for all AWS Accounts within the PG&E LandingZone:
