It is worth noting that there will be both Production and Non-Prod instances of each of these roles, with unique membership, leveraging Active Directory Security Groups and MyElectronicAccess.
In addition to these ‘meta’ roles, which will exist across all accounts - there will also be application-specific roles, used in support of the system, that will need to be uniquely created, and deployed, across various Prod and non-Prod application workload accounts.  This is an evolving activity:  Currently roles are expected to be defined by the application / development teams - but will currently be deployed (at least in Production accounts) by Cloud COE personnel.  We will continue to evaluate this as the Landing Zone continues to mature and evolve, with an eye on self-service for the Developments teams wherever it makes sense.

51874649.title: Choosing the correct authentication, authorization flows
51874649.plain_text:
We all have experienced this when dealing with modern apps - A mobile app wants to have access to your contact details or photos; a job portal wants to access your LinkedIn profile; or Facebook wants access to your cloud-based photo repository to fetch selected pictures of yours to post in your wall. In the past we ended up giving our user id and password to this third-party apps/ portal. This was such a bad idea and a big security risk. This pain point gave birth to OAuth 2.0.
OAuth 2.0 stands for
Open Authorization
Framework and is the industry-standard delegation protocol for authorization. It enables a third-party application to obtain limited access to an HTTP service, either on behalf of a resource owner by orchestrating an approval interaction between the resource owner and the HTTP service, or by allowing the third-party application to obtain access on its own behalf.
In simple words “it’s designed around granting another application permission to do stuff.”
To understand OAuth framework, the following actors and their roles should be understood as it relates to our App development:
Resource owner (PG&E User)
Client (usually an app that requires access to resource. Typically, a mobile app)
Authorization server (PingFederate – that asks the resource owner for access to the resources on behalf of the client)
Resource server (Server/ entity exposing the HTTP API that provides access to resources from an authorized clients)
An important thing to remember is that OAuth specification does not prescribe how a resource owner using the client authenticates to get access to a resource. Authentication is out of scope for the OAuth specification. What OAuth prescribes is a set of usable authorization grants that can be used to coordinate authentication of a user and grant access to resources owned by that use
These
grants
include:
Authorization Code Grant
Implicit Grant
Resource Owner Password Credential Grant
Client Credential Grant
The OAuth 2.0 specification defines several authorization grants that can be used to coordinate authentication of a user and grant access to resources owned by that user. To address the user identification and authentication OpenID Connect 1.0 specification was drafted and approved by IETF
OpenID Connect 1.0 (OIDC) is an interoperable authentication protocol based on the OAuth 2.0 family of specifications. It is a simple identity layer on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol. For the app builder, it uses straightforward REST/JSON message flows and provides a secure verifiable, answer to the question: “What is the identity of the person currently using the browser or native app that is connected to me?”. Clients receive the user's identity encoded in a secure JSON Web Token (JWT), called an ID token.
The OIDC spec adds to OAuth grants list by providing a set of authentication
flows
:
Authorization Code Flow
Implicit Flow
Hybrid Flow
The old Vanguard team and the Cloud CoE team have tested couple of OAuth grant types and OIDC flows respectively.
We talked about grants, flows. Now, let us talk about the main clients and then stitch everything together.
Public Clients – as the name specifies, these are untrustworthy clients. Typical examples are Mobile Apps, JavaScript based Apps, Single Page Applications (SPA). Any secret stored in a mobile application (like a client id) must be considered public, and therefore untrustworthy
Confidential Clients – Traditional web applications that can confidentially handle secrets and communication.
Use
è
Authorization Code Flow with Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE) for Native Mobile Apps.
As mentioned earlier, mobile/ native apps are untrusted public clients. They cannot store secret and can reveal secrets when decompiled. It may make use of a custom URL scheme to capture redirects (e.g., MyApp://) potentially allowing malicious applications to receive an Authorization Code from your Authorization Server. (we will see an illustration on how this happens)
To mitigate this, OAuth 2.0 provides a version of the Authorization Code Flow which makes use of a Proof Key for Code Exchange (PKCE), pronounced as
pixy/ pixie (yes..like the pixie dust).
The PKCE-enhanced Authorization Code Flow introduces a secret created by the calling application that can be verified by the authorization server; this secret is called the Code Verifier. Additionally, the calling app creates a transform value of the Code Verifier called the Code Challenge and sends this value over HTTPS to retrieve an Authorization Code. This way, a malicious attacker can only intercept the Authorization Code, and they cannot exchange it for a token without the Code Verifier.
Let us first start with Auth Code flow and then go through how an malicious app can hijack the code and how PKCE provides protection against Hijack.
Authorization code Flow for Mobile, native apps:
The flow begins with the Client application starting the      system’s browser (or a web view, more on this on another post) at a URL      with the authorization request.
The browser comes into the foreground and performs a GET      request to the AS authorization endpoint containing the authorization      request.
The same authentication and consent dance occur between the AS      and the User’s browser.
After having authenticated and obtained consent from the user,      the AS returns a HTTP redirect response with the authorization response on      the Location header. This URL contains the the authorization response      parameters. However, there is something special in the redirect URL.      Instead of using a
http
URL      scheme, which would make the browser perform another HTTP request, the      redirect URL contains a custom URI scheme.
As a result, when the browser receives this response and      processes the redirect an inter-application message (e.g. an intent      in Android) is sent to the application associated to this scheme, which      should be the Client application. This brings the Client application to      the foreground and provides it with the authorization response      parameters, namely the authorization code.
From now on, the flow is like the Web based one. Namely, the      Client application uses the code to obtain the access token from the AS      token endpoint. Since the client is a public client, this request      is
not
authenticated, that is no client secret      is used.
Finally, having received the access token, the client      application running on the device can access the User’s resources.
Hijacking the redirect
On the Web application scenario, the GET request with the authorization response has a HTTPS URL, which means that the browser will only send the code if the server correctly authenticates itself. However, on the native scenario, the intent will be sent to any installed application that registered the custom scheme. Unfortunately, there isn’t a central entity controlling and validating these scheme registrations, so an application can hijack the message from the browser to the client application, as shown in the following diagram.
Having obtained the authorization code, the attacker’s application has all the information required to retrieve a token and access the User’s resources.
The PKCE protection
The PKCE specification mitigates this vulnerability by requiring an extra code_verifier parameter on the exchange of the authorization code for the access token.
On step 1, the Client application generates a      random secret, stores it and uses its hash value on the      new
code_challenge
authorization request      parameter.
On step 4, the AS
somehow
associates      the returned code to the code_challenge.
On step 6, the Client includes a code_verifier parameter      with the secret on the token request message. The AS computes the hash of      the code_verifier value and compares it with the original code_challenge      associated with the code. Only if they are equals is the code accepted and      an access token returned.
This ensures that only the entity that started the flow (sent the code_challenge on the authorization request) can end the flow and obtain the access token. By using a cryptographic hash function on the
code_challenge
, the protocol is protected from attackers that have read access to the original authorization request. However, the protocol also allows the secret to be used directly on the
code_challenge
.
Finally, the PKCE support by an AS can be advertised on the OAuth 2.0 or
OpenID Connect discovery
document, using the
code_challenge_methods_supported
field.
Apart from recommending PKCE, the
OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice
also recommends additional practice for redirect- based flows:
Authorization servers shall utilize exact matching of client redirect URIs against pre-registered URIs. This measure contributes to the prevention of leakage of authorization codes and access tokens (depending on the grant type). It also helps to detect mix up attacks.
Clients shall avoid any redirects or forwards, which can be parameterized by URI query parameters, in order to provide a further layer of defence against token leakage. If there is a need for this kind of redirects, clients are advised to implement appropriate counter measures against open redirection, e.g. as described by the OWASP.
Clients shall ensure to only process redirect responses of the OAuth authorization server they send the respective request to and in the same user agent this request was initiated in. In particular, clients shall implement appropriate XSRF prevention by utilizing one-time use XSRF tokens carried in the STATE parameter, which are securely bound to the user agent. Moreover, the client shall memorize which authorization server it sent an authorization request to and bind this information to the user agent and ensure any sub-sequent messages are sent to the same authorization server. Furthermore, clients should use AS-specific redirect URIs as a means to identify the AS a particular response came from. Matching this with the before mentioned information regarding the AS the client sent the request to helps to detect mix-up attacks.
Clients shall use PKCE in order to (with the help of the authorization server) detect and prevent attempts to inject (replay) authorization codes into the authorization response. The PKCE challenges must be transaction-specific and securely bound to the user agent, in which the transaction was started. OpenID Connect clients may use the nonce parameter of the OpenID Connect authentication request as specified in [OpenID] in conjunction with the corresponding ID Token claim of the for the same purpose.
While Implicit grant type is a viable option with public clients, the
IETF recommends strongly against using Implicit grant
on public clients. At PG&E, we should try to avoid any grant type that puts our data and clients at a security risk. The implicit grant has the access token leakage and access token replay attack vulnerabilities. So we strongly suggest Auth. Code flow with PKCE as the viable and secure option.
In the next wiki we will check the secure implementation possibilities and a viable model with Implicit grant type; but till we develop such a confidence we continue to recommend Auth. Code with PKCE as the chosen mode.
Reference Implementation pattern for OAuth, OIDC for Single Page Apps:
Here is a reference implementation pattern using a React App
Links:
https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51151148
https://wiki.comp.pge.com/display/CCE/Authorizer+Integrated+with+PingFederate
https://wiki.comp.pge.com/display/CCE/Deploy+SPA+for+PingOne+%28Implicit%29+Demo
https://tools.ietf.org/id/draft-ietf-oauth-security-topics-05.html
https://medium.com/@robert.broeckelmann/when-to-use-which-oauth2-grants-and-oidc-flows-ec6a5c00d864

49958723.title: Cloud Identity Reference Implementation
49958723.plain_text:
Context
As the computing world have evolved, PG&E has matured from individual userIDs on the mainframe, to individual UserIDs on UNIX servers, and eventually, with the advent of Banyan VINES and then Microsoft Active Directory - to the notion of an Enterprise Directory containing identities that can be leveraged across various systems, in the form of "SingleSignOn" (SSO).  Even SSO can carry different meanings / implications - in some cases, SSO is
transparent
- meaning, we don't bother asking the user for credentials, as they've already authenticated (perhaps to the workstation they're using) and that identity can be leveraged.  In other cases, it simply means re-using the same Username / Password across multiple systems - be they SAP, GIS, Documentum, etc.
Then along comes the Internet.  PG&E did some great work in creating and maintaining SingleSignOn across the portfolio of applications we created for and exposed to our various
external
user populations - be they customers, business partners, regulators, etc.  Not every enterprise was so successful in this endeavor (and sadly - I fear we may have digressed from this strongly-enforced / consistent experience).  Complimentary to the notion of allowing external entities constrained access to our internal systems - through applications exposed via
www.pge.com
- we also began to leverage Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) to augment our on-prem capabilities - solutions such as
concur.com
for Employee Expense Management is a prime example of this.  However, we didn't want to create
additional
usernames / passwords in all these remote systems - instead, we wanted to leverage our existing, corporate identities in these 3rd party (external) systems.
Enter the Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML):  This technology provided a standards-based mechanism for an organization to represent, and assert, a given identity to a partner site.  Today, we have scores of SAML federation arrangements with 3rd party SaaS providers, for both employees
and
customers.  However – in
every
case, we have required that the user first be 'logically' present on the PG&E network, and able to talk to internal systems, in order to authenticate - at which point we would create an outbound, "Identity Provider-initiated" SAML assertion.  But the world has continued to move on - and the aggregation of disparate services, multiple authentication systems, and rapidly-evolving architectures caused us to 'feel the pain' of this constraint.
Overview
Enter the world of
OAuth
(currently
OAuth 2.0
), and subsequently,
OpenID Connect
.  In today's environment - users want to 'take their identities with them' - whether that be a social login (Facebook, Google, Twitter are prominent examples) for consumers, or enterprises wishing to extend their identities into service mashups that we'd like to be able to easily change as frequently as we do our socks.
While there is
much
complexity here - distilled down and simply stated:  OAuth allows for 'delegation of authorization' - a perfect example being "authorizing the Instagram app to post to your Facebook Timeline".  It is worth noting that, by itself, OAuth has no support for
authentication
- e.g. the act of positively identifying a user (or system).
OpenID Connect (OIDC) adds authentication by building additional services on top of the OAuth primitives.  Excerpted from the link above:
OpenID Connect 1.0 is a simple identity layer on top of the OAuth 2.0 protocol. It allows Clients to verify the identity of the End-User based on the authentication performed by an Authorization Server, as well as to obtain basic profile information about the End-User in an interoperable and REST-like manner.
The two combine to provide a powerful yet flexible solution set which can be applied to most use cases.  You'll see very detailed and specific diagrams, along with working reference implementations and detailed READMEs in the associated GitHub repositories - but here's a simple diagram that shows the most basic of interactions and flows:
<image source
https://wiki.mozilla.org/Security/Guidelines/OpenID_connect
>
Getting Started
PG&E corporate identites are stored in our Enterprise Directory - specifically, Microsoft Active Directory.  This is the
system of record
for most of our identities attributes - with some of the most noteworthy being CorpID, password, email address, security group membership.  We have integrated with and built Federation Services on top of that directory - which provide us the ability to integrate with (internal or external) systems and applications which support the following federation standards:
SAML 2.0 (HTTP Browser Post method)
OAuth 2 / OpenIDConnect (often just referred to in aggregate as simply "OIDC")
In order to demonstrate, document, and accelerate learning and adoption of these services - we have developed two key reference implementations which provide working, hands-on examples of these services, as follows:
angular-auth-ping-federate
:  Javascript SinglePageApplication (SPA) hosted on AWS S3 which implements OIDC flows and demonstrates / provides visibility to underlying mechanics (tokens, grants, validation, etc)
api-gateway-ping-authorizer-with-secrets
:  Demonstrates extending identity past the login page and into the application internals / backend.  APIs are validated with OAuth tokens against IdP (PingFed)
Each of these repositories are
rich
with information from topology diagrams, sequence diagrams, configuration details, and full working code that enable developers to reproduce, within their own local environment.  We encourage you to roll up your sleeves and get your hands dirty with these technologies.  The more people that review and consume this material - the stronger the organization becomes in these capabilities - and the better these repositories and wiki pages will grow with your feedback!

60393065.title: Session Tags
60393065.plain_text:
Session tags are key-value pair attributes that you pass when you assume an IAM role or federated user in AWS STS.  Session tags are set by the Identity Provider and can optionally passed during
role chaining
using transitive tags. IAM Policies can implement conditions based on tag values, enabling
ABAC style access control
.
AWS Documentation:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_session-tags.html
Adding Session Tags attributes to the SAML Assertion
Configure the Identity Provider to pass attributes in the format:
https://aws.amazon.com/SAML/Attributes/PrincipalTag
:
KEY
Example assertion for setting tags
LanID
and
PGEOrganization
:
And set both of these tags to be transitive (
warning
:
additional permissions will need to be configured for role chaining to function – see below
):
Enable session tagging & restrict sts:AssumeRole to users with a specific tag value in the master account
Trust policy
for federated role:
Enable transitive tags when role chaining
The source role needs to be allowed to call sts:TagSession on the target role.  Add this to its
permissions
policy
:
Next, the target role needs to trust the source role to apply session tags on it (similar to how in the first step we trust the Identity Provider to apply session tags).
Update the target role
trust policy
to include:
Use tags in a policy to achieve ABAC
In this example we have a role with the AdministratorAccess managed policy assigned to it, but we want to prevent only members of the Cybersecurity team from using S3.
Add the following statement to the role's
permissions policy
:
Questions:
Does this work for CLI and Console?
Yes
Does this work for SAML and OIDC?
Yes
With this configuration, is it possible for users to override transitive tag values?
It's not possible to override transitive tags when assuming the target role:
Why am I getting an error: "<<
source role arn>>
is not authorized to perform: sts:TagSession on resource: <<
target role arn>>
"?
When the Identity Provider indicates a session tag is transitive, every time you assume a role AWS will automatically call sts:TagSession on your behalf for the transitive tags.  This means that any role you assume is required to trust the source role to apply session tags.
Are session tags logged in Cloudtrail?  Yes, but only for AssumeRole* operations:
Can session tags be used in SCPs?  Yes, you can use conditions on
aws:PrincipalTag/
KEY
values in the same way as demonstrated with permissions policies.

240550612.plain_text:
Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps Ops

Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps Ops

Purpose
To provide guidelines to work on SaaS Governance tool available at PG&E -Defender for Microsoft Cloud Apps
Audience
Cyber security Risk team, SIOC & Cloud COE
What is Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps?
Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps is a comprehensive cross-SaaS solution bringing deep visibility, strong data controls, and enhanced threat protection to PG&E cloud apps.
What is Cloud Discovery?
Cloud Discovery is one of the features of Defender for Cloud Apps, which enables PG&E to gain visibility into Shadow IT by discovering cloud apps in use.
What is Cloud Discovery Dashboard?
Defender for Cloud Apps enables PG&E to discover and analyze cloud apps that are in use in PG&E environment. The Cloud Discovery dashboard shows all the cloud apps running in the environment and categorizes them by function and enterprise readiness. For each app, discover the associated users, IP addresses, devices, transactions, and conduct risk assessment without needing to install an agent on any PG&E endpoint devices.
Can we detect new high-volume or wide app use?
Yes, detect new apps that are highly used, in terms of number of users or amount of traffic in your organization.
How to access the Cloud Discovery dashboard.
To access the Cloud Discovery dashboard, use the following procedure:
Navigate to the
Defender for Cloud Apps portal
.
In the navigation pane, select Discover and then select Cloud Discovery dashboard.
In the following screenshot of the Cloud Discovery Dashboard, you can review the following information:
What kinds of apps are being used?
Open alerts
Risk levels of apps in your organization
Top app users
App Headquarter location map.
What to review in the cloud app usage within PG&E?
Review the top app categories used within PG&E for each of the different use parameters.
Determine how much of this usage is by Sanction apps.
Investigate the apps in each specific category by using the Discovered apps tab.
Select the IP addresses tab to review the top users and source IP addresses.
Use the App Headquarters map to determine how the discovered apps spread by geographic location.
Review the risk score of the discovered apps in the App risk overview.
Review discovery alerts status to check how many open alerts should be investigated.
What are Discovery Policies?
Discovery policies enable PG&E to set alerts that notify SIOC team when new apps are detected within PG&E organization.
How to create the Policy's?
In the Microsoft 365 Defender portal, under Cloud Apps, go to Policies -> Policy management. Then select the Shadow IT tab. Select Create policy and then select App discovery policy.
How to Create an app discovery policy
Discovery policies enable PG&E to set alerts that notify SIOC team when new apps are detected within PG&E.
What steps to take to create a discovery policy?
In the Microsoft 365 Defender portal, under Cloud Apps, go to Policies -> Policy management. Then select the Shadow IT tab.
Select Create policy and then select App discovery policy.
Create a Cloud Discovery policy.
Always give a policy a name and description. If you want, you can leverage the existing template.
Set the Severity of the policy.
To set which discovered apps trigger this policy, add filters.
What all the Default policy's templates were provided by Microsoft?
How do we create a policy for PG&E customized requirement?
To create a customized policy for PG&E in the policy template section select "No template"
How to create an App Discovery to meet any of the specific compliance level detections?
How to create a App discovery policy Using the template?
In the create app discovery policy "policy template" select the appropriate policy.
How to Edit the existing App Discovery Policy?
Select the policy which you would like to Edit and see from the below screenshot select the "Edit policy" button.
How to delete the existing App Discovery Policy?
Select the policy which you would like to Delete and see from the below screenshot select the "Delete" button.
How to disable the existing Policy?
Select the policy which you would like to disable and see from the below screenshot select the "Disable" button.
What filter options are available to set the alerts for SIOC team?
Set the alerts based on the below options.
Daily traffic
Downloaded data.
Number of IP addresses
Number of transactions
Number of users
Uploaded data
How does the Cloud Discovery anomaly detection work?
Defender for Cloud Apps searches all the logs in your Cloud Discovery for anomalies. For instance, when a user, who never used Dropbox before, suddenly uploads 600 GB to it, or when there are a lot more transactions than usual on a particular app. The anomaly detection policy is enabled by default. It's not necessary to configure a new policy for it to work. However, you can fine-tune which types of anomalies you want to be alerted about in the default policy.
In the Microsoft 365 Defender portal, under Cloud Apps, go to Policies -> Policy management. Then select the Shadow IT tab.
Select Create policy and select Cloud Discovery anomaly detection policy.
cloud discovery anomaly detection policy menu.
Give your policy a name and description. If you want, you can base it on a template,
To set which discovered apps trigger this policy, select Add filters.
The filters are chosen from drop-down lists. To add filters, select Add a filter. To remove a filter, select the 'X'.
Under Apply to choose whether this policy applies All continuous reports or Specific continuous reports. Select whether the policy applies to Users, IP addresses, or both.
Select the dates during which the anomalous activity occurred to trigger the alert under Raise alerts only for suspicious activities occurring after date.
Set a Daily alert limit under Alerts. Select if the alert is sent as an email. Then provide email addresses as needed.
Selecting Save alert settings as the default for your organization enables future policies to use the setting.
If you have a default setting, you can select Use your organization's default settings.
Select Create.new discovery anomaly policy.
What do all the repeatable ongoing operations activities needs to be performed by PG&E teams?
Create new app discovery policies to detect usage that doesn't meet your organization's policy.
Create continuous reports to discover risk per business unit.
Review untagged cloud apps, and tag them as needed, so it's clear if their use is allowed in your organization.
Explore the Cloud App Catalog to identify alternatives to risky cloud apps that are in use and consider instituting them across your organization.
Contact top risky users to understand needs and mitigate risks. You might want consider setting a new policy to monitor their future use.
Generate block script to block unsanctioned apps using your existing firewall or proxy.
Connect apps with Conditional Access App Control to protect data by applying access and session controls.
Connect apps with API connectors to gain visibility and control into your business apps.
How to generate Cloud Discovery executive report?
From the Cloud Discovery dashboard, select Actions in the upper-right corner of the dashboard, and then choose Generate Cloud Discovery executive report.
Optionally, change the report name.
Select Generate.
How frequently does the executive report need to be shared with the Leadership team?
Monthly review is recommended by the Leadership team to bring the corrective actions.

240550622.title: FAQ - Cloud Governance Framework
240550622.plain_text:
Q1. What is the Cloud Security Framework?
The Cloud Security framework is a set of forms and documents to identify, capture and evaluate SaaS and PaaS Solutions adopted by PG&E (either already existing or newly procured). The framework application result for a specific solution could be either an approval for usage ("sanctioned"), a deny ("unsanctioned") or a conditional approval ("monitored").
Q2. Why does PG&E need Cloud Security Framework?
PG&E needs a defined Cloud Security Framework for SaaS and PaaS solution to securely adopt and govern them in compliance with corporate policies and standards. The application of the framework will guarantee that.
A security evaluation is performed against the vendor and the solution.
End-to-end accountability is clearly identified.
Periodical reporting will be produced to bring the actual SaaS and PaaS security posture to different PG&E leadership and stakeholders.
Q3. How does the Cloud Security Framework work?
The framework details are described by the "PCOK Governance Process flow" document package.
Summarizing, it will capture new SaaS/PaaS solution scenarios as well as automatically discovered existing ones.
A course of action is then carried out by different PG&E teams (i.e. SIOC, Cybersecurity Risk Team, CSCOE, SRB, TSR, ITDR) in order to evaluate if all the PG&E security requirements have been met. In this evaluation flow, the solution requestor (i.e., business owner) could be involved for clarifications.
At the end of the process the solution will be approved (allowed), rejected (blocked) or conditionally approved (monitored) and the requestor informed.
Q4. Is the Cloud Security Framework tied to a PG&E standard?
Yes, one of the reasons of the framework existence is to ensure that PG&E multiple standards are properly enforced at the SaaS and PaaS services adoption and maintained during the whole lifecycle.
Q5. What is considered a cloud application?
PG&E considers a SaaS or PaaS solution the ones having these characteristics:
It is hosted by a 3
rd
party vendor, and it is reachable via the Internet.
PG&E has a valid contract with the vendor for the specific solution.
The solution contains PG&E data.
Q6. What does Sanctioned, Unsanctioned, or Monitored mean?
Sanctioned
: applications that has been evaluated through the PG&E SaaS/PaaS secure onboarding framework, having their APP-ID and flagged in CASB as "Sanctioned".
Vetted for security and compliance.
Integrated into the organization's identity and access management system.
Covered under formal contracts and SLAs.
Unsanctioned
: applications that are not approved and may pose a risk to PG&E, therefore should be blocked.
Evaluated by security team and rejected for use in PG&E.
Monitored:
security exception provided by Cybersecurity Risk team or Leadership team based on business requirement, not fully approved under monitoring.
Has security vetting.
Allowed for specific use-cases or departments on exception basis.
Not fully integrated into the PG&E's systems.
Q7. What is my role and responsibility?
As a Business owner/Application owner you ensure you onboard any of the Cloud applications as per the cloud
security governance process.
Q8. What to do if I'm asked to conduct a BIA, SRB, or TSR?
These activities are carried out by respective PG&E teams. Your role, if requested, is to engage the process with them (as well as following up in case of needs) following specific requirements each team will provide.
Q9. What should I do if I failed to meet the Cloud Security Framework? Would my cloud application gets rejected?
If the SaaS/PaaS solution you requested will not satisfy the security requirements, it could either be rejected and blocked, or an exception could be evaluated based on business reasons. In this case, a risk acceptance must be signed off. Likely this scenario must be considered as temporary (giving the requestor a grace period), requiring a list of actions to be performed to remediate the security non-compliances; during this grace period the SaaS/PaaS solution will be permitted.
Q10. How do I request support or get more information?
Please do refer to the SaaS and PaaS onboarding kit available in the below SharePoint site.
Q11. How Our business unit SaaS and PaaS onboarded applications will be detected and reported if we have not followed the Cloud Security Governance process?
As Part of the Cloud security governance process, we have Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps which will detect your business unit applications traffic and will be notified to Governance team.
The Cyber security Risk team will be contacting you to onboard the SaaS/PaaS Applications with the defined framework.
Q12. When will the Governance process trigger the SaaS/PaaS Application block process?
After the SRB team determine the noncompliance and if the onboarded application posing a security risk for the organization and customer data.
Q13. What is SaaS/PaaS onboarding evaluation process happening as part of Third-party security review?
A set of predefined controls evaluation will be happening part of the process to see the Applications were onboarded to the cloud environments as per PG&E Cloud security governance standards.
Q14. Where do I find the PG&E Cloud SaaS & PaaS Cloud security Governance framework kit?
Access the information from the below SharePoint site.

266339888.title: 5MM meeting
266339888.plain_text:
Topic: Cloud Governance Framework
Date: March 08, 2024
Audience:
Cloud System Owners and IT Support Team
The key thing you need to know
As more and more applications are now hosted in the public cloud, it is imperative on how we effectively and safely use the public cloud.  The cloud makes it easier for teams and individuals to deploy assets and provision infrastructure at the press of a button.  This is all being done without the oversight that traditional application deployments are required to comply.
Why it matters to you
Going to the cloud brings agility, but also more risks to the company.  Protecting the company from cybersecurity incident is of the utmost importance despite the benefits of going to the cloud.  This is why the Cloud Governance Framework is so important. It defined a set of rules and policies that dictates how we will operate services in the cloud.
Key discussion points
·       All cloud application must comply with the Public Cloud Onboard Kit (PCOK).
·       Discovered apps will be required to be onboarded through our governance framework process, which includes completing a Business Impact Analysis and acquiring an App-ID.  A Third-Party Security Review and the Software Review Board must be engaged for new vendors and software purchase.
·       A Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) must be used to integrate with SaaS and PaaS providers.
·       PG&E data must be encrypted in transit and at rest at all times within a SaaS solution regardless of the data classification.
Questions for understanding
For more information, please go the
SaaS Security 2.0 - Cloud COE - PGE Wiki
.  For additional questions about, please contact the Cybersecurity COE team (CCOE-Security).
Who do I contact with questions
Type
security
in your browser window for our Security intranet site. You can find information about all things security there. If you have questions about reporting, email
Security Communications
.

305075284.title: Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps (CASB)
305075284.plain_text:
Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps, formerly known as Microsoft Cloud App Security, is a Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) that enhances cloud application security by providing visibility into cloud app usage, protecting sensitive data, and offering advanced threat protection. It helps organizations manage compliance, secure OAuth-enabled apps, and enforce real-time controls to monitor and respond to anomalous behavior. This service leverages Microsoft’s extended detection and response (XDR) capabilities to safeguard cloud environments effectively.

Bastion Host Network Routing
The AWS Bastion Host network is Transit-Pub VPC which shares an overlapping block with Transit VPC that contains the Palo Alto Firewall/Route Appliances. This network overlap is intentional to ensure that traffic can not get to/from the Transit-Pub VPC without first transitioning the Transit VPC. All routing tables are defined to ensure this is done and if a peering connection is ever put in place, the routing tables would need to be intentional configured to allow direct routing.
The other intent for the Transit-Pub VPC is to allow for communication to the transit VPC from or to and network that needs fixed IP either public or private for verification.
Outside of the Transit VPC this is the only other VPC with an Internet Gateway that will allow host to have Public IP(s).
Devices Defined for the Bastion Host Network
Linux Bastion Host:
This is the primary use of this network and this Host is used for creating tunnels into AWS from public or private to support AWS Administration
DNS Cache/Proxy Server:
This is the frontend for the the external AWS DNS. It supports all the Internet based DNS traffic and pass it back to the actual DNS Server in the Management VPC.
Windows Bastion Host:
This host allows for Windows RDP and may only be accessible from within the PGE secure network due to the number of exploits that are associated with windows. It will be protected by the Transit PAN and AWS Security Groups.
TCP Bridge:
This is a device that allows TCP/UDP ports from AWS internal systems to be exposed to the internet via one-or-many public / private IP. Configuration Access to the TCP Bridge is via SSH on Port 22000.

51883644.title: Future Press Release for Transit VPC - July 2019
51883644.plain_text:
Risk reduction. Automation. Innovation.
Pick three and thank Transit VPC!
What Does Transit VPC Do?
The Transit team is focused on delivering a secure centralized core and edge for AWS.  Partnering with AWS, PAN and F5, they are delivering a pattern that meets PG&E network security requirements and availability requirements.  The team is bridging the connection for Azure and AWS.  They have replaced the Cisco ASA's with Palo Alto PA-5260’s which required the complete build of new environments.
The migration to PA 5260’s provides predictable performance, deep visibility, and control over all traffic, including encrypted traffic. With the new Palo Alto firewall appliances, there is advanced visibility and control of applications, users and content at very high throughput speeds. Dedicated processing resources assigned to networking, security, signature matching and management functions ensure consistent performance
What Has Transit VPC Accomplished?  LOTS!!
Migrated to Palo Alto Network PA-5260’s in Production
Prepared AzB firewalls for attachment
Provided  Baseline internet access for AWS Tenants
Attached  AzB side to PA-5260’s
Configured PA-5260
Tested PA-5260 configuration
Conducted  Disaster Recovery testing with PA-5260
Investigated VPC peering
Migrated  VPC’s to new environment
Attached   CCPA Production account to the new Transit Environment
Performed  Baseline connectivity to Internet resources
Connected   GISCOE Non-production account to the new Transit Environment
Connected AC  Inspection Production account to the new Transit Environment
For more information, visit
Transit VPC (Network Security)
Release Overview
JIRA Links

51883824.title: Testing Transit Connectivity
51883824.plain_text:
Testing Network Connectivity For Workload VPC Connected via Transit
Looking to document the baseline configuration for a new workload account connected via the transit environment.  Absent of the specific rule set, the following connectivity is assumed / inferred.
DNS (TCP/UDP 53) to 10.1.1.1 (and other discrete DNS servers?)
NTP (TCP 123) to ntp01 | ntp02.comp.pge.com
NTP is not included - we leverage native AWS NTP services
SMTP (TCP 25) to
mailhost.comp.pge.com
HTTP (TCP 80) to
www.google.com
and <allowed Intranet destination?>
Will create a simple script to execute these simple tests and give a 'thurmbs-up / thumbs-down' output.  Then make it a Lambda.  Or maybe JUST make it a Lambda.  Or a script that gets launched from a session manager instance.
Note: Per consultation with Billy Glenn, I used  the non-stackset version of the YAML scripts found here:
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-cfn-vpcendpoints
.  Once this was done, I ran and validated the commands shown belowl
Manual Confirmation
Prerequisite:  SessionManager found under Systems Manager access to an ec2 instance within the target VPC, in US-West-2
Run the following commands to test DNS name resolution, SMTP connectivity to on-premise mailhost, and Internet connectivity to
www.google.com:
dig
www.pge.com
10.1.1.1
nc mailhost.comp.pge.com 25
curl -I
www.google.com

58132754.title: Transit Autoscale F5 ADC - Runbook
58132754.plain_text:
The Autoscale F5's used for PSPS Address Search and other highly scalable apps are hosted in "PGE-IT Transit Prod" (account number
811103853713
).  In order to perform necessary work, SuperAdmin is required.
This page will be a walkthrough on how to manually scale up the transit F5's for a PSPS event or load test.  Begin by federating into the master account and assuming the SuperAdmin Role in account:
811103853713.  As of 4/1/2020 there are two stacks that can be scaled up. The two stacks are
F5TransitPRD530
and
F5Transit-QA-530-v2.
For this howto I will be scaling up the QA environment to max 8 nodes.  The cloudformation used as the base to deploy this is here:
https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-aws-cloudformation/tree/master/supported/autoscale/waf/via-lb/1nic/existing-stack/payg
we are currently running version 5.3.0
QA Stack:
F5Transit-QA-530-v2
ELB:
elbstack530qav2
Primary EC2:
Primary (QA v2) - BIG-IP Autoscale Instance: F5Transit-QA-530-v2  (
i-08e4d8e596ca3bb4e
)
DNS Name:
ec2-54-202-64-187.us-west-2.compute.amazonaws.com
Autoscale Group:
F5Transit-QA-530-v2-BigipAutoscaleGroup-1JWB0LQOWR93U
Scaling Up
Open a browser window to the primary node on port 8443
https://ec2-54-202-64-187.us-west-2.compute.amazonaws.com:8443/
. You should expect a cert warning.  This admin URL is only available from PG&E IP Space.
Login as "cscoeadmin"
As of my performing this scale-up there is only one active node. The system is Online/Standalone
Navigate back to the AWS Transit Account (make sure you are in US-West-2. Go to the Services > EC2 > Autoscaling Groups.  Select the F5Transit-QA-530-v2 group. Select Actions dropdown, and click Edit.
Change Desired Capacity, Min & Max capacity to 2 as shown:
Press Save. Navigate back over to EC2 and notice a new EC2 spinning up within a few moments.
You'll then want to return to the browser window where you had logged into the primary F5 node, and monitor it for sync with the new node.  This will take approximately 15-20 minutes to complete. When it completes in the F5 console you will see the status has changed to ONLINE (ACTIVE) In Sync.  If you click In Sync you'll see there are now two devices in the Device Groups: "autoscale-group" "device_trust_group" and "datasync-global-dg".
Before scaling up to a third node, return to your browser window in AWS, navigate to EC2 > Load Balancers.  Select elbstack530qav2.  Within about 15-20 minutes of the autoscale configuration change you will see the node count be 2 and both will be marked as healthy.  You can refresh this page  but generally this will take 15-20 minutes from the autoscale change.
Once both nodes show healthy, navigate back to Autoscaling Groups and repeat steps 4-7, changing Desired, Min, Max capacity to 3.  Repeat all these steps waiting 15-20 minutes and validating the health each time until you reach 8 nodes in sync and healthy.
Once complete you should show 8 healthy nodes as shown here:

121635186.title: Transit.Next Compensating Controls
121635186.plain_text:
New iteration from Transit v2 reduces reliance on Palo Alto Firewalls and F5 Web Application Firewalls in some cases. This page is to review Compensating Controls for accounts using Common Route Tables that are no longer flowing through PANs and F5s.
Compensating controls aim to reduce a blast radius for network exposure and/or lateral movement during active malware event or Advanced Persistent Threat inside our network.
Possible controls we should consider:
Aim to reduce lateral movement within our networks not protected by PANs and F5s
Reduce exposure of using RFC1918 /8 and /16 subnets
Implement automated Security Group management
AWS Firewall Manager and Use Cases
Implement host-based firewalls with Twistlock Agent
IAM resource restrictions
No interactive user access
Automated Pipeline deployments for resource creation and disposal
Immutable infrastructure with CI/CD deployments
Existing Controls
AWS Organization
:  Centralized management and governance of all AWS accounts
Service Control Policies
: Organization-wide controls regarding specific, high-risk activities
AWS Account Boundaries
:  Each workload runs in it's own centrally-managed AWS account
Identity and Access Management
(IAM):  Each AWS account has unique / discrete IAM roles and policies governing the actions of entities within the account
CloudTrail
:  All control-plane / administrative activities are logged in a centralized account, encrypted and validated for integrity.
Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)
:  Each workload has a dedicated VPC with default permissions of 'deny all' external access.  The
only
way to access a resource within a VPC is through an administrator or automation provisioning a Security Group which grants such access.
Security Groups
: Stateful firewalls which control access to the resource to which it is associated.  A single, 'default' security group is created at account provisioning, which allows
only
intra-VPC communications (denying all external access).
Network Access Control Lists
(NACLs):  Rules which can be applied at the subnet level.  Typically unused in our environment due to their stateless nature.
VPC Flow Logs
:
All
traffic, to/from every VPC, Subnet, and individual Resource (Network Interface Card) are captured, centralized, and stored.  This provides for after-the-fact forensics as well as the opportunity for real-time monitoring and detection.
Guard Duty
: AWS threat detection security service which continuously monitors and analyzes workloads to detect anomalous activity.  Enabled at the organization level and centralized in the Cybersecurity account.
Security Hub
: AWS security service which collects security data from across AWS accounts and services.  Enabled at the organization level and centralized in the Cybersecurity account.
Security Information Operations Center (SIOC) Monitoring:  Eyes-on-glass monitoring AWS events consolidated into QRadar.
Prisma Cloud:  Continuous monitoring of AWS environment, resources, and services.
Cloud Knox: Continuous monitoring of AWS IAM policies, and ?
Inter account communications via security groups with RFC 1918 implementation
Proposed Compensating Controls
The latest network design for Transit.Next project includes inspecting traffic for production accounts through a PAN:
Transit.Next Proposed Architecture and Designs - Cloud COE - PGE Wiki
There is no further action needed for compensating controls review as this satisfies Cyber requirement for production accounts. Future action and review may take place at a later date.

90120310.title: Transit.Next Requirements
90120310.plain_text:
Functional and NonFunctional Requirements for PG&E Transit Environment
These requirements directly influence and drive the proposed
Transit.Next Architecture and Design
.
Functional
The following functional requirements apply to the desired target state for PG&E's Transit Environment and Services
Functional Requirement
Acceptance Criteria
Comments
Transit Services are available in multiple regions (us-west-2 and us-east-1)
Additional discussion (and diagram) to discuss 'cross-region'
Provide high-bandwidth connectivity to and between workload accounts
Leverage Transit Gateway and Transit Gateway Attachments in lieu of VPN tunnels to attach workloads to transit environment.  This eliminates the 1.2Gb/s bandwidth limitation of the VPN tunnels.  Transit architecture should allow for scaling to the maximum bandwidth of transit GW, currently 50 Gb/s.
Provide centralized, scalable traffic inspection and enforcement of both North/South and East/West traffic.
Leverage Gateway Load Balancer and Security VPC to centralize and scale Traffic Inspection and maintain traffic flow 'symmetry'. Pending discussion regarding AWS native solutions.
AWS WAF (already in use) and AWS Network Firewall would likely change the design supporting this requirement
Provide Inspection and Enforcement between On-Prem and AWS Environment
Perhaps this is redundant of the "North/South" requirement, above.  I'm just thinking of the functionality of the Remote Access Firewalls at the PG&E Perimeter, vs. the PANs in Transit.  Will / NPS Team?
Layer7 inspections between VPCs
-VPC communication
-Network security strategy (Will or Pavani/Sathish from Cyber Security + COE)
-Tagging strategy
NonFunctional
The following Non-Functional Requirements apply to the desired target state for PG&E's Transit Environment and Services:
NonFunctional Requirement
Comments
Domain
Transit Services are a Tier 1 (Infrastructure Tier 0) service
Need to discuss / agree to RTO/RPO, "Active-Active", etc
Availability
Transit Services should provide transparent/no-touch recovery from anticipated failures
Unleash the Chaos Monkey in DEV and observe results, adjust and repeat
Reliability
Transit Services should have full telemetry reflecting health and status of all services
Includes integration to ENOC (OBM?)
Observability
Transit Services should be provisioned through automation
-migrate existing accounts (need to automate)
-migration of new accounts (need to automate)
-creation of new transit infrastructure (need to automate)
-Streamline Environment Provisioning, Scaling, Workload Connectivity
-Risk: capacity for automation activities
-need to determine timing/budget for account mgmt (validate which goes in phase1/2 etc)
Repeatability
Transit Services should have a Development environment
For validating upgrades / configuration changes / etc
Automate configuring PG&E resources?
Testability
All Transit Services Control Plane Activity Logged
Qradar, Cloudtrail
Auditability
Transit Services should be resilient to any single PG&E Data Center Failure
All functions should survive RCIOC / FXIOC outage
Availability
Transit Services should automatically scale in response to load / demand
Up to known boundaries / hard limits.  Need to document
Scalability
Transit V3 migration must be completed in less than 1 quarter
We cannot support 3 transit environments
scalability

Only supports Java files

330699730.title: F5 L7 DDOS Requirements
330699730.plain_text:
F5 Distributed Cloud (XC) offers robust solutions for DDoS protection, automation, and mitigation. Here are some key features:
DDoS Protection and Mitigation
1. Multi-Layered Protection:
Protects against both volumetric (Layer 3-4) and application-specific (Layer 7) attacks.
Uses a global scrubbing infrastructure to handle large-scale attacks
[1]
.
2. Real-Time Detection and Mitigation:
Detects and mitigates attacks in real-time to prevent them from impacting your network and applications
[1]
[2]
.
3. Cloud-Delivered Service:
Provides cloud-based DDoS mitigation, reducing the need for on-premises hardware
[1]
.
Offers both "Always Available" and "Always On" subscription models
[1]
.
4. Automation:
Reduces manual intervention with automated attack detection and response
[1]
.
Integrates with F5 Security Operations Center (SOC) for continuous monitoring and protection
[2]
.
5. Comprehensive Reporting:
Centralized console for visibility and reporting before, during, and after an attack
[1]
.
DDoS Automation
1. Automated Traffic Scrubbing:
Uses BGP-based redirection to scrub malicious traffic before it reaches your infrastructure
[1]
.
2. Integration with CI/CD Pipelines:
Supports modern application architectures and integrates with development tools for seamless protection
[2]
.
3. Behavioral Analysis:
Employs behavioral analysis to detect and mitigate sophisticated attacks
[2]
.
These features ensure that your applications remain secure and available, even during large-scale DDoS attacks. If you need more detailed information or specific configurations, feel free to ask!
References
[1]
Distributed Cloud DDoS Mitigation Service | F5
[2]
DDoS Attack Prevention - F5
There are 3 conditions that must be met for blocking to take place
Anomalies Observed
Anomalies are identified as latency increase, error codes, and connection drop
Anomalies have to be observed from at least
5 nodes (can be the same node 5 times)
over a rolling 10 second window
Obelix sends events to GC.
If anomaly criteria are satisfied, continue
RPS threshold crossed
Default value - 10k rps
Can be modified per tenant or LB (e.g. 1k)
Obelix tracks
per-node
RPS and sends to GC for aggregate RPS.
If total RPS across all Obelix exceeds defined rate, DDoS is confirmed
alert manager's alerts. labels.alertname: L7DDoS fired, slack notification created
continue to block actions
Blocking of
suspicious sources
evaluated as top talkers during event
identified by:
source IP (can be used as a singular identifier)
JA3 TLS Fingerprint (can be used as a singular identifier)
JA4 TLS Fingerprint (can be used as a singular identifier)
Country of origin
ASN
Path
Anomalies can be defined as
Origin errors
Origin error threshold is at 20% when below errors are from origin: "408", "409", "411", "413", "414", "425", "429", "5xx"
Generic errors
When the Envoy is returning more than 90% generic errors like 429, 502, 403 etc
Latency Anomaly
When there an increase in Origin Latency, the thresholds are reduced to 10% and 80% respectively

326965523.title: AWS IAM Identity Center Onboarding Wiki
326965523.plain_text:
This wiki explains how to onboard to AWS IAM Identity Center using this repository. The process involves creating an Active Directory group and submitting a pull request with your role definition.
Overview
The
aws-idc-policy-sets
repository manages AWS IAM Identity Center permission sets and account assignments through two distinct approaches:
Global Roles
: Organization-wide permissions based on Organizational Units (OUs)
Line of Business (LOB) Roles
: Specific permissions for individual business units based on account name patterns
Onboarding Process
Step 1: Create an Active Directory Group in Microsoft Entra ID (MEA)
Request a new AWS group in Microsoft Entra ID following the standard process:
Follow the AWS group naming convention:
AWS-APPid-Environment-Role
Example:
AWS-A2102-All-Audit
or
AWS-A2781-Test-Secbydesign_Ops
Include appropriate approvals for the access level you're requesting
Make note of the exact group name, as you'll need it for your role definition
Step 2: Clone the Repository
Step 3: Create a New Role Definition
For Line of Business (LOB) Roles (Most Common)
Create a feature branch:
Determine your LOB folder:
For production:
lob-roles/roles/<lob-name>/
Create a new YAML file based on the template:
Edit the YAML file with your role definition:
For Global Roles (Less Common)
Create a feature branch:
Determine your target folder:
For production:
global-roles/roles/
Create a new YAML file based on the template:
Edit the YAML file with your role definition:
Step 5: Submit a Pull Request
they should review the plan results in the PR.
Commit your changes:
Create a pull request on GitHub
Wait for approval from the repository maintainers
Once approved, your role will be deployed based on the repository's CI/CD pipeline
Key Considerations
Account Targeting
LOB Roles
: Target accounts using name patterns:
Global Roles
: Target accounts using OUs:
Environment Targeting for LOB Roles
Use the
environment
parameter in account assignments to target specific environments by matching patterns in account names:
Permission Levels
Follow the principle of least privilege when defining permission sets:
Dev/Test environments:
AdministratorAccess
: Full access with caution - acceptable for development/testing environments
PowerUserAccess
: Everything except IAM user/role management
ReadOnlyAccess
: View-only access for developers needing to inspect resources
QA/Prod environments:
For LOB roles:
Strict read-only access is required
(ViewOnlyAccess or ReadOnlyAccess)
ViewOnlyAccess
: Restricted read-only for monitoring
For specific operational needs: Use inline policies to extend read-only access with specific granular permissions
Example of extending read-only access with specific inline permissions:
Example of service-specific policy with strict restrictions:
Security Guardrails
The system supports optional security guardrails that can be enabled per permission set:
The guardrails provide baseline security by denying access to:
Dangerous services (Lightsail, GameLift, DeviceFarm, etc.)
Dangerous actions (deleting CloudTrail, creating internet gateways)
IAM user/group management
Troubleshooting Common Issues
If your role deployment is not working as expected:
AD Group Sync Issues:
Verify the AD group exists and the exact name matches your configuration
AD group changes can take up to 4 hours to synchronize with IAM Identity Center
Account Pattern Matching Problems:
Test your patterns with actual account names
For regex patterns, validate using a regex testing tool
Remember that global roles use "path" to match OU names, not the full OU path
For LOB roles, the environment parameter is matched against account names
Permission Set Conflicts:
Ensure no conflicting permission sets exist for the same principals in the same accounts
Check the IAM Identity Center console for assignment status
Getting Help
If you need assistance with role creation:
Review the examples in the
examples/
directory
Consult the README files in each directory for detailed documentation
Open an issue in the repository with your specific question
Contact the cloud security team:
Email: cloud-security@example.com
Slack: #aws-idc-support
Office hours: Every Tuesday at 2pm in Teams

342299143.title: AWS Identity Center - MEA Sync Issue
342299143.plain_text:
⚠️ Known Issue: AWS Provisioning Failure Due to Phone Attribute Sync
Overview
We have identified a new issue impacting AWS user provisioning through
MEA (My Electronic Access)
and
Identity Center (EntraID)
. The problem occurs when the
phone attribute
is synchronized for users who have multiple phone numbers. This causes the provisioning process to fail.
Who is Affected?
Users with more than one phone number in their profile.
Any new AWS user being provisioned via MEA and Identity Center.
Current Status
There is
no workaround
for this issue at this time.
The
SSO team is actively working on a permanent fix
.
To mitigate the problem, we will
remove the phone attribute from synchronization
, as it is not required for AWS access.
Action Plan
Attribute removal is in progress and will resolve the issue for all affected users.
No user action is required at this time.
Getting Help
For questions or updates, please reach out via:
Teams Channel:
Cloud Product - AWS Identity Center
Historical Note
The previous timing issue related to MEA nightly sync and AWS group assignment has been resolved. The workaround steps for that issue have been removed from this page.

350696535.title: AWS Identity Center SCIM Group Provisioning Automation
350696535.plain_text:
Overview
This automation tool automatically assigns security groups to Entra ID enterprise applications that use SCIM provisioning and SSO. Once a group is assigned to the enterprise app in Entra ID, Entra ID's built-in SCIM provisioning automatically syncs it to the target system (like AWS Identity Center, Terraform Cloud, Snowflake, etc.).
What this tool does:
Automates the assignment of groups to enterprise apps in Entra ID
What Entra ID does:
SCIM provisioning and SSO to the target system (fully automatic once assigned)
Repository:
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-identity-center-scim-sync
Current Use Case:
AWS Identity Center, but
easily adaptable
to any enterprise app using Entra ID SCIM provisioning (Azure DevOps, Terraform Cloud, Snowflake, etc.)
What Problem Does This Solve?
Manual Problem:
New security groups created in MEA (e.g.,
AWS-A1234-Prod-Developers
)
Entra ID Admin has to manually open Azure Portal and assign each group to the enterprise application
Until assigned, Entra ID doesn't know to provision the group via SCIM
Users can't access the application until manual assignment is done
Repetitive manual work
for every new group
Automated Solution:
Script detects new groups matching your prefix pattern (e.g.,
AWS-*
,
AZR-*
)
Automatically assigns them to the enterprise application in Entra ID
Entra ID's SCIM provisioning takes over from there
- automatically syncing to AWS Identity Center (or other target system)
Users get access immediately after group assignment
Zero manual work
in Azure Portal or target system
How Teams Can Use This
For IT Administrators (Full Access)
Run the script to automatically provision new groups:
./quick_start.sh
# Shows what needs to be added, asks for confirmation, then applies changes
For Team Members (Read-Only Access)
Even without provisioning permissions, you can see what groups need attention:
python3 automate_scim_sync.py --compare
# Shows the gap analysis without making any changes
This helps you:
See which groups need to be provisioned
Request help from someone with appropriate access
Understand the current sync status
For Future Automation
This foundation is
designed to be reusable
for any enterprise application in Entra ID that uses SCIM provisioning and SSO:
Azure Functions deployment
- Runs automatically on a schedule (recommended for Microsoft ecosystem)
AWS Lambda deployment
- Alternative for AWS-centric environments
Adapt for other apps
- Just change the enterprise app ID and group prefix (see "Extending to Other Applications" below)
Examples of other apps this works with:
Azure DevOps (use
AZR-*
prefix)
Databricks workspaces
Snowflake environments
Any SaaS app with Entra ID SCIM provisioning enabled
The Azure Functions deployment guide is already included in the repository (
AZURE_FUNCTIONS_DEPLOYMENT.md
).
Key Features
Automatic Group Discovery
- Finds all AWS-* security groups in Entra ID
Gap Analysis
- Compares Entra ID vs currently provisioned groups
Safe Add-Only Policy
- Never removes groups automatically (manual review required)
Ignore List
- Exclude specific groups from automation (shared team config)
Multi-Prefix Support
- Can sync AWS-, AZR-, or any prefix pattern
Azure Functions Ready
- Deploy for full hands-off automation
Complete Audit Trail
- All operations logged for compliance
Repository Structure
aws-identity-center-scim-sync/
├── quick_start.sh                 # Main entry point (interactive)
├── automate_scim_sync.py          # Core automation logic
├── .env.example                   # Configuration template
├── .scim_ignore                   # Team-shared ignore list
├── README.md                      # Full documentation
├── SETUP.md                       # Setup instructions
├── AZURE_FUNCTIONS_DEPLOYMENT.md  # Automation deployment guide
└── scripts/                       # Helper scripts
├── azure_cli_groups.py        # Query Entra ID groups
├── get_all_provisioned_groups.py  # Query SCIM groups
├── compare_groups_final.py    # Gap analysis
└── filter_aws_security_groups.py  # Filter security groups
Example Output
📊 Running analysis to check for differences...
📊 Entra ID Security Groups: 523
📊 Currently Provisioned:    524
✅ Groups in both systems:    522
➕ Missing from SCIM:         1
📊 Coverage:                  99.8% ✅
➕ Groups to add (1):
• AWS-A3612-All-XSOARBG
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
⚠️  Found 1 group(s) that need to be added to SCIM
Would you like to apply these changes? (yes/no): yes
🔄 Applying changes...
✅ Added: AWS-A3612-All-XSOARBG
✅ Changes applied successfully!
Safety Features
Add-Only Policy
The automation will
never
automatically remove groups
Groups that exist in SCIM but not in Entra ID are flagged for manual review only
Interactive Confirmation
When run manually, the tool always asks for confirmation before making any changes
You see exactly what will be added before it happens
Ignore List
Groups in
.scim_ignore
are excluded from comparison and won't trigger alerts
Perfect for distribution lists or intentionally out-of-sync groups
Troubleshooting
Authentication Errors
If you see "grant has expired" errors, this happens after password changes or after 90 days.
Solution:
az logout
az login
Permission Errors
Verify you have:
Group.Read.All
- Read Entra ID groups
Application.ReadWrite.All
- Manage enterprise app assignments
Script Errors
Check the logs in the
logs/
directory for detailed error messages.
Prerequisites
Azure CLI
- Installed and authenticated (
az login
)
Python 3
- Python 3.x installed on your system
Entra ID Permissions
- Read groups (Group.Read.All)
Application Permissions
- Manage enterprise app assignments (Application.ReadWrite.All)
Configuration
Required Settings (.env file)
ENTERPRISE_APP_ID=<your-aws-identity-center-app-id>
APP_ROLE_ID=<your-app-role-id>
GROUP_PREFIXES=AWS-
Finding Your IDs
Enterprise App ID:
az ad sp list --filter "startswith(displayName,'AWS')" \
--query "[].{displayName:displayName, id:id}" -o table
Copy the
id
(Object ID) value to your .env file
App Role ID:
az ad sp show --id <ENTERPRISE_APP_ID> \
--query "appRoles[].{displayName:displayName, id:id}" -o table
# Look for the "User" role
Copy the role
id
to your .env file
See
SETUP.md
in repository for detailed step-by-step instructions.
Ignore List (Team Shared)
The
.scim_ignore
file is
committed to the repository
and shared across the team.
Add groups that should NOT be automatically provisioned:
# Distribution groups / Non-security groups
AWS-Data_Labelers
# Break glass accounts / Special access groups
AWS-Prod-SOMAH_BreakGlass
When you add a group to this file and commit,
everyone on the team benefits
- no need for each person to configure their own ignore list.
How It Works
The automation runs in 4 steps:
Query Entra ID for all AWS-* security groups
Query SCIM to get currently provisioned groups
Compare the two lists to find gaps
Add missing groups to SCIM provisioning
Extending to Other Applications
This tool is
designed to be reusable
for any enterprise application in Entra ID that uses SCIM provisioning.
How Entra ID SCIM Works:
You assign a security group to an enterprise application in Entra ID
Entra ID automatically provisions that group to the target system via SCIM
This tool automates step #1 - the assignment to the enterprise app
To adapt for another application (e.g., Databricks, Azure DevOps, Snowflake):
Update
ENTERPRISE_APP_ID
to your target application's Object ID
Update
GROUP_PREFIXES
to match your naming pattern (e.g.,
AZR-
,
GCP-
,
DBX-
)
Update
.scim_ignore
with application-specific exclusions
Run the same automation
Real-world examples:
AWS Identity Center
-
AWS-*
groups (current implementation)
Azure DevOps
-
AZR-*
groups
Databricks
-
DBX-*
groups
Snowflake
-
SNW-*
groups
Deployment options:
Azure Functions
- Recommended for Microsoft ecosystem (deployment guide included)
AWS Lambda
- Alternative for AWS-centric environments
Manual runs
- On-demand execution when needed
Support and Contribution
Repository:
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-identity-center-scim-sync
Documentation:
README.md
- Complete user guide
SETUP.md
- First-time setup
AZURE_FUNCTIONS_DEPLOYMENT.md
- Automation deployment
Issues or Questions:
Create an issue in the GitHub repository
Contact the Identity & Access Management team
Production Status
This tool is currently in production use for AWS Identity Center SCIM provisioning. It has successfully provisioned
500+ groups
and maintains
99.8% synchronization coverage
.
Last Updated: October 2025
Maintained by: CSCOE

79566104.title: Ansible vs SCCM
79566104.plain_text:
Background
:
Ansible was selected as a tool to perform auto remediation of STMP findings.  The overall goal would be to automate finding resolution where applicable to simplify the effort required from the IT Admins and the Tool SMEs to maintain the health and coverage of the various security tools.  The work began as a proof of concept where Ansible would use STMP data to target remediation of QRadar agent findings.
The tools teams already have the capability of working with the SCCM team to target remediation efforts for STMP findings, but due to workload and resource constraints this process is not performed in any normal fashion.  Instead, tools teams resolve systemic issues on the server and admin side and rely on the IT Admins to install the tool on the asset and to maintain the health of the asset itself.  STMP cases are assigned to the IT Admins and they are expected to resolve the issues as appropriate.
Options
:
Use SCCM to fix issues with Windows assets – QRadar WinCollect Agent (as an example) could be installed by SCCM or can kick off repair scripts as necessary.
Pros –
Software is already packaged
SCCM already is configured on assets
Logic could be created in SCCM to target any assets that are missing WinCollect agent
Process already exists for packaging/deploying software/CRQs
SCCM can be used to remediate other STMP finding types with manual work from the tools teams
Cons –
Tool teams would be responsible for reviewing STMP reports regularly and would provide list of assets to SCCM team to target for remediation
SCCM currently has no way to pull finding data from STMP, targeting remediation efforts based on finding definition would be manual process by tool teams
Tool Admins would need to pull reports from STMP and work with SCCM admins to perform cleanup.
Logic targeting assets missing WinCollect agent would need maintenance to ensure new asset types are targeted as new assets are onboarded into STMP
Logic would need to be developed to automate finding remediation
either on the SCCM side (pull findings from STMP) or
STMP end (STMP tool would push commands to tell SCCM which package to deploy to the specific target assets)
Only supports Windows assets
Use Ansible to automate finding fixes
Pros –
Ansible can be used to automate fixes on any OS type, only one “tool” to use for remediation solutions
Ansible can use existing SCCM packages to deploy software
Ansible can automate remediations outside of the SCCM client (uses WinRM)
Works on assets where SCCM client is broken, could possibly fix SCCM client issues
Ansible can pull data via the STMP API, which allows for automation of remediation depending on finding type
Cons –
Not “plug and play” – we are designing a solution as tool is not currently used for this function at PG&E (Balaji supporting currently)
Still in build process, will not have a complete working POC until EOY
Solution must be thoroughly tested before releasing to production
No process exists for automating finding remediation via Ansible, multiple business constraints must be identified and resolved
Determine when Ansible is allowed to perform remediation actions (after business hours, etc)
Determine which assets are allowed to be remediated automatically (UDN non-nerc may be free reign, ODN would need to be clearly defined)
Determine what level of approval is needed to perform remediation actions (Ansible can execute whenever vs submit CRQ and get approval)
No process exists for determining “who” will own Ansible and the various aspects of auto remediation
Cloud Automation Team
Maintain Ansible server
Work with SMEs to design and update remediation scripts
Work with CMR team to design remediation scripts for new finding types
CMR Team
Work with tool SMEs to identify findings to remediate based on STMP trend data
work with Ansible team to provide trigger for auto remediation (hostname list, finding type, etc)
Tool SME
Work with CMR and Ansible teams as necessary to support remediation actions

350698843.title: PG&E AWS Identity Center Access Guide
350698843.plain_text:
Overview
This guide provides step-by-step instructions for accessing AWS resources through PG&E's AWS Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO). The process is straightforward and involves requesting the appropriate access group through MEA (My Electronic Access) and then using the Identity Center portal.
Prerequisites
Valid PG&E employee credentials
Access to MEA (My Electronic Access) system
Knowledge of your team's designated AWS access group
Step 1: Request Access Through MEA
Identify the correct access group
: Work with your team to determine the specific AWS access group that has been set up for your team's needs
Submit MEA request
: Request the identified AWS access group through the My Electronic Access (MEA) system
Wait for approval
: Once approved, you'll be granted access to the appropriate AWS resources
Step 2: Access AWS Identity Center
Navigate to the Identity Center
: Go to
https://pge.awsapps.com/start
Login
: Use your PG&E credentials to authenticate
Step 3: Select Your AWS Account
Once logged in, you'll see the AWS access portal with:
Accounts tab
: Displays all AWS accounts you have access to (typically your team's specific accounts)
Each account shows:
Account name and ID
Associated email address
Available access roles (varies by team and access requirements)
Step 4: Access AWS Resources
Choose your account
: Click on the AWS account you want to access
Select access method
: You have several options for accessing AWS resources:
Access Keys Option
Click "Access keys" next to your desired role
This provides you with temporary AWS credentials
Direct Console Access
Click directly on the role name to access the AWS Management Console with that role's permissions
Available Access Roles
Based on your team's setup, you may see roles such as:
ReadOnly
: View-only access to AWS resources and configurations
[TeamName]ReadOnlyAccess
: Team-specific read-only permissions
PowerUser
: Elevated permissions for development and deployment tasks
AdminAccess
: Full administrative access to team resources
BreakGlass
: Emergency access for critical situations (restricted use)
Note
: Most teams have access to their individual accounts with role-based permissions tailored to their specific needs and responsibilities.
Getting Credentials for Programmatic Access
If you need to use AWS CLI or SDKs, click on "Access keys" for your role to get:
AWS Access Key ID
AWS Secret Access Key
AWS Session Token
The portal provides multiple options for using these credentials:
Environment variables
: Export commands for your terminal
AWS credentials file
: Text to add to your ~/.aws/credentials file
Individual values
: Copy specific credential values for use in your applications
Troubleshooting
Can't see expected accounts
: Verify your MEA access group request was approved
No access roles available
: Contact your team lead to confirm the correct access group
Authentication issues
: Ensure you're using valid PG&E credentials
Important Notes
AWS Identity Center credentials are temporary and will expire
Always use least-privilege access principles - only request the minimum access needed
Contact your team's AWS administrator if you need additional permissions or have questions about access groups
Support
For technical support or questions about AWS Identity Center access:
Primary Support Channel
:
Cloud Product - AWS Identity Center Teams Channel
Use this Teams channel for:
AWS Identity Center access issues
Questions about access groups and permissions
Technical troubleshooting
General AWS Identity Center suppor
