51056301.title: Register a Relying Party Application in PingOne
51056301.plain_text:
Log in to the PingOne admin console
https://admin.pingone.com
.Your login credentials will be unique to PingOne, using your PGE lanid email as the username (e.g. abcd@pge.com).
Click the "Applications" tab, followed by the "OIDC" tab, followed by the "Add Application" button
Add details for the application name, description, business purpose, etc. Step 2 involves selection of "Authorization Code" or "Implicit" for the OAuth2 grant type. If you are unsure of your needs in this regard, please refer to the
Ping OAuth2 Developer Guide
. Step 3 involves registration of the "Start SSO URL" and the "Redirect URIs" - the Start SSO URL can be thought of as your login page. If your application has a /login endpoint which will trigger the use of the angular-oauth2-oidc login process, place that full URL here. Otherwise, you could use "
https://localhost
:{portnumber}" for now. The redirect URIs are very important to register correctly. This is the page to which users will be redirected after successful authentication. If you do not have a /callback endpoint established, then register the home page of your application (i.e.
https://localhost
:{portnumber}). When you deploy your application to other environments beyond your local dev server, you will need to register the redirect URI's of those environments in this PingOne configuration. Continue selecting the options that you would like for your application, but note that default settings will allow successful creation of the PingOne client.
When you have saved the configuration settings, the application will show up in your list of OIDC applications and you will be able to see configuration details. You will need to configure your application with some of these details, including the Client ID, Client Secret (if applicable), Issuer, and possibly other values.
So long as your callback endpoint is running and receiving traffic, you can test that your configuration works successfully by clicking the "Authentication Flow" tab and visiting the "Initiate Single-Sign On URL" - this will take you to the PGE login form hosted by Ping, and upon successful authentication you will be redirected to your newly registered application. Note that if you have an active session with Ping (i.e. if you've logged in to a federated application with your lanid and password) you may not notice the redirect, and you will just see the rendering of your application's callback endpoint.

Troubleshooting
Some users see a 'User does not exist' error after logging into Ariba via SSO
This is a known issue on Ariba. Ariba accounts are created with uppercase usernames and if the SAML token contains lowercase characters, then a matching user is not found during SSO. In order to fix this, the attribute contract in the PingFederate/PingOne bridge was changed to pass all user IDs as uppercase

50875954.title: SSO for Sumo Logic
50875954.plain_text:
Link to initiate SSO to Sumo Logic for PGE users with the SMARTworksDevelopers AD Role:
https://sso.connect.pingidentity.com/sso/sp/initsso?saasid=c95a0423-a5f2-4aa6-87a6-d359edffcda0&idpid=a6405f3b-818d-4474-b224-f91beda1fca9
This page is a step-by-step guide for setup of Sumo Logic with PingOne to enable SSO to other applications in the PGE ecosystem which are registered with PingOne (i.e. GitHub, Slack, AWS, etc).
Topics covered:
Steps for SSO Integration, Role-based Access Provisioning, SP-Initiated Login, and Provisioning New Users in Sumo Logic
Steps for Simple SSO Integration with Sumo Logic
Troubleshooting
Note: Only One of the SSO Integration Types can be set up for a given Service Provider (specifically, per unique Entity ID). If you choose one procedure below for setup, you will need to delete it entirely in order to proceed with the other setup option.
Steps for SSO Integration, Role-based Access Provisioning, SP-Initiated Login, Provisioning New Users in Sumo Logic
In order to set up Sumo Logic with the ability to provision new users and to have users automatically assume specific roles in SumoLogic upon SSO login, you must set up Sumo Logic as a SAML integration
without using PingOne's pre-configured settings for Sumo Logic
. If you don't need the ability to provision new users and to have users automatically assume specific roles in Sumo Logic, you can skip to the easier setup procedure
below, linked here.
Log in to the
PingOne admin console
with your PGE email (not LanID) and password. You must be a PingOne admin in order to set up a new PingOne SAML integration with Sumo Logic.
Navigate to the "My Applications" tab, and the "SAML" sub-tab. Click the button for "Add Application" and click "New SAML Application"
Give the application a name, description, category, and icon graphic if desired, then click "Continue to Next Step"
You should be on page with the headline "2. Application Configuration" (see image below)
Download the PingOne Account Origination Certificate. Now head to Sumo Logic and configure a new SAML integration from the Sumo Logic end. The
official Sumo Logic documentation for SAML integrations can be found here.
After you have added the new IdP SAML integration in Sumo Logic, select the new configuration from the Sumo Logic Configurations list. You will see a url called the "Assertion Consumer" - this will be entered in the PingOne configuration. Back in the PingOne admin console, you should still be on Step 1 - SSO Instructions. Click "Continue to Next Step" - this should bring you to Step 2 - Configure Your Connection. Paste the "Assertion Consumer" url received from Sumo Logic in the "ACS" field. For the "Entity ID" field, use
https://service.us2.sumologic.com/ui/
. Click "Continue to Next Step"
You should be on step "3. SSO Attribute Mapping" in PingOne. Create four attribute maps according to the values in the screenshot below:
Application Attribute
Identity Bridge Attribute
SAML_SUBJECT
email
GIVEN_NAME
fname
LAST_NAME
lname
SUMO_ROLE
* requires "Advanced" mapping
* SUMO_ROLE is intended to send relevant Active Directory user roles to Sumo Logic so that users can be provisioned into accurate user groups at Sumo Logic. The current role being mapped is the "SMARTworksDevelopers" role. To extract this role from authorized users, click the "Advanced" button in the SUMO_ROLE attribute row. In the "IdP Attribute Name or Literal Value", use the "memberOf" attribute. In the drop-down Function selector, choose "ExtractByRegularExpression" and enter .*(SMARTworksDevelopers).* in the expression field. See the screenshot below for reference
Save the settings and click "Save and Publish" to complete the SSO Integration setup between Sumo Logic and PingOne. Once the configuration is saved, PingOne will provide a link to use for SSO to Sumo Logic. Click this link and use the LanId and password of an authorized SMARTworksDevelopers user to access Sumo Logic. If the SSO is unsuccessful, please see the
Troubleshooting
section below.
Steps for SSO Integration with Sumo Logic
Log in to the
PingOne admin console
with your PGE email and password. You must be a PingOne admin in order to set up a new PingOne connection with Sumo Logic.
Navigate to the "My Applications" tab, and the "SAML" sub-tab. Click the button for "Add Application" and click "Search Application Catalog"
Search for "Sumo Logic" and select the "Sumo Logic" search result, then click the "Setup" button
Download the PingOne Account Origination Certificate and make note of the issuer parameter shown on this screen. Provide these to your Sumo Logic admin for integration configuration on the Sumo Logic side of the integration.
Refer to the
Sumo Logic SAML Integration documentation
for reference about where to find the integration settings for using SAML with a new IdP. Complete the steps through Step 9. In Step 8, select "Use SAML Subject". Do not configure additional optional steps beyond Step 9.
After you have added the new IdP SAML integration in Sumo Logic, select the new configuration from the Sumo Logic Configurations list. You will see a url called the "Assertion Consumer" - this will be entered in the PingOne configuration. Back in the PingOne admin console, you should still be on Step 1 - SSO Instructions (see image in step 4). Click "Continue to Next Step" - this should bring you to Step 2 - Configure Your Connection. Paste the "Assertion Consumer" url received from Sumo Logic in the "ACS" field. Paste "
https://service.us2.sumologic.com/ui/
" in the "Entity ID" field. Click "Continue to Next Step"
In the Step 3 - Attribute Mapping section, use "email" in the field form input for "Identity Bridge Attribute or Literal Value. Click "Continue to Next Step"
Click "Save & Publish". The integration is complete. Now, copy the "Initiate SSO URL" and paste it in a new browser window
The new URL will either prompt you for a login at a Ping Federate - hosted HTML form (
https://itiampingdev....)
or it will initiate SSO if you already have a valid session open with a different PingOne-registered service provider
Troubleshooting
If you receive an error page response from Sumo Logic that looks like the one below, it means that your user is not registered as a Sumo Logic user. Users must be registered in Sumo Logic with their PGE email, as this is the attribute that is mapped from PingOne to Sumo Logic for user login. In the case of the error below, the user
csco@pge.com
successfully logged in via the Ping Federate login screen to initiate SSO, but that email was not associated with a user in Sumo Logic.

99421660.title: PAN Firewall Sizing Considerations
99421660.plain_text:
Transit.Next PAN Firewall Sizing Considerations
The Transit.Next design pattern Introduces new AWS services including Transit Gateway and Gateway Load Balancer, greatly simplifying and streamlining the architecture and traffic flows.  It also collapses the three discrete "pairs" of PAN firewalls currently deployed in TransitV2 (PROD, CORE, and INT) into a single, combined, load-balanced fleet spread across 3 Availability Zones.
TransitV2 Resources And Utilization
TransitV2 is comprised of the following PAN Firewall Instances (NPS to confirm / validate function, sizing, historic performance metrics).  Note that while there is a 'pair' for each of these functions, only a single firewall is active and passing traffic at any given time.
CORE:  These firewalls terminate the VPN tunnels from On-Prem (Remote Access Firewalls) and then route to the PROD firewalls

The proposed design fully utilizes all deployed assets - so that we're not licensing for 200% of our target capacity (Active / Standby).  It also means that failure of an appliance results in a 30% reduction in capacity, instead of total loss of functionality.

334368574.title: AWS IAM Identity Center - Troubleshooting
334368574.plain_text:
This is mainly for CCOE and Cyber as most of the steps involve looking in Identity Center
Terraform Plan Errors
Invalid Index - The given key does not identify an element in this collection value
Solution
Ensure the account assignment permission set is spelled correctly. Notice that the permission set in the account assignment is missing an "s" after Operation.
Error: reading IdentityStore Group - ResourceNotFoundException: GROUP not found
Solution
Ensure the MEA Group is spelled correctly and the case matches. If it does, check Identity Center if the group is in there.
Log into the Cyber Prod account,
553067951317
Go to Identity Center → Groups
Search for the MEA Group
You will need to type in the full name or at least the beginning of it
Ensure the MEA Group shows up in Identity Center
If the groups do not show up, check back the next day
It takes about 1 day to show up
You will need to close your original pull request and create a new one to trigger another Terraform plan
Unable to see Ops role in Access Portal
Check that the person has the MEA Group
Go to
https://sailpointui.utility.pge.com/
Go to Manage Access → Manage User Access
Search for the user and select it
Click on Remove Access
I'm sure there's better ways to check if someone is part of an MEA Group, but this is the way I do it
Verify the person has the AWS MEA Group
If the MEA Group does not show up, make sure they received an email that their MEA request was approved
It could take a few hours for the MEA Group to show up after approval
If the MEA Group is there, follow
Check in Identity Center that the person is a user in the associated MEA Group
Check in Identity Center that the person is a user in the associated MEA Group
Log into the Cyber Prod account,
553067951317
Go to Identity Center → Groups
Search for the MEA Group
You will need to type in the full name or at least the beginning of it
Click on the Group Name
Check that the user is listed
If the user is not listed, you may need to wait up to 24 hours from the time your MEA Request was approved for it to show in Identity Center
If it's been more than 24 hours, please contact Cyber. See
You most likely do not have the MEA Group
Apr-A2102-All-AWS-IDC-SCIM-Users
Check in Identity Center that the Account has the MEA Group listed under Users and Groups
This sometimes occurs if the MEA Group isn't fully provisioned before creating the group in Identity Center
Log into the Cyber Prod account,
553067951317
Go to Identity Center → Multi-account permissions
Search for the account
Click on the Account Name
Verify the MEA Group shows under Users and groups
If there's a Group not found uner the Username / group name, re-run the Terraform workspace

364978956.title: Alert Response by Type
364978956.plain_text:
Quick reference for SIOC M&R — what to do for each major category of Wiz alert.
Credential Misuse
What it looks like:
"EC2 Instance Credentials Used Outside Of AWS"
"Lambda Function Credentials Used Outside Of AWS"
"ECS Task Credentials Used Outside Of AWS"
AttackSequence:IAM/CompromisedCredentials
Why it matters:
Cloud credentials should only be used from within the cloud environment they belong to. If EC2 instance creds show up in an API call from an external IP, someone likely stole them.
What to do:
Check the principal and source IP in the alert
Is this a known CI/CD system or approved external tool? → Contact owner to confirm
If not →
Escalate to IR immediately.
This is likely credential theft.
Root / Privileged Identity Activity
What it looks like:
"Failed AWS console login by root user"
"Password recovery requested by root user"
"Root Activity From Unusual Country"
"Root Federation Token Generation"
Why it matters:
Root is the god-mode account. It should almost never be used. Any root activity is suspicious by default.
What to do:
Was this pre-approved? Check for change records or prior notification.
Contact the account owner immediately.
If no one claims it →
Escalate to IR.
If it's from an unusual country →
Escalate to IR.
Malicious IP / Tor Activity
What it looks like:
"AWS API Call Was Made From A Very Malicious IP Address"
"Access from a Tor exit node"
"SSH Communication With Malicious IP"
Any GuardDuty alert with "TorIPCaller" or "MaliciousIPCaller"
Why it matters:
Wiz cross-references source IPs against threat intelligence feeds. Hits on known-bad IPs are high confidence.
What to do:
Check the alert for the source IP and the action taken
Was this a successful action or just an attempt?
If successful →
Escalate to IR.
If attempt only → Document, monitor for follow-up activity
Public Exposure
What it looks like:
"S3 object configured as publicly exposed via ACL"
"RDS DB snapshot configured as publicly exposed"
"EBS snapshot was configured to be public"
"OpenSearch domain endpoint configured as publicly exposed"
"Storage Account Made Publicly Accessible"
Why it matters:
Making a cloud resource public that contains data is an immediate data exposure risk.
What to do:
Check: does this resource contain sensitive data? (Wiz often tells you in the description)
Contact the owner — was this intentional?
If it contains sensitive data and wasn't approved →
Escalate to IR
If it doesn't contain sensitive data but still wasn't approved → Close with a note, recommend the owner fix it
Crypto Mining
What it looks like:
"CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B"
"CryptoCurrency:Runtime/BitcoinTool.B"
"Impact:Runtime/CryptoMinerExecuted"
"Digital currency mining related behavior detected"
"CloudFormation Stack created for aws-ethereum-miner"
Why it matters:
Crypto mining in your cloud = someone compromised your environment and is spending your money.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
This is always malicious.
The underlying resource is compromised and needs to be isolated.
Known Threat Actors
What it looks like:
"Call to CreateUser API with parameters associated with threat actor JavaGhost"
"Call to CreateUser API with parameters associated with threat actor EMERALDWHALE"
"Call to CreateUser API with parameters associated with threat actor Legion"
Any alert mentioning a named threat actor
Why it matters:
Wiz's threat intelligence team tracks specific attack campaigns. If your environment matches their signatures, it's not a guess — it's a confirmed pattern.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
Named threat actor matches are high confidence.
Include the threat actor name in your escalation — IR may have additional context.
Privilege Escalation
What it looks like:
"Unusual Attempt By Principal To Edit Their IAM Policy"
"IAM Policy Attachment Bruteforce Attempt"
"PrivilegeEscalation:Runtime/CGroupsReleaseAgentModified"
"PrivilegeEscalation:Runtime/RuncContainerEscape"
"Microsoft Entra ID (AAD) principal escalated itself to Global Administrator"
Why it matters:
An identity trying to give itself more permissions is a classic attack pattern.
What to do:
Check who/what is doing the escalation
Is this an admin performing approved work? → Contact to confirm
If it's a service account or unknown identity →
Escalate to IR
Container escapes (CGroups, Runc) →
Always escalate
Data Exfiltration / Destruction
What it looks like:
"Exfiltration:S3/AnomalousBehavior"
"Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Delete"
"Unusual DB Export Task To External Bucket Started"
"Multiple Storage Accounts With Sensitive Data Deleted Within A Short Timeframe"
Why it matters:
Data leaving the environment or being destroyed at scale is one of the worst outcomes.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
Check scope — is it one bucket/account or multiple?
Do not wait for owner confirmation if the behavior is clearly anomalous.
Kubernetes / Container Threats
What it looks like:
"Pentesting Pod Created in Cluster"
"Command Executed in kube-system Pod"
"Anonymous workload was created"
"Known Offensive Tool Namespace Used"
Anything with "EKS", "AKS", or "Kubernetes" in the name
Why it matters:
Kubernetes clusters run production workloads. Suspicious activity in a cluster can mean an attacker has a foothold.
What to do:
Check: which cluster? Prod or Dev?
Pentesting tools, offensive tool namespaces, or kube-system access →
Escalate to IR
Anonymous access granted →
Escalate to IR
If unsure about Kubernetes context, loop in Cloud Security for help
Supply Chain / Wiz Threat Intelligence
What it looks like:
"Wiz-Identified Compromised Secret Usage"
"Shai-Hulud leaked secrets used in the last day"
"Publicly exposed AWS access keys found on public Github repository"
Wiz IR emergency email notifications
Why it matters:
These are Wiz's own threat intelligence team telling you something is actively compromised.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
This is not theoretical — it's active.
Include all details from the Wiz alert.
For Wiz email notifications: engage SIOC leads + Cloud Security + IR on-call simultaneously.
Azure Identity (Entra ID) Threats
What it looks like:
"Impossible travel: successful Microsoft Entra ID user login from multiple countries"
"Successful Signin Using a User Agent Associated With Credential Stuffing Attacks"
"Admin consent given to a risky third-party application"
"Microsoft Entra ID principal escalated itself to Global Administrator"
"User Authenticated To Malicious Application"
Why it matters:
Entra ID is the identity backbone for Azure. Compromised identities here can affect everything.
What to do:
Impossible travel / credential stuffing → Contact the user. Were they traveling? Did they log in from both locations?
Admin consent to risky apps / Global Admin escalation →
Escalate to IR
Malicious application authentication →
Escalate to IR

364978958.title: Incident Response with Wiz
364978958.plain_text:
When Does IR Get Involved
IR gets involved when SIOC M&R escalates a Wiz alert. The escalation criteria are simple:
Situation
Action
Activity is clearly malicious
Escalate immediately
Account/app owner cannot be reached
Escalate
Owner says "that wasn't us"
Escalate
Critical severity + can't quickly confirm benign
Escalate
Wiz IR team sends an emergency notification
Escalate immediately
When in doubt, escalate.
A false escalation costs a conversation. A missed incident costs a lot more.
What Wiz Gives IR
When IR receives an escalation, all of the evidence is already packaged in the Wiz alert. No one needs to go pull raw CloudTrail logs or stitch together a timeline manually.
Already Available in Every Alert
Evidence
Where in Wiz
What happened
Alert description + AI analysis
Who did it
Principal / identity section — IAM user, role, service account, IP address
What was affected
Resource details — account, region, resource type, ARN
When
Event timeline — full chronological list of cloud events with timestamps
Attack chain
Investigation graph — visual diagram of the full activity sequence
MITRE mapping
MITRE ATT&CK tactic and technique (e.g., Execution > Command and Scripting Interpreter)
Cloud account context
Subscription tags — AppID, Environment (Prod/Dev), LOB, owner
AI assessment
Blue Agent / Mika analysis — malicious vs. benign, confidence, narrative
Related events
All correlated cloud events across the timeline
How To Export
JSON Export
— Three-dot menu (top right) > Export as JSON. This is the full machine-readable record.
Print View
— Printer icon (top right) > generates a clean printable summary.
Direct Link
— Copy the alert URL and include it in the IR ticket. Anyone with Wiz access can see the full investigation.
IR Workflow for Wiz Alerts
Phase 1: Triage (SIOC M&R — already done before escalation)
Alert reviewed
Context gathered (account, owner, environment)
Intent checked with owner (or owner unreachable)
Evidence exported (JSON)
Decision to escalate documented in Wiz comment
Phase 2: Investigation (IR)
Using the Wiz alert as the starting point:
Review the investigation graph
— understand the full attack chain
Check the event timeline
— look for events before and after the triggering alert
Assess scope
— is this isolated to one account or is the same principal active elsewhere?
Check for related alerts
— search Wiz for the same principal, IP, or resource across other alerts
Determine blast radius
— what data/systems were accessible from the compromised identity?
Wiz can answer most of these questions without leaving the platform:
Threats
— filter by principal or account to see related alerts
Cloud Events
— full event log searchable by identity, action, resource
Security Graph
— visualize what the compromised identity has access to
Phase 3: Containment
Depending on the threat type:
Threat
Containment Action
Who
Compromised IAM credentials
Disable access keys, rotate credentials
App Owner (directed by IR)
Compromised EC2/Lambda credentials
Rotate instance role, investigate instance
App Owner + Cloud Security
Unauthorized root login
Reset root password, enable MFA, check for persistence
Cloud Security + App Owner
Public exposure (S3, RDS, etc.)
Remove public access immediately
App Owner (directed by IR)
Crypto miner / malware
Isolate instance, preserve forensics
IR + Cloud Security
Tor/malicious IP access
Block IP, investigate identity
IR + Cloud Security
Phase 4: Recovery & Documentation
Confirm threat is contained
Document the full timeline (Wiz JSON export + IR notes)
Identify root cause
Recommend preventive measures
Close the Wiz alert with resolution notes
Wiz Emergency Notifications
Wiz's own IR team occasionally sends
direct email notifications
when they detect an active threat campaign targeting our environment. This has happened before and was accurate.
Item
Details
What
Email from Wiz IR about an active threat targeting our tenant
Frequency
Rare — maybe once or twice a year
Accuracy
High — previous notifications were confirmed and actionable
Distribution List
(Cloud Security to provide)
Action
Treat as Critical. Immediately engage SIOC M&R, Cloud Security, and IR.
Action Required:
Cloud Security must provide the emergency email DL.
Wiz IR Readiness
Wiz tracks our
Incident Response Readiness
based on log source coverage. The more logs we feed Wiz, the better our ability to detect and investigate.
Where to find it:
Wiz >
Threat Center
>
Response Readiness
This readiness score accounts for:
Are cloud management logs flowing? (CloudTrail, Activity Logs, Audit Logs)
Are threat detection sources connected? (GuardDuty, Defender for Cloud)
Are identity logs ingested? (Entra ID, Okta)
Are network flow logs enabled? (VPC Flow Logs)
Are data access logs enabled? (S3 Data Events, Storage Account logs)
Is the Wiz Runtime Sensor deployed?
Is the EDR integration active? (CrowdStrike)
Gaps in coverage = blind spots in detection. This is a metric we should track and report.
Key Contacts
Role
Responsibility
Contact
SIOC M&R (on shift)
Alert triage, initial investigation, escalation
(standard SIOC contact)
Cloud Security
Wiz platform, rule configuration, cloud expertise
(TBD)
IR On-Call
Incident containment and response
(standard IR contact)
Wiz Emergency
Active campaign notifications
(email DL — Cloud Security to provide)

364977556.title: Wiz Monitoring & Response Runbook
364977556.plain_text:
For SIOC M&R Engineers
Audience
SIOC Monitoring & Response Engineers
Scope
All Wiz Critical and High severity threat alerts
Last Updated
February 26, 2026
Owner
Cloud Security Team
Contents:
What Is This
What You Monitor
What a Wiz Alert Looks Like
What To Do When You Get an Alert
How To Collect Evidence
When To Escalate
RACI
Common Questions
1. What Is This
Wiz is a cloud security platform used by major enterprises and government agencies worldwide. It monitors our AWS and Azure cloud environments and fires alerts when it detects threats — things like credential theft, privilege escalation, malicious IP activity, crypto mining, and more.
Wiz's threat detection rules are written by a
professional security research team
. When Wiz rates something as Critical or High, it means their researchers have determined it represents a real security risk. These are not guesses — they are backed by threat intelligence, MITRE ATT&CK mappings, and correlation across cloud events.
Your job:
When Wiz fires a Critical or High alert, you investigate it and decide whether to close it or escalate it.
That's it. Wiz does the hard part. You do the response part.
2. What SIOC Monitors — Exactly
Every Wiz alert rated Critical or High. All of them. No subset, no filtering, no exceptions.
This is not negotiable. Critical and High are the only two severity levels that reach SIOC. Wiz has five severity levels — SIOC owns two of them:
Wiz Severity
Who Owns It
What SIOC Does
Critical
SIOC
Investigate immediately. Active exploitation, exposed credentials, confirmed attack sequences.
High
SIOC
Investigate promptly. Malicious IP activity, privilege abuse, suspicious behavior, public exposure.
Medium
Cloud Security
Nothing. Not yours.
Low
Cloud Security
Nothing. Not yours.
Informational
Cloud Security
Nothing. Not yours.
There is no "Major" severity in Wiz. Only the five levels above.
What "investigate" means — specifically
For every Critical or High alert, you do three things:
Read the alert
in Wiz (description, AI analysis, investigation graph)
Decide
: close it (with documented reason) or escalate to IR
Document
your decision in the alert and your ticket
That's it. You are not building detections. You are not tuning rules. You are not analyzing raw cloud logs. Wiz already did the detection, correlation, and analysis. You are reading the result and making a triage decision.
How many alerts are we talking about?
Right now:
46 open threats
at Critical or High across our entire environment. This is not a flood.
Wiz has
367 detection rules
at Critical and High severity (232 AWS, 135 Azure). Not every rule fires every day — these are the rules that
can
fire. The actual alert volume is low.
The full rule list is in the
Threat Detection Rules Reference
.
What SIOC does NOT need to do
Not Your Job
Who Does It
Write or tune detection rules
Cloud Security / Wiz
Query CloudTrail or raw cloud logs
Wiz does this automatically
Decide which rules to enable
Cloud Security / Wiz
Monitor Medium, Low, or Informational alerts
Cloud Security
Perform containment actions
Incident Response
Fix the underlying issue
App / Account Owner
3. What a Wiz Alert Looks Like
Wiz gives you
everything you need
right in the alert. You do not need to go dig through CloudTrail logs or hunt through multiple consoles. Here's what you'll see:
The Alert Summary
Every alert shows you at a glance:
Field
What It Tells You
Threat name
What happened (e.g., "CreateAssociation API execution targeting all instances")
Severity
Critical or High
Status
Open, In Progress, or Resolved
Description
Plain-English explanation of what this rule detects and why it matters
Principal
Who did it — the IAM user, role, or service account
Resource
What was affected
Subscription
Which AWS account or Azure subscription
Event Origin
Where the event came from (e.g., AWS CloudTrail Events)
MITRE ATT&CK
What attack tactic this maps to (e.g., Execution, Persistence, Exfiltration)
Context Tags
Automated enrichment — things like "Automation User Agent", "Unseen Principal Type Rule"
Created At / Last Updated
When it happened
The AI Analysis (Blue Agent / Mika)
Wiz includes an
AI-powered analysis
right in the alert. It will tell you:
Whether it thinks the activity is malicious or benign
A confidence level
A plain-English
story
of what happened, step by step
The
potential impact
if it were malicious
In many cases, the AI will straight-up tell you:
"This threat was classified as Not Malicious"
with an explanation like
"This represents legitimate AWS Systems Manager automation for inventory collection."
You can use this to help your decision, but always verify.
The Investigation Graph
Wiz shows a
visual graph
of the activity chain:
Who initiated the action (IP, service, user)
What identity was used (IAM role, service account)
What action was taken
What resource was affected
The full timeline of events (with timestamps)
This is your investigation — Wiz already did it for you. You're reviewing it, not building it from scratch.
The Event Timeline
Below the graph, Wiz shows every related cloud event in chronological order:
AttachRolePolicy
CreateRole
CreateAssociation (the triggering event)
PutBucketEncryption
Each event shows
who
did it,
what
they did, and
when
— pulled directly from cloud logs.
4. What To Do When You Get an Alert
Step 1: Read the alert
Open the Wiz alert. Read the description. Look at:
Severity
— Critical or High?
Subscription
— which account? Prod or Dev?
Principal
— who or what did this?
AI Analysis
— what does Wiz think?
Step 2: Make a call
What You See
What You Do
AI says "Not Malicious" + activity is from a known service account / automation
Close it.
Document why (e.g., "Legitimate SSM automation per Wiz AI analysis").
Activity looks expected but you're not 100% sure
Contact the account/app owner.
Ask if this was planned. Their contact info is in the subscription tags (AppID, LOB) or CMDB.
Owner confirms it was expected
Close it.
Document the owner confirmation.
Owner doesn't respond or says it wasn't them
Escalate to IR.
Activity is clearly suspicious (malicious IP, Tor exit node, crypto miner, exfiltration)
Escalate to IR immediately.
Don't wait for owner response.
Step 3: Document your decision
Change the alert status in Wiz and add a comment explaining your decision. That's your audit trail.
5. How To Collect Evidence
All of the evidence is already in the Wiz alert.
You do not need to go to the AWS console, pull CloudTrail logs, or build a timeline manually. Wiz already did this.
To export the full alert details:
Open the alert in Wiz
Click the
three-dot menu
(top right corner of the alert)
Select
Export as JSON
Save this JSON to your ticket / system of record
The JSON export contains
everything
: the alert details, the triggering rule, MITRE mappings, the affected subscription, the principal, cloud account info, timestamps, and all related events.
Here's what a real export looks like (abbreviated):
{
"id": "823ce26a-dad9-5fe6-8b8f-a4416cc62c23",
"type": "THREAT_DETECTION",
"severity": "HIGH",
"status": "OPEN",
"description": "This rule detects a CreateAssociation execution targeting all instances...",
"sourceRule": {
"name": "CreateAssociation API execution targeting all instances",
"ruleSeverity": "HIGH",
"origins": ["AWS_CLOUDTRAIL"],
"securitySubCategories": [
{ "title": "Command and Scripting Interpreter", "category": "Execution" }
]
},
"cloudAccounts": [
{ "name": "PGE-AcademyReporting-Dev", "externalId": "171870468977", "cloudProvider": "AWS" }
],
"entitySnapshot": {
"name": "PGE-AcademyReporting-Dev",
"cloudPlatform": "AWS",
"tags": { "AppID": "APP-3006", "Environment": "Dev", "LOB": "IT" }
}
}
That's it.
Drop the JSON into your ticket. Done.
Screenshots
If your process requires screenshots, Wiz also has a
print
button (printer icon, top right) that generates a clean printable view of the alert.
6. When To Escalate
Escalate to Incident Response when
any
of these are true:
The activity is clearly malicious (Wiz AI agrees, or the rule name makes it obvious)
The account/app owner cannot be reached
The owner says "that wasn't us"
The alert involves production systems and you can't confirm it's expected
The alert is
Critical
severity and you can't quickly confirm it's benign
When in doubt, escalate. It is always better to escalate something that turns out to be benign than to close something that turns out to be an incident.
7. RACI
Activity
SIOC (M&R)
Cloud Security
App Owner
IR
Monitor and triage alerts
A
I
-
-
Investigate and validate
R
I
C
-
Escalate when needed
R
I
I
A
Contain and respond to incidents
I
C
R
A
Fix the issue
-
C
A
I
Maintain Wiz rules & config
-
A
-
-
R
= does the work /
A
= owns it /
C
= consulted /
I
= informed
In plain English:
You own the alerts. Cloud Security keeps the platform running and is kept in the loop. App owners answer your questions and fix things. IR handles confirmed incidents.
8. Common Questions
Q: Do I need cloud expertise to do this?
No. Wiz translates cloud events into plain-English alerts with AI-powered analysis. You read the alert, check if the activity was expected, and make a call. If something is over your head, that's what Cloud Security is here for — reach out.
Q: What if the AI says it's "Not Malicious" — can I just close it?
Use the AI as a strong signal, but apply your own judgment. If the AI says "Not Malicious" and the principal is a known automation account in a Dev environment, close it with confidence. If the AI says "Not Malicious" but the activity looks weird to you, dig a little more or ask Cloud Security.
Q: What if I don't understand a specific alert type?
Ask Cloud Security. That's literally what they're here for. Over time you'll learn the common patterns. Most alerts fall into a handful of categories (root login, credential misuse, public exposure, suspicious API calls).
Q: Didn't we used to use GuardDuty for this?
Yes. Wiz ingests GuardDuty findings AND adds its own detection rules on top. It also correlates across multiple sources (CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, Entra ID, etc.) and adds AI analysis. It's a strict upgrade. You should be using Wiz, not looking at raw GuardDuty.
Q: Where do I find Wiz?
Navigate to
Threats
in the left sidebar of Wiz. Filter by
Severity equals Critical or High
and
Status equals Open
. That's your queue.
Q: How do I export evidence?
Three-dot menu (top right of any alert) > Export as JSON. Drop it in your ticket.

364978963.title: Wiz for SIOC Engineers
364978963.plain_text:
What Is Wiz
Wiz is a cloud security platform. It connects to our AWS and Azure environments and monitors everything — who's logging in, what API calls are being made, what's exposed to the internet, what's communicating with known malicious IPs, and a lot more.
When Wiz sees something bad, it creates an alert. That alert lands in your queue.
Where To Find Wiz
Log in to Wiz at your organization's Wiz URL
Click
Threats
in the left sidebar
Filter:
Severity = Critical, High
and
Status = Open
That's your queue.
What You'll See
Every alert has:
Section
What It Shows
Summary bar
Severity, status, rule name, timestamps
Description
Plain-English explanation of what happened and why it matters
AI Analysis
Wiz's AI (Blue Agent / Mika) tells you if it thinks this is malicious or benign, with a confidence level and a story
Investigation Graph
Visual diagram: who did what, to which resource, in what order
Event Timeline
Every related cloud event, in order, with full details
Context
Cloud account, subscription tags (AppID, LOB, Environment), resource details
Wiz Severity Levels
Severity
What It Means
Your Job
Critical
Active exploitation, exposed creds, confirmed attack sequences
Investigate immediately
High
Malicious IP, privilege abuse, suspicious behavior, public exposure
Investigate promptly
Medium
Moderate risk, may be expected
Not your job
Low
Low risk
Not your job
Informational
Baseline telemetry
Not your job
How Wiz Compares to GuardDuty
If you've used GuardDuty before:
GuardDuty
Wiz
Scope
AWS only
AWS + Azure + GCP + OCI + Kubernetes
Detection
AWS-managed rules
Wiz security research team rules + ingests GuardDuty
Context
Alert with basic details
Alert with AI analysis, investigation graph, identity context, MITRE mapping
Evidence
You have to go dig in CloudTrail
Everything is right in the alert
Cross-cloud
No
Yes — correlates across cloud providers
Wiz ingests GuardDuty findings AND adds its own rules on top. It's a strict upgrade.
Your First Alert — What To Do
Open the alert
— read the description
Check the AI analysis
— does Wiz think it's malicious?
Look at the account
— Prod or Dev? Check the tags (Environment, AppID)
Make a call:
Clearly benign / expected automation → Close it, add a comment explaining why
Not sure → Contact the app/account owner (check tags for AppID)
Suspicious or owner says "wasn't us" → Escalate to IR
Export evidence
— three-dot menu (top right) → Export as JSON → attach to your ticket

81953452.plain_text:
Environment
Preferred Silverline WAF FQDN/URL
Backend AWS API FQDN/URL
Blacklist, Whitelist, Custom requests
Charge Code
Sample
Dev, Test, QA or Prod
appsyncdev.cloudapi.pge.com
7o7tgl7qwvcwdhh4c2iobw7ziy.appsync-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com
Allowed /graphql URI for file upload
Timesheet labor billing
1
Dev
search.
dev.cloudapi.pge.com
tr4bttdx5zbyvldpl4fbicucsm.appsync-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com
2
QA
search.qa
.cloudapi.pge.com
TBD
3
Prod
search.
cloudapi.pge.com
TBD
4
Notes:
1) DNS request (*.cloudapi.pge.com domain) will be processed by John Stanley
2)
Allow F5 Silverline IP Space
(
107.162.0.0/21
and
107.162.48.0/20
)
unrestricted through any firewall or router
. Ideally, you want to
only
allow the Silverline subnets, exclusively.  This will ensure that only Silverline-routed traffic can reach your network, and thus prevent attackers from being able to circumnavigate the Silverline infrastructure.
.
3) iRule host-header rewrite provided by Silverline:
when HTTP_REQUEST priority 350 {
set agl [call ag_log0::open -rulename "host-rewrite-mvieq_nonprod" -rulever 1]
if {![class exists [call ag_info0::datatable_name host-rewrite-mvieq-nonprod]]} {
call ag_log0::http_kvp $agl WARN action "blocking due to missing data-table 'host-rewrite' see ESC-10161" ticket ESC-10161 datatable [call ag_info0::datatable_name host-rewrite-mvieq-nonprod]
HTTP::respond 502 content "Bad Gateway" noserver Connection close
TCP::close
return
}
if {[set dest [class match -value -- [string tolower [HTTP::host]] equals [call ag_info0::datatable_name host-rewrite-mvieq-nonprod]]] ne ""} {
HTTP::header replace Host $dest
set sni_value [getfield $dest ":" 1]
}
}
when SERVERSSL_CLIENTHELLO_SEND priority 350 {
SSL::extensions insert [binary format SSScSa* 0 [expr { [set sni_length [string length $sni_value]] + 5 }] [expr { $sni_length + 3 }] 0 $sni_length $sni_value]
}

Purchasing additional license(s) base on FQDN/URL of the application is now done through AWS Market Place Private Offer so billing goes to the AWS account and charged to the business owner accordingly.  Refer to the following steps to get started:
Have the project to get pre-approval with this approximate cost:
$205.55/mo or $1644.43/yr co-term with existing contract for each FQDN/URL.
Email to F5 (Nicole Powers <n.powers@
f5.com
>; Akira Miyazono <
A.Miyazono@F5.com
>) for a quote with the number FQDN/URL(s).  It's usually 3-4 licenses for each application (Dev, Test/optional, QA and Prod).  It's important to mention that the license is co-term with existing PG&E Silverline WAF contract; otherwise, the cost will be very high.

74450910.title: AWS S3 Bucket is not Blocking Public Access - cf-templates
74450910.plain_text:
Overview
Issue
When deploying CloudFormation stacks in an account and you upload a template file, AWS will automatically create an S3 bucket named cf-templates-[unique id]-[region] if the bucket does not already exist. The default settings for this bucket is set to be public.
Resolved
Criteria
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
Automatic Remediation
A scheduled job named s3-block-public-access-cf-templates-schedule will run at 5:00 pm every day to update the public settings for the cf-templates buckets to block all public access.
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
None
Useful Links

74450916.title: AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy - cf-templates
74450916.plain_text:
Overview
Manual Remediation
IaC remediation via CF
Automated programatic remediation
Issue
When deploying CloudFormation stacks in an account and you upload a template file, AWS will automatically create an S3 bucket named cf-templates-[unique id]-[region] if the bucket does not already exist. The default settings for this bucket does not require to use secure transport.
Manual Remediation
How To Remediate the Bucket Policy JSON
Ensure that you have the below JSON block added to your Bucket Policy to
Deny
access to your buckets when
SecureTransport
is not being used. This policy can be updated directly from the S3 web console or CLI but we suggest that you use infrastructure as code (TF, CFT) as a best practice.
CF Stack Remediation
How to Remediate the Bucket Policy Via CloudFormation Template
The example YAML CF template block shows how to create and attach a bucket policy which has a
Deny
for traffic that is not using
SecureTranport
for a bucket with the resource name rExampleBucket.
Resolved
Criteria
Automated programatic remediation
Overview
:
Cloud COE has developed an automated programmatic remediation of 'AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy' through the use of a Lambda function that is scheduled to run daily. This Lambda function will check all S3 buckets in the account which were not created by ClouFormation or Terraform and update the security policy to attach a bucket policy which has a
Deny
for traffic that is not using
SecureTranport.
Note:
Buckets created by CloudFormation or Terraform will be skipped by this Lambda function, and should be remediated through changes in the stack. This Function will not over write existing bucket policies instead checks if the secure transport is denied.
Git Repo CF template
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-s3-security-remediation/blob/master/s3_secure_transport.yaml
Steps:
1) Download or store the CF template locally
If using git you can clone the file/repo using the following command
git clone
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-s3-security-remediation.git
example shown below
2) Deploy CF Template
Log in the AWS account with sufficient permissions (admin or above to be able to deploy resources) and navigate to AWS cloud formation service in the search bar
In cloud formation head to stacks and create stack new resources
Navigate to the named Lambda function and configure the test event
The lambda function has a time out of 3 minutes
inputs for the test event are shown below
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
None

212304382.title: Bentley SAML SSO - 26 Jun 2023
212304382.plain_text:
Summary
Bentley application reported SAML SSO issues after
itiampingrc.cloud.pge.com
was removed from the
itiamping.cloud.pge.com
GTM, due to Rancho private cloud/network issues.  Issues are with a thick client on Citrix network only.  App did not fail on VPN.
Temporary solution:
F5 team (Chiraag Patel) re-enabled the itiampingrc address in the itiamping GTM pool at 4:21 PM.  This appears to have resolved the issue.
Long-term fix:
Not sure - why is Citrix seems to be dependent on itiampingrc being part of the itiamping GTM pool.
Root Cause:
Timeline (06/26/2023):
Errors began around 1 PM.  Initial errors may have been due to intermittent problems with Rancho private cloud.  Errors after 1:41 PM were due to itiampingrc being "disabled" in the itiamping pool.  The VIP itself was never disabled.
I got called into bridge at 4 PM.  Enabled itiampingrc in itiamping pool at 4:21 PM.  No reports after 4:21 PM.
Helpful Splunk search terms:
index=ping "connectionid=
https://ims.bentley.com
"
Does indicate WSFED as the protocol, not sure if that is related?
Details
Bentley thick client failed on Citrix when itiampingrc was taken out of the
itiamping.cloud.pge.com
GTM pool.

219483093.title: PF OUD Patching August 2023
219483093.plain_text:
Background
Recent OUD crashes have Oracle Support recommending we apply the latest patch bundle to PingFederate OUD Session Store.  Currently, it is version 11.1.2.3.0 11.1.2.3.0 (Build 20150414142803Z).  However, currently, the opatch utility used to apply patches to OUD is throwing error 73 and doesn't work, as it think OUD is still installed in /opt.  So, Oracle Support provided steps to fix this discrepancy and opatch.  Below are the instructions to fix opatch and to apply the latest patch bundle to OUD.
Also included are the steps to change OUD to use JDK 1.7 (aka JDK 7).
OPatch Fix
Edit /opt/oud/oraInventory/ContentsXML/inventory.xml
DEV:
Replace the line:
<HOME NAME="OH371002176" LOC="/opt/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
with:
<HOME NAME="OH371002176" LOC="/
app
/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
PROD:
Replaces the lines:
<HOME NAME="OH152105084" LOC="/opt/oud/Middleware/OAUTH" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
<HOME NAME="OH1175260011" LOC="/opt/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="2"/>
with:
<HOME NAME="OH1175260011" LOC="/app/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
Restart OUD.
OPatch Validation
Run opatch lsinventory to confirm it fixed the issue.
Apply Latest Patch Bundle
Download p31541461_111230_Generic.zip to /home/pingfed/src.
Unzip to a temporary location (such as /logs/oud_latest_bundle).
export ORACLE_HOME=app/oud/Middleware/OUD .
export PATH=$PATH:/app/oud/Middleware/OUD/OPatch:/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin
cd to temporary location/31541461
Run:
stop-ds
opatch apply
start-ds
Change OUD Java
Install Oracle JDK 1.7 (aka JDK 7)
Download p32464070_170301_Linux-x86-64.zip to /home/pingfed/src.
mkdir /app/oracle
cd /app/src
unzip ~/src/p32464070_170301_Linux-x86-64.zip
cd /app/oracle
tar zxvf /app/src/jdk-7u301-linux-x64.tar.gz
mv jdk1.7.0_301 java
ln -s java jdk1.7.0_301
Update OUD Java
export PATH=/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin:$PATH
cp -p app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/config/java.properties ~/archive/java.properties.[DATE, e.g 08-02-23]
cd /app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin
./stop-ds
vi /app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/config/java.properties
Edit default.java-home and change value to /app/oracle/java/jre
Edit overwrite-env-java-home and change value to true
Save
./dsjavaproperties
./start-ds
Confirm the version of Java that OUD is now running with ps -ef |grep OUD.

190942562.plain_text:
Summary
One of the OUD instances (pritappgenlc027) had a corrupted database, possible caused by removal of files during clean-up activities.  This caused invalid_grant errors in PingFederate and for some OAuth clients.
Temporary solution:

213714823.title: PingFederate OUD Issues - 6 and 7 Jul 2023
213714823.plain_text:
Summary
MRAD apps, SOMAH and Agency Pledge reported issues with PingFederate.  Checked the PF logs and saw some errors related to OUD on pritappgenlc023.  Noticed email alerts from SiteScope indicating OUD was unavailable at the same time as the errors.  Checked OUD error logs, which were empty.  Checked access logs and saw a gap in activity during the same time period.  No root cause at this time.
Temporary solution:
None.  No changes were made.  Issue cleared after 1 hour.
Current Status:  No issues have been reported since issue cleared.
Long-term fix:  TBD
Root Cause:  TBD
Timeline (07/06/2023):
Errors began at 4:51 PM per Angus Tsoi (MRAD).
Errors stopped at 5:45 PM per Angus Tsoi (MRAD).
OUD gap in access log activity from 16:53:43 to 17:45:47.
From 16:51:21 to 16:53:43, no actual searches happen, just CONNECT and DISCONNECT entries.
CONNECT/DISCONNECT coming from PingFederate Engines/Admin, and F5s.
During this same period, I see entries in the PF Admin request logs implying that the Admin instance and server were still receiving traffic, just issues with OUD and/or port 1389.
Timeline (07/07/2023):
5:13 PM - noticed email alert sent by SiteScope at 5:11 PM
Checked logs and found similar behavior to day before (CONNECT/DISCONNECTS and then no activity).
5:15 PM - stopped OUD on 023 with stop-ds as pingfed user
5:16 PM - started OUD on 023 with start-ds as pingfed user
Activity immediately appeared in the OUD access logs.  Directory started successfully in error logs.  Messages during shutdown about DB corruption, but that may be "normal" during shutdown.
Helpful Splunk search terms (SPLUNK doesn't seem to have all the entries, local logs saved off to /logs/pf_engine/tmp):
index=ping status=failure source="/opt/pingfederate/engine/current/pingfederate/log/splunk-audit.log" description="description=\"Access Grant Management Exception\""
"Unable to establish a connection to server
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
"
Follow-up Items
ELG6 created Outlook rule to FW'd SiteScope OUD pritappgenlc023 failure errors to ENOC-SHIFT until better alerting can be set up (right now, just an email to Ping Operations, seemingly).
Need to work with ENOC Monitoring team to set up better alerts - either integrate with PagerDuty or get alerts on ENOC Dashboard for callouts.
Need to get F5 VIP in front of OUD in PROD, as PingFederate doesn't appear to failover to 027 in this situation.  May need to test whether it does better with F5 VIP in lower environments.
Open Oracle Support case to get a root cause or to determine how to get a root cause in the future.
Look into OUD and Java version to see if upgrade(s) might resolve this.  Check App Health version and timeline for replacing PingFederate OUD.

190942565.title: PingFederate OUD Issues 16 Mar 2023
190942565.plain_text:
Summary
One of the PROD OUD instances (pritappgenlc027) experienced some issues around 4:10 PM.  This caused invalid_grant errors in PingFederate and for some OAuth clients.  Mostly Mobile apps appear to be impacted.  It should be only those using Persistent Grants or Access Grants.
Temporary solution:
Removed pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com:1389 from the "
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389 (cn=Directory Manager)" Data Store Hostnames field in PingFederate Admin console.  Originally just stopped OUD on 027, but the errors persisted, along with some additional reported slowness.
Current Status:
PingFederate is currently only configured to connect to pritappgenlc023.  OUD is running on pritappgenlc027, but PingFederate doesn't have it listed in the Data Store Hostname list.  However, the access logs on pritappgenlc027 do show some activity.
Bridge call team decided they wanted to leave pritappgenlc027 out of the mix for now, to see if we can determine a root cause (unlikely, since there are no errors in the OUD logs).  They want to bring it back into the mix tomorrow (Friday 3/17) at 4 PM PST).  They will need a Ping Resource to make the change and monitor if there are any errors in PingFederate or OUD after the change.  If the issue occurs again, we may need to remove 027 from PingFederate Data Store again.  They may want us to create a CRQ for the Ping change.  If so, you can use the PIngFederate M&O Activity Standard CRQ.
Instructions:
Login to PROD PingFederate Admin console
Navigate to System → External Systems → Data Stores
Click on third Data Store in the list named "
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389 (cn=Directory Manager)"
Click on LDAP Configuration
Click on Edit to the right of
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389 in the Hostnames section
Add "
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389" to the end of the value (final valued should be "
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389"
Click on Save
Replicate Changes
If there are issues later reported for the mobile apps and there are indications of issues again, remove
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389 from the list again - follow the above instructions, just change step 6 to remove "
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389" from the end of the line.  Final value would be just
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389.
Long-term fix:  TBD
Root Cause:  TBD
Timeline (03/16/2023):
Errors began at 4:08 PM per app teams.
First email report at 4:25 PM.  PagerDuty incident notified ELG6 at 4:41 PM.
Joined bridge at 4:45 PM.
OUD stopped on pritappgenlc027 at 5:08 PM.
pritappgenlc027 removed from PingFederate Admin Data Store Hostname list around 5:12.
Apps reported working by 5:15 PM.
OUD restarted on pritappgenlc027 at 5:14 PM, but left out of PF Data Store list.
Helpful Splunk search terms:
invalid_grant
"Unable to establish a connection to server
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
"
Details
Noted the following errors in PingFederate logs, but it was not repeating continuously and thus made it harder to find:
PingFederate Error (/logs/pf_engine/server.log)
2023-03-16 16:12:09,273  WARN  [org.eclipse.jetty.server.HttpChannel] /idp/userinfo.openid
java.io
.IOException: LDAPException(resultCode=91 (connect error), errorMessage='Unable to establish a connection to server
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com/10.30.4.182:1389
within the configured timeout of 6000 milliseconds.', ldapSDKVersion=4.0.13, revision=f2bf4773e5efb286552d3fa7288eebcdfb31e086)
2023-03-16 16:12:09,274  ERROR [org.sourceid.servlet.ErrorServlet] Top level error (ref#4221-4742): javax.servlet.ServletException: javax.servlet.ServletException: com.pingidentity.sdk.accessgrant.exception.AccessGrantManagementException: javax.naming.NamingException: [LDAP: error code 91 - An error occurred while attempting to connect to server
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389:  IOException(LDAPException(resultCode=91 (connect error), errorMessage='Unable to establish a connection to server
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com/10.30.4.182:1389
within the configured timeout of 6000 milliseconds.', ldapSDKVersion=4.0.13, revision=f2bf4773e5efb286552d3fa7288eebcdfb31e086))] [Root exception is LDAPException(resultCode=91 (connect error), errorMessage='An error occurred while attempting to connect to server
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389:  IOException(LDAPException(resultCode=91 (connect error), errorMessage='Unable to establish a connection to server
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com/10.30.4.182:1389
within the configured timeout of 6000 milliseconds.', ldapSDKVersion=4.0.13, revision=f2bf4773e5efb286552d3fa7288eebcdfb31e086))')]
...
Network connectivity issues
Tried using telnet to connect to pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com on port 1389 from PF Engines in both data centers.  Engines in both data centers had slow connections (it would hang for several seconds).  Speedy responses from 023.  This is why I shut down 027 and removed it from the config.  Network team did not see any issues during this time.  Cloud team did not see any issues during this time.
OUD Error (/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/logs/errors and access)
There were no errors in the OUD logs on pritappgenlc027  at the time of the issue.  Access logs started writing at 4:10 PM.  Previous error had been from 8:27 PM the night before and was unrelated.
Other Helpful Notes
Not sure why 027 even had activity, but it had activity in the day or so leading up to this.  Maybe due to OUD-related work performed the previous evening?
Follow Up
Recommend we follow up with Ping to better understand the load-balancing algorithm on the data stores.  We keep seeing unexpected activity in "secondary" server (027).
Consider putting OUD behind a load-balancer, so we have better control over where PingFederate is connecting for OUD.  Consider seeing if we can keep connections within each data center?
Bridge Link
https://teams.microsoft.com/l/meetup-join/19%3ameeting_NjAwMjBlODctMzkxOS00NjVhLTg4NTEtMjE0NWFlYWVlYzZl%40thread.v2/0?context=%7b%22Tid%22%3a%2244ae661a-ece6-41aa-bc96-7c2c85a08941%22%2c%22Oid%22%3a%22770adf41-0671-4a13-aa9c-3dd7d0816057%22%7d

206800343.title: PingFederate Patching Validation Errors 01 June 2023
206800343.plain_text:
Summary
After automated patching of PROD FXIOC PingFederate Engines, ELG6 was performing validation and noticed that Splunk was showing an unusual error starting at 6:25 PM.  Initially suspected it was an issue with OUD, as the timing was similar and we have been having OUD issues recently.  However, it appeared to be issues related to a specific IP address, 10.30.4.183 -
pritappgenlc028.comp.pge.com
.  So, to fix things as quickly as possible, I did a quick stop and start of PingFederate on pritappgenlc028.  This seemed to fix the errors.  Below are the various details I found in the log files that might be a clue as to the root cause.
Long-term fix:
Restart PingFederate on pritappgenlc028 resolved the issue.
Root Cause:
PingFederate did not start properly on pritappgenlc028 after RHEL patching reboot.  Root cause of restart problem unknown.
Timeline:
06/01/2023 from 6:25 PM to 7:12 PM
6:24 PM PinFederate started on pritappgenlc028 after patching
6:25 PM PingFederate Engine all clear email for pritappgenlc028
6:25 PM PingFederate errors began: Unexpected problem looking up authorization code
7:12 PM PingFederate shutdown on pritappgenlc028
7:12 PM PingFederate errors end
7:14 PM PingFederate started on pritappgenlc028
Helpful Splunk Queries
index=ping "Unable to retrieve artifact from"
index=ping "Unexpected problem looking up authorization code"
So, the first error above indicates, in the long Java error this:
Unable to retrieve artifact from 10.30.4.183:7600
This is the error that made me decide to restart PingFederate on pritappgenlc028.
Details
Most of the errors indicated issued with pritappgenlc028, so ELG6 did a stop and start on that one server and confirmed the errors stopped.
Copied off server.log on 028 during the time to /logs/pf_engine/tmp on 028.
LOTS of these errors for several minutes:
2023-06-01 18:27:36,250  WARN  [org.eclipse.jetty.server.HttpChannel] /idp/userinfo.openid
javax.servlet.ServletException: javax.servlet.ServletException: java.lang.RuntimeException: All nodes have failed to return a valid response from RPC 'get'. Failing call.
...
Caused by: javax.servlet.ServletException: java.lang.RuntimeException: All nodes have failed to return a valid response from RPC 'get'. Failing call.
I don't know what the above error means and it stops at 6:29, when the Unable to retrieve artifact errors started.
And in the
028
log files, it says it is
Unable to retrieve artifact from 10.31.5.22/10.31.5.23/10.31.5.24
(RCIOC Engines - pritappgenlc024, pritappgenlc025, pritappgenlc026).
Note - The PingFederate Admin console Cluster Management did not indicate an issue with pritappgenlc028 (it was listed in the cluster) and pritappgenlc028 was responding to HeartBeat requests starting at 6:25 PM, so the alerting scripts did not indicate an issue with pritappgenlc028.

212304417.title: Rancho Private Cloud Network - 26 Jun 2023
212304417.plain_text:
Summary
Issues were seen today for both PingAccess and PingFederate.  Basically, lots of connection time out errors in both PingAccess (PA) and PingFederate (PF) logs seen starting on Saturday (6/24), some on Sunday (6/25) and then again this morning on and off.
Temporary solution:
Issues may have resolved on their own.  Network or spanning tree changes may have been removed that were put in place on Friday.
Long-term fix:
Not sure if one was applied or not.  Need to watch out for the errors.
Root Cause:
Timeline (06/26/2023):
Ping errors were seen starting primarily on Saturday, again on Sunday, but the first large spike was at 7 AM this morning.  The Saturday and Sunday errors MAY have been unrelated.  MyAccount reported issues on Saturday morning, around the same time.
1:29 PM:  Last of the Connect timed out errors seen in the Ping logs.
1:35 PM:  Last of the Rancho errors seen in the network/cloud.
1:41 PM:  PingFederate and PingAccess Rancho VIPs were disabled in the itiamping.cloud.pge.com and itiampingaccess.cloud.pge.com GTM pool.
4:21 PM:  PingFederate Rancho VIP was enabled in the itiamping.cloud.pge.com GTM pool.
5:26 PM:  PingAccess Rancho VIP was enabled in the itiampingaccess.cloud.pge.com GTM pool.
Helpful Splunk search terms:
index=ping "Unexpected problem looking up authorization code" OR "Connection timed out"
Details

330702894.plain_text:
Status
Control Definition

Control Development
DEFINED
Service Overview
Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed service that offers a choice of high-performing foundation models (FMs) from leading AI companies like AI21 Labs, Anthropic, Cohere, Meta, Mistral AI, Stability AI, and Amazon via a single API, along with a broad set of capabilities you need to build generative AI applications with security, privacy, and responsible AI. Using Amazon Bedrock, you can easily experiment with and evaluate top FMs for your use case, privately customize them with your data using techniques such as fine-tuning and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), and build agents that execute tasks using your enterprise systems and data sources. Since Amazon Bedrock is serverless, you don't have to manage any infrastructure, and you can securely integrate and deploy generative AI capabilities into your applications using the AWS services you are already familiar with.

354847903.title: AWS Bedrock AgentCore
354847903.plain_text:
Status
Control Definition
DEFINED
Control Development
DEFINED
Service Overview
Amazon Bedrock AgentCore extends the approved Amazon Bedrock service to enable deployment and operation of highly effective AI agents securely at scale. AgentCore builds upon Bedrock's enterprise-grade security foundation, adding agent-specific capabilities for production deployments.
AgentCore is composed of 7 distinct services. SAF approval status:
✅ APPROVED SERVICES (Top 3)
AgentCore Runtime
- Serverless runtime for deploying containerized AI agents with session isolation and extended execution support
AgentCore Memory
- Persistent storage for conversation history and agent context with encryption at rest
AgentCore Code Interpreter
- Secure sandboxed environment for Python code execution with network isolation
⚠️ RESTRICTED SERVICES (Require Special Approval)
AgentCore Gateway
- API and tool connectivity layer (REQUIRES SECURITY REVIEW - external service integration)
AgentCore Browser
- Web interaction capabilities (REQUIRES SECURITY REVIEW - public internet access)
AgentCore Observability
- OpenTelemetry tracing and monitoring (REQUIRES SECURITY REVIEW - data collection scope)
🚫 NOT APPROVED SERVICE
AgentCore Identity
- OAuth token vault and credential management (NOT APPROVED - high risk third-party data access, see separate security analysis)
Note:
Amazon Bedrock Guardrails are
REQUIRED
for all PG&E generative AI applications using AgentCore. Guardrails must be implemented for all applications, particularly those dealing with Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and other systems that require stringent controls. AgentCore agents should integrate with Bedrock Guardrails to ensure applications are developed with the highest standards of security, privacy, and responsible AI practices. For AI inferences, we will continue to use only the foundational models provided by Amazon Bedrock.
Encryption Requirements:
Per PG&E CS-TECH-020 Standard, customer managed keys (CMKs) must be used with 'Confidential' or 'Restricted' data. AWS managed keys (KMS) are approved for general encryption at rest requirements where Data Classification is 'Internal' or 'Public'.
Service References
Console
Developer Guide
Service Pricing
(AgentCore pricing included in Bedrock pricing)
API Reference - Runtime
API Reference - Control Plane
SAF Approval Summary
Service
Approval Status
Risk Level
Summary
Runtime
✅ APPROVED
LOW
Containerized agent deployment with IAM controls
Memory
✅ APPROVED
LOW
Encrypted conversation storage within AWS
Code Interpreter
✅ APPROVED
LOW
Sandboxed Python execution with network isolation
Gateway
⚠️ RESTRICTED
MEDIUM
API integration - requires security review
Browser
⚠️ RESTRICTED
MEDIUM
Web access - requires security review
Observability
⚠️ RESTRICTED
LOW-MEDIUM
Telemetry collection - requires security review
Identity
🚫 NOT APPROVED
CRITICAL
OAuth token vault - external data access risk
Deployment Guidance:
✅
APPROVED services
can be used following the adoption requirements below
⚠️
RESTRICTED services
require individual security review and CISO approval
🚫
Identity service
is NOT APPROVED - see alternative approaches in requirements section
Managed Service Security Coverage
As a fully managed AWS service, Amazon Bedrock AgentCore
automatically handles
the following security controls without customer configuration:
AWS-Managed Controls (No Customer Action Required):
✅ Encryption at rest for all AgentCore data using AWS managed keys
✅ Infrastructure patching and vulnerability management
✅ DDoS protection via AWS Shield
✅ Service-to-service authentication between AgentCore components
✅ Container runtime security for AgentCore Runtime
✅ Network isolation for Code Interpreter sandboxes
Customer Responsibility:
The policies below focus on
customer-configurable settings
that must be enforced.
Bedrock AgentCore Policies (APPROVED Services)
The following policies apply to
APPROVED services only
(Runtime, Memory, Code Interpreter). These are
enforceable, checkable configurations
that teams must implement.
Runtime Configuration Policies
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Network Security
AgentCore Runtime must deploy in VPC mode (NOT Public)
Critical
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore Runtime must use IAM authentication (NOT JWT tokens)
High
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore Runtime must use HTTP protocol (unless A2A/MCP justified)
Medium
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore agents must be associated with Bedrock Guardrails
Critical
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore Runtime must use IAM execution roles with least privilege permissions
High
Detective
Logging and Monitoring
AgentCore Runtime must enable CloudWatch logging
High
Rationale for Runtime Policies:
VPC Deployment (NOT Public):
Prevents internet exposure of agent endpoints. Public mode allows unrestricted access and is not appropriate for enterprise use.
IAM Authentication:
Uses AWS-native authentication instead of managing JWT tokens. Simpler and more secure than external token management.
HTTP Protocol:
Standard REST API protocol. MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) are specialized protocols that should be used only with security justification.
Memory Encryption Policies
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Encryption and Key Management
AgentCore Memory must use customer managed keys (CMK) for Confidential or Restricted data per CS-TECH-020
High
Detective
Logging and Monitoring
AgentCore Memory operations must be logged to CloudWatch or S3
Medium
Rationale for Memory Policies:
CMK Requirement:
PG&E CS-TECH-020 mandates customer managed keys for Confidential/Restricted data. AWS managed keys are acceptable for Internal/Public data.
Logging:
Required for audit trails and debugging agent behavior.
Code Interpreter Security Policies
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Network Security
AgentCore Code Interpreter must use SANDBOX network mode (NOT PUBLIC)
Critical
Rationale for Code Interpreter Policies:
SANDBOX Network Mode:
Isolates code execution from internet access. PUBLIC mode allows executed code to make outbound connections, creating data exfiltration risk.
General Governance
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Infrastructure Security
AgentCore resources must be tagged for governance and cost allocation
Medium
RESTRICTED SERVICES: Gateway, Browser, Observability
Status:
⚠️ Requires individual security review and CSCOE approval before use.
These services enable capabilities that introduce additional risk:
Gateway:
API integration with external services
Browser:
Web scraping and interaction with public internet
Observability:
Enhanced telemetry collection beyond CloudWatch
Approval Process:
Submit request to Security Architecture Forum with use case justification
Security team will assess risk and define additional controls
CSCOE approval required for production use
Note:
Each service will require custom security requirements based on specific use case.
NOT APPROVED SERVICE: Identity
Status:
🚫
NOT APPROVED
Risk Level:
CRITICAL - Third-party OAuth token storage and external data access
Reason for Denial:
Stores OAuth tokens for 25+ external services (Google, Slack, Salesforce, GitHub, etc.)
Enables AI agents to access user data outside PG&E infrastructure
High risk of data exfiltration, compliance violations, and credential theft
Conflicts with NERC CIP, SOX, and data residency requirements
Alternative Approaches:
Use AgentCore Gateway with IAM roles instead of OAuth
Store credentials in approved vault (EntraID, AWS Secrets Manager)
Direct AWS SDK integration without external services
Exception Process:
If Identity use is deemed business-critical:
Submit exception request to Security Architecture Forum
Provide detailed security controls implementation plan
Demonstrate no alternative approaches exist
Limit to Internal/Public data classification only
Requires CSCOE approval
Note:
The "Bedrock Agent without guardrails" Wiz policy applies to both Bedrock Agents and AgentCore agents. This policy is currently enforced in the PG&E environment and will automatically detect AgentCore agents deployed without Guardrails.

Service Overview
Amazon Comprehend uses natural language processing (NLP) to extract insights about the content of documents without the need to build, train, and deploy machine learning models. It can analyze text documents to identify entities, key phrases, language, sentiments, and other common elements with high accuracy. The Entity Recognizer feature allows you to create custom entity recognition models that can identify specific business-related entity types not covered by the standard entity types. These custom models help organizations extract industry-specific information from unstructured text across various document formats, enabling more targeted data analysis and automated document processing workflows.

Service Overview
AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service that lets you run code without provisioning or managing servers, creating workload-aware cluster scaling logic, maintaining event integrations, or managing runtimes. With Lambda, you can run code for virtually any type of application or backend service with zero administration. Lambda automatically scales your application by running code in response to each event, from a few requests per day to thousands per second. You only pay for the compute time you consume—there's no charge when your code isn't running. Lambda supports multiple programming languages and provides seamless integration with other AWS services, enabling you to build sophisticated event-driven applications that automatically respond to changes in data, shifts in system state, or actions by users.

334367063.title: AWS DynamoDB
334367063.plain_text:
Status
Control Definition

Service Overview
Amazon DynamoDB is a key-value and document database that delivers single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. It's a fully managed, multi-region, multi-active, durable database with built-in security, backup and restore, and in-memory caching for internet-scale applications. DynamoDB can handle more than 10 trillion requests per day and can support peaks of more than 20 million requests per second. Many of the world's fastest growing businesses such as Lyft, Airbnb, and Redfin as well as enterprises such as Samsung, Toyota, and Capital One depend on the scale and performance of DynamoDB to support their mission-critical workloads. DynamoDB offers encryption at rest, point-in-time recovery, and on-demand backup capabilities to help protect your data and provide robust data management.

342299798.title: AWS Managed Services (AMS)
342299798.plain_text:
Status
- Control Definition: DEFINED
- Control Development: DEFINED
- Approval Recommendation: APPROVED
Service Overview
AWS Managed Services (AMS) is a fully managed service that takes over operational management of AWS accounts, including provisioning, monitoring, patching, backup, security, and compliance. It supports two modes:
- AMS Accelerate: Lightweight operational support.
- AMS Advanced: Full lifecycle management including landing zones, change management, access control, and endpoint security.
AMS enforces security baselines (CIS, NIST, PCI), automates operations, and integrates with AWS native security tools (GuardDuty, Macie, Security Hub). It operates under a shared responsibility model.

330702239.title: AWS API Gateway
330702239.plain_text:
Status
Control Definition

Control Development

Service Overview:
Amazon API Gateway is a fully managed service that makes it easy for developers to create, publish, maintain, monitor, and secure APIs at any scale. APIs act as the "front door" for applications to access data, business logic, or functionality from your backend services. Using API Gateway, you can create RESTful APIs and WebSocket APIs that enable real-time two-way communication applications. API Gateway supports containerized and serverless workloads, as well as web applications.
API Gateway handles all the tasks involved in accepting and processing up to hundreds of thousands of concurrent API calls, including traffic management, CORS support, authorization and access control, throttling, monitoring, and API version management. API Gateway has no minimum fees or startup costs. You pay for the API calls you receive and the amount of data transferred out and, with the API Gateway tiered pricing model, you can reduce your cost as your API usage scales.

330702290.title: AWS Backup
330702290.plain_text:
Status

Control Development

Service Overview:
AWS Backup enables you to centralize and automate data protection across AWS services. AWS Backup offers a cost-effective, fully managed, policy-based service that further simplifies data protection at scale. AWS Backup also helps you support your regulatory compliance obligations and meets your business continuity goals. Together with AWS Organizations, AWS Backup enables you to centrally deploy data protection (backup) policies to configure, manage, and govern your backup activity across your organization’s AWS accounts and resources, including Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instances, Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes, Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) databases (including Amazon Aurora clusters), Amazon DynamoDB tables, Amazon Elastic File System (EFS), Amazon FSx for Lustre, Amazon FSx for Windows File Server, and AWS Storage Gateway volumes.

Service Overview:
Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) is an object storage service that offers industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance. This means customers of all sizes and industries can use it to store and protect any amount of data for a range of use cases, such as websites, mobile applications, backup and restore, archive, enterprise applications, IoT devices, and big data analytics. Amazon S3 provides easy-to-use management features so you can organize your data and configure finely-tuned access controls to meet your specific business, organizational, and compliance requirements. Amazon S3 is designed for 99.999999999% (11 9's) of durability, and stores data for millions of applications for companies all around the world.  S3 and it's sub-capabilities such as intelligent tiering options are covered and approved.
