213713688.title: Candidate AADDS Runbooks
213713688.plain_text:
Powershell Steps to join a Windows EC2 instance to
lab.pge.com
, AWS account 514712703977
Use admin credentials for SignOn to
Microsoft Azure
.
Run Powershell as Administrator
Enter these commands to configure DNS for the local host's ethernet interface…
$Domain
=
'
lab.pge.com
'
$IfIndex = (Get-NetRoute | % { Process { If (!$_.RouteMetric) { $_.ifIndex } } }) Set-DNSClientServerAddress –interfaceIndex $IfIndex –ServerAddresses ("10.94.0.36","10.94.0.37")
Set-DnsClient
-ConnectionSpecificSuffix
$Domain
-InterfaceIndex $IfIndex
add-computer –domainname $Domain -restart
See also,
Join Computer to Domain Using PowerShell
Steps to Find EC2 Joined to AADDS
Pre-conditions:
Use admin credentials for SignOn to
Microsoft Azure
.
Create a “Bastion Host” VM in Azure to manage ADDS
Navigate Home/Virtual Machines
Create/Azure Virtual Machine
Select same resource-group used for ADDS,
rg-cyber-cscoe-sapns2-dev01
Select an Image of type Windows Server 2019 Data Center
Architecture: Intel , Size: Standard_DS1_v2
Configure Network to allow RDP connectivity
Add an inbound network security group rule with destination port 3389.
Download an RDP file
Navigate to Home/Virtual Machines/Bastion-ADDS-01/Connect
Select Private IP address and port 3389
Push Download
Steps:
Use admin credentials for SignOn to
Microsoft Azure
.
Navigate to Home/Azure Active Directory/Devices
Filter on the hostname of your Virtual Machine, ie Bastion-ADDS-01
Post-condition:
Confirm or deny host joined domain.
Windows GUI Steps to join a Windows EC2 instance to
lab.pge.com
, AWS account 514712703977
Launch RDP Client
Import RDP file
Use admin credentials for SignOn.
Navigate to Control Panel/Network and Internet/Network Connections
Right-click Ethernet/Properties/Internet Protocol
Ensure the DNS Settings 10.94.0.36, 10.94.0.37
Navigate to Control Panel/System
Select Change Settings within the Computer name, domain and workgroup settings
Select System Properties/Change
Enter
lab.pge.com
in the “Member Of/Domain” text field.
Windows responds with username/password dialog box.
See Zac for credentials
AWS Systems Manager Steps to join a Windows EC2 instance to
lab.pge.com
, AWS account 514712703977
Deploy an EC2 Instance using PGE Golden Windows AMI.
Windows role must have these policies…
arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/
AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore
arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/
AmazonSSMDirectoryServiceAccess
Navigate to the Systems Manager Console/Fleet Manager
Confirm that the instance is managed by SSM; given you used CCOE automation which uses PGE’s Golden AMI; it should.
Navigate to the Systems Manager Console/Run Command
Search for Command document,
AWS-JoinDirectoryServiceDomain
Populate the form…
Directory Id = d-9267475ab4
Directory Name =
lab.pge.com
DNS Ip Addresses = 10.94.0.37, 10.94.0.36
Choose your instance however you want; I do it manually with the ec2’s instance id.
Given that I don’t have S3 bucket, key, policies that grant access, I use cloudwatch logs for output, accept defaults.
Push Run
See also,
https://youtu.be/qaj9Titw0MM

246417348.title: Create an RDS Custom Engine Version for SQL Server 2019
246417348.plain_text:
Here we will create an RDS Custom Engine Version(CEV) leveraging an AWS published WindowsServer2019 AMI with SQL Server 2019 pre-installed, licenses included.
Steps:
AWS CLI
Required AWS Resources
Open the context (right-click) menu for the link
custom-sqlserver-onboard.zip
and choose
Save Link As
.
Save and extract the file to your computer.
Although this template creates all of the required resources, you won't need them all, most of them are already provided by the CCOE.
For example, account 514712703977 only required these resources.
Another example for a plain'ole unchanged free-tier account.
Deploy the resource using commands similar to this.  AWS requires us to upload large templates (>5k?) to an S3 bucket.
Launch an EC2 using an AWS published Windows AMI, licenses included.

246417457.title: Create an RDS Database using a SQL Server 2019 Custom Engine Version (CEV)
246417457.plain_text:
So, a CEV is a pre-requisite.  Either reuse an existing CEV or
make your own
.
To see if an existing CEV exists, navigate to RDS Console/Custom engine versions
Choose each entry to see its description.  You are looking for something like "Custom SQL Server 15.00.4322.2" which is "Microsoft SQL Server 2019 (RTM-CU22) (KB5027702) - 15.0.4322.2 (X64)"
Note the matching engine "version name", ie 15.00.4322.2.cev-13.
Navigate to RDS Console/Create Database
Choose "Standard Create" and "Microsoft SQL Server"
Choose management type, "Amazon RDS Custom"
Choose edition, "SQL Server Standard Edition"
Check engine version to be "Use custom engine version"
Then select "15.00.4322.2.cev-13"
Choose Templates, "Production"
Give your database a unique name, choose a username and password.
Set the storage options that you want, we beleive that 100MB is a minimum, 3000 iops.
Set your options similar to above, however, your VPC & DB Subnet Group will be unique to your stacks.
Deny public access to prevent exposure to attacks.
Take note of the security groups; there are a couple from the CCOE and one from my stack.
Choose a pre-made instance profile and kms key. I used the ones from my CloudFormation stack.
Choose "Full Automation"; I don't require backups for my experiments.
Check I'm all set and push Create database.

364979030.title: Playbook: Computing Resource Compromise
364979030.plain_text:
What This Covers
A compute resource (EC2 instance, Lambda function, container, VM) has been compromised — malware is running on it, it's communicating with malicious infrastructure, or it's being used for unauthorized activity (crypto mining, C2, lateral movement).
What It Looks Like in Wiz
Alert Examples
Severity
CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B
High
CryptoCurrency:Runtime/BitcoinTool.B
High
Impact:Runtime/CryptoMinerExecuted
High
Execution:Runtime/MaliciousFileExecuted
High
Execution:Runtime/ReverseShell
High
Backdoor:EC2/C&CActivity.B
High
Backdoor:Runtime/C&CActivity.B
High
Trojan:EC2/DNSDataExfiltration
High
DefenseEvasion:Runtime/ProcessInjection.Proc
High
High severity malware discovered by GD
High
Mass Uplink Communication With a Malicious IP
High
SSH Communication With Malicious IP
High
Instance Involved in SSH Brute Force Attempt
High
Suspicious EDR and cloud defense evasion activities originated from an EC2 instance
Critical
Enumeration and RCE on instance(s) using EC2 UserData
Critical
Network scanning and attempt to change RDS master password originated from an EC2 instance
Critical
MITRE ATT&CK:
Execution, Persistence, Command and Control, Impact, Lateral Movement
Investigation Steps
Step 1: Identify the compromised resource
From the Wiz alert, capture:
Field
Where in Wiz
Resource ID
Instance ID, Lambda ARN, container/pod name
Resource type
EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS pod, Azure VM, AKS pod
AWS account / Azure subscription
Cloud account section
Region
Resource details
Public IP (if any)
Network properties
Instance role / identity
What IAM role is attached
Step 2: Understand what the resource is doing
From the Wiz alert:
Investigation graph
— what actions has this resource taken?
Event timeline
— full sequence of events
AI analysis
— is this a known malware family? What's the behavior pattern?
Key questions:
Is it communicating with known malicious IPs? (C2)
Is it mining cryptocurrency?
Is it scanning the network? (lateral movement)
Is it exfiltrating data?
Did it create new IAM users/roles/keys? (pivoting to IAM compromise)
Step 3: Check for lateral movement
In Wiz:
Did the instance role make API calls it shouldn't have?
Are there alerts on other resources in the same account?
Did the attacker move from this resource to other accounts or services?
Evidence Collection
From Wiz (primary)
[ ] Alert JSON export (three-dot menu > Export as JSON)
[ ] Investigation graph screenshot
[ ] Event timeline
[ ] AI analysis
[ ] Resource details (instance ID, role, region, public IP)
[ ] Network connections (malicious IPs communicated with)
[ ] MITRE classification
From Wiz Runtime Sensor (if deployed)
If the Wiz sensor is on the compromised host, you may also see:
Process execution details (what binary ran, parent process, command line)
File system changes
Network connections at the process level
Manual Fallback
AWS — EC2:
# Get instance details
aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-ids <instance-id>
# Check security groups (network access)
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <sg-id>
# Get console output (may show boot-level compromise)
aws ec2 get-console-output --instance-id <instance-id>
# Check instance metadata for role credentials
# (from within the instance if accessible)
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role-name>
# Check VPC Flow Logs for network activity
aws logs filter-log-events \
--log-group-name <flow-log-group> \
--filter-pattern "<instance-private-ip>"
AWS — Lambda:
# Get function details
aws lambda get-function --function-name <function-name>
# Check recent invocations
aws logs filter-log-events \
--log-group-name /aws/lambda/<function-name> \
--start-time <timestamp>
Azure — VM:
# Get VM details
az vm show --resource-group <rg> --name <vm-name>
# Check NSG rules
az network nsg list --resource-group <rg>
# Get boot diagnostics
az vm boot-diagnostics get-boot-log --resource-group <rg> --name <vm-name>
Containment
Priority: Isolate the compromised resource to stop lateral movement and C2 communication.
AWS — EC2 Instance
Step
Action
Who
1. Isolate network
Replace all security groups with an empty "isolation" SG (no inbound or outbound rules)
IR / Cloud Security
2. Preserve evidence
Create an EBS snapshot of all attached volumes
before
termination
IR
3. Revoke instance role sessions
Add deny-all inline policy to the instance role
IR
4. Capture memory (if possible)
SSM RunCommand to dump memory, or use Wiz sensor forensics
IR / Cloud Security
5. Stop (don't terminate)
aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids <id>
— preserves the disk for forensics
IR
Do NOT terminate the instance immediately
— you need the disk for forensics.
AWS — Lambda
Step
Action
Who
1. Disable function
Set reserved concurrency to 0:
aws lambda put-function-concurrency --function-name <name> --reserved-concurrent-executions 0
IR / Cloud Security
2. Preserve code
Download the deployment package for analysis
IR
3. Check environment variables
aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name <name>
— look for injected secrets/C2 URLs
IR
AWS — EKS / Container
Step
Action
Who
1. Cordon the node
kubectl cordon <node>
— prevent new pods from scheduling
IR / Cloud Security
2. Capture pod details
kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <namespace>
IR
3. Delete the compromised pod
kubectl delete pod <pod> -n <namespace>
IR
4. Investigate the node
If the node itself is compromised, isolate via security group
IR
Azure — VM
Step
Action
Who
1. Isolate network
Apply an NSG with deny-all rules
IR / Cloud Security
2. Snapshot disk
az snapshot create --resource-group <rg> --source <disk-id> --name <snapshot-name>
IR
3. Revoke managed identity sessions
Disable the managed identity if compromised
IR
4. Stop VM
az vm stop --resource-group <rg> --name <vm-name>
IR
Post-Containment
[ ] Analyze disk snapshots / memory captures for IOCs
[ ] Identify how the resource was compromised (vulnerable app, SSRF, exposed management port, supply chain)
[ ] Check if the instance role credentials were used elsewhere (pivot to
IAM Playbook
)
[ ] Scan for persistence mechanisms (cron jobs, systemd services, modified AMIs, Lambda layers)
[ ] Rebuild the resource from a known-good image — do not simply "clean" a compromised instance
[ ] Document the full timeline in the IR ticket
[ ] Update Wiz alert status to Resolved with notes

364979032.title: Playbook: Data Exfiltration
364979032.plain_text:
What This Covers
Data is leaving the environment or being destroyed — S3 buckets being copied to external accounts, database snapshots shared publicly, storage accounts deleted at scale, or anomalous data transfer patterns.
What It Looks Like in Wiz
Alert Examples
Severity
Exfiltration:S3/AnomalousBehavior
High
Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Delete
High
Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Permission
High
Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Write
High
Unusual DB Export Task To External Bucket Started
High
S3 object configured as publicly exposed via ACL
High
RDS DB snapshot configured as publicly exposed
High
EBS snapshot was configured to be public
High
OpenSearch domain endpoint configured as publicly exposed
High
Storage Account Made Publicly Accessible
High
Multiple Storage Accounts With Sensitive Data Deleted Within A Short Timeframe
Critical
S3 bucket policy changed to allow public access on a bucket with sensitive data
Critical
Sensitive data publicly exposed following S3 bucket policy change
Critical
Mass data download from sensitive S3 buckets
Critical
MITRE ATT&CK:
Exfiltration, Impact, Collection
Investigation Steps
Step 1: Identify what data is involved
From the Wiz alert, capture:
Field
Where in Wiz
Resource name
S3 bucket, RDS instance, storage account, EBS snapshot
Resource type
Storage, database, snapshot
AWS account / Azure subscription
Cloud account section
Data classification
Does Wiz flag this resource as containing sensitive data?
Who did it
Principal — IAM user, role, source IP
What happened
Specific API calls (GetObject, PutBucketPolicy, ModifySnapshotAttribute, etc.)
Step 2: Assess scope and severity
From the Wiz alert:
Investigation graph
— what sequence of actions led to this?
Event timeline
— how much data was accessed? Over what time period?
AI analysis
— is this a known exfiltration pattern?
Key questions:
Is this a single resource or multiple resources?
Was data copied to an external account or made public?
How much data was transferred? (check byte counts in VPC Flow Logs if available)
Does the resource contain sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial data)?
Is this still actively happening?
Step 3: Identify the actor
In Wiz:
Who performed the action? (IAM user, role, service account)
What IP did the action originate from? (internal vs. external)
Is this a legitimate identity that was compromised, or an attacker-created identity?
Did this identity also appear in other alerts? (pivot to
IAM Playbook
)
Evidence Collection
From Wiz (primary)
[ ] Alert JSON export (three-dot menu > Export as JSON)
[ ] Investigation graph screenshot
[ ] Event timeline (all related cloud events with timestamps)
[ ] AI analysis assessment
[ ] Resource details (bucket name, database ID, storage account)
[ ] Data classification (does Wiz flag sensitive data on this resource?)
[ ] Principal details (who did it, from where)
[ ] MITRE ATT&CK classification
Manual Fallback
AWS — S3 Exfiltration:
# Check bucket policy (was it changed to allow public/external access?)
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <bucket-name>
# Check bucket ACL
aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket <bucket-name>
# Check S3 access logs (if enabled) for bulk downloads
aws s3api get-bucket-logging --bucket <bucket-name>
# Check CloudTrail for S3 data events
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=ResourceType,AttributeValue=AWS::S3::Bucket \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--max-results 50
# Check for bucket replication to external accounts
aws s3api get-bucket-replication --bucket <bucket-name>
AWS — Database Exfiltration:
# Check if RDS snapshots were shared
aws rds describe-db-snapshot-attributes --db-snapshot-identifier <snapshot-id>
# Check for recent snapshot exports
aws rds describe-export-tasks --filters Name=source-arn,Values=<db-arn>
# Check if EBS snapshots were made public
aws ec2 describe-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id <snapshot-id> --attribute createVolumePermission
AWS — General:
# Check CloudTrail for the principal's recent activity
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=<username> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--max-results 50
# Check VPC Flow Logs for large data transfers
aws logs filter-log-events \
--log-group-name <flow-log-group> \
--filter-pattern "<instance-private-ip>"
Azure — Storage Exfiltration:
# Check storage account access level
az storage account show --name <account-name> --resource-group <rg> \
--query "allowBlobPublicAccess"
# Check storage account network rules
az storage account show --name <account-name> --resource-group <rg> \
--query "networkRuleSet"
# Check activity log for storage modifications
az monitor activity-log list \
--resource-id <storage-account-resource-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
# List storage account keys (check if keys were accessed)
az storage account keys list --account-name <account-name> --resource-group <rg>
Azure — Database Exfiltration:
# Check SQL database export operations
az monitor activity-log list \
--resource-id <sql-server-resource-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
# Check Key Vault access logs for secret/key retrieval
az monitor activity-log list \
--resource-id <keyvault-resource-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
Containment
Priority: Stop data from leaving and preserve evidence of what was accessed.
AWS — S3 Public Exposure / Exfiltration
Step
Action
Who
1. Block public access
aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket <bucket> --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true
IR / Cloud Security
2. Revert bucket policy
Restore the previous bucket policy (from CloudTrail event history)
IR / App Owner
3. Revoke external sharing
Remove any cross-account grants from bucket ACL or policy
IR
4. Disable replication
If replication was configured to an external account, delete the replication rule
IR
5. Contain the identity
If the acting principal is compromised, pivot to
IAM Playbook
IR
AWS — Database / Snapshot Exposure
Step
Action
Who
1. Make snapshot private
aws rds modify-db-snapshot-attribute --db-snapshot-identifier <id> --attribute-name restore --values-to-remove all
IR / App Owner
2. Make EBS snapshot private
aws ec2 modify-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id <id> --attribute createVolumePermission --operation-type remove --user-ids all
IR / App Owner
3. Cancel export tasks
aws rds cancel-export-task --export-task-identifier <task-id>
IR
4. Contain the identity
Pivot to
IAM Playbook
if identity is compromised
IR
AWS — Active Bulk Download
Step
Action
Who
1. Restrict bucket access
Add a deny-all bucket policy (or restrict to known IPs)
IR / Cloud Security
2. Revoke the principal's access
Deactivate access keys or add deny-all inline policy
IR
3. Assess what was taken
Review S3 data events in Wiz or CloudTrail for all GetObject calls
IR
Azure — Storage Account Exposure
Step
Action
Who
1. Disable public access
az storage account update --name <account> --resource-group <rg> --allow-blob-public-access false
IR / Cloud Security
2. Restrict network access
az storage account update --name <account> --resource-group <rg> --default-action Deny
IR / Cloud Security
3. Rotate storage keys
az storage account keys renew --account-name <account> --resource-group <rg> --key key1
App Owner (directed by IR)
4. Contain the identity
Pivot to
IAM Playbook
if identity is compromised
IR
Azure — Mass Deletion
Step
Action
Who
1. Contain the identity
Immediately disable the acting principal
IR
2. Check soft-delete
Verify if deleted resources can be recovered via soft-delete policies
IR / App Owner
3. Check backups
Identify available backups for affected resources
App Owner
4. Lock remaining resources
Apply Azure Resource Locks (CanNotDelete) on remaining sensitive storage accounts
Cloud Security
Post-Containment
[ ] Determine exactly what data was accessed or exfiltrated
[ ] Assess whether the data was sensitive (PII, credentials, financial, regulated)
[ ] If sensitive data was exposed, determine notification requirements (privacy, legal, compliance)
[ ] Identify how the exfiltration happened (compromised credentials, misconfiguration, insider)
[ ] If credentials were involved, complete the
IAM Compromise Playbook
[ ] Review and harden data access policies for affected resources
[ ] Document the full timeline in the IR ticket
[ ] Update Wiz alert status to Resolved with notes

364979034.title: Playbook: IAM Compromise
364979034.plain_text:
What This Covers
An IAM identity (user, role, access key, or service account) has been compromised — either the credentials were stolen, or an attacker is using them to perform unauthorized actions.
What It Looks Like in Wiz
Alert Examples
Severity
EC2 Instance Credentials Used Outside Of AWS
High
Lambda Function Credentials Used Outside Of AWS
High
ECS Task Credentials Used Outside Of AWS
High
AssumeRole from a malicious AWS account
High
Publicly exposed AWS access keys found on public Github repository
High
Wiz-Identified Compromised Secret Usage
High
AttackSequence:IAM/CompromisedCredentials
High
Impossible travel: same user/role login from multiple countries
High
Virtual Machine Credential Used Outside Azure
High
Credential theft activity following cloud activity associated with long-term AWS keys
Critical
Publicly exposed highly privileged AWS access keys found on public Github repository
Critical
MITRE ATT&CK:
Credential Access, Initial Access
Investigation Steps
Step 1: Identify the compromised identity
From the Wiz alert, capture:
Field
Where in Wiz
Identity name
Principal section (IAM user ARN, role ARN, or access key ID)
Identity type
User vs. role vs. service account vs. access key
Source IP
Event details — where did the API call originate?
AWS account / Azure subscription
Cloud account section
Timeframe
Alert created/first event timestamps
Step 2: Assess scope of compromise
In the Wiz alert:
Review the
investigation graph
— what actions did this identity take?
Review the
event timeline
— how far back does the suspicious activity go?
Check: did this identity create new users, roles, or access keys? (persistence)
Check: did this identity access data stores, modify security groups, or disable logging? (lateral movement / defense evasion)
Step 3: Check for related activity
In Wiz Threats, search for:
Same principal across other alerts
Same source IP across other alerts
Same AWS account / Azure subscription
Evidence Collection
From Wiz (primary)
All of this is in the alert:
[ ] Alert JSON export (three-dot menu > Export as JSON)
[ ] Investigation graph screenshot
[ ] Event timeline (all related cloud events with timestamps)
[ ] AI analysis assessment
[ ] Principal details (ARN, access key ID, source IP)
[ ] MITRE ATT&CK classification
Manual Fallback (if Wiz is unavailable)
AWS:
# List recent API calls by the compromised identity
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=<username> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--max-results 50
# Check if new access keys were created
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name <username>
# Check if new users were created recently
aws iam list-users --query "Users[?CreateDate>=\`<timestamp>\`]"
# Check recent role assumptions
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=AssumeRole \
--start-time <timestamp>
Azure:
# Check recent sign-in activity
az monitor activity-log list \
--caller <principal-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
# Check Entra ID sign-in logs (requires Azure AD access)
az ad audit-logs sign-ins list \
--filter "userPrincipalName eq '<user>'" \
--top 50
Containment
Priority: Stop the attacker from using the stolen credentials.
AWS — IAM User Compromise
Step
Command / Action
Who
1. Deactivate access keys
aws iam update-access-key --access-key-id <key> --status Inactive --user-name <user>
App Owner (directed by IR)
2. Invalidate sessions
Attach an inline deny-all policy with a condition on
aws:TokenIssueTime
before the compromise
IR / Cloud Security
3. Reset console password
aws iam update-login-profile --user-name <user> --password-reset-required
App Owner
4. Review and revoke new persistence
Delete any access keys, users, roles, or policies created by the attacker
IR
AWS — Role / Instance Credential Compromise
Step
Action
Who
1. Revoke active sessions
Update the role's trust policy or add a deny-all inline policy
IR / Cloud Security
2. Investigate the source
If EC2 instance creds were stolen, the instance itself may be compromised — see
Compute Playbook
IR
3. Rotate credentials
Stop/start the EC2 instance to rotate instance role credentials
App Owner
Azure — Entra ID Compromise
Step
Action
Who
1. Revoke user sessions
az ad user update --id <user-id> --account-enabled false
IR / Cloud Security
2. Reset password
az ad user update --id <user-id> --force-change-password-next-sign-in true
App Owner
3. Revoke OAuth tokens
Azure Portal > Entra ID > Users > Revoke sessions
IR
4. Review app consents
Check for newly granted app permissions that should be revoked
IR
Post-Containment
[ ] Confirm attacker access is fully revoked
[ ] Identify how credentials were compromised (leaked on GitHub, phishing, SSRF, etc.)
[ ] Check for any data accessed during the compromise window
[ ] Generate new credentials for the affected identity
[ ] Document the full timeline in the IR ticket
[ ] Update Wiz alert status to Resolved with notes

--- Page: Terraform Cloud at PG&E ---

---

Business value(s)
Terraform
allows users to leverage infrastructure as code for provisioning, compliance, and management of any cloud, infrastructure, and service.
Terraform Cloud
is a SaaS solution to enable auto provisioning of AWS resources, which provides pipelines which is invoked when there is change to the source code repository (GitHub). GitHub invokes web hooks which whenever there are changes made to the code (infrastructure as code) can invoke the pipeline in Terraform cloud.
Terraform IaC modules
provide a standardized IaC modules for PG&E teams to create AWS resources which will be compliant with our SAF.
Sentinel PaC policy sets
help PG&E teams to deploy only SAF compliant resources and identify non-compliant SAF resources before being created in cloud environment.

--- Page: Terraform Modules ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=96527470
Labels: tfc
Chunk 1/1
---

The Cloud Center of Excellence (
CCoE
) team at Pacific Gas and Electric (
PG&E
) has developed a suite of Terraform (
TF
) modules designed to streamline the creation of resources in commercial cloud environments. These modules are crafted with a focus on security and compliance, adhering to PG&E's cyber security guidance and the Service Adoption Framework (
SAF
).
Key Features
Security and Compliance
: The TF modules are implemented in accordance with PG&E's cyber security guidance and the SAF. This ensures that all resources created using these modules meet the highest standards of security and compliance.
Opinionated Design
: The modules are opinionated, meaning they come with predefined configurations and best practices. This design approach helps to cover more than 95% of use cases, providing a robust and reliable foundation for cloud resource management. These modules are specifically created to jump-start teams who are new to Terraform, allowing them to quickly and efficiently create cloud resources without needing to start from scratch.
Ease of Use
: By using these modules, users can quickly and efficiently create cloud resources without needing to start from scratch. The modules encapsulate complex configurations, making it easier for users to deploy resources in a consistent and repeatable manner.
Customization and Expertise
While the TF modules are designed to be broadly applicable, feature requests to customize the modules for specific teams can break their usability for the broader company. We congratulate teams who are requesting such customizations, as it indicates they have gained significant expertise in Terraform. These teams are encouraged to write their own Terraform code with the required features, as customizing a standard module will defeat the purpose of being useful to others.
CCoE Platform Team and Terraform Cloud Orchestration
The CCoE platform team, along with Terraform Cloud Orchestration, has created more than 50 Terraform modules. These modules enable our partner teams to get a head start in creating fully SAF-compliant resources using Terraform. These modules are available in our Terraform private registry:
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/registry/private/modules
.
Benefits
Consistency
: Ensures that all cloud resources are created following the same standards and practices, reducing the risk of misconfigurations.
Efficiency
: Saves time and effort by providing ready-to-use modules that cover a wide range of use cases.
Security
: Aligns with PG&E's stringent security requirements, providing peace of mind that resources are secure and compliant.
Getting Started
To get started with the TF modules, users can refer to the detailed documentation provided by the CCoE team. The documentation includes step-by-step guides, examples, and best practices to help users effectively utilize the modules.
For any questions or support, users can reach out to the CCoE team through the designated support channels.
For starters, Terraform module usage is explained with examples in the repository -
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tf-modules-examples
PGE Terraform module repository -
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-terraform-modules
Sonar status -
-
https://sonarqube.nonprod.pge.com/projects?search=pge-terraform-modules
The following Terraform modules are
PG&E SAF2.0
compliant and are available as of
07-Feb-2024

--- Page: Terraform Modules and KMS ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=226660390
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What is AWS KMS?
AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) is a managed service that makes it easy for you to create and control the cryptographic keys that are used to protect your data.
Link for detailed information:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/kms/latest/developerguide/overview.html
As per the updated SAF standards KMS is not required when DataClassification is internal or public.
Though using KMS is optional when DataClassification is internal or public, there is no restriction using it.
All PGE terraform modules have a validation in place to check for
KMS
key
based
on the
DataClassification
, validation is based on the following table
S.No
DataClassification
KMS
1
Internal
Optional
2
Public
Optional
3
Confidential
Mandatory
4
Restricted
Mandatory
5
Confidential-BCSI
Mandatory
6
Restricted-BCSI
Mandatory
All terraform modules have
KMS
as optional variable and the default value is set to
null
.
All terraform module examples are set with
DataClassification
tag value as
internal/public
and doesn't require
KMS
.
It is important to understand when using
DataClassification
tag value other than
internal/public
a
KMS
key/ARN is mandatory.
It is a pre-requisite to have a valid
KMS
before using it with modules when the
DataClassification
tag is not
internal/public
.
In other words, a valid
KMS
should be available at the time of
terraform
plan
Code to create
KMS
is included in all module examples; KMS should created first and the rest of the example can be included in the plan
It is not necessary to create KMS keys to use with the modules,
existing KMS keys can also be used
and passed as variable values
If one wants to use the KMS key that is created in the same workflow (terraform file), it has to be done in a two step process of just creating the KMS alone in the first place and add the rest of the code that uses the KMS key with the module

--- Page: Terraform Cloud Route53 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=206799390

Chunk 1/1
---

Terraform Cloud (TFC) Usage Policies at PG&E
Overview
This document outlines our organization's policies for using Terraform Cloud (TFC) to manage AWS Route 53 records in shared services. These policies ensure that Route 53 records are provisioned correctly according to the respective environments.
Environment Definitions
We maintain several environments for different stages of software development and deployment:
Development (dev):
This environment is for developing new features and initial testing.
Testing (test):
This environment is for rigorous testing of new features.
Quality Assurance (QA):
This environment is for final checks before deploying to production.
Production (prod):
This environment hosts the live application.
Each of these environments has access to a set of shared services (shared svc) for managing Route 53 records.
Route 53 Records Access Policies
Access to manage Route 53 records in shared services is regulated based on the environment. The policies are as follows:
Non-Production Shared Services:
The dev and test environments are allowed to provision Route 53 records in the non-production shared services. This supports robust testing and development without affecting the production environment.
The following hosted zones (sub-domains) are allowed from dev, test environments:
nonprod.pge.com
np-dev-api.pge.com
np-dev.pge.com
Production Shared Services:
The QA and production environments are allowed to provision Route 53 records in the production shared services. This policy ensures that DNS records critical to the live application are only managed in a controlled production setting.
The following hosted zones (sub-domains) are allowed from QA and prod environment:
alerts.pge.com
cloudapi.pge.com
dc.pge.com
dcs.pge.com
digitalcatalyst.pge.com
io.pge.com
pgecloud.net
pgepspsmap.com
pgepspsmaps.com
ss-dev.pge.com
ss.pge.com
Compliance
All team members are expected to follow these policies when using TFC to manage Route 53 records. Failure to do so can lead to issues with DNS record allocation. If you have any questions about these policies or how to use TFC within these guidelines, please contact the
CCOE Team
.

--- Page: Terraform Cloud - Infrastructure pipeline ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=96522791
Labels: tfc
Chunk 1/1
---

Terraform cloud is a cloud infrastructure automation platform. Terraform allows users to leverage infrastructure as code for provisioning, compliance, and management of any cloud, infrastructure, and service. To enable auto provisioning of AWS resources, Terraform cloud provides pipelines which is invoked when there is change to the source code repository (github). Github invokes webhooks which whenever there are changes made to the code (infrastructure as code) can invoke the pipeline in Terraform cloud.
A consistent provisioning workflow with Terraform Cloud means one workflow to learn, secure, govern, and audit.
Terraform Cloud
provides a central way for many users within the organization to safely and efficiently provision and manage their infrastructure. The largest business benefits that our existing customers have seen from Terraform Cloud relate to operational efficiency, controlling cloud costs and, reducing risk.
Operational efficiency
Operational efficiency is achieved through strong developer-operator team workflows integrated with workspaces, modules, and the Terraform Cloud configuration designer. Terraform Cloud provides a logical unit to break down infrastructure as code into workspaces.
A
workspace
is everything Terraform needs to run: a configuration file, input variables, and a statefile. Workspaces can convert monolithic infrastructure into modular infrastructure. They help reduce surface area and impact of errors to a single workspace and simplify debugging errors.
The
private module registry
and
configuration designer
allow users to leverage pre-built libraries of infrastructure. The combination of these can reduce configuration creation time for developers by up to 80%. Now, users only have to define values for their configuration variables (cloud credentials, region, # of resources), requiring less employee time and resources.
Modules can help further reduce the number of full-time employees per year that are dedicated to provisioning and managing a single vendor by leveraging the same team of individuals to provision and manage all vendors. Results can be seen for onboarding new vendors faster compared to learning cloud-native tools.
Risk reduction
Sentinel
policy as code
provides risk reduction by enforcing security, compliance and operational best practices for how resources can be consumed. It’s a powerful language framework built into all of HashiCorp's  products to enforce guardrails, business requirements, and legal compliance in real-time. With Sentinel and the private module registry, organizations can ensure that only the appropriate cloud resources are deployed in the way they are intended, following regulatory compliance to avoid fines, and prevent vulnerabilities and breaches.
Controlling cloud costs
Terraform Cloud can help organizations systematically control costs by up to 30-40% using modules and Sentinel policies to enforce operational best practices. Sentinel can help ensure operational best practices are followed to prevent overspending on cloud costs. Sample policies to enforce include preventing overprovisioning, approval process for expensive resources used in dev/test, and spinning down resources after they’re no longer needed.
Additional details regarding the feature-specific benefits are outlined in the tables and diagram below.
Collaboration Capabilities
Feature
Functionality
Benefits
Workspaces
Allow teams to map functional responsibilities to individual workspaces & link workspaces via APIs.
Teams work in-parallel, leverage other workspaces, errors contained to workspace.
Team Management
Administrative management to create separate 'orgs'. Create multiple teams and assign permissions.
Multi-tenancy for separate BU. Govern separate teams with correct permissions.
Private Module Registry
A central library of versioned & validated modules.
Pre-built sets of resources with best practices built-in; self-service model of infrastructure.
Configuration Designer
GUI-based workflow to select, combine, define variables and create unique workspaces.
Developers build configuration from pre-defined IaC modules; provision on-demand.
Remote Operations
Execute commands for TFE directly from the CLI (plan/apply).
Allows developers to use preferred workflows while still having the benefits of TFE.
Governance and Policy Capabilities
Feature
Functionality
Benefits
Sentinel
Create policies as code and enforce them on every provisioning run.
Eliminate provisioned resources that don't follow security, compliance, or operational policies.
SAML
SAML for SSO provides the ability to govern user access to your applications.
Determine read and write controls for all users; easily automate and manage user control in TFE GUI.
Audit
Track historical changes across all providers.
Associate changes with correlated workspaces to know who is doing what to the infrastructure. Reduce cloud costs (
$10B is overpaid annually
) by understanding what can be de-provisioned.
Terraform Cloud Workflow Diagram
Terraform Architecture and Components through diagram

Each workspace in TFC will be linked to a single VCS in PG&E Enterprise GitHub. The TFC workspace will be linked to a single AWS account. A GitHub repository can have several branches as the working branches in TFC. --- Page: Sentinel Policies ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=122689200
Labels: tfc
Chunk 1/1
---

What is Sentinel?
Sentinel
is an embedded policy-as-code framework integrated with the HashiCorp Enterprise products. It enables fine-grained, logic-based policy decisions, and can be extended to use information from external sources. Additional information and how to develop Sentinel policies are available at:
https://www.terraform.io/cloud-docs/sentinel
PGE Sentinel repository -
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-sentinel-policies
Sentinel  Policy and PolicySets
Policies
are written using the Sentinel
language
. Policies are the guardrails that prevent Terraform runs from performing dangerous actions.
Policy sets
are groups of policies that can be enforced on workspaces. A policy set can be enforced on designated workspaces, or to all workspaces in the organization. After the plan stage of a Terraform run, Terraform Cloud checks every Sentinel policy that should be enforced on the run's workspace.
Terraform
development
team
has implemented automation framework to automatically attach policy sets to work spaces when they are created in PGE terraform cloud. Policy sets include various level of enforcement for each of the policies based on the environment for which the workspace is configured.
Sentinel provides three enforcement modes:
work spaces targeting AWS
dev
environment, the policy enforcement level is set to
advisory
, this will never interrupt the run, and instead will only surface policy failures as informational to the user
work spaces targeting AWS
qa and test
environments, the policy enforcement level is set to
soft-mandatory
, this will halt the run, but allows any user with the Manage Policy Overrides permission to override policy failures on a case-by-case basis
work spaces targeting AWS
prod
environment, the policy enforcement level is set to
hard-mandatory
this requires that the policy passes. If a policy fails, the run is halted and may not be applied until the failure is resolved
Typical policy sets
Sentinel Policy Development Analysis

--- Page: Terraform Cloud Design Architecture ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=112822961
Labels: tfc
Chunk 1/1
---

Terraform Cloud HLA
Terraform Cloud is a SaaS product created by HashiCorp. PG&E has opted to use Terraform Cloud as our IaC tool.
TFC sits between GitHub.com and AWS. The only way to deploy infrastructure to AWS using TFC at PG&E, is to update code in a connected GitHub repository.
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/users-teams-organizations/users#api-tokens
Terraform Cloud Workspace Design
Workspaces in TFC are created via IaC found in this repository:
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tfc-workspaces
.
Users can submit a PR in this repository to add/update workspaces in TFC.
Terraform Cloud Provisioning to AWS
Each AWS account will need an IAM User (SVC) with access key shared with TFC as a variable set.
Variable sets is what TFC uses for AWS account specific details including access key and secret needed for provisioning.
Variable sets can be shared with one or many workspaces in TFC.
This diagram illustrates how the variable sets for workspaces are created and updated.
IaC for varset can be found here:
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tfc-varset
TFC Agents
deployed in Shared Services Prod (686137062481):
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tf-agents
Infrastructure is deployed via TFC with these workspaces:
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/workspaces/tfc-agent-compute
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/workspaces/tfc-agent-pool
Workspaces were separated, so we can provision the compute separate from the pool. Compute from other accounts can be added to the single pool.
Agent pool is assigned to each workspace as the execution mode.
Stacksets for IAM User and Role deployed to Shared Services Prod (686137062481):
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-landing-zone/blob/master/pge-templates/01-all-accounts/35-terraform-user-dev.yaml
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-landing-zone/blob/master/pge-templates/01-all-accounts/35-terraform-user-prod.yaml
The stacksets are environment specific.

--- Page: Terraform Cloud Run Tasks ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=228036684
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Terraform Cloud Run Tasks allow integrating third-party services into the Terraform run workflow. Services can be triggered at different stages to perform actions like:
Validating Terraform configuration
Scanning execution plans
Checking for security issues
Custom integrations
How Run Tasks Work
Created at the organization level and associated with specific workspaces
Triggered at pre-plan, post-plan, and pre-apply stages
Payload sent to external service with info about the run
External service processes data and returns pass/fail status
Terraform Cloud uses status to determine if run can proceed
Creating a Run Task
Provide name, endpoint URL, description, HMAC key
Make available for workspaces in the organization
Adding to a Workspace
Choose what stage to trigger run task
Set enforcement level:
Advisory - warnings only
Mandatory - can block runs
Viewing Results
Status and messages displayed in Terraform Cloud UI
Most restrictive enforcement level applied if multiple tasks

Ensuring accountability and cost control across various internal teams or departments can be challenging. This is where implementing a Terraform Cloud chargeback mechanism for internal partners can make a significant difference.
Implementation and execution steps.
Manual execution - Local
Copy the script to local directory.
export python.
Execute the following steps.
B3KS@LTGO658171 scripts % python tfworkspacecount.py -h
usage: tfworkspacecount.py [-h] [-o ORG] [-t TOK] [-u URL] [-q QUERY] [--pgsize PG_SIZE]
[--date-start DT_START] [--date-end DT_END] [--cert-file CERT_FILE]
[--type OUTPUT_TYPE] [--file-name FILE_NAME]
optional arguments:
-h, --help            show this help message and exit
-o ORG, --org ORG     organization name
-t TOK, --tok TOK     API token of the terraform server or set TF_TOKEN in environment
-u URL, --url URL     url of the TFE or TFC server
-q QUERY, --query QUERY
query a specific workspace.
--pgsize PG_SIZE      page size for api requests, default:100
--date-start DT_START
start date in YYYY-MM-DD format or by default retrieves last month data.
--date-end DT_END     end date in YYYY-MM-DD format or by default retrieves last month data
--cert-file CERT_FILE
path of the .pem file
--type OUTPUT_TYPE    type of output file format: csv/excel/none
--file-name FILE_NAME
name of the output file
B3KS@LTGO658171 scripts %
Export the below variables.
export TFC_TOKEN=XXXXXXX " you can get it from TFC settings from your profile"
export REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE="${HOME}/.aws/pge-aws-cert-bundle.pem"
Run the script.
B3KS@LTGO658171 scripts % python tfworkspacecount.py
enter month in MM-YYYY format or press enter for default last month data.
04-2024
enter output format: csv/excel/none.
excel
enter the name of the output file.
Type a workspace name to filter or press enter for no filters.
enter cert file path.
/Users/B3KS/.aws/pge-aws-cert-bundle.pem
Result saved in current directory.
Automation
: Lambda execution to send result to desired email.
Clone the scripts from terraform modules directory.
Execution steps
Master lambda: tfc_ws_name_id : generates ws ids and names and triggers 4 lambdas async way.
Lambda 1: tfc_applies_count_1 : gets ws applies count and stores data into s3.
Lambda 2: ws_accounts_varsets : gets ws account ids from varsets url and stores data to s3.
Lambda3: ws_acccounts : gets ws account ids from variables url and stores data to s3
Lambda 4: ws_applies_accounts : gets data from s3(generated from above 3 lambdas) and merge the data and sends mail
How to test:
Step 1: open
tfc_ws_name_id
(master lambda)
Step 2: click on test and edit the event json
To get last month's data by default, leave start and end empty as below.
{
"start": "",
"end": ""
}
To get customized dates data, enter start and end dates in yyyy-mm-dd format
{
"start": “2024-02-01,
"end": “202”4-02-29
}
Step 3: save event payload and test.
Step 4: Result will receive it via email.

--- Page: Prisma cloud policies ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=259752814
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Prisma policies are a crucial aspect of
Prisma Cloud Application Security
. They allow you to define rules and checks for your cloud resources, ensuring security and compliance.
These policies can be expressed using
YAML-based
definition files, which provide a structured way to specify attributes, connections, and other conditions.
When it comes to creating
custom policies
for
Prisma
using
Checkov
, you have a couple of options:
YAML Custom Policies
:
Prisma Cloud’s custom policies in code utilize the
Terraform attribute library and syntax
.
These policies are checked during scans of both build-time and runtime resources for all supported cloud providers.
An example of a YAML-based policy definition:
# Example custom policy for AWS resources
metadata:
id: "CKV2_CUSTOM_1"
name: "Ensure bucket has versioning and owner tag"
category: "BACKUP_AND_RECOVERY"
guideline: "[^1^][1]"
definition:
# Attribute Block: Ensure automated snapshot retention period is not zero
- cond_type: "attribute"
resource_types:
- "aws_redshift_cluster"
attribute: "automated_snapshot_retention_period"
operator: "not_equals"
value: "0"
In this example:
The
metadata
section provides information about the policy, including its name, ID, category, and guidelines.
The
definition
section specifies the policy rules.
We check that Redshift clusters have a non-zero
automated_snapshot_retention_period
.
What is checkov?
Checkov
is a
static code analysis tool
that scans for security vulnerabilities. Originally developed by
Bridgecrew.io
, it is now owned by
Prisma Cloud
. With Checkov, you can easily find vulnerabilities before deploying your infrastructure code.
It’s particularly useful for ensuring the security of your
infrastructure as code (IaC)
templates, container images, and pipeline configurations
1
. Here are some key features and use cases:
Policy-as-Code Scanning
:
Checkov allows you to express security policies using
YAML-based definitions
.
You can create custom policies to validate cloud resources against these rules.
Supported platforms include
Terraform
,
CloudFormation
,
Kubernetes
,
Helm
,
ARM Templates
, and the
Serverless framework
2
.
Resource Verification
:
Checkov verifies changes to hundreds of supported resource types across major cloud providers.
It ensures that attributes and connections adhere to your defined policies.
Graph-Based Policies
:
Policies can be attribute-based or graph-based.
Attribute-based policies scan cloud resources for misconfigured attributes.
Graph-based policies analyze connections between resources.
Integration and Extensibility
:
Embed Checkov into your existing developer workflows.
Integrate it with custom support for platforms, build processes, and release systems.
How to install checkov?
Installation
:
To install Checkov, follow these steps based on your system:
Using Pip (Python package manager):
37
incomplete
Ensure you have the latest version of Python installed.
38
incomplete
Open your terminal and run
pip install checkov
Using Homebrew (for macOS):
39
incomplete
In your Mac terminal, run:
brew install checkov
Usage:
Once installed, you’re ready to use the Checkov command-line interface (CLI).
Here are some common commands:
Scan a Folder:
40
incomplete
To scan an entire folder containing your infrastructure code (e.g., Terraform files), use:
checkov --directory /path/to/your/iac/code
Scan a Specific File:
41
incomplete
To scan a specific file (e.g., a Terraform file), use:
checkov --file /path/to/your/tf/
example.tf
Scan Multiple Specific Files:
42
incomplete
If you have multiple files, you can scan them together:
checkov -f /path/to/cloudformation/example1.yml -f /path/to/cloudformation/example2.yml
Scan a Terraform Plan in JSON:
43
incomplete
Generate a Terraform plan file (e.g., tf.plan).
44
incomplete
Convert it to JSON format
terraform init
terraform plan -out tf.plan
terraform show -json tf.plan > tf.json
47
incomplete
Run Checkov on the JSON file:
checkov -f tf.json

--- Page: Terraform Development Peer Code Review (PR) ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=319727571
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Critical Role of Peer Code Review
Peer code review is a crucial practice in software development where developers examine and critique each other's code. Here are several reasons why this process is essential:
Improves Code Quality
: Peer reviews help identify bugs, logical errors, and potential security vulnerabilities early in the development process, leading to higher quality and more reliable code.
Enhances Learning and Knowledge Sharing
: Developers learn new techniques, best practices, and different approaches to problem-solving by reviewing their peers' code, fostering a culture of continuous learning and knowledge sharing within the team.
Ensures Consistency and Standards Compliance
: Code reviews ensure that the codebase adheres to the team's coding standards and guidelines, making the code easier to read, maintain, and extend.
Promotes Collaboration and Team Cohesion
: Regular code reviews encourage collaboration and open communication among team members, leading to a more cohesive and supportive team environment.
Reduces Technical Debt
: By catching issues early, peer reviews help prevent the accumulation of technical debt, resulting in fewer problems down the line and a more maintainable codebase.
Increases Accountability
: Knowing that their code will be reviewed by peers encourages developers to write cleaner, more efficient code, leading to better overall code quality.
Facilitates Onboarding
: New team members can get up to speed more quickly by participating in code reviews, gaining insights into the codebase and the team's development practices, which helps them integrate more smoothly.
Importance of CCOE Terraform Team Reviewing Pull Requests
The Cloud Center of Excellence (CCOE) Terraform (TF) team plays a vital role in maintaining the integrity and quality of the infrastructure codebase. Here are several reasons why it is essential for the CCOE TF team to review pull requests:
Ownership and Enforcement
: As the owners of the source code repositories that create the workspaces and modules, the CCOE TF team is responsible for enforcing coding standards, best practices, and security guidelines. Their review ensures that all contributions align with these standards.
Visibility and Accountability
: Reviewing pull requests provides the CCOE TF team with visibility into what is being committed to the codebase. This transparency helps in maintaining accountability and ensures that all changes are thoroughly vetted.
Preventing Potential Failures
: By reviewing pull requests, the CCOE TF team can identify and address potential issues before they become problems in production. This proactive approach helps in mitigating risks and ensuring the stability and reliability of the infrastructure.
Consistency and Quality Assurance
: The CCOE TF team's review process ensures that all code changes are consistent with the overall architecture and design principles. This consistency is crucial for maintaining a high-quality, maintainable codebase.
Knowledge Sharing and Collaboration
: The review process facilitates knowledge sharing and collaboration within the team. It allows team members to learn from each other's work and contribute to continuous improvement.
Terraform Cloud Orchestration
Terraform Cloud Orchestration automates the provisioning and management of cloud infrastructure. Key aspects of the process include:
Architected and Designed Implementation
: The implementation of Terraform Cloud (TFC) Open ID Connect (OIDC) has eliminated the dependency on Terraform Agents, transitioning TFC orchestration to a fully SaaS model. This enhances security and streamlines authentication processes by integrating OIDC, ensuring robust and efficient access management.
Orchestration V2
: The new version introduces functionalities such as user-specified workspace names, support for multiple AD groups for read and apply, and options to choose Terraform versions. It also includes features like projects, file triggers, trigger patterns, ephemeral (auto destroy), drift detection, and controlling speculative plans, improving the flexibility and usability of the orchestration system.
Migration Strategy
: Over 800 TFC workspaces were migrated from Orchestration V1 to V2, ensuring minimal downtime and disruption to operations. This involved coordinating with multiple teams to ensure a smooth transition and promptly addressing any issues that arose.
Peer Code Review Process for creating workspaces / orchestration
Enhanced Security and Reliability
: Peer code reviews have identified potential security vulnerabilities and logical errors in the Terraform Cloud Orchestration codebase, leading to more secure and reliable orchestration processes.
Knowledge Sharing and Collaboration
: The peer review process has facilitated the sharing of best practices and innovative solutions among team members, leading to continuous improvement in the orchestration process.
Consistency and Standards Compliance
: Regular code reviews have ensured that the Terraform Cloud Orchestration code adheres to the team's coding standards and guidelines, resulting in a more maintainable and consistent codebase.
Execute
orchestration.py
: As part of peer code review process, developers are required to execute
orchestration.py
python script. This program is designed to validate YAML files and generate corresponding JSON files. It performs a series of checks and validations to ensure that the YAML files are correctly formatted, free of errors, and adhere to the required schema. This helps to catch potential issues early in the development process, which helps maintain better code quality and reduces the likelihood of errors in production.
Reasons how it aids in better code quality
Schema Validation
: The program validates the schema of the YAML files using the schema library. This ensures that the files adhere to the expected structure and contain all the necessary fields.
Duplicate Key Checks
: It checks for duplicate keys in the YAML files, preventing conflicts and ensuring that each key is unique.
Environment and Account Verification
: The program verifies that AWS account numbers match the environment specified in the sentinel account map file. This helps ensure that the correct accounts are used in the appropriate environments.
GitHub Branch and Access Validation
: It performs validation checks for GitHub branches and access, ensuring that the specified branches exist and that the CCoE-Pipeline user has the necessary permissions.
JSON Generation
: The program generates JSON files based on the validated YAML files and saves them in specified locations. This helps in maintaining a consistent and standardized format for the data.
Reasons why it is necessary as part of peer code review process
Early Detection of Issues
: By running this program as part of the peer code review process, you can catch potential issues early, before they become bigger problems. This helps in maintaining a high standard of code quality.
Consistency and Standardization
: The program ensures that the YAML files are consistently formatted and adhere to the required schema. This makes it easier for reviewers to understand and review the code.
Automated Validation
: The program automates the validation process, reducing the manual effort required for code reviews. This allows reviewers to focus on more critical aspects of the code.
Improved Collaboration
: By ensuring that the YAML files are correctly formatted and free of errors, the program facilitates better collaboration among team members. Reviewers can trust that the files have been validated and can focus on providing valuable feedback.
In summary, it is crucial for maintaining
better code quality
and ensuring a smooth and efficient peer code review process. It helps catch potential issues early, ensures consistency and standardization, and improves collaboration among team members.
Terraform Module Development Process
The development and enhancement of Terraform modules are crucial for maintaining a robust and efficient infrastructure. Highlights of the process include:
Terraform Test Command
: The usage of the Terraform Test Command has streamlined the testing process for Terraform modules, reducing the typical testing time from three days to one day. This has improved the reliability and efficiency of module testing, enabling faster deployment cycles and reducing time-to-market.
Module Updates
: The entire EKS module was re-architected to enable direct usage from TFC, removing the dependency on Terraform Agents. Major design changes were implemented with internal pipelines to update add-ons, making the system more future-ready.
Version Upgrades
: Over 50 Terraform modules were upgraded to use the latest AWS provider, ensuring compatibility and leveraging new features. All modules were released to minor versions from earlier patch versions, standardizing versioning and improving maintainability.
Specific benefits from Peer Code Review Process in Terraform Module Development
Improved Module Quality
: Peer code reviews have helped identify bugs and inefficiencies in Terraform modules early in the development process, leading to higher quality and more reliable modules.
Accelerated Development and Deployment
: The collaborative nature of peer reviews has enabled faster identification and resolution of issues, reducing the overall development and deployment time for Terraform modules.
Enhanced Learning and Skill Development
: By participating in code reviews, developers have gained valuable insights into different approaches and techniques, fostering continuous learning and skill development within the team.
Detailed Terraform module development guidelines and process is described
here
, expand the section below to read more

--- Page: Terraform Usage Dashboards ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=323291354
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Introduction
In today's data-driven world, having a clear and comprehensive view of infrastructure usage is crucial for effective management and decision-making. Terraform Usage Dashboards provide a powerful way to visualize and analyze the usage of workspaces and modules within your Terraform Cloud environment. By leveraging PowerBI, these dashboards offer an intuitive and interactive platform for data visualization.
Why We Need Dashboards
Dashboards are essential for several reasons:
Centralized Monitoring
: Dashboards consolidate data from various sources into a single, unified view, making it easier to monitor and manage your infrastructure.
Data-Driven Decisions
: Visualizing data helps in identifying trends, patterns, and anomalies, enabling informed decision-making.
Resource Optimization
: By analyzing usage data, you can optimize resource allocation, reduce costs, and improve efficiency.
Accountability and Reporting
: Dashboards provide a transparent view of infrastructure usage, facilitating accountability and simplifying reporting processes.
PowerBI as the Visualization Platform
PowerBI
is chosen as the visualization platform for the following reasons:
User-Friendly Interface
: PowerBI offers an intuitive and interactive interface, making it easy for users to create and customize dashboards.
Integration Capabilities
: PowerBI seamlessly integrates with various data sources, including JSON files generated by our backend scripts.
Advanced Analytics
: PowerBI provides advanced analytics features, such as data modeling, custom visuals, and AI-driven insights.
Collaboration and Sharing
: PowerBI allows for easy sharing and collaboration, enabling teams to work together on data analysis and reporting.
Backend Scripts
To generate the data required for the dashboards, I have developed two Python scripts:
workspaces-accounts.py
and
explore-modules.py
. These scripts fetch workspace and module usage information from Terraform Cloud and save it in JSON format, which can then be imported into PowerBI for visualization. JIRA user story:
CLOUDCOE-12384
Source code
GitHub repository
:
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-terraform-modules/pull/1591
Files
(
PR #1591
)
scripts/dashboard/workspaces-accounts.py
scripts/dashboard/explore-modules.py
workspaces-accounts.py
Description:
This script generates a JSON file containing workspace information from Terraform Cloud. The generated JSON file can be used with PowerBI for data visualization and analysis.
Functionality:
Command-line Arguments:
--pgsize
: Specifies the page size for API requests (default: 100).
--pgbegin
: Specifies the beginning page for API requests (default: 1).
--pgend
: Specifies the ending page for API requests (default: all).
--outfile
: Specifies the output file to save the results (default:
tfcworkspaces.json
).
--accmap
: Specifies the account map file to map account numbers to account names (default:
accountmap.json
).
AWS account mapping information to generate the account name from the account numbers derived, passed as JSON file, similar to the snippet shown below
Data Fetching:
Fetches workspace information from the Terraform Cloud API.
Includes details such as workspace name, account, account name (if available), resource count, repository, branch, and working directory.
Handles pagination and fetches all pages of workspace data as specified by the command-line arguments.
Output:
Saves the fetched workspace information in a JSON file specified by the
--outfile
argument.
Error Handling:
Handles errors gracefully and provides meaningful error messages.
explore-modules.py
Description:
This script generates a JSON file containing module usage information from Terraform Cloud. The generated JSON file can be used with PowerBI for data visualization and analysis.
Functionality:
Command-line Arguments:
--pgsize
: Specifies the page size for API requests (default: 100).
--pgbegin
: Specifies the beginning page for API requests (default: 1).
--pgend
: Specifies the ending page for API requests (default: all).
--outfile
: Specifies the output file to save the results (default:
tfcmodules.json
).
--wsmap
: Specifies the workspace to account number to account name map file (default:
wsmap.json
).
Data Fetching:
Fetches module usage information from the Terraform Cloud API.
Includes details such as module name, submodule (if available), version, workspace count, and list of workspaces.
Handles pagination and fetches all pages of module data as specified by the command-line arguments.
Output:
Saves the fetched module information in a JSON file specified by the
--outfile
argument.
Provides an option to flatten the data and save it in a CSV file.
Error Handling:
Handles errors gracefully and provides meaningful error messages.
References
Terraform Cloud API Documentation - List Workspaces
Terraform Cloud API Documentation - Explorer (Modules)
Dashboards and its data sources
Existing dashboards
TFC workspaces accounts
TFC modules usage
Data sources
- JSON files generated recently are uploaded to the SharePoint location
terraform_data
tfcworkspaces.json
tfcmodules_flat.json
Conclusion
By using these scripts to
generate JSON files
and importing them into
PowerBI
, one can create powerful and interactive dashboards that provide valuable insights to our Terraform Cloud usage. These dashboards will help monitor, analyze, and optimize infrastructure, leading to better resource management and data-driven decision-making.

--- Page: Using AWS Identity Center with Terraform CLI Locally ---

Labels: none

This page assumes you've already onboarded to AWS Identity Center (shortened often to IDC, and is the service formerly know as AWS Single Sign-on (SSO))
This guide (as of this latest update) assumes the modules have not been updated to work with both TFC and local TF CLI testing. So this guide goes over the necessary updates to get the module examples to work with both deployment methods.
1. Identity Center Setup
Open your
~/.aws/config
file, and you'll need 3 entries that should look similar the following:
2. Provider Configuration
The provider block should include logic to determine whether to use the Identity Center profile (for local runs) or default credentials (for Terraform Cloud).
The critical parts here are the profile argument, and the
local.is_tfc
variable.
local.is_tfc
should be defined in a
locals {}
block shown below.
For the
profile
argument, the value in quotes (shown below as "750") will be the name of your profile you defined above that you are using to deploy your infrastructure into (above that would be the 750 profile).
Given the above config file setup and guidelines, your provider block should look like this:
If the module you are using has no route53 resources, and you aren't updating the module to permanently work both locally with IDC and with TF Cloud, then you can simply delete the
assume_role
block, and put it back when you're done testing.
4. Example: s3web_react Module
Refer to the
s3web example in the pge-terraform-modules
repository for the single example that has been updated with this pattern.

--- Page: Terraform on Terraform ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=354856177
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Terraform on Terraform: Automating TFC Management
Use
Terraform
to manage
Terraform Cloud (TFC)
resources programmatically.
Goal: Automate lifecycle of
Projects
,
Teams
, and
Workspaces
.
Two-Layer Design
Foundational Layer
Creates
Projects
for grouping workspaces.
Creates
Teams
linked to AD groups for SSO.
Assigns teams to projects with high-level permissions.
Workspace Layer
Manages individual workspaces and permissions.
Dynamic Workspace Setup
Workspace definitions from external data files.
Loop-driven creation of hundreds of workspaces via reusable template.
Module configures VCS repo, Terraform version, auto-apply settings.
Granular Access Control
Separate module assigns AD groups to
read-only
and
apply
roles per workspace.
Workflow
Define workspace requirements in simple format.
Orchestration converts to Terraform-ready data.
Run
Foundational Layer
→ Projects & Teams.
Run
Workspace Layer
→ Workspaces & Permissions.

--- Page: Terraform Cloud Orchestration V2 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=240553792
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview:
Section 1: Adding your new workspace to the required files
Section 2: Validating your changes and generating required files
Section 3: Deleting a workspace
Troubleshooting
Section 1: Adding your new workspace to the required files
Request membership to the following GitHub team:
https://github.com/orgs/pgetech/teams/pge-tfc-workspace-developers
Clone the repository:
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tfc-workspaces
Create a local branch in the format
<your-lan-id>/<jira-user-story>
(e.g:
b1v6/CLOUDCOE-5414
)
Add a new workspace block to any of the
workspaces-aws/wsv2-*/*.yaml files.
The following is a code snippet showing what it would look like with all mandatory fields:
Below is a table showing all available settings for your workspace, including the name, its default value, whether its mandatory, and whether there are additional limitations on what it can be.
Key Name
Type
Default
Mandatory
Valid values and limits
Description
Links
account
string
-
The AWS account number
appid
string
-
Your APP-ID as listed in AMPS
https://amps.cloud.pge.com/portal
apply_ad_groups
list[string]
-
The Active Directory/MEA groups that should be able to run "terraform apply" on this workspace.
read_only_ad_groups
list[string]
-
The Active Directory/MEA groups that should be able to view the workspace.
branch
string
-
The branch in your GitHub repo your workspace should monitor.
environment
string
-
dev, test, qa, prod
The environment level of your workspace/repo.
github_repo
string
-
The name of your GitHub repo.
auto_apply
boolean
false
Whether your workspace will automatically run an apply after the plan.
auto_destroy
int
null
1 < x <= 2160
How long your repo should wait after the last activity in it before destroying all resources, in hours. Do not include this field if you do not want auto destruction of resources.
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/workspaces/settings/deletion#automatically-destroy
drift_detection
boolean
false
env_vars
dict{k,v}
{}
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/workspaces/variables
file_triggers_enabled
boolean
true
File triggers allow you to queue runs in HCP Terraform when files in your VCS repository change.
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/workspaces/settings/vcs#automatic-run-triggering
github_org
boolean
"pgetech"
The GitHub organization your repo is in. In nearly all case this will be the default value.
parallelism
int | string
10
1 < x <= 10
You should rarely need to change this.
https://support.hashicorp.com/hc/en-us/articles/10348130482451-Considerations-when-setting-the-TFE-PARALLELISM-environment-variable
project
string
"TFCv2_Default"
The project you want your workspace to belong to. Consider grouping related workspaces.
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/projects
run_tasks
list[string]
[]
Used to integrated third-party tools and services at certain stages in the HCP Terraform run lifecycle. Wiz scanners are always included.
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/workspaces/settings/run-tasks
speculative_enabled
boolean
false
Test changes to a configuration during editing and code review. Speculative plans are plan-only runs.
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/run/remote-operations#speculative-plans
structured_run_output_enabled
boolean
true
https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cloud-docs/workspaces/settings#user-interface
tags
list[string]
[]
Tags to apply to your workspace.  Limitation: Tags can't be updated after initial workspace creation.
terraform_version
string
"1.6.2"
The default Terraform version for your workspace to use.
tf_vars
dict
{}
trigger_patterns
list[string]
[]
List of
glob patterns
that describe the files Terraform Cloud monitors for changes
https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/tfe/0.53.0/docs/data-sources/workspace#trigger_patterns-1
trigger_prefixes
list[string]
[]
List of trigger prefixes that describe the paths Terraform Cloud monitors for changes, in addition to the working directory.
https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/tfe/0.53.0/docs/data-sources/workspace#trigger_prefixes-1
var_file
string
""
working_directory
string
""
The directory your workspace monitors for changes and executes in.
https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/tfe/0.53.0/docs/data-sources/workspace#working_directory-1
If you are member of only one AD group, use your AD group in
apply_ad_groups
and set the
read_only_ad_groups
as
dummy.
Additional Notes:
A branch with the name matching your target environment should be used (i.e. if target environment is "dev", branch should be called "dev")
If no
working_directory
is specified, the root of your repo will be used. Ref:
Terraform Cloud - Working Directories
Ensure the repo and branch specified exist in the remote GitHub repo, and is not just a local repo and branch.
Section 1 summary:
Became a member of the pge-tfc-workspace-developers team.
Cloned the pge-tfc-workspaces repository to your local machine.
Added a block of YAML code that declaratively defines your new workspace.
Move on to section 2 to validate your updates, and generate the required JSON files.
Section 2: Validate your updates and generate JSON file(s)
Now you need to run a script that validates your new workspace code. It ensures all the required fields are there, with valid values, that your repo and branch exist, as well as other things. This script is referred to as
orchestration.py
and is located in the
scripts
folder of the
pge-tfc-workspaces
repository.
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tfc-workspaces/blob/main/scripts/orchestration.py
Prerequisites:
You must have a personal GitHub PAT created with appropriate permissions and have it set to the $GH_TOKEN environment variable in your shell. Ref:
CCOE Wiki: Creating a Github PAT
You must have added the
CCoE-Pipeline
GitHub user to your repository with
Admin
privileges. This can be done in your repository settings.
Open your terminal, and navigate to the root of your local
pge-tfc-workspaces
repository.
Run one of the following (option
a
is preferred if you are only adding a single workspace, option
b
will validate every single workspace in the entire file, leading to a much longer output):
./scripts/orchestration.py
-
w <directory_name>
-f <filename_without_extension> -n <workspace_name>
./scripts/orchestration.py
-
w <directory_name>
-f <filename_without_extension> -a
Windows users only:
You may need to prepend the above command with "python":
python
./scripts/orchestration.py
-
w <directory_name>
-f <filename_without_extension> -n <workspace_name>
PowerShell users only:
To set your GitHub PAT as the appropriately named environment variable:
$Env:GH_TOKEN='ghp_XXX'
After a few seconds, you'll see output similar to the following, assuming a successful run (troubleshooting steps at the end if needed):
Commit your files: If you now run a
git status
you'll see your YAML file you edited, as well as 1 or more JSON files that were automatically edited by this script. Commit all these files and push to the remote repo.
Create a PR: Ensure you follow the rules specified in
prlint.json
. Failure to follow that may result in delayed workspace creation.
Make a post in
Terraform PR Requests
requesting review of your PR.
Include a clickable, working hyperlink to your PR. Not just a plain-text URL.
Wait for all the PR checks to complete before requesting review of your PR. Also ensure that no resources are being destroyed (unless you're intentionally removing something)
Once someone has reviewed, approved, and merged your PR, it can take a few minutes to create the workspace in Terraform Cloud. If the workspace has not been provisioned after 1hr, please contact the CCOE TF team or the person who approved your PR for assistance.
Once the workspace shows up in
Terraform Cloud
, you may start using your new workspace.
Section 3: Deleting your workspace
Workspace deletion follows the same flow, except you are deleting the workspace block you added, and passing a different flag to
orchestration.py
:
Prerequisite
: All resources must be deleted from your workspace prior to requesting workspace deletion.
Delete your workspace block
Run:
./scripts/orchestration.py -w <directory_name> -f <filename>
--
remove
You should see output similar to:
your-workspace namespace has been removed
Now run the validation for the file:
./scripts/orchestration.py
-
w <directory_name>
-f <filename_without_extension> -a
Follow the rest of the steps from above.
Troubleshooting:
This will update your PR with the latest changes.
