Congratulations on choosing to contribute to PG&E Terraform Modules. The source code repository for the terraform modules is
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-terraform-modules
. The first step is to become member of the github team
https://github.com/orgs/pgetech/teams/pge-tf-module-contributors
so you are able to clone, create your branch and submit a pull request to merge to the repository.
The repository has multiple directories at the root, this document will describe the relevance of each of the directory (at the time of writing this wiki page).
A simple module has the following structure, with just the module related terraform files and examples associated for unit testing the module
An elaborate module with sub-module structure, multiple examples etc can be seen below
Terraform modules can be tested in multiple ways
a. once examples are created, they can be tested using the same.
b. with recent hashicorp "tests" functionality which helps in automating the testing on TFC, see below screenshot for structure lay out. tests scripts should be under /aws/modules/<module>/tests.
if any submodule that is a supported infrastructure and can't be used on it's own must be placed under "internal" section within modules ex. /aws/modules/<module>/modules/internal. Refer to screenshot below.
New module development:
Create module.info, begin with version 0.0.1 as shown below,
File header -  you could add file header, look at any existing module for file header structure
From the root of the repository, execute
terraform fmt --recursive
Execute
terraform init
,
terraform validate
and
tflint
Change your directory to the examples folder and execute the commands, as shown below
Generate tests config files , as shown below
NOTE: Terraform version of 1.8 and above can only run the terraform test command.
Running configurations generated under/tests, remember this will create and also destroy the provisioned resources.
Execute
terraform plan
and
terraform apply
, make sure they are both successful
Validate the resources are created in the AWS account configured in the
tfvars
file
Execute
terraform destroy
Execute
terraform state list
, make sure it is empty - all resources are deleted
Now remove
tfstate
,
tfstate.backup
files.
Again from the root of the repository run
terraform fmt -recursive
Now generate the
README
files executing the script from the module root
note:
terraform-doc
is a prerequisite ensure you have installed it using your package manager of choice. (example:
https://brew.sh/
brew install terraform-docs )
Now delete all the artifacts, make sure you have removed the .terraform files from the module directory, sub-directories, examples etc. and run scripts to generate readme
Once successfully completing the above steps,
commit
and
push
the changes to your branch
After pushing your changes, a pull request (
PR
) can be generated
Updating an existing module
Exactly the same as new module development, in addition after updating,
increment module.info
version
Guidelines check list for TCS development team

--- Page: Terraform Modules Best Practices Guide ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=354849878
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Module Structure
Standard Directory Layout
terraform-module-name/
├── README.md
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── versions.tf
├── examples/
│   ├── basic/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   └── complete/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       └── outputs.tf
├── modules/
│   └── submodule/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       └── outputs.tf
└── tests/
├── integration/
└── unit/
File Organization
main.tf: Primary resource definitions
variables.tf: All input variable declarations
outputs.tf: All output value declarations
versions.tf: Terraform and provider version constraints
README.md: Comprehensive module documentation
examples/: Working examples demonstrating module usage
modules/: Nested/child modules (if needed)
tests/: Automated tests for the module
Naming Conventions
Module Names
Use lowercase with hyphens:
terraform-aws-vpc
or
terraform-azure-aks
Include provider in name:
terraform-<PROVIDER>-<NAME>
Be descriptive but concise:
terraform-aws-s3-bucket
not
terraform-aws-s3
Resource Names
Use underscores for resource names:
aws_instance.web_server
Use singular nouns:
aws_s3_bucket.backup
not
aws_s3_bucket.backups
Prefix with type when helpful:
var.vpc_cidr_block
Variable Names
Use lowercase with underscores:
instance_type
,
vpc_cidr_block
Be explicit:
enable_encryption
not
encryption
Group related variables:
vpc_cidr_block
,
vpc_name
,
vpc_tags
Input Variables
Variable Definition Best Practices
variable "environment" {
description = "Environment name (e.g., dev, staging, prod)"
type        = string
validation {
condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
}
}
variable "instance_type" {
description = "EC2 instance type for the application servers"
type        = string
default     = "t3.micro"
}
variable "tags" {
description = "Common tags to apply to all resources"
type        = map(string)
default     = {}
}
variable "enable_monitoring" {
description = "Enable detailed CloudWatch monitoring"
type        = bool
default     = false
}
variable "subnet_ids" {
description = "List of subnet IDs for resource deployment"
type        = list(string)
validation {
condition     = length(var.subnet_ids) > 0
error_message = "At least one subnet ID must be provided."
}
}
Variable Guidelines
Always provide descriptions: Clear, helpful descriptions for every variable
Use appropriate types: Leverage
string
,
number
,
bool
,
list()
,
map()
,
object()
,
set()
Set sensible defaults: Where appropriate, provide safe default values
Add validations: Use validation blocks to enforce constraints
Avoid nullable: Explicitly set
nullable = false
when a value is required
Document constraints: Include units, formats, or ranges in descriptions
Output Values
Output Best Practices
output "vpc_id" {
description = "The ID of the VPC"
value       = aws_vpc.main.id
}
output "private_subnet_ids" {
description = "List of private subnet IDs"
value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}
output "security_group_id" {
description = "Security group ID for application resources"
value       = aws_security_group.app.id
}
output "connection_string" {
description = "Database connection string"
value       = "postgresql://${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}"
sensitive   = true
}
Output Guidelines
Output useful values: IDs, ARNs, endpoints that consumers need
Use descriptions: Explain what each output represents
Mark sensitive data: Use
sensitive = true
for secrets/credentials
Use consistent naming: Match resource attribute names when possible
Output child module values: Expose important outputs from nested modules
Documentation
README.md Example in Markdown to be Included in Code Repo
# Module Name
Brief description of what this module does.
## Features
- Feature 1
- Feature 2
- Feature 3
## Usage
```hcl
module "example" {
source = "git::https://github.com/org/terraform-module.git?ref=v1.0.0"
environment     = "prod"
instance_type   = "t3.medium"
enable_monitoring = true
}
```
## Requirements
| Name | Version |
|------|---------|
| terraform | >= 1.5.0 |
| aws | >= 5.0 |
## Providers
| Name | Version |
|------|---------|
| aws | >= 5.0 |
## Inputs
| Name | Description | Type | Default | Required |
|------|-------------|------|---------|:--------:|
| environment | Environment name | `string` | n/a | yes |
| instance_type | EC2 instance type | `string` | `"t3.micro"` | no |
## Outputs
| Name | Description |
|------|-------------|
| vpc_id | The ID of the VPC |
## Examples
See the [examples](./examples) directory for working examples.
## Contributing
Guidelines for contributing to this module.
### Documentation Guidelines
1. Keep README current: Update with every change
2. Use terraform-docs: Automate documentation generation
3. Provide examples: Include at least basic and complete examples
4. Explain architecture: Describe what resources are created
5. Document assumptions: Note any prerequisites or dependencies
## Versioning
### Semantic Versioning
Follow [SemVer](https://semver.org/) principles:
- MAJOR: Incompatible API changes
- MINOR: Backward-compatible functionality additions
- PATCH: Backward-compatible bug fixes
### Version Constraints
```hcl
# versions.tf
terraform {
required_version = ">= 1.5.0"
required_providers {
aws = {
source  = "hashicorp/aws"
version = ">= 5.0, < 6.0"
}
}
}
Versioning Guidelines
Pin provider versions: Use version constraints for providers
Set minimum Terraform version: Specify required_version
Tag releases: Use Git tags for version releases
Maintain changelog: Document changes between versions
Test upgrades: Verify compatibility with provider updates
Testing
Testing Strategy
# tests/unit/example_test.go
package test
import (
"testing"
"github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/terraform"
"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
)
func TestBasicExample(t *testing.T) {
terraformOptions := &terraform.Options{
TerraformDir: "../../examples/basic",
}
defer terraform.Destroy(t, terraformOptions)
terraform.InitAndApply(t, terraformOptions)
vpcId := terraform.Output(t, terraformOptions, "vpc_id")
assert.NotEmpty(t, vpcId)
}
Testing Guidelines
Write automated tests: Use Terratest, Terraform test, or similar
Test examples: Ensure all examples work correctly
Validate inputs: Test variable validation rules
Test error cases: Verify proper error handling
Use temporary resources: Clean up after tests
Run in CI/CD: Automate testing in pipelines
Security Considerations
Security Best Practices
# Good: Use IAM roles instead of access keys
resource "aws_iam_role" "app" {
name = "${var.environment}-app-role"
assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
Version = "2012-10-17"
Statement = [{
Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
Effect = "Allow"
Principal = {
Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com"
}
}]
})
}
# Good: Enable encryption by default
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
bucket = "${var.environment}-data-bucket"
}
resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "data" {
bucket = aws_s3_bucket.data.id
rule {
apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
sse_algorithm = "AES256"
}
}
}
# Good: Restrict network access
resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
name        = "${var.environment}-app-sg"
description = "Security group for application"
vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id
ingress {
description = "HTTPS from VPC"
from_port   = 443
to_port     = 443
protocol    = "tcp"
cidr_blocks = [aws_vpc.main.cidr_block]
}
}
Security Guidelines
Never hardcode secrets: Use variables, AWS Secrets Manager, or Vault
Enable encryption: Default to encryption at rest and in transit
Apply least privilege: Minimal IAM permissions required
Use private networks: Default to private subnets where possible
Enable logging: CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, etc.
Scan for vulnerabilities: Use tfsec, checkov, or terrascan
Mark sensitive outputs: Prevent accidental exposure
Module Composition
Composability Principles
# Good: Focused, single-purpose module
module "vpc" {
source = "./modules/vpc"
cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr_block
environment = var.environment
}
module "app" {
source = "./modules/app"
vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id
subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids
environment = var.environment
}
Composition Guidelines
Single responsibility: Each module should do one thing well
Avoid deep nesting: Limit module nesting to 2-3 levels
Pass outputs explicitly: Don't create implicit dependencies
Make modules reusable: Avoid environment-specific logic
Use child modules: Break complex modules into submodules
Examples and Usage
Provide Multiple Examples
Basic Example (
examples/basic/main.tf
):
module "basic" {
source = "../.."
environment = "dev"
}
Complete Example (
examples/complete/main.tf
):
module "complete" {
source = "../.."
environment       = "prod"
instance_type     = "t3.large"
enable_monitoring = true
tags = {
Team        = "platform"
CostCenter  = "engineering"
ManagedBy   = "terraform"
}
}
Example Guidelines
Keep examples simple: Easy to understand and run
Make examples runnable: Should work with
terraform apply
Show common patterns: Demonstrate typical use cases
Document prerequisites: Note required setup or resources
Include outputs: Show useful information after apply
Additional Best Practices
General Guidelines
Use data sources: Fetch existing resource information
Implement conditionals carefully: Use
count
and
for_each
wisely
Avoid state manipulation: Don't use
terraform state
commands in modules
Use locals: Simplify complex expressions
Format consistently: Run
terraform fmt
before committing
Validate regularly: Run
terraform validate
in CI/CD
Keep it DRY: Don't repeat yourself, use modules and loops
Plan for upgrades: Make modules backward-compatible when possible
Code Style
# Good: Clear, well-formatted code
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
instance_type = var.instance_type
subnet_id     = var.subnet_id
tags = merge(
var.common_tags,
{
Name = "${var.environment}-web-server"
}
)
lifecycle {
create_before_destroy = true
}
}
# Good: Use locals for complex logic
locals {
common_tags = {
Environment = var.environment
ManagedBy   = "terraform"
Module      = "web-server"
}
instance_name = "${var.environment}-${var.application}-${var.component}"
}
Checklist for Module Release
Before releasing a new module or version:
[ ] All variables have descriptions
[ ] All outputs have descriptions
[ ] Validation rules added where appropriate
[ ] README.md is complete and accurate
[ ] Examples are provided and tested
[ ] Tests are written and passing
[ ] Security scan completed (tfsec/checkov)
[ ] Version constraints are set
[ ] CHANGELOG.md is updated
[ ] Git tag is created for the version

Terraform Module Registry
Terraform Best Practices
Terraform Style Guide
Semantic Versioning

Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides fully managed shared storage built on Windows Server, and delivers a wide range of data access, data management, and administrative capabilities. Publishing Release notes
By default, when you merge a PR in the "pge-terraform-modules" repo, a CodeBuild process is initiated that will publish the release notes that you included in your PR (webhook post to the Terraform Announcements channel in MS Teams).
If there is a syntactical error or logic error in your release notes, it will publish an error to that channel. These errors may look like:
Incomplete or missing release notes
More than 1 module detected as having been updated
A mismatch between the module listed and the module actually updated
An issue with the module version
If your published notes say "A Terraform Module PR was created with an issue" then you incorrectly added release notes. To fix:
Go to GitHub and open your merged PR
Update the release notes section to correct the issue
Save the updated PR body
Proceed to the next section
Republishing Release Notes
If you want to republish release notes to MS Teams, you can find the script to do this in the "scripts/release-notes-pipeline/lambda" folder in the "pge-terraform-modules" repo. The lambda ("parse_release_notes.py") contains a "main" function that you can run from the CLI to publish the notes.
Using the --help output, we can see that if we want to republish the notes for PR 1234, we would run:
We can also see that we can list multiple PRs
Ignore the --test flag, as that is used for testing the script during development and updates.

S3web delivers internal and external static websites ( i.e. HTML, React, Angular etc) leveraging AWS S3 capabilities.
S3web facilitates the user to enter their desired website url and the application code repository information. It not only setups the website but also creates the SAF2.0 compliant code pipeline to deploy the application leveraging AWS services.

Default value - (secret name:key)
Wiki
1
SonarQube Token
secretsmanager_sonar_token
sonar:token
SonarQube Token
2
JFFROG Artifactory Username
secretsmanager_artifactory_user
jfrog:user
3
JFFROG Artifactory Token
secretsmanager_artifactory_token
jfrog:token
4
GitHub PAT Token
secretsmanager_github_token
required (typically github:token)
First Deployment:
Ensure you have completed the
OnBoarding Terraform Cloud
steps and have the above Prerequisites completed.
Using either the quick start code below or from the S3web module example, fill out all the required variables and arguments.
Commit and push your .tf file(s) to your GitHub repo that is connected to your Terraform workspace.
View the status of your current run in Terraform Cloud
here
.
Due to a bug in the AWS provider v4.67.0 that results in tags not being applied to the git webhook resource on your first S3Web Terraform apply,
you need to run a second
apply
without making any changes
. This will ensure those tags get applied. Failure to do this may result in unexpected outcomes.
If you data classification is "Public" or "Internal", it is no longer required to use a KMS key for your resources, though you still may do so. For instructions on how to use a KMS key, please
click here
.
Here is the PGE Terraform S3web module:
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/registry/modules/private/pgetech/s3web/aws
WAF related information - see
PG&E Outbound PAT Pools
Here is the reference code for S3web creation (you can also reference the
example
in the S3web module folder):
Test accounts used for testing S3web module using
- version 0.0.25
Account # - 477027688362 - SecureByDesign Test
Account # - 384861874741 - Shared Services V2 Prod
Account # - 750713712981 - Non Prod V2 Dev
Regarding Logging:
The S3web module uses 3 logging buckets for the resources it deploys:
S3 access logs
WAF logs
CloudFront logs
All PGE AWS accounts are created with the following buckets specifically for these logs:
ccoe-s3-accesslogs-spoke-[aws-region]-[aws-account-num]
pge-[aws-account-num]-waflogs-[aws-region]
pge-[aws-account-num]-cloudfront
While you may specify your own buckets for the logs, it should be noted that the CloudFront logs must go to the above CloudFront logging bucket. Also, WAF logs
must be
delivered to a destination (Kinesis Firehose, S3 bucket, etc) with the prefix
aws-waf-logs-
.

S3Web Exception

To make sure that accounts map file in pge-sentinel-policies repository is updated with new accounts as soon as it is created in the AWS Organizations service.
Steps
:
Create a Git token with commit and push
Use a Git token
n AWS Lambda
to fetch
repository
and commit
Everyday AWS Cloudwatch Event rule will trigger Lambda to scan organization to see if any new accounts are added/deleted
And commit to Github only if it detects a change in the file
After detecting the change a PR is automatically raised by the Lambda Github API itself for approval
Workflow

1.Dynamic data is required
2.Developed
Control:
Ensure that a CloudFront CDN exists with the AWS Amplify application domain as the origin and it passes along the Authorization header.
Ensure that AWS Amplify applications are configured with basic auth.
PG&E Documentation:
AWS Config Rule detection:
cscoe-config-amplify-enforce-cdn-compliance
cscoe-config-amplify-has-basic-auth-compliance
Context:
AWS Amplify does not allow for configuring traffic to come through a WAF. To enforce traffic through the WAF, we can use CloudFront as a CDN with the Amplify application as the origin, and set a basic auth requirement for the default Amplify domain. This does have a caveat that if the default Amplify domain is discovered, a user from the public internet could see the prompt for the password at that URL.
https://github.com/aws-amplify/amplify-console/issues/36
(Web Application Firewall Integration issue for Amplify)
CloudFront WAF compliance is handled by CloudFront compliance rules.
AWS Amplify does not support VPC endpoints at this time. Calls to the Amplify API from resources hosting Amplify-consuming applications will require internet access.
Since PG&E VPCs are configured to use PAN firewalls and F5, this control is partially handled by that service.

All HR disable activity is processed within 24 hours, and actually closer to real time. Once the terminated user's access is disabled and AD security groups removed, they no longer have access to AWS.

part of:

AWS Amplify uses Amazon Cognito as its main authentication provider.  When authentication is added to an amplify application, a Cognito User or Identity pool is created on the back-end for the application to use for auth.

AppStream 2.0 supports persistent application settings for Windows-based stacks
. This means that your users' application customizations and Windows settings are automatically saved after each streaming session and applied during the next session.
Amazon AppStream 2.0 now provides users with persistent storage, backed by
Amazon S3
. Users can access a home folder on their streaming instance, and save content in this folder for use between streaming sessions. Users can also download and upload files in the home folder directly from a web browser, when connected to a streaming session. All files are stored in an S3 bucket, which is automatically created in your AWS account.
To provide persistent storage for your users, you will need to enable the home folders feature for each AppStream 2.0 stack that you create via the AWS Management Console or the AWS SDK. When you enable home folders for the first time in an AWS region, AppStream 2.0 will create an Amazon S3 bucket in your account (one per AWS Region), which is used to store all home folders files associated with your AppStream 2.0 stacks. Home folders are created for each user the first time they start a streaming session using a stack where home folders has been enabled. Users have secure access to their home folder across all the AppStream 2.0 stacks they can access in an AWS region. To enable home folders, stacks must use fleets running images that were built from an AWS published image created on or after May 18th. To learn more about enabling persistent storage for your AppStream 2.0 users, see
Persistent Storage with Home Folders
.

Setting Up SAML - Amazon AppStream 2.0
To enable users to sign in to AppStream 2.0 by using their existing credentials, and start streaming applications, you can set up identity federation using SAML 2.0. To do this, use an IAM role and a relay state URL to configure your SAML 2.0-compliant identity provider (IdP) and enable AWS to permit your federated users to access an AppStream 2.0 stack. The IAM role grants users the permissions to access the stack. The relay state is the stack portal to which users are forwarded after successful authentication by AWS.

When you set up AppStream 2.0, you must specify the virtual private cloud (VPC) and at least one subnet in which to launch your fleet instances and image builders.
When you configure your VPC for AppStream 2.0, you can specify either public or private subnets, or a mix of both types of subnets. A public subnet has direct access to the internet through an internet gateway. A private subnet, which doesn't have a route to an internet gateway, requires a Network Address Translation (NAT) gateway or NAT instance to provide access to the internet.

AWS WAF can be used to protect AppSync API from common web exploits, such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks. These could affect API availability and performance, compromise security, or consume excessive resources. For example, you can create rules to allow or block requests from specified IP address ranges, requests from CIDR blocks, requests that originate from a specific country or region, requests that contain malicious SQL code, or requests that contain malicious script.
Since PG&E uses the Silverline SaaS WAF offering, AWS WAF's primary function in this configuration would be to ensure the source IP of requests bound to AppSync is that of Silverline.  Silverline's IP Set which should be configured for WAF is 107.162.0.0/16.

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Context:
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling conforms to the AWS shared responsibility model, which includes regulations and guidelines for data protection. AWS is responsible for protecting the global infrastructure that runs all of the AWS services. AWS maintains control over data hosted on this infrastructure, including the security configuration controls for handling customer content and personal data. AWS customers and APN Partners, acting either as data controllers or data processors, are responsible for any personal data that they put in the AWS Cloud.
Additional Notes:
Control to ensure the proper configuration of EBS Volumes is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
2
Control is implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Context:
You can configure your Auto Scaling group to encrypt Amazon EBS volume data stored in the cloud with AWS Key Management Service customer master keys (CMK). Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling supports AWS managed and customer managed CMKs to encrypt your data. Note that the KmsKeyId option to specify a customer managed CMK is not available when you use a launch configuration. Use a launch template instead.
Additional Notes:
Control to ensure the proper configuration of EBS Volumes is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Controls to ensure a CMK is used in Auto Scaling launch configurations are covered in item 8, below.

While TLS for the Auto Scaling API is enforced, the version of TLS cannot be configured.
TLS to the to auto-scaling group is dependent on the configuration of the individual EC2 instances.

S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
covered in item#2
Context:
The way to configure encryption differs depending on the resource type. Certain resource types support the ability to encrypt your backups using a separate encryption key from the key used to encrypt the source resource. This capability adds another layer of protection for your backups.
The controls for Encryption at Rest for Backup-managed services are including in the following SAF Documents:
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic File System - EFS
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS EC2
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS DynamoDB
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Storage Gateway - File Gateway
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Lustre
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Windows
Additional Notes:
Currently only EFS supports independent encryption via AWS Backup Vaults
2
CLOUDCOE-4752
ensure-aws-backup-vaults-using-customer-managed-kms-key
Developed
Controls:
Ensure AWS Backup Vaults use PG&E-managed CMKs
AWS Config Detection Rule:
cscoe-config-backup-vault-customer-cmk-compliance
Context:
The AWS KMS encryption master key is used to protect your backups in this backup vault. By default, AWS Backup creates a master key with the alias
aws/backup
for you. You can choose that key or choose any other key in your account.
SAF Controls for Encryption Keys for services whose backups are managed by AWS are defined in the following documents:
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic File System - EFS
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS EC2
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS DynamoDB
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Storage Gateway - File Gateway
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Lustre
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS FSx - Windows
Additional Notes:
In AWS Backup, a
backup vault
is a container that you organize your backups in. You can use backup vaults to set the AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) encryption key that is used to encrypt backups in the backup vault and to control access to the backups in the backup vault. If you require different encryption keys or access policies for different groups of backups, you can optionally create multiple backup vaults. Otherwise, you can have all your backups organized in the default backup vault.

AWS Backup is integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service in AWS Backup. CloudTrail captures all API calls for AWS Backup as events. The calls captured include calls from the AWS Backup console and code calls to the AWS Backup API operations. If you create a trail, you can enable continuous delivery of CloudTrail events to an Amazon S3 bucket, including events for AWS Backup. If you don't configure a trail, you can still view the most recent events in the CloudTrail console in
Event history
. Using the information collected by CloudTrail, you can determine the request that was made to AWS Backup, the IP address from which the request was made, who made the request, when it was made, and additional details.

When you request a public certificate, AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) generates a public/private key pair. For imported certificates, you generate the key pair. The public key becomes part of the certificate. ACM stores the certificate and its corresponding private key, and uses AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) to help protect the private key.
An AWS managed key is used to encrypt the data. This is a non-configurable option.

Context:
AWS managed keys are used to encrypt data for AWS Certificate Manager. AWS managed keys are automatically rotated every 3 years by AWS.
Prisma detection policies:
AWS ACM Certificate has auto-renewal enabled but is not renewing successfully
AWS ACM Imported Certificate is not rotating once a year
AWS ACM imported certificate does not have the correct lifespan
AWS ACM Certificate is either Expired or is no longer a valid certificate
6
Compliance covered by Cloud Trail
Context:
To guard against SSL/TLS certificates that are issued by mistake or by a compromised CA, some browsers require that public certificates issued for your domain be recorded in a certificate transparency log. The domain name is recorded. The private key is not. Certificates that are not logged typically generate an error in the browser.
Before the Amazon CA issues a publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificate for your domain, it submits the certificate to at least two certificate transparency log servers. These servers add the certificate to their public databases and return a signed certificate timestamp (SCT) to the Amazon CA. The CA then embeds the SCT in the certificate, signs the certificate, and issues it to you. The timestamps are included with other X.509 extensions.
AWS Certificate Manager is integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service in ACM. CloudTrail is enabled by default on your AWS account. CloudTrail captures API calls for ACM as events, including calls from the ACM console and code calls to the ACM API operations.

Context:
Build artifacts, such as a cache, logs, exported raw test report data files, and build results, are encrypted by default using customer master keys (CMKs) for Amazon S3 that are managed by the AWS Key Management Service. If you do not want to use these CMKs, you must create and configure a customer-managed CMK.
You can store the identifier of the AWS KMS key that CodeBuild uses to encrypt the build output artifact in the CODEBUILD_KMS_KEY_ID environment variable.
The Amazon Elastic Block Store volumes of your build fleet are encrypted by default using CMKs managed by AWS.

Context:
Typically, AWS CodeBuild cannot access resources in a VPC. To enable access, you must provide additional VPC-specific configuration information in your CodeBuild project configuration. This includes the VPC ID, the VPC subnet IDs, and the VPC security group IDs.
You can improve the security of your builds by configuring AWS CodeBuild to use an interface VPC endpoint. Interface endpoints are powered by PrivateLink, a technology that you can use to privately access Amazon EC2 and CodeBuild by using private IP addresses. PrivateLink restricts all network traffic between your managed instances, CodeBuild, and Amazon EC2 to the Amazon network. (Managed instances don't have access to the internet.)

You must specify privilegedMode with a value of true only if you plan to use this build project to build Docker images, and the build environment image you specified is not provided by CodeBuild with Docker support. Otherwise, all associated builds that attempt to interact with the Docker daemon fail. You must also start the Docker daemon so that your builds can interact with it. One way to do this is to initialize the Docker daemon in the install phase of your buildspec file by running the following build commands. Do not run these commands if you specified a build environment image provided by CodeBuild with Docker support.

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: Virtual Private Cloud
.
Context:
AWS CodePipeline supports VPC endpoints which allows you to privately connect to the service from the VPC without requiring access to the internet. The VPC endpoint must be attached to at least 1 security group which controls layer 3 access to the endpoint. Best practice is not allow overly permissive openings on security groups. Since specific IP ranges aren’t known until the workloads are deployed, a control should check for any security group rules allowing the 0.0.0.0/0 address space.

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: Virtual Private Cloud

Ensure all pipelines that are using GitHub as a source repository provider have an OAuth token or a customer managed personal access token.
Context:
CodePipeline uses GitHub OAuth tokens and personal access tokens to access your GitHub repositories and retrieve the latest changes. There are two ways to configure authentication in GitHub:
AWS creates a default AWS managed OAuth token when you use the console to create or update pipelines.
You can create and manage your own customer-generated personal access tokens. You need personal access tokens when you use the CLI, SDK, or AWS CloudFormation to create or update your pipeline

SSL/TLS can be enforced on all DMS endpoints by the DMS service except for Amazon Redshift target endpoints. AWS DMS uses S3 buckets to transfer data to Redshift. This transmission is encrypted by the Amazon Redshift service.

Ensure there is a subscription on all log groups starting with "/aws/docdb" to send logs to a central location
AWS Config Rule Detection:
cscoe-config-docdb-cluster-audit-logs-compliance
cscoe-config-docdb-cluster-profiler-logs-compliance
cscoe-config-docdb-centralized-logs-compliance
Context:
When auditing is enabled, Amazon DocumentDB records Data Definition Language (DDL), authentication, authorization, and user management events to Amazon CloudWatch Logs. When auditing is enabled, Amazon DocumentDB exports your cluster’s auditing records (JSON documents) to Amazon CloudWatch Logs. You can use Amazon CloudWatch Logs to analyze, monitor, and archive your Amazon DocumentDB auditing events.
A new log group will be automatically created for the DocumentDB cluster under the following prefix, in which
cluster-name
represents the DB cluster name, and
log_type
represents the log type:
/aws/docdb/
cluster-name
/
log_type

ensure_documentdb_retention_backup_period_is_atleast_15_days
Development
Controls:
Ensure automated backups are enabled on the Amazon DocumentDB Cluster with the retention period of at least 15 days.
AWS Config Rule Detection:
cscoe-config-docdb-cluster-backup-retention-compliance
Context:
Amazon DocumentDB backs up your cluster volume automatically and retains restore data for the length of the
backup retention period.
DocumentDB backups are continuous and incremental so you can quickly restore to any point within the backup retention period. You can specify a backup retention period, from 1 to 35 days, when you create or modify a DB cluster.

S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
Context:
Amazon EBS encryption is an encryption solution for your EBS volumes and snapshots. It uses AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) customer master keys (CMK).
The data on NVMe instance store volumes is encrypted using an XTS-AES-256 cipher implemented on a hardware module on the instance. The encryption keys are generated using the hardware module and are unique to each NVMe instance storage device. All encryption keys are destroyed when the instance is stopped or terminated and cannot be recovered. You cannot disable this encryption and you cannot provide your own encryption key.
Additional Information:
This control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
2
control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
Context:

Additional Information:
This control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
3
Shared(Not Applicable)
Shared responsibility
-
LOB is responsible for ensuring applications that are hosted on EC2 instances have encryption in transit throughout the application using TLS v1.2 or greater. Encryption in transit to and from an EC2 instance can be managed by AWS if the following requirements are met:
The instances use the following instance types: C5a, C5ad, C5n, G4, I3en, M5dn, M5n, P3dn, R5dn, and R5n.
The instances are in the same Region.
The instances are in the same VPC or peered VPCs, and the traffic does not pass through a virtual network device, such as a load balancer or a transit gateway.

In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution, PingFederate, which enforces multi-factor authentication where applicable. The EC2 services does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.
Direct access to the EC2 instance is not supported and all access must use SSM Session Manager which requires AWS IAM access.
5
managed by the

Amazon EC2 integrates with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) for server side encryption. Rotation of the customer managed key used for encryption will be handled by the Key Management Service (KMS).
Additional Notes:

6

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail

Amazon EC2 and Amazon EBS are integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or an AWS service in Amazon EC2 and Amazon EBS. CloudTrail captures all API calls for Amazon EC2 and Amazon EBS as events, including calls from the console and from code calls to the APIs.
Logging from within the EC2 instance is not covered by CloudTrail.
Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure CloudTrail is enabled and configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail

Developed

Context:
Per PG&E policy, user's should not be able to log in directly to EC2 instances and should use SSM Session Manager. Not having key pair defined removes ability for a user to directly RDP/SSH into that instance. Use SSM Session Manager if login to instance is required as it allows more fine-grained access controls.

The instances use the following instance types: C5a, C5ad, C5n, G4, I3en, M5dn, M5n, P3dn, R5dn, and R5n.
The instances are in the same Region.
The instances are in the same VPC or peered VPCs, and the traffic does not pass through a virtual network device, such as a load balancer or a transit gateway.

Developed
Controls:
Ensure Redis cluster is encrypted at rest
Context:
Ensure that your AWS ElastiCache Redis clusters are encrypted in order to meet security and compliance requirements (keep Personally Identifiable Information safe). Data encryption helps prevent unauthorized users from reading sensitive data available on your Redis clusters and their associated cache storage systems. This includes data saved to persistent media, known as data at-rest.
Prisma Cloud Detection Policy:
AWS ElastiCache Redis cluster not encrypted at rest

By providing in-transit encryption capability, ElastiCache gives you a tool you can use to help protect your data when it is moving from one location to another. For example, you might move data from a primary node to a read replica node within a replication group, or between your replication group and your application

Controls:
Ensure automated backups have a retention policy of at least 15 days
Context:
For any Redis cluster, you can enable automatic backups. When automatic backups are enabled, ElastiCache creates a backup of the cluster on a daily basis. Automatic backups can help guard against data loss. In the event of a failure, you can create a new cluster, restoring your data from the most recent backup. The result is a warm-started cluster, preloaded with your data and ready for use.
Prisma Cloud Detection Policy:
AWS ElastiCache Redis cluster retention policy less than 15 days

Ensure that your Redis clusters have the Auto Minor Version Upgrade feature enabled in order to receive automatically minor engine upgrades. The automatic upgrades are applied to the Redis cluster during system maintenance window, defined by the day of the week, the time of day, and the time zone (UTC by default). Each minor version upgrade is fully available only after it is approved by Amazon Web Services.

CLOUDCOE-3782
ensure-at-rest-and-transit-encryption-enabled-for-redis
Developed
Controls:
Ensure Redis Cluster has encryption in-transit enabled (OOTB Prisma: AWS ElastiCache Redis cluster with in-transit encryption disabled)
Context:
To help keep your data secure, Amazon ElastiCache and Amazon EC2 provide mechanisms to guard against unauthorized access of your data on the server. By providing in-transit encryption capability, ElastiCache gives you a tool you can use to help protect your data when it is moving from one location to another. For example, you might move data from a primary node to a read replica node within a replication group, or between your replication group and your application.
In-transit encryption is optional and can only be enabled on Redis replication groups when they are created. You enable in-transit encryption on a replication group by setting the parameter TransitEncryptionEnabled to true (CLI: --transit-encryption-enabled) when you create the replication group. You can do this whether you are creating the replication group using the AWS Management Console, the AWS CLI, or the ElastiCache API. If you enable in-transit encryption, you must also provide a value for CacheSubnetGroup.
Prisma Cloud Detection Policy:
AWS ElastiCache Redis cluster with in-transit encryption not enabled

chunks.content:

References:
ECR JIRA Epic:
CLOUDCOE-4192
Sentinel Policy Implementation Notes:
S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
Not applicable
Context:
AWS automatically encrypts at rest using Amazon S3 server-side encryption (ECR is backed by S3)
Amazon ECR stores images in Amazon S3 buckets that Amazon ECR manages. By default, Amazon ECR uses server-side encryption with Amazon S3-managed encryption keys which encrypts your data at rest using an AES-256 encryption algorithm. This does not require any action on your part and is offered at no additional charge.
2
CLOUDCOE-4193
ensure-ecr-repository-is-encrypted-with-cmek
Developed
Controls:
Ensure ECR repository is encrypted using a customer managed KMS key
Context:
For more control over the encryption for your Amazon ECR repositories, you can use server-side encryption with customer master keys (CMKs) stored in AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS). When you use AWS KMS to encrypt your data, you can either use the default AWS-managed CMK, which is managed by Amazon ECR, or specify your own CMK (referred to as a customer-managed CMK).
Note: S3 encryption is managed by AWS for ECR
AWS Config Detection Rule:
cscoe-config-ecr-not-using-cmk-compliance
3
CLOUDCOE-4523
ensure-ecr-transfers-container-images-over-https
Developed
Context:
Amazon ECR transfers container images over HTTPS.
You use AWS published API calls to access Amazon ECR through the network. Clients must support Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.0 or later. We recommend TLS 1.2 or later.
Additional Notes:
This controls ensures TLS communication but  the version of TLS used cannot be configured.
4
Not applicable
Not Applicable
- User access to AWS services is being granted by IAM policies attached to IAM roles. In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution, PingFederate, which enforces multi-factor authentication where applicable. The ECR services does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.
5
Control will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
Amazon ECR integrates with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) for server side encryption. Rotation of the customer managed key used for encryption will be handled by the Key Management Service (KMS).
Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure the Key Management Service (KMS) is configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Key Management Service
6
Control will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail
.
Context:
Amazon ECR is integrated with AWS CloudTrail, a service that provides a record of actions taken by a user, a role, or an AWS service in Amazon ECR. CloudTrail captures the following Amazon ECR actions as events:
All API calls, including calls from the Amazon ECR console
All actions taken due to the encryption settings on your repositories
All actions taken due to lifecycle policy rules, including both successful and unsuccessful actions
Additional Notes:
The controls to ensure CloudTrail is enabled and configured correctly will be managed by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS CloudTrail
.
7
Not applicable
Shared responsibility
- External to the service; requirements to be defined by Business. If detect controls are needed to ensure resources are picked up by the backup plan, contact the cybersecurity team.
Amazon ECR lifecycle policies enable you to specify the lifecycle management of images in a repository. A lifecycle policy is a set of one or more rules, where each rule defines an action for Amazon ECR. The actions apply to images that contain tags prefixed with the given strings. This allows the automation of cleaning up unused images, for example expiring images based on age or count. You should expect that after creating a lifecycle policy the affected images are expired within 24 hours.
See:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECR/latest/userguide/LifecyclePolicies.html
https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/clean-up-your-container-images-with-amazon-ecr-lifecycle-policies/
Regarding backups, implement cross-regional backups for critical images based on the needs of the business
For Future Consideration:
Implement policy that checks for a cross-regional backup tag (need to define that tag name) - If tag is found, confirm that a recent backup has been made. This is the same as noted in the
SAF 2.0 S3 controls
.
8

Developed
Controls:

Context:
Identify any exposed Amazon ECR image repositories available within your AWS account and update their permissions in order to protect against unauthorized access. Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR) is a managed Docker registry service that makes it easy for DevOps teams to store, manage and deploy Docker container images.
See
ECR IAM and VPC Controls
for full details on the controls to implement for VPC interface endpoints and IAM.
Prisma detection policy:
AWS ECR repository is exposed to public

Context:
AWS ECR supports VPC endpoints which allows you to privately connect to the service from the VPC without requiring access to the internet. Since PG&E VPCs are configured to use PAN firewalls and F5, this control is handled by those service and an additional service level control is not-applicable.

Cross account access to ECR repos will only be allowed only from accounts in the same environment (i.e. Dev to Dev, QA to QA, Prod to Prod). Each AWS account that needs access must be listed in the Principals section of the ECR Repository Policy.
"Allow"

Context:
You can improve the security posture of your VPC by configuring Amazon ECR to use an interface VPC endpoint. VPC endpoints are powered by AWS PrivateLink, a technology that enables you to privately access Amazon ECR APIs through private IP addresses. AWS PrivateLink restricts all network traffic between your VPC and Amazon ECR to the Amazon network. You don't need an internet gateway, a NAT device, or a virtual private gateway.
Additional Notes:
Compliance of the this control will be covered by Item #22-Restrict traffic between systems to permit only as designed and required.

Context:
You can improve the security posture of your VPC by configuring Amazon ECR to use an interface VPC endpoint. VPC endpoints are powered by AWS PrivateLink, a technology that enables you to privately access Amazon ECR APIs through private IP addresses. AWS PrivateLink restricts all network traffic between your VPC and Amazon ECR to the Amazon network. You don't need an internet gateway, a NAT device, or a virtual private gateway.
com.amazonaws.
region
.ecr.dkr
This endpoint is used for the Docker Registry APIs. Docker client commands such as push and pull use this endpoint. When you create the com.amazonaws.region.ecr.dkr endpoint, you must enable a private DNS hostname. To do this, ensure that the Enable Private DNS Name option is selected in the VPC console when you create the VPC endpoint.
com.amazonaws.
region
.ecr.api
This endpoint is used for calls to the Amazon ECR API. API actions such as DescribeImages and CreateRepositories go to this endpoint.
The specified
region
represents the Region identifier for an AWS Region supported by Amazon ECR, such as us-east-2 for the US East (Ohio) Region.
23
Control will be implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: Virtual Private Cloud
.
Context:
AWS ECR supports VPC endpoints which allows you to privately connect to the service from the VPC without requiring access to the internet. The VPC endpoint must be attached to at least 1 security group which controls layer 3 access to the endpoint. Best practice is not allow overly permissive openings on security groups. Since specific IP ranges aren’t known until the workloads are deployed, a control should check for any security group rules allowing the 0.0.0.0/0 address space.
Additional Notes:
This control is being implemented as part of the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: Virtual Private Cloud
.

Since specific IP ranges aren’t known until the workloads are deployed, a control should check for any security group rules allowing the 0.0.0.0/0 address space.

Service Adoption Framework 2.0: Virtual Private Cloud
.

ECR encrypts data in transit between the service and other integrated AWS services. All data that passes between ECR and integrated services is encrypted using Transport layer Security (TLS).

Not applicable
Context:
User access to AWS services is being granted by IAM policies attached to IAM roles. In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution. Multi-factor authentication is also enforced where applicable by the SSO solution. Identities are being populated by Active Directory. Access for a user can be terminated by removing that user from the security group granting access or by disabling the AD user. This process is currently configured outside of AWS and there is no service level control needed
.

Not applicable
Not Applicable
- User access to AWS services is being granted by IAM policies attached to IAM roles. In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution. Authentication and authorization are handled via PingFederate and Active Directory. No service level controls are needed.
See
Accessing AWS Accounts via IAM Federation and AssumeRole
28
Not applicable
Shared responsibility
-
Alerting thresholds should be defined by the business and will vary based on the usage pattern of AWS ECR.
Context:
Application teams can collect CloudWatch usage metrics and inspect the number of authorization, image push, and image pull calls, for the purpose of preventing outages due to reaching service quotas. From the
ECR monitoring documentation
: "
Amazon ECR metric data is automatically sent to CloudWatch in one-minute periods". This is stored for two weeks. In order to implement a monitoring approach, application teams would need to first establish a baseline of normal usage, and store that baseline data for reference. Then alarms can be created based on the usage and/or service quotas, or limits increased as needed.
29
Not applicable
Shared responsibility
-  IAM policies are used to assign permissions to IAM users, groups, and roles. It is up to the business to decide the minimum required permission needed.

References:

Sentinel Policy Implementation Notes:
S.No
Jira Story
Policy Name
Status
Details / Notes
1
Met
Context:
For Amazon ECS on Fargate tasks using platform version 1.4.0 or later, each task receives 20 GB of ephemeral storage. The amount of storage is not adjustable.
For tasks using platform version 1.4.0 or later that are launched on May 28, 2020 or later, the ephemeral storage is encrypted with an AES-256 encryption algorithm using an AWS Fargate-managed encryption key.
AWS Elastic Container Registry will be used to store container images.
Additional Notes:
Controls to ensure encryption of container images at rest will be covered by
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Container Registry
For approved AWS storage options, see individual SAF2.0 criteria:
S3
,
EFS
,
EBS
Unapproved Options
These storage options are not approved as we have yet to implement detection:
Host-based persistence shared among containers
Host-based persistence per container
Distributed filesystems like NFS
Docker Volumes
Deferred
– For no choice situations such as COTS packages,
encrypt filesystems with
cryptsetup (dm-crypt)
and implement a
custom config rule
to detect.
2
Not Met/Not Supported
Context:
Persistent container storage EFS is supported by AWS and are governed by PGE SAF documents
EFS
For tasks using platform version 1.4.0 or later that are launched on May 28, 2020 or later, the ephemeral storage is encrypted with an AWS Owned Key using an AWS Fargate-managed encryption key. This is not configurable.  AWS has on the roadmap to support Customer Managed CMK, when this is available the document will update to reflect the new capability.
3

r.

The ECS services does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.

Amazon ECS service does not integrates with the AWS Key Management Service to manage customer master keys (CMKs) for server side encryption. Rotation of the AWS managed key used to encrypt ephemeral storage will be done automatically by AWS every 3 years.

Context:
Amazon ECS tasks using Fargate require the awsvpc network mode, which provides each task with an elastic network interface (ENI) and a primary private IP address. When you run a task or create a service with this network mode, you must specify one or more subnets to attach the network interface to and one or more security groups to apply to the network interface. Because each task gets its own ENI, you can also take advantage of other Amazon EC2 networking features like VPC Flow Logs so that you can monitor traffic to and from your tasks.
PG&E VPCs are configured to use PAN firewalls and F5 for network threat assessment.
A VPC endpoint should be deployed in each VPC to allow for communication to the ECS API without going over the internet.
Additional Notes:
Compliance of the this control will be covered by Item #22-Restrict traffic between systems to permit only as designed and required

CLOUDCOE-3372
ensure-efs-is-encrypted
Developed
Controls:
Ensure EFS file system is encrypted
Context:
Ensure that your Amazon EFS file systems are encrypted in order to meet security and compliance requirements. Your data is transparently encrypted while being written and transparently decrypted while being read from your file system, therefore the encryption process does not require any additional action from you or your application.
Prisma Policy:
AWS Elastic File System (EFS) with encryption for data at rest is disabled
2
CLOUDCOE-3375
ensure-efs-is-encrypted-using-customer-managed-keys
Developed
Controls:
Ensure EFS file systems is encrypted using PG&E Managed KMS keys
Context:
Ensure that your Amazon EFS file systems are encrypted using KMS CMK customer-managed keys instead of AWS managed-keys (default keys used by the EFS service when there are no customer keys defined) in order to have more granular control over your data-at-rest encryption/decryption process
Prisma Policy:
AWS Elastic File System (EFS) not encrypted using Customer Managed Key
3
CLOUDCOE-3373
ensure-to-block-efs-non-tls-traffic
Developed
Controls:
Ensure a EFS file system policy exists that blocks non-TLS traffic
Context:
This policy was created using the policy wizard that is part of the EFS console. You can use the wizard to add this policy to a particular EFS file system. The policy listed above should not be copied as it specific to a particular EFS file system used for development.
Enabling encryption of data in transit for your Amazon EFS file system is done by enabling Transport Layer Security (TLS) when you mount your file system using the Amazon EFS mount helper.
When encryption of data in transit is declared as a mount option for your Amazon EFS file system, the mount helper initializes a client stunnel process. Stunnel is an open source multipurpose network relay. The client stunnel process listens on a local port for inbound traffic, and the mount helper redirects Network File System (NFS) client traffic to this local port. The mount helper uses TLS version 1.2 to communicate with your file system.
AWS Config detection rule:
cscoe-config-efs-tls-compliance

The EFS services does not have any further controls needed to check for MFA.

taken care in item#2
Controls:
Ensure AWS KMS is used with envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets
Context:
Envelope encryption of Kubernetes secrets allows you to encrypt your secrets with a unique data encryption key (DEK). The DEK is then encrypted using a key encryption key (KEK) from AWS KMS which can be automatically rotated on a recurring schedule. With the KMS plugin for Kubernetes, all Kubernetes secrets are stored in etcd in ciphertext instead of plain text and can only be decrypted by the Kubernetes API server.
Prisma Cloud detection policies:
AWS EKS cluster is not using Secrets encryption with KMS
Additional Notes:
Controls to check for the encryption of the EBS volumes attached to EKS worker nodes will be covered by the
Service Adoption Framework 2.0: AWS Elastic Block Store
.
Controls for how to configure storageClasses in Kubernetes will be covered as part of the Kubernetes Platform SAF document.
Docker is designed to be stateless.

S3
,
EFS
,
EBS,
MySQL
,
Oracle
,
Postgres

Context:
Amazon EKS control plane logging provides audit and diagnostic logs directly from the Amazon EKS control plane to CloudWatch Logs in your account. These logs make it easy for you to secure and run your clusters. You can select the exact log types you need, and logs are sent as log streams to a group for each Amazon EKS cluster in CloudWatch.

Control plane log retention is a business decision.
Application logs (container level logs) are not captured as part of EKS logging.
Keeping K8s logs inside the cluster is a security threat in case of compromise. Security-sensitive logs should be transported outside the cluster to prevent tampering in the event of a breach.

Additional Notes:
Encryption in transit within the EKS platform will depend on the Container Network Interface (CNI) being used along with the service mesh being implemented.

Kubernetes container and service network endpoints are configured by default with a cluster-internal IP thus they are unreachable.

Practice defense-in-depth with SecurityGroups by permitting only TLS-based protocols.
By default, Kubernetes API Server exposes only TLS endpoint.
26
Not Applicable
Not Applicable
-
Access to AWS is granted by Active Directory security groups mapped to AWS IAM Roles. PG&E has an Identity Access Management program that automates the disablement and removal of Active Directory security groups. All HR disable activity is processed within 24 hours, and actually closer to real time. Once the terminated user's access is disabled and AD security groups removed, they no longer have access to AWS
27
Not Applicable
Not Applicable
-
User access to AWS services is being granted by IAM policies attached to IAM roles. In order to assume the role, a user must authenticate via PGE’s enterprise SSO solution. Authentication and authorization are handled via PingFederate and Active Directory. No service level controls are needed.
Additional Kubernetes Best Practices:
Ensure service account tokens are not automatically mounted to pods and must be specified
28
Not Applicable
Shared responsibility
-
Alerting thresholds should be defined by the business and will vary based on the usage pattern of the service
Additional Notes:
Monitoring of the Kubernetes platform will depend on the monitoring solution deployed (i.e. CloudWatch Container Insights, Prometheus, 3rd party tool, etc.)
Additional Kubernetes Best Practices:
Ensure all namespaces have a resource quota set
Ensure each pod has a resource request and resource limit set
29
Require dynamic data
Shared responsibility
-  IAM policies are used to assign permissions to IAM users, groups, and roles. It is up to the business to decide the minimum required permission needed.
Additional Notes:
Additional controls will be used to secure the Kubernetes platform.
Minimize administrative access to Kubernetes nodes.
Each image-family should own its execution role.
Each node-group should have a unique node-group role.
Each EKS-Cluster should have unique worker-node role.
Additional Kubernetes Best Practices:
Ensure only IAM roles are listed in the aws-auth ConfigMap, no users
Ensure ClusterRoles don't allow ["*"]
Ensure Roles don't allow ["*"]
Ensure each application is using a dedicated service account
Ensure pods are not running as root
Use IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA)
Ensure the IAM role trust policy for IRSA is scoped to the service account name
Ensure pod security policy disables the ability to escalate privilege

chunks.page_title: TFC Agents V1 workspace CVE / Instance Update
chunks.content:
--- Page: TFC Agents V1 workspace CVE / Instance Update ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=334368464
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Git Repo:
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tf-agents
Workspace:
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/workspaces/tfc-agent-compute
Prerequisite
Join Terraform Cloud organization "
pgetech
".
Terraform Cloud onboard
2. You need to be
added to a team
that has access to the workspace.
Teams are managed under
Settings > Teams
.
The team must have at least
"Write"
access to the workspace to run
apply
.
If you need access. Reach out internally to CCOE or post question to
terraform chat in teams
Overview
This document outlines the process for managing Terraform Cloud (TFC) agent EC2 instances that are dynamically updated with a new Amazon Machine Image (AMI) ID. The AMI ID is retrieved from AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Parameter Store, and the Auto Scaling Group (ASG) is updated accordingly. Upon AMI update, the associated TFC workspace is automatically re-run to reflect the changes.
Background.
MRAD is the current team that has not migrated their workspaces to V2. Currently the TFC agents are deployed in shared services prod which the team leverages to deploy IaC (Infrastructure as Code). The Agents are installed on Ec2 instances and leverage the Golden AMI via a launch template which is deprecated. From time to time CVE's will crop up and the underlining image must be patched. Thankfully the agents are deployed via Iac and the underlining instance image is fetched dynamically from ssm parameter store via the data block in terraform. Please review the git repo to see the configuration. Thus to effectively patch ensure the ssm parameter value is updated (review golden ami release schedule) and rerun the workspace.
🔁 Workflow Summary
AMI ID is updated
in the SSM Parameter Store.
Terraform detects the change
via a
data
block referencing the SSM parameter.
ASG launch template
is updated with the new AMI ID.
ASG triggers instance refresh
, replacing old EC2 instances.
TFC workspace is re-run
to apply any additional configuration or validation.
To update the instances, simply rerun the workspace to which will fetch the Golden AMI dynamically via a the terraform data block. This will replace and update the following ASG (Auto Scaling Group) and 1 by 1 replace the existing instances effectively patching the instances themselves.

--- Page: Prisma Cloud - Run Task ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=228036670
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Integrating Prisma Cloud with Terraform Cloud
Prisma Cloud can scan Terraform configuration files at different run stages like pre-plan, post-plan, and apply.
It checks for IaC misconfigurations that may result in a finding if provisioned to cloud.
Failures can be set to either advisory or blocking mode:
In Terraform Cloud, policies can be set to advisory or mandatory. Advisory is only a warning, but mandatory can block the run depending on the severity level.
In Prisma Cloud, severity levels are info, low, medium, high and critical. Hard failures can be configured for high and critical levels, which will stop the run.
Integrate Prisma Cloud with Terraform Cloud (Run Tasks) to scan workspaces in Terraform Cloud. The integration enables Prisma Cloud to use policies and regulate runs in Terraform Cloud. Through the integration, Prisma Cloud manages run-related information of a workspace and communicates the status of the run (either pass or fail) accessible on the Prisma Cloud console.

chunks.page_title: Terraform-Managed TFC Infrastructure - Unified Design Solution
chunks.content:
--- Page: Terraform-Managed TFC Infrastructure - Unified Design Solution ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=354856382
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview: Terraform on Terraform
Core Concept
Self-Service Infrastructure
: Uses Terraform to manage Terraform Cloud (TFC) resources programmatically
Automation-First
: Eliminates manual workspace creation and permission management
Scalable Architecture
: Supports hundreds of workspaces through declarative configuration
Architecture: Two-Layer Design
Layer 1: Foundational Infrastructure
Projects → Teams → Project Access
Components
projects
Logical grouping containers for workspaces
Namespace isolation for different applications/teams
Foundation for permission inheritance
teams
AD group integration via SSO
Organization-level permissions (workspace admin, policy management)
Reusable security principals across projects
Project-Level Access
High-level team-to-project assignments
Read-only access for AD groups (e.g., viewers, auditors)
Establishes baseline permissions before workspace creation
Layer 2: Workspace Infrastructure
Workspaces → Variables → Run Tasks → Team Access
Components
Workspaces
Individual workspace provisioning
VCS integration (GitHub repository connection)
Terraform version pinning and working directory specification
Variable Management
Terraform variables (application-specific)
Environment variables (credentials, settings)
Auto-injected metadata (workspace ID, cloud provider accounts)
Security & Compliance
Policy set attachments (Sentinel policies)
Run task integration (Prisma Cloud, cost estimation)
Drift detection and auto-destroy scheduling
Workspace-Level Access
Granular AD group permissions per workspace
Read-Only
: View runs and state
Apply
: Execute runs, manage variables, write state
Dynamic Provisioning Model
Data-Driven Workflow
YAML/JSON Definitions → Orchestration → Terraform Modules → TFC Resources
Step 1: Define Requirements

:
["network-viewers"]
apply_teams
:
["network-admins"]
Step 2: Orchestration Layer
External data processing (Lambda, GitHub Actions, etc.)
Converts YAML/JSON into Terraform-compatible structures
Enriches with environment-specific defaults
Validates against organizational standards
Step 3: Loop-Driven Provisioning
# example workspace creation
module
"workspaces"
{
source
=
"
./tfe/modules/workspaces
"
for_each
=
local
.
workspace_definitions
name
=
each
.
value
.
name

=
each
.
value
.
project

=
each
.
value
.
vcs_repo
# ...
}
Step 4: Access Control Application
# example workspace access
module
"workspace_access"
{
source
=
"
./tfe/modules/teams/modules/team-access
"
for_each
=
local
.
workspace_permissions
workspace_id
=
module
.
workspaces
[
each
.
key
]
.
id

=

.

.

[
each
.

.

.

=
"
read
"
}
Granular Access Control
Multi-Tier Permission Model
Organization → Project → Workspace → Resource
Team Types & Permissions
Team Type
Scope
Capabilities
Platform Teams
Organization
Manage all projects, teams, policies
Project Admins
Project
Create workspaces, assign teams
Workspace Admins
Workspace
Configure settings, manage runs, apply changes
Workspace Apply
Workspace
Execute runs, update variables
Workspace Read
Workspace
View runs, download state (read-only)
AD Group Mapping
# Example: Platform Infrastructure Team
module
"platform_team"
{
source
=
"
./tfe/modules/teams
"
name
=
"
ad-platform-infrastructure
"

=
{
manage_workspaces
=
true
manage_policies
=
true

=
true
}
}
# Example: Application Team
module
"app_team_read"
{

=

./tfe/modules/teams

=
"
ad-app-developers-read

=
false
# Read-only access
}

Complete Workflow: End-to-End
Step 1: Infrastructure Foundation
Project Creation
Define logical boundaries (networking, data, applications)
Execute projects
Team Provisioning
Map AD groups to TFC teams
Execute teams
Assign organization-level permissions
Project Access
Link teams to projects
Establish baseline read permissions
Step 2: Workspace Provisioning
Requirement Definition
# Simple format for developers
workspace
:
name
:
my-app-prod
cloud
:
aws
repository
:
pgetech/my-app-infra
Orchestration Processing
Validate against standards
Enrich with defaults (Terraform version, policies)
Generate Terraform variables
Workspace Creation
Loop through definitions
Execute workspaces
Configure VCS, variables, run tasks
Permission Assignment
Apply workspace-level team access
Enforce least-privilege principle
Step 3: Continuous Management
Change Detection
Monitor workspace definition files
Trigger Terraform apply on updates
Drift Remediation
Detect manual changes in TFC
Re-apply Terraform to restore desired state
Lifecycle Automation
Auto-destroy for ephemeral environments
Tag management via OIDC Lambda integration
Key Design Benefits
Scalability
53
complete
Template-Based
: One module supports hundreds of workspaces
54
complete
Loop-Driven
:
for_each
eliminates repetitive code
55
complete
Data-Driven
: Adding workspaces = updating data files
Security
56
complete
AD Integration
: SSO with existing identity provider
57
complete
Least Privilege
: Granular permissions at workspace level
58
complete
Policy Enforcement
: Sentinel policies attached automatically
59
complete
Audit Trail
: All changes tracked in Terraform state
Developer Experience
60
complete
Self-Service
: Teams request workspaces via simple YAML
61
complete
Consistency
: Standardized workspace configuration
62
complete
Fast Provisioning
: Automated creation in minutes
Operations
63
complete
Infrastructure as Code
: TFC infrastructure versioned in Git
64
complete
Idempotent
: Safe to re-run without side effects
65
complete
Multi-Cloud
: AWS/Azure support with conditional logic
66
complete
Compliance
: Run tasks (Prisma Cloud) enforce security standard
Technical Implementation Details
Module Hierarchy
tfe/
├── modules/
│   ├── projects/
│   │   ├── main.tf              # Project resource
│   │   └── modules/
│   │       └── project-access/  # Team-to-project binding
│   ├── teams/
│   │   ├── main.tf              # Team resource
│   │   └── modules/
│   │       └── team-access/     # Team-to-workspace binding
│   └── workspaces/
│       ├── main.tf              # Workspace resource
│       ├── variables.tf         # Multi-cloud variables
│       └── outputs.tf           # Workspace metadata
Data Flow
External Data Source (YAML/JSON)
↓
Orchestration Layer (Lambda/Actions)
↓
Terraform Variables (tfvars)
↓
[Layer 1] Projects + Teams + Project Access
↓
[Layer 2] Workspaces + Variables + Team Access
↓
Terraform Cloud Infrastructure
Multi-Cloud Support
# Conditional variable injection
locals
{
aws_vars
=
var
.

==
"

"

{
account_num

{
arm_subscription_id
=
var.arm_subscription_id
arm_tenant_id
=
var.arm_tenant_id
}
:
{}
}
Example: Complete Workspace Lifecycle

{

=

./tfe/modules/projects
"

=
"

=

ad-data-engineers
"

./tfe/modules/projects/modules/project-access
"

=

Summary: Unified Design Principles
Declarative
: Infrastructure defined as code
Modular
: Reusable components with clear boundaries
Automated
: Minimal manual intervention
Secure
: AD-integrated, policy-enforced, least-privilege
Scalable
: Supports enterprise-scale workspace management
Auditable
: Complete change history in version control

Terraform Cloud - Working Directories

Section 1 summary
Became a member of the pge-tfc-workspace-developers team.
Cloned the pge-tfc-workspaces repository to your local machine.
Added a block of YAML code that declaratively defines your new workspace.
Move on to section 2 to validate your updates, and generate the required JSON files.
Section 2: Validate your updates and generate JSON file(s)
Now you need to run a script that validates your new workspace code. It ensures all the required fields are there, with valid values, that your repo and branch exist, as well as other things. This script is referred to as
orchestration.py
and is located in the
scripts
folder of the
pge-tfc-workspaces
repository.
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-tfc-workspaces/blob/main/scripts/orchestration.py
Prerequisites:
You must have a personal GitHub PAT created with appropriate permissions and have it set to the $GH_TOKEN environment variable in your shell. Ref:
CCOE Wiki: Creating a Github PAT
You must have added the
CCoE-Pipeline
GitHub user to your repository with
Admin
privileges. This can be done in your repository settings.
Open your terminal, and navigate to the root of your local
pge-tfc-workspaces
repository.
Run one of the following (option
a
is preferred if you are only adding a single workspace, option
b
will validate every single workspace in the entire file, leading to a much longer output):
To validate a single workspace:
./scripts/orchestration.py
--path path/to/file.yaml
-n workspace_name
To validate multiple workspaces:
./scripts/orchestration.py
--path path/to/file.yaml
-n workspace_name -n workspace_name_2
To validate all workspaces in the given file:
./scripts/orchestration.py
--path path/to/file.yaml
-a
Windows users only:
You may need to prepend the above command with "python" or "python3"
.
PowerShell users only:
To set your GitHub PAT as the appropriately named environment variable:
$Env:GH_TOKEN='ghp_XXX'
After a few seconds, you'll see output similar to the following, assuming a successful run (troubleshooting steps at the end if needed):
Commit your files: If you now run a
git status
you'll see your YAML file you edited, as well as 1 or more JSON files that were automatically edited by this script. Commit all these files and push to the remote repo. Do NOT edit JSON files directly. Do NOT run orchestration then edit any file without re-running orchestration.
Create a PR: Ensure you follow the rules specified in
prlint.json
. Failure to follow that may result in delayed workspace creation.
Make a post in
Terraform PR Requests
requesting review of your PR.
Include a clickable, working hyperlink to your PR. Not just a plain-text URL.
Wait for all the PR checks to complete before requesting review of your PR. Also ensure that no resources are being destroyed (unless you're intentionally removing something)
Once someone has reviewed, approved, and merged your PR, it can take a few minutes to create the workspace in Terraform Cloud. If the workspace has not been provisioned after 1hr, please contact the CCOE TF team or the person who approved your PR for assistance.
Once the workspace shows up in
Terraform Cloud
, you may start using your new workspace.
Section 3: Deleting your workspace
Workspace deletion follows the same flow, except you are deleting the workspace block you added, and passing a different flag to
orchestration.py
:
Delete your workspace block
Run:
./scripts/orchestration.py --path path/to/file.yaml
--
remove
You should see output similar to:
your-workspace namespace has been removed
Follow the rest of the steps from above.
Troubleshooting
This will update your PR with the latest changes.
Examples
These are actual workspace definitions pulled from various files, and demonstrations of the appropriate command to run given that workspace.
Note: All these examples are run from the root of the repo. This is not necessarily required. You can run the script from anywhere in the repo, you just need to make sure your
--path
is correct. Use tab auto-complete to ensure a valid path.
Example 1: Validating a single AWS workspace
Workspace Definition
Running the script
Example 2: Validating multiple workspaces at once
Workspace Definition
Running the script
Example 3: A run failure due to invalid branch
Workspace Definition
Running the script
Video Walkthrough
This is a 10m long video walkthrough of the above process
create-a-new-tfc-workspace.mov

Terraform recap
Terraform is an infrastructure as code tool to create resources, PG&E has
requirement
that all cloud resources are created using IaC (Infrastructure as Code).
Terraform workflow is as follows
Write – Design and Develop infrastructure configuration files
Init – Initialize directory containing terraform configuration files
Plan – Review the changes to your infrastructure
Apply – Provision your infrastructure
Terraform remembers infrastructure provisioned by updating a state file
Terraform opensource
Typical users’ exposure to terraform
Open source terraform client
Plan/apply is executed from users’ desktop
State is stored locally
Workspaces are not mandatory
Terraform Cloud at PG&E
PG&E CCoE supported infrastructure as code
Version controlled using GitHub
Plan/apply is executed from terraform cloud
State is managed in terraform cloud
Workspaces are mandatory
```
