chunks.page_title: Terraform Module PRs - Release Notes Pipeline
chunks.content:
--- Page: Terraform Module PRs - Release Notes Pipeline ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=319719814
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What is it?
It is a CodePipeline/Lambda script that parses release notes as entered by TF module developers and sends the updates to MS Teams. It also checks for certain criteria that must be met in the PR and the updates made .
Why?
The release notes Lambda has two purposes:
To update users of module development and let them know when there are updates (and what those updates are)
To enhance the standards of TF module developers.
How does it work?
On PR merge*, a CodepPipeline pipeline is started via GitHub webhook. This pipeline invokes a Lambda function that retrieves the newly merged PR from GitHub, parses out the "Release Notes" section entered by the developer, runs checks against those release notes as well as the files that were updated. Assuming all is well, a "Successful" Adaptive Card JSON payload is sent to an MS Teams channel with the validated release notes. If the validation and checks fail, a "Failure" Adaptive Card JSON payload is sent to an MS Teams channel. In both cases, links to the PRs are included.
*
eventually this should be a pre-merge hook
Developer pushes and merges a TF module update PR
GitHub sends message to Codepipeline via webhook notifying of the PR merge
Codepipeline invokes the Lambda
Lambda parses release notes and runs checks on the PR, and sends results to MS Teams
MS Teams replies with HTTP status code of success or failure
Lambda reports results to Codepipeline job.
What checks does it perform?
Ensures only 1 module was updated by checking what files were updated in the PR
Ensures the module name and version in the release notes matches what module was actually updated and its actual version in the module.info
Ensures that if a module was updated, release notes were submitted (there are times where there will be PRs in that repo where someone isn't actually updating a module. Updates to this script for instance)
Ensures the inverse of the above bullet point, that if there are release notes, that a module was actually updated (we don't want erroneous updates being sent to Teams)
Other notes
It interacts with two different Github API endpoints:
https://api.github.com/search/issues
https://api.github.com/repos
The first is used for finding and retrieving the PR itself. The second is for retrieving the PR files.
The Lambda uses raw GitHub API queries instead of something like PyGithub, due to PyGithub's cryptography dependency not playing well with Lambda at the time of creation. It was trying to invoke that module multiple times for some reason. Its possible it was a temporary issue and it may be worth revisiting in the future.
Account:
514712703977
Codepipeline ARN:
arn:aws:codepipeline:us-west-2:514712703977:tf-module-release-notes
Lambda ARN:
arn:aws:lambda:us-west-2:514712703977:function:tf-release-notes
Github Repo:
Uses the pge-terraform-modules repo, exists in the "scripts" subfolder.
TFC Workspace:
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/workspaces/tf-release-notes-pipeline
- Working directory is the "terraform" subfolder. Therefore if updating the Lambda code, it requires a manual run. In both cases, a manual apply is required.

--- Page: Terraform Module CI Validation Pipeline ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=360437661
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

<work in progress>
Here is the initial Jira story I wrote up before splitting it up into smaller chunks (at least 2 stories: plan, implenentation):
Summary
As a Cloud Platform Engineer, I want to implement a Terraform CI validation pipeline that runs automatically for newly submitted or updated Terraform modules.
Description
Create a CI-based validation pipeline that executes on pull request creation or updates for Terraform modules. The pipeline will enforce formatting, linting, documentation, and baseline quality requirements before merge. This will be used as a preliminary review of the update and does not replace human review as it will be impossible to account for nuance and every possibility of “issues” that could arise.
Scope includes (mostly a list of ideas that we COULD include):
terraform fmt -check
terraform init -backend=false -upgrade=false
tflint
Terraform documentation validation (e.g., generated README exists and is up to date)
Ensure TF tests exist and are up-to-date
Validate directory and file structure conformance
root contains
main.tf
,
variables.tf
,
outputs.tf
,
module.info
there is an example subfolder, and tests subfolder (modules subfolder may not always be a thing - perhaps generate a warning if missing?)
Additional static analysis or standards checks as required, some ideas:
Module interface quality checks
variables must have descriptions, explicit types, avoid
any
(we have tons of any unfortunately - would need to start off as a warning to encourage correction - the type should be defined, especially on complex objects and maps where we use
any
a lot right now)
outputs must have descriptions
??
These can possibly be accomplished with various tools including tflint custom rules.
This validation will avoid security scanning (via something like Checkov or tfsec) as that is already handled by the repo’s Wiz integrations.
Result:
The pipeline runs automatically on PRs
Checks are enforced as required status checks before merge
Ensures consistent quality and standards across Terraform modules
Buyer:
@Balaji Venkataraman
Seller:
@Eric Barnard
---

--- Page: RDS Aurora PostgreSQL ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=148668531
Labels: tfc
Chunk 1/1
---

The Terraform RDS Aurora Postgresql submodule can be found
here
in the PGETech Terraform private registry.
Limitations
At this time of writing, the RDS modules are still undergoing testing and should not be used to deploy Production grade DB instances.  While most SAF compliance is built into this module, we are still working out the issues surrounding the implementation of the Secrets Manager Rotation.
Terraform module which creates an RDS Aurora Postgresql Database.
Pre-Requisites:
1. IAM Role rds-enhanced-monitoring-role exists within your account (should be automatically deployed to PG&E accounts)
2. RDS VPC Endpoint exists for the account.  If not, you can deploy one in the account as shown with the rds_vpc_endpoint example module (rds/examples/rds_vpc_endpoint)
Notes:
1. At this time, the secrets manager is not implemented to store the master credentials.  User will need to do so manually and ensure that the secret is rotated before 90 days. All other Saf           Compliance is implemented.
2. This module will also create various SSM Parameters to store data pertaining to the DB Cluster and it's instances.
3. This module includes logic to pass in private subnets and vpc(/vpc/privatesubnet1/id, /vpc/privatesubnet2/id, /vpc/privatesubnet3/id, /vpc/id).  Be sure that the account in which you are using has adequate IP space for the subnets stored in Parameter Store.
4. Serverless v1 clusters do not support managed master user password, Aurora Serverless v2 is supported for MySQL 8.0 onwards & PostgreSQL 13 onwards.
5. if the DataClassification is not internal or public, passing null KMS will result in error, hence create the KMS key using the module in the code, its a 2 step process
a. first comment all the code except for kms module, run terraform apply, this create the KMS key and stores in state file.
b. uncomment remaining code, replace null for kms for all the arguments with the module created value.
In your Terraform project, you'll want to use the configuration block provided in the terraform module registry in your
main.tf
file.  You can use the rds_aurora_postgresql example module as a guide in your development, but be aware that the example module is using a relative path for the module source.  You will want to replace that section with the source and version attributes shown in the code block below.
This module is designed to deploy resources with the same identifying name.
Dependencies for RDS Aurora PostgreSQL
The dependences used for RDS Aurora PostgreSQL are main_security_group (0.0.7), ssm_parameter (0.0.8), validate-pge-tags (0.0.5), ws (0.0.5).
These dependencies are collectively contribute to the setup, management and security of an RDS Aurora PostgreSQL instance on AWS using Terraform.
main_security_group
which defines the network access rules for this module and also used to controls inbound and outbound traffic, ensuring proper security for database.
ssm_parameter
is used for storing configuration parameters, secrets, and other sensitive information securely and also used to manage
database collection strings and API keys.
validate-pge-tags
are metadata labels which helps to organize and manage resources associated with Aurora PostgreSQL instances.
ws
refers to a workspace where configuration, scripts and other relevant files as stored and also help to maintain a structured approach to managing resources.

--- Page: RDS Aurora MYSQL ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=148668537

---

here
in the PGETech Terraform private registry.
Terraform module which creates an RDS Aurora mysql Database.
Pre-Requisites:
1. IAM Role rds-enhanced-monitoring-role exists within your account (should be automatically deployed to PG&E accounts)
2. RDS VPC Endpoint exists for the account.  If not, you can deploy one in the account as shown with the rds_vpc_endpoint example module (rds/examples/rds_vpc_endpoint)
Notes:
1. At this time, this module is not fully SAF 2.0 Compliant.  Compliance will be implemented at a later date.
2. This module will also create various SSM Parameters to store data pertaining to the DB Cluster and it's instances.
3. This module includes logic to pass in private subnets and vpc(/vpc/privatesubnet1/id, /vpc/privatesubnet2/id,
/vpc/privatesubnet3/id, /vpc/id).  Be sure that the account in which you are using has adequate IP space for the subnets stored in Parameter Store.
4. Serverless v1 clusters do not support managed master user password, Aurora Serverless v2 is supported for MySQL 8.0 onwards & PostgreSQL 13 onwards.
5. if the DataClassification is not internal or public, passing null KMS will result in error, hence create the KMS key using the module in the code, its a 2 step process
a. first comment all the code except for kms module, run terraform apply, this create the KMS key and stores in state file.
b. uncomment remaining code, replace null for kms for all the arguments with the module created value.
In your Terraform project, you'll want to use the configuration block provided in the terraform module registry in your
main.tf
file.  You can use the rds_aurora_mysql example module as a guide in your development, but be aware that the example module is using a relative path for the module source.  You will want to replace that section with the source and version attributes shown in the code block below.
This module is designed to deploy resources with the same identifying name.
module "rds_aurora-mysql" { source = "app.terraform.io/pgetech/rds/aws//modules/aurora-mysql" version = "0.0.11" # insert required variables here }
Dependencies for RDS Aurora MYSQL
The dependences used for RDS Aurora MYSQL are main_security_group (0.0.7), ssm_parameter (0.0.8), validate-pge-tags (0.0.5), ws (0.0.5).
These dependencies are used for security enhance and enables automation in managing Aurora MYSQL.
`
main_security_group
is used to control the inbound and outbound traffic in MYSQL instances and it will act as virtual firewalls.
ssm_parameter
is used for storing configuration parameters, secrets, and other sensitive information securely and also used to manage
database collection strings and API keys.
validate-pge-tags
are metadata labels which helps to organize and manage resources associated with Aurora MYSQL  instances.
ws
refers to a workspace where configuration, scripts and other relevant files as stored.

--- Page: RDS Oracle ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=148668527
Labels: tfc
Chunk 1/1
---

The Terraform RDS Oracle submodule can be found
here
in the PGETech Terraform private registry.  Please refer to the
example
rds_oracle module on how to configure your oracle module.  The example is deployed with an Amazon RDS Oracle Database 19c.
Limitations
At this time of writing, the RDS modules are still undergoing testing and should not be used to deploy Production grade DB instances.  While most SAF compliance is built into this module, we are still sorting out issues that began after the implementation of the Secrets Manager Rotation.  While the secrets manager rotation executes successfully in our example Oracle module, the configurations necessary for the rotation have caused the kerberos enablement to fail.  Additionally, the use of Kerberos for authentication is still in its beginning phases.
Terraform module which creates SAF2.0 Oracle RDS Database.
Pre-Requisites:
1. Directory Service and it's domain are shared within your account.  Your security group egress cidr should reflect the VPC of the Managed AD account.
2. IAM Role rds-directoryservice-kerberos-access-role exists within your account (should be automatically deployed to PG&E accounts).If not available work with respective team in PG&E.
3. S3 integration requires an s3 bucket has been previously created and the arn passed in this module as a variable
4. S3 key must exist/ uploaded to S3 bucket before running the module
5. To have automated replication, multi region KMS must be created before running the module
6. RDS VPC Endpoint exists for the account.  If not, you can deploy one in the account as shown with the rds_vpc_endpoint example module (rds/examples/rds_vpc_endpoint)
7. if the DataClassification is not internal or public, passing null KMS will result in error, hence create the KMS key using the module in the code, its a 2 step process
a. first comment all the code except for kms module, run terraform apply, this create the KMS key and stores in state file.
b. uncomment remaining code, replace null for kms for all the arguments with the module created value.
Notes
If the password you pass in does not satisfy the minimum length as per SAF compliance which is min 16 chars, a validation is put in place to meet the requirement, Password will be stored in secrets manager.
If argument manage_master_user_password is set to "true", then user provided password will be disregarded, and AWS creates random password and creates a secret manager and stores this password, rotation is enabled and default is 7 days.
In your Terraform project, you'll want to use the configuration block provided in the terraform module registry in your main.tf file.  Be sure to configure your module with a valid AWS Directory Service domain ID and with the IAM Role 'rds-directoryservice-kerberos-access-role'.  Both should be shared within your aws account.  You should also ensure that a RDS VPC endpoint exists in the account.  You can use the rds_oracle example module as a guide in your development, but be aware that the example module is using a relative path for the module source.  You will want to replace that section with the source and version attributes shown in the code block below.
To implement the Secrets Manager rotation, you'll need to provide a secrets manager rotation function
template
(provided by AWS) and the necessary libraries for your lambda layer.   Please note that the function templates/libraries require a runtime of Python 3.7, and it is recommended that you use an Amazon Linux 2 AMI to download the necessary libraries.  Please see the attached document for directions on installing the necessary libraries for the cx_Oracle import.  Be sure to ensure that the Secrets Manager and RDS vpc endpoints have been deployed in your account with DNS enabled.  In order for the Secrets Manager rotation to work, both vpc endpoints should include the same security group, or a security group with the same permissions as that of the RDS security group as shown in the example module.  The security group in the example module allows traffic on the necessary RDS ports and port 443 for the vpc cidrs.
Dependencies for RDS Oracle
The dependencies for Submodule Oracle are aws_iam_role (0.0.8), lambda_layer (0.0.15), main_security_group (0.0.7), rotation_lambda_function (0.0.15),
rotation_lambda_iam_role (0.0.8), secrets_manager (0.0.8), validate-pge-tags (0.0.5), ws (0.0.5).
These dependencies provide secure credential management, automated rotation, network security.
aws_iam_role
is likely used to grant necessary AWS services and resources interacting with Oracle. It also provides permissions for Lambda functions, Secrets Manager and other services.
lambda_layer
is used to share code and dependencies across multiple Lambda Functions. This lambda layer contains functions and libraries used by lambda functions associated with oracle databases.
main_security_group
is used to define the network access rules for Oracle related resources, it controls the inbound and outbound traffic to oracle instances, ensuring proper network security.
rotation_lambda_function
is responsible for the credential rotation and other automated tasks related to oracle database management, this is triggered periodically to update credentials securely.
rotation_lambda_iam_role
which provides the necessary permissions to the Lambda function to interact with Oracle RDS instance, secrets manager, and other required AWS services
secrets_manager
is used to securely store and manage sensitive information, such as oracle database credentials.
validate-pge-tags
is used for validating tags associated with AWS resources, Tags can be used for organization management ensuring that resources related to Oracle.
ws
is workspace or environment that represent a designated space for organizing configurations, scripts, or files specific to Oracle and is used to maintain clean and organized structure.
Design Requirements for RDS Oracle
https://wiki.comp.pge.com/display/CCE/AWS+RDS+Module+for+Oracle+-+Findings+and+Requirements+for+Design

--- Page: RDS start stop ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=300754986
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Problem description:
In AWS RDS, a database instance that has been stopped will automatically start after 7 days, and this behavior is by design.
Unfortunately, there's no direct way to disable the automatic restart feature entirely, as it's an AWS-imposed limit to prevent the instance from remaining in a stopped state indefinitely.
Workaround implementation:
you can implement a workaround by automating the process of stopping the instance again after it auto-starts. Here’s how you can prevent it from staying up by setting up automation to stop the instance:
Use AWS Lambda with a CloudWatch Rule
You can create a scheduled AWS Lambda function that checks if the RDS instance has started, and if so, automatically stops it.
Terraform submodule is developed to create the necessary AWS resources to automate the rds start and stop as per the enduser requirements.
please note: when creating the RDS cluster/instance , make to sure to add tags autostart = "yes" and autostart ="no" because lambda functions retrieves the tags from the rds instances and based on its status the action to "start" or "stop" will be performed.
Below is the link for pge private registry for this module.
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/registry/modules/private/pgetech/rds/aws/0.0.19/submodules/rds-start-stop
Usage of this module is shown in the examples for the same.
https://app.terraform.io/app/pgetech/registry/modules/private/pgetech/rds/aws/0.0.19/examples/rds_start_stop

--- Page: RDS SQLServer ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=148668529
Labels: tfc
Chunk 1/1
---

The Terraform RDS SQLServer submodule can be found
here
in the PGETech Terraform private registry.
Limitations
At this time of writing, the RDS modules are still undergoing testing and should not be used to deploy Production grade DB instances.  Currently, no SAF compliance exists for this RDS engine type, and you are encouraged to migrate to Aurora Postgresql for improved performance and to save on licensing.
Terraform module which creates an RDS SQL Server Database.
Pre-Requisites:
1. IAM Role rds-enhanced-monitoring-role exists within your account (should be automatically deployed to PG&E accounts)
2. RDS VPC Endpoint exists for the account.  If not, you can deploy one in the account as shown with the rds_vpc_endpoint example module (rds/examples/rds_vpc_endpoint)
Notes:
1. At this time, this module is not fully SAF 2.0 Compliant.  Compliance will be implemented at a later date.
2. This module will also create various SSM Parameters to store data pertaining to the SQL Server DB.
3. This example should take roughly one hour to deploy if you are running it locally.  Be patient my friend.
4. This particular module is modeled from [link](
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-cfn-sqlserver
).  If you are creating the SQL Server Enterprise Edition, db_name variable must be null.
5. if the DataClassification is not internal or public, passing null KMS will result in error, hence create the KMS key using the module in the code, its a 2 step process
a. first comment all the code except for kms module, run terraform apply, this create the KMS key and stores in state file.
b. uncomment remaining code, replace null for kms for all the arguments with the module created value.
In your Terraform project, you'll want to use the configuration block provided in the terraform module registry in your
main.tf
file.  You can use the rds_sqlserver example module as a guide in your development, but be aware that the example module is using a relative path for the module source.  You will want to replace that section with the source and version attributes shown in the code block below.
This module is designed to deploy resources with the same identifying name.
Dependences for RDS SQLServer
The dependences used for RDS Aurora SQLServer are main_security_group (0.0.7), ssm_parameter (0.0.8), validate-pge-tags (0.0.5), ws (0.0.5).
These dependencies are used for Network security, parameter management, and enables automation in managing and configuring the SQLServer RDS database.
main_Security_group
is used to define network access rules for SQLserver instance and controls the inbound and outbound traffic, ensuring proper security configuration for SQL server instance.
ssm_parameter
is used for store and retrieve configuration parameters related to SQLserver instance and also provides a secure and centralized way to manage sensitive information.
validate-pge-tags
is used for validating the tags associated with SQL server, these tags can be used for organization, management and for tracking purposes.
ws
refers to a workspace or environment used for organizing configurations, scripts, or files specific to SQLserver instance..

chunks.page_title: Terraform Module: s3web (Non-Static Site with CloudFront OAC)
chunks.content:
--- Page: Terraform Module: s3web (Non-Static Site with CloudFront OAC) ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=342302514
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
The AWS S3Web module is a comprehensive Terraform module that creates a complete web hosting solution on AWS. It uses
S3 as object storage
combined with
CloudFront CDN
for global content delivery, along with WAF security, SSL certificates, and CI/CD pipelines for automated deployments.
🚀
Current Version: 0.1.7 (Latest)
What's New in Version 0.1.7
This is the
latest and most advanced version
of the S3Web module, featuring significant architectural improvements and new capabilities:
🆕
Key Architectural Changes from Previous Versions
S3 as Pure Object Storage
: No longer uses S3 static website hosting (more secure and performant)
CloudFront as Primary Web Server
: All traffic flows through CloudFront for better control
OAC Security Model
: Replaces OAI with more secure Origin Access Control
Function-Based Routing
: CloudFront Functions handle SPA routing at the edge
🔄
Migration Benefits from Older Versions
Feature
v0.1.0-0.1.6
v0.1.7 (Current)
S3 Configuration
Static website hosting
Pure object storage
Security
OAI (Legacy)
OAC (Modern & Secure)
SPA Support
Limited
Full Angular/React support
Edge Functions
None
CloudFront Functions
Architecture Components
Core Infrastructure
S3 Bucket
:
Object storage
for web content with versioning and encryption support (not configured as static website)
CloudFront Distribution
:
Primary web server
and global CDN with custom domain and SSL/TLS termination
Origin Access Control (OAC)
: Secure access from CloudFront to S3 objects
ACM Certificate
: Automatic SSL certificate provisioning and validation
Route53 Records
: DNS management for custom domains
WAF v2
: Web Application Firewall with IP filtering and managed rules
Key Architectural Points
S3 as Storage Backend:
S3 bucket serves as
object storage only
(not static website hosting)
No S3 website endpoints are used
All web traffic flows through CloudFront
S3 bucket is private and only accessible via CloudFront OAC
CloudFront as Web Server:
CloudFront distribution acts as the
primary web server
Handles all HTTP/HTTPS requests from users
Provides global edge caching and CDN functionality
Integrates with CloudFront Functions for SPA routing
Serves content directly from S3 objects via OAC
Content Delivery Flow
User Request → CloudFront (CDN/Web Server) → S3 (Object Storage)
↑
CloudFront Functions
(SPA routing, redirects)
Benefits of This Architecture
Benefit
Description
Security
S3 bucket remains private, only CloudFront can access
Performance
Global edge caching reduces latency
Scalability
CloudFront handles traffic spikes automatically
SSL/TLS
Centralized certificate management at CloudFront
Custom Domains
Clean URLs without S3 website endpoint complexity
Advanced Features
CloudFront Functions, WAF integration, custom headers
Security Features
Origin Access Control (OAC)
: Secure S3 access from CloudFront only
WAF Protection
: IP-based filtering with PGE network allowlists
SSL/TLS
: Automatic certificate management with ACM
S3 Bucket Policies
: Least-privilege access controls
CI/CD Pipeline
CodePipeline
: Automated deployment pipeline
CodeBuild
: Build and test automation
GitHub Integration
: Source code management with webhooks
SonarQube
: Code quality scanning
Artifactory
: Dependency management
CloudFront Functions
SPA Routing
: Automatic handling of Single Page Application routing
Request Processing
: Custom JavaScript functions for path rewriting
Edge Computing
: Sub-millisecond response times
Supported Application Types
Type
Description
Features
HTML
Static HTML websites
Basic hosting, no build process
Angular
Angular  applications
Angular CLI build, routing support
React
React applications
React build process, routing support
Custom
Custom build processes
Flexible configuration
Module Configuration
Required Providers
terraform {
required_providers {
aws = {
source = "hashicorp/aws"
version = ">= 5.0"
configuration_aliases = [aws.us_east_1, aws.r53]
}
github = {
source = "integrations/github"
version = ">= 5.0"
}
}
}
Provider Setup
# Main AWS provider
provider "aws" {
region = var.aws_region
assume_role {
role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::${var.account_num}:role/${var.aws_role}"
}
}
# US East 1 provider (required for CloudFront/ACM)
provider "aws" {
alias = "us_east_1"
region = "us-east-1"
assume_role {
role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::${var.account_num}:role/${var.aws_role}"
}
}
# Route53 provider (for DNS management)
provider "aws" {
alias = "r53"
region = "us-east-1"
assume_role {
role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::${var.r53_account_num}:role/${var.aws_r53_role}"
}
}
Usage Examples
Basic HTML Website
module "s3web_html" {
source = "
app.terraform.io/pgetech/s3web/aws
"
version = "0.1.0"
providers = {
aws = aws
aws.us_east_1 = aws.us_east_1
aws.r53 = aws.r53
}
# Required parameters
tags = module.tags.tags
custom_domain_name = "
mysite.example.com
"
s3web_type = "html"
github_repo_url = "
https://github.com/myorg/mysite.git
"
github_branch = "main"
secretsmanager_github_token = "github:token"
secretsmanager_sonar_token = "sonar:token"
project_name = "My Website"
cloudfront_oac_name = "mysite-oac"
# Optional parameters
bucket_name = "mysite-bucket"
kms_key_arn = module.kms_key.key_arn
cloudfront_priceclass = "PriceClass_100"
}
Angular/React
module "s3web_spa" {
source = "
app.terraform.io/pgetech/s3web/aws
"
version = "0.1.0"
providers = {
aws = aws
aws.us_east_1 = aws.us_east_1
aws.r53 = aws.r53
}
# SPA-specific configuration
s3web_type = "angular" # or "react"
cf_function_name = "spa-router"
cf_function_comment = "SPA routing handler"
cf_function_publish = true
# Build configuration
nodejs_version = "18"
package_manager = "npm"
project_root_directory = "."
build_args1 = "CI=false"
# Additional parameters...
}
Required Parameters
Parameter
Type
Description
tags
map(string)
PGE-compliant resource tags
custom_domain_name
string
Domain name for the website
github_repo_url
string
GitHub repository URL
github_branch
string
Git branch to deploy
secretsmanager_github_token
string
GitHub token location in Secrets Manager
secretsmanager_sonar_token
string
SonarQube token location
project_name
string
Project name for SonarQube
cloudfront_oac_name
string
CloudFront Origin Access Control name
Security Features
IP Allowlisting
The module automatically configures WAF rules to allow traffic from PGE networks:
Internal corporate networks
VPN ranges
Approved external networks
Encryption
S3
: Optional KMS encryption for bucket contents
CloudFront
: TLS 1.2+ enforced
CodePipeline
: KMS encryption for artifacts
Access Control
S3 bucket blocked from public access
CloudFront-only access via OAC
IAM roles with least-privilege permissions
Monitoring and Logging
Available Logs
S3 Access Logs
: Bucket access logging
CloudFront Logs
: Distribution access logs
WAF Logs
: Security event logging
CodePipeline Logs
: Build and deployment logs
arrange this professionally
Best Practices
Domain Management
Use PGE-approved domain suffixes
Implement proper DNS delegation
Configure health checks for critical sites
Performance Optimization
Use appropriate CloudFront price class
Configure proper caching policies
Implement compression for static assets
Security Hardening
Enable WAF for all public sites
Use KMS encryption for sensitive content
Implement proper CORS policies
Regular security scanning with SonarQube
CI/CD Pipeline
Use semantic versioning for releases
Implement proper branch protection
Configure automated testing
Monitor build performance
Troubleshooting
Common Issues
Certificate Validation Failures
Error: Certificate validation failed
Solution
: Ensure Route53 hosted zone exists and is properly configured.
CloudFront Function Errors
Error: Missing required argument "name"
Solution
: Ensure
cf_function_name
is provided for SPA applications (Angular/React).
Pipeline Build Failures
Error: npm install failed
Solution
: Check Artifactory connectivity and credentials in Secrets Manager.
WAF Access Denied
Error: 403 Forbidden from CloudFront
Solution
: Verify your IP address is included in the PGE allowlist or use VPN.
*Last Updated: August 2025*
*Module Version: 0.1.4*
*Maintained by: PGE CCOE Team*

chunks.page_title: 2020 Cybersecurity Technology Standard - Cloud Instance Security Group Policy V20
chunks.content:
--- Page: 2020 Cybersecurity Technology Standard - Cloud Instance Security Group Policy V20 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=115343435
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Document Information
ID:
Version: v2.0
Last Updated:
02/10/2020
Review Cycle:
Annual
Next
Review:
Author:
Jason Green, Cybersecurity
Accountable:
Cybersecurity Risk & Strategy Architecture
Consulted:
Cybersecurity Risk & Strategy Consulting
Cybersecurity Services
IT Client Experience
Informed:
Cybersecurity
Related Standards:
What Is It?
The Cloud Instance Security Group Policy establishes a set of minimum-security requirements for security groups. In conforming to best practices PG&E should limit network access to only that which is required. All Non-Cybersecurity appliances (instances) requiring public IP access must be documented and approved by Cybersecurity.
When and Where Do We Use It?
Wherever security groups can be used in PG&E's public cloud environments.
Standard
Inbound access from 0.0.0.0/0 to common port is not permitted. Security groups are required to be least privilege. This would always include the network Port, Protocol and smallest network range or security group possible.
Best practice is to use security group to security group rules, or when necessary the minimum required CIDR.

chunks.page_title: AWS DMZ Network Access Security Standard
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS DMZ Network Access Security Standard ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=58896726
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What Is It?
Establishes a set of requirements for network communication to and from the a DMZ within the PGE AWS Cloud
When and Where Do We Use It?
This standard applies to:
Access to and from an AWS DMZ environment.
Standard
All access to and from an AWS DMZ Environment will require a Firewall Exception Request, FER. Please note, traffic initiated from within the DMZ to external assets requires a
FER.
By default there are two FERs implemented in a AWS DMZ Account:
DMZ to QRadar using the syslog protocol  for Log monitoring.
DMZ to
www.paloaltonetwork.com
using https for security monitoring and endpoint protection
There is no other access to or from the AWS DMZ Account without a FER.
Security Requirements applied to traffic
EDL blocking & alerts
Banned countries blocking & alerts
Advanced Security Services are enabled on both the Firewall and Application Delivery Controllers as defined best practices from the vendors.
Transit through our PAN and F5 security stack which provides:
Anti-malware
IPS/IDS
Advanced WAF
AppID (vs port)
GeoIP Blocking
Security Rational
The general purpose of DMZ environment is to provide external facing services in a network environment that is known and expected to be hostile. Because this is an environment that is expected to be attacked by external entities we add additional protections and controls. For this standard we adding additional FER Requirements so that only explicitly defined and validated network traffic is enabled. This enables us to have detailed understanding of normal traffic and to detect anomalous network traffic.
Roles and Responsibilities:
Cybersecurity
Provide security controls to ensure secure connectivity with the internet and associated services
LOB Account Users
Leverage the FER process to request and document access to and from the DMZ.

--- Page: AWS IAM User Policy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=58896032
Labels: iam, users
Chunk 1/1
---

User authentication to AWS shall only be by
Federation
, therefore, an IAM Users in AWS is reserved for
service accounts
.
To learn more about service accounts refer to the
service account policy
.
IAM Users that are dedicated for programmatic access should not have console password enabled.
How to remove console access:
Login to AWS console and switch role to account in question.
Choose IAM from services and select Users from the left panel
Find and select the IAM user from the users list.
Click on Security credentials tab for the user in question.
Click on Manage next to Console password if Enabled.
Select the Disable radio button, and apply to prevent this user from being able to use console access

--- Page: AWS Internet Access Security Standard ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=55150513
Labels: aws, security, cloud
Chunk 1/1
---

What Is It?
Establishes a set of minimum security requirements for communication with the internet from the PGE AWS Cloud
When and Where Do We Use It?
This standard applies to:
Access to and from the internet from our AWS environment.
IaaS
PaaS
Standard
There is no direct access between the internet and PG&E AWS cloud compute resources.  All network communication with the internet will traverse the Transit network to utilize the appropriate security controls.
Examples of prohibited Services in accounts
Internet Gateways (IGW)
NAT Gateways
The Default VPC and associated IGW will be removed and is not authorized for use.
If created, resources in the default VPC may be deleted without notice at any time as part of a normal clean up process.
Use of AWS Regions outside of the United Sates.
If created, resources in AWS Regions outside of the US will be deleted without notice as part of a normal account monitoring processes.
Security Requirements in the Transit network
EDL blocking & alerts
Banned countries blocking & alerts
Advanced Security Services are enabled on both the Firewall and Application Delivery Controllers as defined best practices from the vendors.
Transit through our PAN and F5 security stack which provides:
Anti-malware
IPS/IDS
Advanced WAF
AppID (vs port)
Roles and Responsibilities:
Cybersecurity
Provide transit network and associated security controls to ensure secure connectivity with the internet.
LOB Account Users
Leverage Transit network for access to and from the internet.

--- Page: AWS Organizational Service Control Policies ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=60399411
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

By default the
FullAWSAccess
Service Control Policy (SCP) is attached to Root and all OUs in the tree. SCP's filter down the OU's starting at Root all the way to individual accounts, so the allow list SCP attached to Root or any ancestor OU determines which services will be available in an account. At PG&E we've replaced the default policy FullAWSAccess with a refined list of services
PGEProdQAAllowedServices
and attached this SCP to PGE-ProdAndQA.
Only the services in policy
PGEProdQAAllowedServices
attached PGE-ProdAndQA can be used in accounts in this OU tree, even if a child OU or account is allowing the service.
To allow for more granular control of which services can be used by an account, we attach a standard policy
SCPAllowStandardServices
that has the minimal approved services needed for all accounts, and an account specific policy
SCP-012345678910
with only the services allowed for that account.
CSCOE's Service Control Policies are maintained here...
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-lz-org-policies/tree/master/policies
The diagram illustrates where policies are attached, putting in guardrails of what services are allowed.
OutdatedList of services included and excluded from the
PGEProdQAAllowedServices policy.

--- Page: AWS Service Account ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=52691383
Labels: iam, users, svc, account
Chunk 1/1
---

A service account (SVC) in AWS usually refers to an IAM user that's created solely for a service or an application rather than a human user. These accounts often have programmatic access enabled, allowing them to interact with AWS resources via APIs, SDKs, or CLI.
A service account is appropriate when an application or service requires direct access to AWS resources and the application doesn't support role-based access or temporary security credentials. In such cases, the application may require an access key ID and a secret access key, which can be created for an IAM user (service account).
If the application or service can use role-based access or can assume a role, there is no need to use a service account. Roles provide a secure way to grant permissions to entities that you trust within your AWS environment. You can achieve cross-account access by creating a role in your account that other AWS accounts can assume. This avoids the need for sharing access keys or other credentials between accounts.
Restricting Access by IP Address
When a request comes in, we first check if it's coming from an AWS service
If it is an AWS service request, we allow access (internal operations)
If not from an AWS service, we check if the source IP is from approved networks
If from an approved network, allow access
Otherwise, deny the request
To enhance security, you must restrict access to IAM users (service accounts) by implementing an IP-based access control policy. This is critical because IAM users with access keys can be accessed from anywhere if compromised. A deny policy should be attached to every service account to ensure access is only allowed from approved networks or AWS services.
IMPORTANT: You must verify your specific IP ranges before implementing this policy. The IP ranges shown below are only examples and will not work for your environment. Consult with your network team to determine the correct CIDR blocks for your organization.
Below is the standard deny policy that should be attached to all IAM users/service accounts:
This policy works as follows:
It denies ALL actions on ALL resources UNLESS one of the following conditions is met:
The request is coming from an AWS service (aws:ViaAWSService is true) - this allows internal AWS service calls such as key rotation
The request is coming from one of the approved IP CIDR ranges (you must replace the example IPs with your actual approved networks)
When working with vendors, replace the CIDR blocks with their specific IP ranges. You can add multiple IP ranges as needed in the array. Do not proceed with implementation until you have confirmed the correct IP ranges for your environment.
Note: The "aws:ViaAWSService" condition is crucial for allowing AWS internal service operations to work properly, including key management operations that might be initiated through the console or programmatically.
Important Notice: Service Account Security Service accounts pose a significant risk due to the access keys that can be accessed from anywhere if compromised. To mitigate this risk, it is crucial to understand and review each use case thoroughly.
All requests for service accounts, including GitHub pull requests, must be accompanied by a Jira story. This story should clearly highlight why an access key is needed and provide detailed information on how it will be used. This ensures that the approver can make an informed judgment based on the details provided upfront.
Access Key Management
This page documents the standards for creation and maintenance of new and existing SVC accounts, and the distribution and rotation of the AWS access keys.
To request a new a service account use the
IAM pipeline
.
Create a new YAML file with the SVC account name under the relative path in the resources folder like the example below:
aws-lz-iam
/
resources
/
AccountNumber
/
service-accounts
/
SVC-A2077-Prod-Brinqa
.yaml
SVC account naming convention as: SVC-A{AMPS-ID}-{SCOPE}-{ServiceName}. Example: SVC-A2077-Dev-AppName.
Make sure the Name: in the yaml matches the name of the file.
Requests for a new SVC account go through Cybersecurity review
pgetech/ccoe-iam-security-reviewers
.
For initial creation of the IAM User the AWS access key will need to be generated by someone with admin access. One way to safely provide the access key is using secrets manager, attaching this policy in secrets manager will ensure only the right person can retrieve the access key.
After the owner is supplied with the access key, it will be up to the owner to rotate and maintain the access keys and secrets as needed.
A policy will automatically be attached to the service account to allow for access key rotation.
Note:
The resource may include /service-account: "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:user/
service-account/
${aws:username}"
Here are some examples of how to rotate the access key using CLI, but can also be adapted to other programming languages using API.
Rotating Access Keys FAQ
Q:
I no longer have my access key, how do I get a new one?
A:
Go into AWS IAM and generate a new key and delete the old one.
note: If your role does not have the necessary permissions, contact someone with CloudAdmin role or greater to create a new access key on your behalf.
Q:
What is an access key and when is it needed?
A:
An access key consists of an access key ID and secret access key, which are used to sign programmatic requests to AWS. They're required when an application only supports these static credentials for authentication, and can't assume an IAM role or use other means of temporary security credentials.
Q:
What credentials do I use to manage my access key?
A:
The IAM User has a policy attached that allows for the access keys to be managed, so use the existing access key to retrieve a new key and delete the old one.
Q:
Can I automatically rotate the access key?
A:
Yes, you can add the API calls to your program and rotate.
Q:
Once we disable a key would we have the ability to Re-Enable if needed?
A:
Yes, but a new key should be created to replace the aged key, otherwise it will get disabled again.
Q:
What is recommended method to store access keys?
A:
Secrets Manager in AWS can be used to securely store access keys.
Q:
How can I tell if an IAM User or access key is in use?
A:
Locate the User in AWS IAM, and check the Last Activity.
CloudTrail can also be used to check activity for an access key
https://aws.amazon.com/premiumsupport/knowledge-center/cloudtrail-search-for-activity/
Q:
How often will access keys over 90 days be disable?
A:
Approximately every 10 days, the 5th, 15th and 25th of each month.
Q:
Is it possible to use a service account for cross-account access?
A:
While technically possible by generating keys for a service account and manually inputting them into the other account, this practice is generally discouraged due to security reasons. Roles with cross-account policies are the preferred method for granting access across AWS accounts.
Legacy Service Accounts
There is a legacy process to create service accounts in the master account, this can be found on
Landing Zone
. This CloudFormation template is an accumulation of all IAM Users in the master account, and the template needs to be updated manually, there is no CodePipeline. It is recommended to use the new process in aws-lz-iam even for new SVC accounts in master.
Refer to IAM Users policy for more information about AWS IAM Users :
Or here for more information on access keys:

Labels: none
Chunk 1/1

What Is It?
The Cybersecurity Cloud Accounts AWS Access Key Policy establishes security requirements for AWS access key. Typically, AWS access keys are used in REST or HTTP query protocol requests against cloud service providers instead of username and password. Some providers (esp., cloud providers such as AWS), AWS access key is associated with a secret as well and are used together for the said operations. Any AWS access key not conforming to policy requirements must be reviewed and approved by Cybersecurity. An AWS access key failing to comply with the policy requirements may be disabled after subsequent warning(s).
When and Where Do We Use It?
In AWS Cloud’s PGE-Master account, users are created for specific accounts.  Each of these account-users has an AWS Key generated for programmatic access. i.e. 3
rd
party tools requiring access to cloud. Those are detected and remediated.  Although AWS command-line users generate AWS Access Keys, those keys expire in 1 hour thus they are compliant and not remediated. Finally, Cybersecurity looks across accounts of interest for non-compliant users and their access keys.  Those are detected and remediated.
Standard
AWS access keys and associated secrets if any needs to be re-generated every 90 day(s).
AWS access key-owners notified when keys age > 60 days
AWS access key-owners notified when keys age > 70 days
AWS access key-owners notified when keys age > 80 days
AWS access keys disabled when keys age > 90 days
Additional References
Below is the PDF file attachment and more standards are available at
PGE Cybersecurity standards
.

--- Page: Cloud Accounts Password Policy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=57185785
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What Is It?
The Cybersecurity Cloud Accounts Password Policy establishes security requirements for user and service account passwords. A password policy is a set of rules that define the type of password that can be set. Any user or service account with password not confirming to any of the policy requirements must be reviewed by Cybersecurity and approved. Any user or service account with password failing to comply with the policy requirements will be removed without any warning.
When and Where Do We Use It?
Whenever an individual user account or a service account is created in any of the cloud service providers (AWS Azure, GCP). The password for the accounts, i.e., AWS, Azure, GCP must comply with the standards below.
Standard
Minimum password length is 15 characters
Require at least one uppercase letter from Latin alphabet (A-Z)
Require at least one lowercase letter from Latin alphabet (a-Z)
Require at least one number
Require at least one non-alphanumeric character (!@#$%^&*()_+-=[]{}|')
Password expires in 120 day(s)
Allow users to change their own password
Remember last 4 password(s) and prevent reuse
Exceptions
A user or service account without any password is exempt from this password policy.
Additional References
PGE Cybersecurity standards
document
SEC-3005S
section “
Appendix A- Authentication and Password Settings”
The standard is attached to this wiki page

URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=57188316

Document Information
ID: CSTD-2020
CLOUDCOE-4436
Version: 1.0
Last Updated:
3/4/2020
Review Cycle:
Monthly
Next Review:
3/24/2020
Authors:
Daniel Wong, Cory Kantar and Balaji Venkataraman
Accountable:
Cybersecurity Cloud Security Center of Excellence
Consulted:
Cybersecurity Risk & Strategy Consulting
Cybersecurity Services
Informed:
Cybersecurity
Related Standards:
What Is It?
Establishes a lifecycle standard in the Commercial Cloud where non-serverless instances are to be replaced every 15 days for vulnerability mitigation and to embrace immutable infrastructure practices where our instances are cattle vs pets.
When and Where Do We Use It?
This standard applies any non-serverless environments especially EC2 AMIs and/or Containerized Docker instances.
Standard
Currently most servers follow the “mutable” infrastructure practice where upgrades are done in-place, v1 to v2 patching and so on.  Data and/or customized configurations are already available so it’s a convenience to perform an in-place patching and/or upgrade.  This upgrade process has the downside of introducing risk, upgrade complexity and is not scalable.
The more mature model is to adopt the “immutable” infrastructure approach where upgrade is
never
done in-place. Once the server exists, we never try to upgrade it to V2.  Instead a new version is created off a golden AMI and data is to be attached since it’s always externalized: S3, EFS, EBS etc. Any running non-severless instances with an up-time of 15 days or more will have to be re-instantiated with the latest available golden AMI.
Roles and Responsibilities:
Cybersecurity
Develop a process of auditing and alerting instances that have a runtime greater than 15 days old.
Cloud COE Ops
Develop a continuous deployment and testing process to provision the non-serverless instances every 15 days or ANY time when risk mitigation is required.
LOB Account Users/Developers
users responsibility for making sure they are complying with the policy users responsibility for making sure they are complying with the policy
Application owners are responsible to comply with this policy.
All data need to be externally stored as the non-serverless instances will be re-created every 15 days.
Start adapting the serverless mythology in the application development life cycle when and where possible such as AWS Fargate, Lambda, S3, Elastic Beanstalk and others.
References:
AWS Serverless Application Model,
https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/
AWS Fargate,
https://aws.amazon.com/fargate/
AWS Lamdba,
https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/
What is Mutable vs. Immutable Infrastructure?
https://www.hashicorp.com/resources/what-is-mutable-vs-immutable-infrastructure

Related Standards:
What Is It?
The Cloud Instance Security Group Policy establishes a set of minimum-security requirements for security groups. In conforming to best practices PG&E should limit network access to only that which is required. All Non-Cybersecurity appliances (instances) requiring public IP access must be documented and approved by Cybersecurity.
When and Where Do We Use It?
Wherever security groups can be used in PG&E's public cloud environments.
Standard
Inbound access from 0.0.0.0/0 to common port is not permitted. Security groups are required to be least privilege. This would always include the network Port, Protocol and smallest network range or security group possible.
Best practice is to use security group to security group rules, or when necessary the minimum required CIDR.

--- Page: Cloud Interactive Server Access Standard ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=60392524
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What Is It?
The Cybersecurity Cloud Interactive Access Standard establishes allowed boundaries when it is appropriate to have remote interactive access (SSH or RDP) to cloud workloads, and what tools support those conditions. This standard is line with SEC-3006S and takes into account conditions such as Segregation of duties (SOD) and the nature of cloud environments where roles are different than traditional datacenter environments (e.g. full stack engineering teams). Server Access is defined as SSH or RDP access to an ec2 instance, container, etc.
Within the cloud environment, direct access from user networks on ports 22 and 3389 for administration are expressly prohibited.
When and Where Do We Use It?
This standard should be used in conjunction with all Cloud Service Providers (CSP’s). Commercial cloud environments should leverage immutable infrastructure concepts where workloads are treated as cattle vs pets.  When organizations are successful in this shift and have matured their CI/CD and configuration management practices, interactive access to QA and Prod environments is no longer necessary.
Standard
Exceptions
Exceptions to this will be granted on a case by case basis and documented using the Cyber Risk Exceptions for Risk Acceptance process.  Exceptions for using PAPM in QA/Prod environments take into consideration requirements from SEC-3005S and SEC-3006S such as activity monitoring, audit trails and management supervision.  Exception intake is found under “
Exceptions for Cybersecurity Risk Assessment
”

document

and

Enterprise Zone Strategy – AWS Addendum
The purpose of this document is to define security Zones for our AWS Public Cloud Environment. This document extends the Network Protection Services Enterprise Zone Strategy found
here.
This strategy is based on the AWS Best Practices for an AWS multi-account landing zone,
link.
Commercial Cloud network is based on Zero Trust
AWS Trust Level 0
– Zones that contain ICS or Control system data.
AWS Trust Level 1
– Zones containing Business systems that are owned and operated by PGE
AWS Trust Level 2
– Zones containing Business systems but are managed by a 3
rd
party on behalf of PGE
AWS Trust Level 6
– Internet
Commercial Cloud Zone definition:
A Commercial cloud zone is the combination of an Application and its associated environment. For example, App-2101 and non-prod would define a zone.
In our current AWS design we have a 1:1:1 relationship between Account, Application and Environment.  Therefore, an AWS Account is synonymous with a Commercial Cloud Zone.
Connectivity between Security Zones
General requirements
These requirements apply to all network communication in AWS:
All communication between zones must be inspected for threats, virus and other malicious traffic using PGE standard tools.
Intra-Zone traffic must be restricted to least privilege using AWS Native Security Groups
AWS ACL’s are generally not used and enable any:any traffic by default.
Base Rules Access to AWS TL6
Http/Https traffic with Url Filtering the only specified categories

Users access AWS Zones
All access to the AWS environment is through a user access system,
SSM and Beyond Trust are the current tools used for IRA to EC2 Instances.
IRA to the AWS Platform will use MEA Groups and the IAM Pipeline.
Firewall Ruleset
All firewalls will deny traffic until otherwise permitted
All ports opened on any firewall must have accompanying justification, documented using the Firewall Exception Request process.
Security rules that allow interactive remote access, will include a matching condition of an Active Directory user or group.
All Security rules will be configured to employ intrusion detection/prevention capabilities.
All security rules, where possible, will include the vendors firewall application definitions vice port and protocol.
All security rules will log at session end to the approved syslog collector.
Intrusion detection/prevention capabilities will be maintained for
client-to-server
and
server-to-client
directional flows.
Firewall rules that do not have valid-logged-traffic will be disabled after 365 days of non-use. Validity of traffic will be based on rule analysis, session counts, amount of data moved, and frequency of use.

Cloud Specific Key Management Service enables customers to manage cryptographic keys to encrypt and protect data, along with controlling access to applications.  PGE requires all data and services that operate in a cloud environment utilize a key for encryption of data at rest and in transit, per our Cloud Security Standard.  Furthermore, our Cyber Security’s Service Adoption Framework (SAF) identifies
customer managed keys (CMKs) must be used with 'Confidential' or 'Restricted' data. AWS managed keys (KMS) are approved for general encryption at rest requirements.
When and Where Do We Use It?

Why use Native KMS options:
·         Seamless integration between services within a cloud service provider
·         Programmatic management can be achieved
·         Creation and Ownership can be distributed to Account Owners
·         Less reliance on central personal to provide account specific options
·         SLA parity to regional services for a cloud service provider
Functions of a KMS:
·         Create, edit, and view symmetric and asymmetric keys
·         Enable and disable keys
·         Create, edit, and view key policies and grants for your keys
·         Enable and disable automatic rotation of the cryptographic material in a key
·         Tag your keys for identification, automation, and cost tracking
·         Create, delete, list, and update aliases, which are friendly names for your keys
·         Delete keys to complete the key lifecycle management
Function of a Key:
·         Symmetric or asymmetric encrypt and decrypt functions
·         Message Signing and Verification
·         Generate random numbers suitable for cryptographic applications
·         Envelope Encryption Functions
Key Types/Usages:
·         Symmetric Keys – Single key used for data encryption and decryption functions
·         Asymmetric Keys – Consist of a Public/Private key pair used for signing, verification and encryption functions.

--- Page: Cloud Security SuperAdmin Role Access Policy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=308773093
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Introduction
The
AWS-A2102-All-SuperAdmin
role grants full access to all AWS accounts, including the master/organization account. To ensure security and manage access effectively, we propose setting a cap on the number of users with this role.
Policy Statement
Full-Time Access:
A maximum of 5 full-time employees will be granted the AWS-A2102-All-SuperAdmin role.
POD Access:
Additional users can be granted the AWS-A2102-All-SuperAdmin role on a temporary basis (POD) for a maximum of 72 hours, with a typical duration of 24 hours.
Implementation Details
Approval Process:
All requests for full-time access must be approved by the Cloud Security Team. POD requests will go through MEA, and a duration must be set to a maximum of 72 hours for approval to be considered.
Access Review:
Regular reviews will be conducted to ensure compliance with this policy. Any unauthorized access will be revoked immediately.
Logging and Monitoring:
All activities performed using the AWS-A2102-All-SuperAdmin role will be logged and monitored for security purposes.
Responsibilities
Cloud Security Team:
Responsible for managing and enforcing this policy.
Users:
Must adhere to the access limits and ensure that their activities comply with organizational security standards.
Conclusion
By implementing this policy, we aim to enhance the security of our AWS environment and ensure that access to critical resources is managed effectively.

--- Page: Cloud Vulnerability Management Policy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=57188554
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Document Information
ID: CSTD-2020
CLOUDCOE-4603
Version: 1.0
Last Updated:
5/2/2022
Review Cycle:
Annually
Next Review:
5/2/2023
Author:
Accountable:
Cybersecurity Cloud Security Center of Excellence
Consulted:
Cybersecurity Risk & Strategy Consulting
Cybersecurity Services
Informed:
Cybersecurity
Information Technology
Related Standards:
Cloud Instance Lifecycle Policy
What Is It?
Establishes a vulnerability management policy to ensure that critical and high vulnerabilities are patched and remediated.
When and Where Do We Use It?
Servers (EC2, etc)
Containers (EKS, ECS, Fargate, etc)
Serverless (Lambda)
Source Code (vulnerabilities within code in Github, etc.)
SAF and Prisma Configuration Detection
Any approved AWS services
Standard
As a part of our move towards an immutable infrastructure, we leverage our policies to influence how applications will be designed in the cloud to be compliant with our policies. We leverage a number of factors such as  the
CVSS weighting scores
and Environment to determine the Severity of a vulnerability.  The Severity of the vulnerability determines the SLA.  The SLA's for the various patch severity is as follows:
Severity
SLA
Sev 5*
72 hours
Sev 4
14 days (Windows Cloud Host Vulnerabilities: 60 Days)
Sev 3
21 days
Sev 5 vulnerabilities take into consideration additional context such as active exploits in the wild, zero day, context of the asset such as internet facing or crown jewel.
Termination Policy
After the determined SLA's have expired, without a documented exception through the Cybersecurity exception process, the instances could be shutdown if there is a critical business risk.  Any future instances within the application running these vulnerabilities can have a policy applied that prevents the application from execution (run time enforcement).
Roles and Responsibilities:
Cloud Security COE and Compliance Metrics and Reporting
Measure the SLA and provide notifications as time is nearing expiry
Determine if the termination instances that have not been remediated within SLA is required based on risk to business
Determine if it is required to prevent further application launch with similar vulnerability in the application landscape due to business risk
Partner with LOB for remediation of all Cloud Vulnerabilities
LOB Account Users/Developers
Leverage best practices and follow the cloud policies.
Application owners are responsible to comply with this policy.
Develop a patching and upgrade process patch critical and high vulnerabilities within 15 days.
Maintain up to date asset information and meta-data
Document any exceptions that are unable to be remediated per IT-5301P-01
All data needs to be externally stored as the non-serverless instances will be re-created every 15 days.
Start adapting the serverless mythology in the application development life cycle when and where possible such as AWS Fargate, Lambda, S3, Elastic Beanstalk and others.
References:
AWS Serverless Application Model,
https://aws.amazon.com/serverless/sam/
AWS Fargate,
https://aws.amazon.com/fargate/
AWS Lamdba,
https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/
What is Mutable vs. Immutable Infrastructure?
https://www.hashicorp.com/resources/what-is-mutable-vs-immutable-infrastructure
CVSS
https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss

--- Page: IAM Least Privilege ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=229311359
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Implementing the Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP) in AWS
Objective
: To ensure that all entities within our AWS environment (users, roles, services) have only the necessary permissions to perform their tasks, thereby reducing the potential attack surface and protecting our resources.
1. AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM)
:
Federated Roles:
User roles are cross account assume roles from pge-master account to the line of business account.
Accessing AWS Accounts via IAM Federation and AssumeRole - Cloud COE - PGE Wiki
Roles for AWS Services
: Use IAM roles for AWS services that need to interact with resources. Assign permissions to roles and let services assume these roles.
Roles Anywhere:
Roles Anywhere is a feature in
AWS IAM
that lets you use temporary credentials to access AWS resources from a non-AWS workload or application. For example, you could use Roles Anywhere to allow an on-premises database to access data in an S3 bucket.
Example:
https://github.com/pgetech/pge-roles-anywhere
IAM Users for Service Accounts:
In most cases we do not permit IAM Users due to their long term credentials. Engage the Product Owner and CSCOE for design review.
AWS Service Account - Cloud COE - PGE Wiki
2. Multi-Account Strategy
:
Separate AWS accounts for different environments (dev, staging, prod) or departments. This isolates resources and reduces the risk of accidental changes or exposures.
3. Permission Boundaries
:
Set permission boundaries for IAM entities. These act as a maximum permissions limit that can be granted to the entities.
Using Permissions Boundaries
4. Use AWS Organizations
:
Implement Service Control Policies (SCPs) to define a baseline of permissions across accounts.
5. Regularly Audit Permissions with Prisma Cloud CIEM
:
Use Prisma Cloud CIEM Policy
"
AWS IAM effective permissions are over-privileged (90 days)"
to see service permissions granted to a user or role and when those services were last accessed. Revoke permissions that aren't being used.
6. Monitor and Alarm with AWS CloudTrail and AWS Config
:
Monitor API calls and changes to resources using CloudTrail. Set up alarms for unexpected or unauthorized actions.
Use AWS Config to continuously monitor and record configuration changes and evaluate these against desired configurations.
7. Implement Just-In-Time Privilege Escalation
:
Users start with minimum permissions. If elevated permissions are needed temporarily, grant them on-the-fly and auto-revoke after a specified period.
8. Rotate Credentials Regularly
:
Rotate access keys, passwords, and tokens regularly. Implement automatic key rotations for IAM roles if possible.
9. Secure Access Points
:
Lock down security groups, VPCs, and network ACLs. Ensure resources are not unintentionally exposed to the public.
10. Educate and Train Teams
:
Continuous training sessions for the team on the importance of PoLP and best practices in AWS.
Closing Thoughts
: Implementing PoLP in AWS requires an ongoing commitment to security, regular audits, and continuous training. The steps outlined above serve as a foundational approach, but a successful PoLP strategy must be adaptable to the unique needs and architecture of each AWS environment.

--- Page: MCP (Model Context Protocol) Security Policy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=338472104
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Policy Overview
This policy establishes mandatory security requirements for all Model Context Protocol (MCP) implementations within our organization. MCP enables AI systems to interact with external tools and services, but this capability introduces significant security risks that require strict controls.
OWASP LLM Risk Alignment
This policy directly addresses several OWASP Top 10 LLM (2025) security risks:
*
LLM06: Excessive Agency (HIGH RISK)
* - MCP tools can grant AI systems dangerous levels of autonomy
*
LLM03: Supply Chain (MEDIUM RISK)
* - Untrusted MCP servers introduce supply chain vulnerabilities
*
LLM01: Prompt Injection (HIGH RISK)
* - Malicious prompts could trigger unauthorized MCP tool usage
*
LLM02: Sensitive Information Disclosure (HIGH RISK)
* - MCP tools may access or expose confidential data
Core Requirements
1. Human-in-the-Loop Mandatory (Mitigates: LLM06 Excessive Agency)
All MCP tool actions require explicit human approval
No automated or batch approvals permitted
Users must review tool name, parameters, and impact before approval
*
Rationale
*: Prevents AI systems from executing actions autonomously without human oversight
2. MCP Server Security Controls (Mitigates: LLM03 Supply Chain)
MCP servers follow standard open source software security practices
All servers must pass JFrog Xray security scanning
Servers distributed only through JFrog Artifactory proxy
Use only MCP servers from trusted, well-maintained sources
*
Rationale
*: Prevents supply chain attacks from malicious or vulnerable MCP servers
3. Comprehensive Logging and Monitoring (Mitigates: Multiple Risks)
All MCP interactions logged with full audit trail
Integration with SIEM for anomaly detection
Regular compliance audits required
*
Rationale
*: Enables detection of prompt injection attempts and unauthorized tool usage
4. Data Protection Controls (Mitigates: LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure)
MCP servers must operate under least privilege principles
No access to sensitive data without explicit approval
Data classification requirements for MCP tool inputs/outputs
*
Rationale
*: Prevents accidental exposure of confidential information through AI tool usage
MCP-Specific Risk Assessment
Implementation Timeline for Teams
Development teams wanting to use MCP must ensure compliance with all security requirements outlined in this policy, including:
Human-in-the-loop approval for all MCP tool actions
Comprehensive logging and monitoring of MCP interactions
Standard open source software security practices
Data protection controls and least privilege access
Related Documentation
OWASP Risk Details:
OWASP LLM Top 10 Mitigation Guide
Official PG&E GenAI Governance:
Generative AI at PG&E
Compliance and Enforcement
Violation of this policy may result in:
Immediate suspension of MCP access
Security incident investigation
Disciplinary action per company policy
Contact and Support

MCP Security Questions
*: CSCOE_OPS@pge.com
*
General AI Governance
*: ai.data.science@pge.com
*
Technical Implementation Support
*: Contact DevSecOps team

chunks.page_title: OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Models - Mitigation Guide
chunks.content:
--- Page: OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Models - Mitigation Guide ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=338472097
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Models (2025) - Mitigation Guide
Document Information
*
Audience
*: Security engineers, AI developers, and platform owners deploying LLM applications
*
Owner
*: Cloud Security COE Operations Team
*
Contact
*: CSCOE_OPS@pge.com
LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection (Risk Level: HIGH)
Risk Description
A Prompt Injection Vulnerability occurs when user prompts alter the LLM's intended behavior, potentially leading to unauthorized actions, data disclosure, or privilege escalation.
Mitigation Strategies
Implement input validation and sanitization
Use structured prompts with clear role definitions
Deploy prompt injection detection tools (e.g., Microsoft Presidio, Lakera Guard)
Implement human-in-the-loop for sensitive operations
Use separate models for different privilege levels
Implementation
Deploy content filtering before model input
Use prompt templates with parameter binding
Monitor for unusual prompt patterns in SIEM
Regular security testing with injection payloads
—
LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure (Risk Level: HIGH)
Risk Description
Sensitive information can affect both the LLM and its application through unintentional disclosure of confidential data, PII, or proprietary information through model responses.
Mitigation Strategies
Implement data loss prevention (DLP)
Use data classification and labeling
Deploy sensitive data detection tools
Implement output filtering and redaction
Implementation
Pre-processing data sanitization
Real-time output scanning for PII/secrets
Access controls based on data classification
Regular privacy impact assessments
—
LLM03:2025 Supply Chain (Risk Level: MEDIUM)
Risk Description
LLM supply chains are susceptible to various vulnerabilities which can affect the integrity of training data, ML models, and deployment platforms.
Mitigation Strategies
Vet all third-party AI components
Use only approved model repositories
Implement vulnerability scanning
Maintain AI component inventory
Implementation
JFrog Xray scanning for AI dependencies
Approved AI service registry (AWS Bedrock, etc.)
Regular security assessments of AI suppliers
Component versioning and update management
—
LLM04:2025 Data and Model Poisoning (Risk Level: MEDIUM)
Risk Description
Data poisoning occurs when pre-training, fine-tuning, or embedding data is manipulated to introduce vulnerabilities, backdoors, or biases that could compromise the model's security, effectiveness, or ethical behavior.
Mitigation Strategies
Use only approved, vetted training datasets
Implement data provenance tracking
Regular model behavior auditing
Secure the training pipeline
Implementation
Data source validation and certification
Automated anomaly detection in training data
Model performance baseline monitoring
Secure development lifecycle for AI models
—
LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling (Risk Level: MEDIUM)
Risk Description
Improper Output Handling refers specifically to insufficient validation, sanitization, and handling of the outputs generated by large language models before they are passed downstream to other components and systems.
Mitigation Strategies
Validate and sanitize all LLM outputs
Implement output encoding for web contexts
Use allowlists for acceptable output formats
Apply principle of least privilege to output processing
Implementation
Content Security Policy (CSP) for web outputs
Output validation libraries and frameworks
Sandboxed execution environments
Regular security testing of output handlers
—
LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency (Risk Level: HIGH)
Risk Description
An LLM-based system is often granted a degree of agency by the developer – the ability to call functions or interface with other systems to undertake actions in response to a prompt.
Mitigation Strategies
Implement human oversight requirements
Use role-based access controls
Limit autonomous operation scope
Implement approval workflows
Implementation
Mandatory human-in-the-loop controls
Action confirmation systems
Audit trails for all AI actions
Escalation procedures for high-risk operations
—
LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage (Risk Level: MEDIUM)
Risk Description
The system prompt leakage vulnerability in LLMs refers to the risk of exposing underlying system prompts, instructions, or configuration details that were intended to remain hidden.
Mitigation Strategies
Design robust system prompts that resist leakage attempts
Implement output filtering to detect system prompt exposure
Use separate models for system vs. user interactions
Regular testing for prompt leakage vulnerabilities
Implementation
System prompt isolation techniques
Output monitoring for leaked instructions
Prompt engineering best practices
Regular security assessments
—
LLM08:2025 Vector and Embedding Weaknesses (Risk Level: MEDIUM)
Risk Description
Vectors and embeddings vulnerabilities present significant security risks in systems that rely on Large Language Models, Vector databases, and embedding technologies.
Mitigation Strategies
Implement secure vector storage and retrieval
Validate embedding inputs and outputs
Use access controls for vector databases
Monitor for embedding manipulation attacks
Implementation
Secure vector database configuration
Embedding validation frameworks
Access logging for vector operations
Regular integrity checks of embeddings
—
LLM09:2025 Misinformation (Risk Level: MEDIUM)
Risk Description
Misinformation from LLMs poses a core vulnerability for applications relying on these models, as they can generate content that appears credible but is factually incorrect, outdated, or misleading.
Mitigation Strategies
Implement fact-checking mechanisms
Provide confidence scores for outputs
Require human validation for critical decisions
Maintain fallback procedures
Implementation
Multi-source validation requirements
Output confidence indicators
Critical decision approval processes
Regular accuracy auditing
—
LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption (Risk Level: MEDIUM)
Risk Description
Unbounded Consumption refers to the process where a Large Language Model is fed variable-length inputs that exceed its context length or computational limits, leading to resource exhaustion, degraded performance, or denial of service.
Mitigation Strategies
Implement rate limiting and throttling
Set resource quotas per user/application
Monitor resource consumption patterns
Use caching for common requests
Implementation
API rate limiting (requests per minute/hour)
Circuit breakers for overloaded services
Cost monitoring and alerting
Load balancing and auto-scaling
—
Enterprise Implementation Checklist
Monitoring and Detection
Deploy SIEM rules for AI-specific threats
Monitor API usage patterns and anomalies
Track model performance and accuracy metrics
Regular vulnerability assessments
Penetration testing including AI-specific scenarios
Related Documentation
../Policies-and-Standards/MCP-Security-Policy.txt
../Implementation-Guides/Human-in-the-Loop-Standards.txt
../Security-Reviews-and-Compliance/AI-Security-Assessment.txt
PG&E GenAI Acceptable Use Policy
(IT-2002)
Support and Questions
*
Security Questions
*: CSCOE_OPS@pge.com
*
General AI Governance
*: ai.data.science@pge.com
*
Technical Implementation
*: Contact DevSecOps team

--- Page: Capabilities expected in PAC products ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=271615303
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Environment
: Product should have capability to maintain different enforcement level per environment. For ex. a policy in dev is made soft-mandatory while it is mandatory in production
Suppression
: A policy should be able to get suppressed in a pipeline and that has to be memorized with pipeline as scope
Module vs Resource
: Product should be able to support both Terraform module and Terraform resource validation.
Nested module
: Product should have the capability to validate deep nested Terraform modules. For ex. product should look for compliance in all resources in all child modules starting from root module however deep they are nested.
Point out code
: When a product identifies an issue about a non compliant resource block, it should  be able to point to the piece of code that is causing this non-compliance
Automation
: Product should be able to support a pipeline or some form of automation to publish the custom policy that is being developed
One policy for both build and Runtime
: It would be nice to have this feature where a custom policy once built supports both runtime and build time validation instead of writing separate policies for each
