--- Page: Claude Code — Safe Usage Guide ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364979118
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Purpose
This guide establishes mandatory safety practices for all engineers using Claude Code (Anthropic's CLI agent). Claude Code is a powerful tool that can read, write, and execute code on your behalf. That power demands disciplined usage. The guidelines below are non-negotiable until the security team indicates otherwise.
You are responsible for everything Claude Code does on your behalf.
AI-generated code, commands, and changes are executed under your identity, with your permissions, on your machine. "The AI did it" is not a valid justification. If you approve a command, you own the outcome — the same way you own any code you commit or any command you run manually. Review accordingly.
1. Use Claude Code Only Through Portkey
All Claude Code usage
must
be routed through
Portkey
(our AI gateway). Portkey provides:
Centralized logging and audit trails for every request and response
Spend controls and rate limiting
Policy enforcement and content guardrails
Visibility into what models are being invoked and by whom
Do not
connect Claude Code directly to the Anthropic API using personal API keys. If you do not have Portkey access configured, contact the platform team before using Claude Code.
2. Do Not Install MCP Servers or Skills From the Public Internet
This is an extreme supply chain risk.
MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers and third-party skills extend what Claude Code can access and do. Any MCP server you attach runs with the same permissions as your local session — it can read your files, access your credentials, and execute commands.
Do not
install MCP servers from GitHub, npm, PyPI, or any other public registry
Do not
attach community-built skills or plugins to your Claude Code configuration
Do not
copy-paste MCP server configurations from blog posts, tutorials, or social media
We are actively building an
MCP Gateway
that will provide vetted, centrally managed MCP servers with proper security controls, allow-listing, and audit logging.
Until the MCP Gateway is available, treat all third-party MCP servers as untrusted code running on your machine.
If you have a legitimate need for an MCP server integration, raise it with the security team so we can evaluate and provision it safely.
3. Always Review What Claude Code Is Executing
Claude Code will ask for permission before performing potentially dangerous actions (file writes, shell commands, etc.).
Do not blindly approve these requests.
Specifically:
Never select "Always Allow"
for broad permission categories. Review each action individually. It takes seconds to read what is being run — it can take hours to recover from a mistake.
Never use
--dangerously-skip-permissions
or any flag that bypasses the permission model. These flags exist for narrow CI/CD automation scenarios, not for interactive use.
Read the command before approving it.
Claude Code is not infallible. It may generate a command that:
Deletes files or directories unintentionally
Overwrites uncommitted work
Installs unexpected dependencies
Runs destructive operations (force pushes, database drops, etc.)
Exposes credentials or secrets in shell history
This is usually not malicious — it's just wrong
Claude Code does not have adversarial intent. When mistakes happen, it is almost always because the model misunderstood context, hallucinated a path or flag, or chose an unnecessarily destructive approach to a simple problem. The permission prompts are your safety net.
Use them.
4. General Hygiene
Practice
Why
Do not paste secrets, tokens, or passwords into Claude Code prompts
They may be logged, cached, or echoed in output
Work in version-controlled directories with committed state
So you can
git diff
and revert if something goes wrong
Do not run Claude Code with elevated privileges (sudo, root, admin)
Limits blast radius of any mistake
Review generated code before merging or deploying
Treat it like any other code review — it needs human sign-off
Keep Claude Code updated
Anthropic ships security and stability fixes regularly
5. Summary of Hard Rules
Rule
Status
Route all usage through Portkey
Mandatory
No third-party MCP servers or skills
Blocked until MCP Gateway is available
No "Always Allow" for permissions
Mandatory
No
--dangerously-skip-permissions
Blocked for interactive use
Review every command before approval
Mandatory
Questions or Exceptions
If you have a use case that conflicts with these guidelines, reach out to the security team. We would rather help you find a safe path than have you work around the guardrails.
Resources:
Acceptable AI use policy:
AICOE Sharepoint

chunks.page_title: OWASP Top Ten Responsible AI standards
chunks.content:
--- Page: OWASP Top Ten Responsible AI standards ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=308775871
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

When developing a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) chatbot using AWS Bedrock, it’s essential to consider security best practices to protect against common vulnerabilities. The OWASP Top Ten for Large Language Models (LLMs) provides a useful framework for this. Here are some key considerations tailored to a RAG chatbot:
Insecure Output Handling
: Ensure that the chatbot’s responses are properly sanitized to prevent the disclosure of sensitive information.
Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
: Use trusted sources for all components and regularly update dependencies to mitigate risks from third-party software.
Sensitive Information Disclosure
: Implement strict access controls and data encryption to protect sensitive data.
Excessive Agency
: Limit the chatbot’s capabilities to prevent it from performing unintended actions.
Overreliance
: Avoid over-dependence on the chatbot for critical decisions without human oversight.
Prompt Injection
: Validate and sanitize user inputs to prevent malicious commands from being executed.
Model Exploitation
: Regularly update and monitor the LLM to protect against known vulnerabilities.
Insecure Communication Channels
: Use secure protocols (e.g., HTTPS) to protect data in transit.
Inadequate Logging and Monitoring
: Implement comprehensive logging and monitoring to detect and respond to security incidents.
Lack of Responsible AI Governance
: Establish policies for ethical AI use, including bias detection and mitigation.
References
:
https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/llm-top-10-governance-doc/LLM_AI_Security_and_Governance_Checklist-v1.pdf

--- Page: AWS Landing Zone ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=49962426
Labels: ccoe-product, ccoe-landing-zone
Chunk 1/1
---

Valid status: Concept, Development, Preview, Pilot, General Availability, Decommission
List of Lifecycle Stage
Description
Scalable, secure environment to run PG&E workloads in AWS.
CCOE Team
List of Cloud COE Teams
Teams Channel
dynTeam-Landing Zone
Product Owner
Overview
The AWS Landing Zone for the PG&E environment is solution that helps PG&E product teams quickly setup secure accounts for their project needs.  The Landing Zone enables teams to spend less time on account level tasks and allows them to spend more time providing value to the business by focusing on their application code and business logic.
This solution can help save time by automating the set-up of an environment for running secure and scalable workloads while implementing an initial security baseline through the creation of core accounts and resources. It also provides a baseline environment to get started with a multi-account architecture, identity and access management, governance, data security, network design, and logging.
The Landing Zone 'bakes in' a large number of features and capabilities that product teams have traditionally had to wrestle with in the past.
Features and Benefits
Accounts are federated with PG&Es Active Directory service so you do not have to worry about secure login to the AWS console or remember to remove employees that leave the company
AWS Config will be automatically enabled in your account enabling configuration monitoring and even the new remediation features AWS recently released
AWS Guard Duty will be automatically enabled in your account enabling intelligent threat detection and continuous monitoring
Accounts automatically integrate with Logging Central, a centralized log collection and archiving solution for enforcing the collection of and storage of logs produced in PG&E Organization AWS Accounts
S3 access logs are automatically captured and sent to Logging Central and the Security Incident Operations Center (SIOC)
Audit logs for changes in AWS and S3 Access Logs are collected by CloudTrail and sent to S3 buckets managed by Logging Central.
Network traffic logs (a.k.a., VPC Flow Logs), CloudFront, and Relational Database Services logs are also collected and stored in Logging Central managed S3 buckets.
Logs are being generated in PG&E Organization AWS Accounts for Lambda, API Gateways, Elastic Load Balancers (ELB/ALB/NLB), EC2 instances, and CloudWatch (coming to Logging Central shortly)
All Landing Zone accounts are create in AWS organization allowing for consolidated billing.  You no longer have to strike your own contract with AWS or put your AWS bill on your P-Card
Landing Zone accounts have Tag enforcement and notification for many of the services allowing for proper accounting as well as aligning with AMPS
Redlock is automatically enabled to monitor your AWS configuration for errors, configurations that may expose us to unintended risk or configuration that are not consistent with Best Practices.
Coming Soon (early April)
- Lambda Authorizers will automatically be included in your account upon provisioning so that you can quickly and securely deploy APIs
Coming Soon (early April)
- Tenable for monitoring your instances for vulnerabilities and assigning a risk to each vulnerability so that we prioritize and remediate based on risk
Getting Started
Contact the Cloud CoE and one of their engineers will sit down with you and help you kick off your account creation.  Come prepared with an AMPS ID and information about your application (e.g. application owner, notification email address, order number, etc.).  The CoE engineer will help guide you through sizing your account and the appropriate network allocation.  Once the account creation form is filled out, you will kick off account creation before end the sit down.  Account creation takes less than 45 minutes and integrates all of the features and benefits listed above automatically.
FAQs
Q. How do I get an account in the landing zone for my team?
A. While the Intake process is evolving, please go to
https://wiki.comp.pge.com/display/CCE/Intake+Process
to start the process.
Q. How long will it take to get an AWS account for my team?
A. Currently, account creation takes about a day plus any necessary time required for the PG&E Cybersecurity team to connect your account VPNs
Q. I can already provision an AWS account by logging in to the AWS console and giving them my credit card.  Why can’t I just do that for my team?
A. Landing zone accounts have numerous security and operational features built in saving you time and money.  Plus, only Landing Zone accounts can connect to the PG&E back office via AWS Direct Connect.
Q. What is AWS Direct Connect?
A. AWS Direct Connect is a cloud service solution that makes it easy to establish a dedicated network connection from your premises to AWS. Using AWS Direct Connect, you can establish private connectivity between AWS and your datacenter, office, or colocation environment, which in many cases can reduce your network costs, increase bandwidth throughput, and provide a more consistent network experience than Internet-based connections.
Q. If AWS Direct Connect connects my AWS account to the PG&E back office, does that mean I can make calls to SAP, GIS, CC&B, and other enterprise systems from my new AWS account?
A. This is a popular question and rest assured that we are putting large amounts of effort in to bring this feature to you as soon as possible.  Our goal is to provide you secure access to back office resources via AWS API Gateway secured by Lambda Authorizers that leverage PG&E Federated Account Access.
Q. You mention
proper required asset tagging.
What does that mean and where can I find a list of proper tags?
A. AWS Asset Tagging will be used for automation and billing.  Assets within AWS that can support tags will be required to maintain a minimum number of valid tags.  Assets without required tags will either be denied creation or will be stopped/terminated/deleted shortly after creation.  More details can be found at
https://wiki.comp.pge.com/display/CCE/AWS+Asset+Tagging+and+Enforcement
Q. This is the first I have heard about
Logging Central.
I don’t believe that
Logging Central
is an AWS product.  What is it and why do I need it?
A. Logging Central is a centralized log collection and archiving solution for enforcing the collection of and storage of logs produced in PG&E Organization AWS Accounts.  Logs are collected and stored in a separate dedicated logging account to limit access and prevent tampering.  To reduce storage costs logs are archived into progressively less costly storage tiers and then automatically deleted after they exceed their records retention lifetime.  Enterprise log analysis tools can be granted read-only access to the logs in the central account to permit easier integration into AWS and allow for immediate access to logs for new accounts and services.
Service Level Agreements
Since the landing zone is composed of many different AWS services (e.g. Lambda, CloudFormation, IAM, StepFunctions, etc) it is very difficult to quantify the SLA numbers for the environment that is created for you through the account creation process.  However, we can provide a service agreement around account creation.  If you sit down with a Cloud CoE Engineer before 3pm, your new account will be ready before close of business that same day.
Roadmap
Long-term
(12 months)
Mid-term
(6 months)
Near-term
(3 months)
Immediate
(in-sprint)
Released
(Completed)
Self-service by the customer
without needing to sit down
with a CoE Engineer
Full automation of all Stacks
via AWS CodePipeline for
more rapid deployments of
feature upgrades
Single Page app enabling
anyone in the cloud CoE
to provision an account
in minutes
Improving account creation
time with parallel processing
of tasks
One-click account provisioning
allowing for a 45-minute
account creation time
Support, Troubleshooting and Suggestions
Support
Support is available during normal business hours. To get support or assistance, go to:
ChatOps:
AWS Landing Zone Channel in Microsoft Teams
Assumptions are that you have access to MS-Teams
Expectations on response:
Normal questions: response within one business day
Time sensitive/urgent/broken questions: instructions on how to escalate
Suggestions
To make suggestions for enhancements:
Use GitHub to create a feature request using the
Create a New Issue
functionality
Post a message in the
AWS Landing Zone Channel in Microsoft Teams
channel
Contributions are most welcomed on this effort. Please review the
CONTRIBUTING.md
instructions.

chunks.page_title: AWS Landing Zone - Asset Management Integration
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - Asset Management Integration ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=49941884
Labels: amps, atlas, ccoe-config, ccoe-operational-integration, ccoe-infrastructure
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
Integration of AWS with AMPS and ATLAS (Asset Management) is key to managing the commercial cloud environment. AMPS integration provides below business services and integration into multiple Asset management tools and Security tools.
Enables Asset certification
Security remediation/vulnerability  integration tools.
SIOC integration.
MEA Integration.
Asset Health Index.
Ideally, the process flow would include validation and record association during the creation of an AWS resource (e.g. Self Service Service Catalog interface).
At this time, we are creating a multiple account for an AppID.
Out of Scope : In future we might create services for multiple AppID in the same account. At this time its Out of Scope. We will integrate the services as a last phase to the AMPS Integration.
Current Logical design of AWS account mapping with APPID
There are five phases of Asset Mgmt Integration:
Structure - what are the required metadata elements (tags) for each account as part of the landing zone account creation.
Detection - how to detect accounts that  does not have required metadata elements (tags)
Enforcement - how to enforce metadata elements (or populate metadata based on a lookup)
Validation - how to validate the metadata is accurate (or update the metadata based on event triggers)
Compliance - how to ensure compliance across the landscape, including reporting
Remediation - how to automate the remediation of missing metadata
Each phase has one or more steps:
Manual - manual population, remediation, validation
Error detection - post creation error detection
Pre-validated - pre-populate the values using a workflow
A call from AMPS to AWS AMPS API will return relevant Metadata:
APP ID
AWS account Information.
Pre-validated - pre-populate the values using a workflow
Ideally, the process flow would include validation and record association during the creation of an AWS resource (e.g. Self Service Service Catalog interface).
Until that is a capability we include, we will need to have reactive / event-based solutions.
Aside: at one point there was an effort to buy/deploy a Cloud Broker Mgmt platform that would be this. Instead of buying something we don't know if we need, the team decided to use as much native AWS capabilities as possible. Once the team finds the rough edges, we determine if those rough edges warrant the TCO of a new solution. This is a great thing about the cloud - we can try until the solution quickly, find where it doesn't meet our needs, then make a decision on the path forward.
There are an ever increasing number of services available in AWS. The Asset Mgmt integration is driven using metadata populated from resource tagging. AWS service must allow for automated tagging population / enforcement. Since AWS is primarily API driven, there are not any services that do not support tagging; however, the enforcement is often a secondary feature on the roadmap.
Service Integration APIs
Here are several scenarios for this integration:
Scenario With AWS account level Integration
Interaction
Return Value
APP(Validate, AppID)
Validate APP_ID
AWS Lambda Reads the APP_ID tag.
Call AMPS_API with the APP_ID.
Return True if valid;
False if not.
APP(GetInformation, AppID)
Populate Required Tags that are sourced in AMPS/ATLAS into the DynamoDB table in Shared Services.
AWS Lambda Reads the APP_ID tag.
Checks to see that the APP_ID is valid.
Calls AMPS_API to get the corresponding Metadata (Owner, App Name, etc)
If valid APP_ID then return a JSON payload with a name-value pair of the metadata.
Scenario with Every Individual Resource
Interaction
Return Value
APP(UpdateResrouces, AppID, ResourceList, Debug Flag)
Update App with new / changed AWS resources
When an update to a resource group is triggered, update the corresponding App Resources in AMPS.
This works for EC2, RDS and TBD
Individual Resources that are related to the corresponding updates have to created updated through
Cloud Formation template to update the tags.
Return True if update is successful.
Return False if update is not successful.
Return pointer to debug log if Debug Flag is set to True.
APP(Create, Application Metadata, Status, Debug Flag)
Create an AppID using application Metadata and status.
Call the AMPS_API to create a new Application using required Metadata.

--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - Asset Tagging and Enforcement ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=49964682
Labels: ccoe-aws-tagging, ccoe-operational-integration, ccoe-landing-zone
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
AWS Asset Tagging will be used for automation and billing.  Assets within AWS that can support tags will be required to maintain a minimum number of valid tags.  Assets without required tags will either be denied creation or will be stopped/terminated/deleted shortly after creation.
Content
Benefit
AWS Asset Tagging will allow automated billing of usage, notification of outages or planned maintenance, automated system maintenance, and the development of fault tolerant applications.
Mandatory Tags
The table below includes the tags that will be required for all AWS Resources.
Platform Tags
Name
Category
Automatically Added
Description
CreatedBy
Technical
S3Webhosting
LandingZone
Notifications
When an asset is created tags assignments will be enforced.  Enforcement will occur using multiple methods which could include IAM Policies (prevent creation), AWS Config (enforcement by stopping, terminating, or deleting assets shortly after creation), or other automation platforms (Ansible, AWS SSM/Chef, etc.).
Roadmap
Additional Information

URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=49961494
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

in Decision Logs
AWS Certificate Manager Characteristics:
ACM issues globally / publicly trusted X.509 certificates with the following characteristics:
Zero-cost Domain-Validated Certificates valid for 13 months
Automatic Renewal and Rotation via
Managed Renewal
Fully-Managed Private Key (handled / propagated as managed service, encrypted by KMS)
Chained to
Amazon Root CA 1, Starfield Services Root Certificate Authority - G2, or Starfield Class 2 Certification Authority
AWS Services Supporting ACM Certificates:
The
following AWS Services
support the use of AWS Certificate Manager Services:
Application Load Balancer (ALB), Elastic Load Balancer (ELB)
CloudFront (Content Distribution Network)
API Gateway
AWS CloudFormation
Elastic Beanstalk
Using a combination of native AWS services - it is possible to
fully automate
the request for, domain validation of, approval, issuance and deployment of a publicly-trusted certificate.  The reduces what is frequently a week-long, multiple-iteration back and forth process - dealing with the creation and handling of private key material - into a secure, managed service which executes, start to finish, in a matter of minutes via a single invocation of a CloudFormation template.
Reference Implementation
This solution is expressed as a fully-automated CloudFormation template - as found here:
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-cfn-certificate
The
README.md
file includes context, prerequisites and usage information for this template and resulting Certificate (and Route53) automation, leveraging an inline Lambda function to create the required Route53 HostedZone records to perform domain validation.
ACM SAF Violation exception
If ACM Certificate exception from
SAF violation, control 22
is needed Risk acceptance is needed as well as partnering with CSCOE (Cyber) for Prisma/Brinqa exception.
Steps
1) Get internal CSCOE approval first. ( email distro to come)
2) Request a risk acceptance
https://pge.sharepoint.com/sites/CybersecurityHub/SitePages/Compliance-Risk-Acceptance.aspx
. This will generate a Work Order that you will be completed and returned in kind
3) Once work order is reviewed by risk acceptance (a call will be set up and case reviewed). Once approved you can access risk acceptance document though (
Electronic Document Routing (pge.com)
)
4) Take the approved document and set a meeting with CSCOE regarding the documented accepted risk and request they mute the Prisma/Brinqa ticket.

--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - Immutable Infrastructure ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=79561954
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What is Immutable Infrastructure?
Immutable infrastructure refers to application/servers that are never modified after deployment.  With the revolution of Cloud,  Virtualization,  automation,  Immutable Infrastructure deployment has become de-facto standard for Cloud.
Benefit of Immutable infrastructure :
Consistent, reliable & predictable infrastructure
Eliminate configuration drift
Easy rollback & instant recovery/restore
Harness the Cloud capabilities
Facilitates pilot experimentation & blue-green deployments
What are the requirements for Immutable Infrastructure?
Servers/Applications in the Cloud environment
Statless application
Service-oriented  & micro-services architecture
All data persistency is externalized.  So no local persistent data at server level
Fully automated CICD deployment pipline.
How Immutable Infrastructure will be updated?
Build a new Server/Container application from the latest Golden AMI Image (golden AMI of EC2/EKS/ECS)
Provision the new Server/deployment to replace the old one (both coexisits)
Validate the server/application
Decommission the old server
How to upgrade Immutable Infrastructure in AWS?
It is highly recommended to treat EC2s as cattle rather than a pet.  Once COE releases the latest version of Golden AMI, each Partner/Application owners should redeploy the EC2s within 2 weeks from the release date.
Customer can perform EC2 upgrades by launching a new Golden AMI in following deployments:
Blue-Green deployment  - EC2s with both old/new Golden AMI levering AWS Route53, ELB & AutoScale Group.
Canary / Rolling deployment. - Some EC2 with the new Golden AMI routing the traffic and slowing unregistering the EC2 with old AMI

chunks.page_title: AWS Landing Zone - Press Release - 2020 Q2
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - Press Release - 2020 Q2 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=60399645
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Press release
Cloud COE Announces AWS Landing Zone in the PG&E environment
PG&E provisioned AWS accounts allow customers to focus on application code
The AWS Landing Zone enabled the ability to receive Trusted Advisor weekly notifications that help optimize your AWS infrastructure, increase security and performance, reduce your overall costs, and monitor service limits. These reports can provide guidance
to help save you money on AWS by eliminating unused and idle resources. It can improve the performance of your service by checking your service limits, ensuring you take advantage of provisioned throughput, and monitoring for overutilized instances. Trusted Advisor also notifies you on best practices on enabling AWS security features and examines permissions such as public access to resources. You will also receive notifications for service usage that is more than 80% of the service limit. In Q3, we will add the ability to monitor more services and notify you on a more frequent basis so you can take action sooner.
The AWS Landing Zone is progressing towards making the foundational resources that are SAF 2.0 compliant. For instance, the Cloud Trail S3 buckets that store the
event history of your AWS account activity, including actions taken through the AWS Management Console, AWS SDKs, command line tools, and other AWS services
now is blocking all public access to the bucket. This ensures that malicious attacks do not have access to view any API calls that were made. Also, we are striving towards least privileged access in the resources we create thus setting up a structure for our partners to adopt. These are just a couple of examples where we are following the Well Architected Framework.

chunks.page_title: AWS Landing Zone - Future Press Release - March 27, 2019
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - Future Press Release - March 27, 2019 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51061230
Labels: ccoe-press-release
Chunk 1/1
---

Press release
Cloud COE Announces AWS Landing Zone in the PG&E environment
PG&E provisioned AWS accounts allow customers to focus on application code
The Amazon Web Services (AWS) Landing Zone is now generally available. The AWS Landing Zone is a solution that helps teams at PG&E quickly set up a secure AWS environment based on best practices. The AWS Landing Zone saves time by automating environment creation. Each environment is usable for running secure and scalable workloads and has an initial security baseline of core AWS resources in each PG&E provisioned account. Each baseline environment includes a suite of capabilities, including identity and access, governance, data security controls, network services, and logging. These services are established on behalf of customers so that teams can focus time and energy on building valuable applications for PG&E.
AWS Landing Zone accounts give customers the ability to
quickly
provision AWS resources.
These accounts will enable customers to gain insight,
comply with
standards and policies, and focus on what matters
. All PG&E provisioned accounts roll up to a master AWS account for consolidated billing.
In addition
, all PG&E provisioned accounts will
automatically
enforce proper tagging. In some cases, tags will
automatically
be added
to assets.
Consolidated billing along with asset tagging allows customers to have clear visibility into their project’s financial usage throughout its lifecycle
.
Using PG&E provisioned accounts allows for the automatic billing of a project order number for the AWS resources consumed
.
Also, customers will experience reduced costs as more PG&E accounts come online and the company can leverage economies of scale across customer accounts
.
Numerous security features are baked into PG&E provisioned accounts. New accounts start by enabling Logging Central, a centralized log collection and archiving solution for enforcing the collection of and storage of logs produced in PG&E AWS Accounts. This eliminates the need for developers to collect S3 access logs, VPC flow logs, RDS database logs, Lambda logs, EC2 logs, CloudTrail logs, etc. and allows them to focus on writing application code by knowing that the PG&E SIOC is automatically helping to achieve needed security monitoring. In addition, new accounts come preconfigured with AWS Guard Duty, a threat detection service that continuously monitors for malicious activity and unauthorized behavior to protect AWS accounts and workloads. Finally, PG&E-provisioned accounts include connectivity to PG&E datacenters through AWS Direct Connect and PG&E approved security appliances allowing secure access to the internet and dedicated and redundant high bandwidth connections from AWS to the PG&E back office.
These features are just the start of things to come in the AWS Landing Zone for PG&E provisioned accounts. Look for more features and benefits as the Cloud COE evolves the AWS Landing Zone over the coming months.

chunks.page_title: AWS Account and VPC Guiding Principles
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Account and VPC Guiding Principles ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=49945964
Labels: ccoe-aws-accounts
Chunk 1/1
---

Narrative
Target State: two account
structures
.  One for formally-supported efforts requiring enterprise connectivity, federation, etc - and another for exploration / learning / innovation.  Some intersections / gray areas will no doubt arise.  It's worth noting that AWS
Organizations
will also influence these capabilities and decisions.  While there's no one
right
answer, and it's clearly a balance and set of trade-offs - AWS
does
provide some valuable insight and guidance around
Multiple-Account Security Strategy
.
Account Creation Strategy
While the statement above may sound trite or too vague to be meaningful - it is an important message.  With each additional AWS account created - and all the isolation / segmentation / billing visibility / other business benefit that is gained - there is corresponding overhead in integrating and managing those accounts.
Benefits To Discrete Accounts
Billing Autonomy / Segregation
Business Process Isolation
Technical Segmentation (Network, Identity, Information, Security)
Overhead Associated With Discrete Accounts
Establishing centralized services (Security Auditing, etc)
Cross-Account Access (users/identities/roles)
Cross-Account Access (VPC peering, shared services, etc)
Additional 'root' account credentials to secure, opportunity for IAM users to lurk
Account Creation Criteria
Brainstorming on some criteria for when it might be appropriate / beneficial to create an additional account
Segregate System / Security / Automation Accounts from Application / Workload Accounts
Segregate Efforts that can/should not leverage Rate Base funding (e.g. PG&E Corporation)
Segregate Heavily Compliance-Laden Environments (e.g. SOX, NERC CIP Information, PCI if we were taking payment cards, etc)
Segregate Production from Non-Production (but be careful - some gray area here for sure)
Segregate by Line-of-Business (more gray area)
Segregate by 'Sensitivity' - really just a variant of the Compliance criteria above
Production Accounts (these include DEV, QA and PROD) managed by Cloud Enablement
The Production Accounts will be backed by PG&E Master Account. This has our Production Dev, QA and Prod environments and is managed by the Cloud COE team.
Innovation and Learning Accounts managed by Cloud COE.
The Innovation and Learning accounts will be backed by a SHI PO / Account structure and is meant for non-production environments (exploration, reference implementations, cloud mentorship, university agreements)
Innovation Zone accounts and their associated learnings were deemed too high of a risk, have been brought into the mainstream controls framework, and in most cases are in the process of being removed.
Related Content

chunks.page_title: AWS Landing Zone - AWS Organizations Configuration
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - AWS Organizations Configuration ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51888086
Labels: aws, organization, ou, account
Chunk 1/1
---

-
Overview
The AWS Organizations configuration details the method in which Organizational Units (OUs) are configured. We will use this structure to apply and enforce different types of policy, depending on the purpose of the grouping. Primarily there are four types of groups:
Production - all production level accounts (Prod, QA)
NonProd - all lower environments (Dev, Test)
Sandbox - internally-centric exploration, not directly connected to PG&E.
Innovation - externally centric exploration, not directly connected to PG&E.
The new organization structure will be as below
The above organization facilitates in applying service control policies easily and also deploying stack-sets across organizational units.
The organization tree will be created as a yml file in the repository
aws-lz-iam
and as shown in the picture below. A new a directory called organizations will have a yml file name
master-tree.yml
PGE will have a python script,
get_orginization_tree.py
which will make API calls to the master account organization, traverse the accounts and organizational units, output the human readable yml, say,
master_tree.yml
file, similar to the one posted above. This will be run periodically or after every new LOB accounts are created and the file will be updated in the git repository to have different versions of the organizational hierarchy growth documented for
audit purpose
.
Also if there is a need to change the organizational hierarchy of accounts, i.e., make some accounts or organizational unit moved to a different organization unit. One would download the existing organization tree structure, make modifications and use a companion python script,
push_organization_tree.py
with the
master_tree.yml
as input that will re-organize the accounts and organizational units in AWS as per the file.After this, re-run
get_organization_tree.py
, compare the output, say
new_tree.yml
with the original uploaded
master_tree.yml
and see if there are accounts and OU's moved to
orphaned_ou
_accounts
, that indicates the accounts and the organizational units have been removed and no longer belong in the hierarchy as uploaded. The administrator can then take appropriate actions, typically one would expect that there will be no accounts or organizational units under
orphaned_ou_accounts
.
References
AWS Accounts
AWS Landing Zone - Account Naming Convention
AWS Landing Zone - Environment Definitions
Previous Organizations Page

This document is intended for a mixed audience, namely Commercial Cloud AWS account owners, and CloudCOE team.  Please take note of Target Audience tag.
Decommissioning an account involves multiple steps some of the steps are required to be done by Accounts Owner.
Topic: How do I request closure of my Commercial Cloud AWS account?
Target Audience: AWS Account Owner
Answer:
Click this link:
http://wwwt2/MyITServices/project
and under "Pre-Project Consultation" select "Cloud Consultation"
For "
Request Title
", use Decommission AWS Account Number XXXXXXXXXXXX
Within the "
Request Description
", please provide the following:
AWS Account Number: This is a twelve digit number
AWS Account Name: Name of the account
AWS Account Owner: LAN ID of the account owner
MEA AD/Group : this is the MEA/AD group created for your team to access the AWS Console
Reason for closure: Please provide an email or request from the account owner requesting deletion
Note: By creating this request, the account owner is explicitly granting the CloudCOE permission to remove and delete all asset's within said account.  If you need to retain any files, data, etc, please contact the CloudCOE prior to creating a request for deletion. You may follow the same process, create a Cloud Consultation to gain help, or work with your Product Owner or other CloudCOE members within the MS Teams channels.
The form will result in a Work Order.  Retain the WO for reference.  The CloudCOE team will endeavor to follow up with you within two days of assignment to our team.
Topic: CloudCOE Process Steps to decommission and delete Commercial Cloud AWS Accounts
Target Audience: CloudCOE team (Intake, Engineering, Cyber)
Create a new JIRA Story for decommission using the WO created above
if no WO, create stories if info above is provided via Teams, emails, or other means.
Locate EPIC for the account or project
Typically, an EPIC is created for the project .. use this for the subsequent Jira Stories
If no EPIC found, create a new one to track stories for decommission
Move initial Jira Story to EPIC
Create new Stories as follows: use label ccoe-decommission for all stories
Story to remove all assets within the account once the owner has provided permission to do so
Story to remove LOB MEA/AD group from
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-lz-iam
resources
Assign to rcg1
Story to mark MEA/AD group used for LOB access as "not available"
CloudCOE does not own the MEA/AD groups, therefore CloudCOE cannot actually perform this .. it has to be communicated to the MEA/AD group owner for this.
Story to remove Transit Connect
assign to Richard Wu to create NPS disconnect order
Story to delete account at root level
Assign to Ashok
Update Wiki page :
populate with the info shown in the table.  Include the "delete account at root" Jira story so interested parties can track final story.
Inform Prisma Admins of the newly deleted account.

is our Prisma contact as of 10/2020. Per convo with Daniel, he will monitor Wiki -
for changes and act accordingly.  This is subject to change as the process matures.

--- Page: SPIKE: Step Function Concurrency ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51154217
Labels: aws, spike, stepfunction
Chunk 1/1
---

Research and Document implications, impacts and constraints of concurrent Step Function execution.
User Story
Spike Objective
Assigned
Date Performed
Result
Document Implications of concurrent Step Function executions
4/8/2019
Success: Confirmed / tested support for multiple concurrent executions of a given Step Function. See below for details
Goal of Spike:
As a Cloud COE account provisioner, I should be able to understand the impacts and implications of concurrent Step Function invocations, so that I can perform multiple account-provisioning activities in the most efficient manner possible.
Goal of the Spike
Research and document the following details regarding concurrent Step Function executions:
Is it even
possible
- or is each
instance
of a given Step Function unique / discrete?
Impacts / Implications of running two instances of the same Step Function in parallel:
Race / Deadlock conditions
Integrity issues
Duration of the Spike
This spike should take no more than 6 hours (one working day) to accomplish, including documentation.
Location of Spike Results
Spike results will be captured in a wiki page using the SPIKE Results template. Link will be provided in this story.
Not in Scope:
Steps to remediate implications of concurrency: e.g. leverage SQS queues for submitting / managing Step Function invocations, etc.
Spike Artifacts:
Wiki
Jira
GitHub
<this page>
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-lz-account-creation-automation
Results Details:
According to AWS Documentation, each
execution
of a given Step Function is it's own unique / discrete activity, such that multiple, concurrent invocations could be launched, per the following excerpt from the Developer Guide:
A state machine
execution
occurs when an AWS Step Functions state machine runs and performs its tasks. Each Step Functions state machine can have
multiple simultaneous executions
, which you can initiate from the
Step Functions console
, or by using the AWS SDKs, the Step Functions API actions, or the AWS CLI. An execution receives JSON input and produces JSON output.
However, the devil is in the details (implementation specifics) of the respective Step Functions, and whether there is any opportunity for:
Race Conditions
Deadlock Conditions
Integrity Issues
Test Scenario / Observations:
Configured a simple Step Function consisting of the following steps:
HelloWorld
Sleep
GoodbyeWorld
The first and third steps are simple "Pass" (e.g. No-Op) functions.  The Sleep steps sleeps for 60 seconds.  With this configuration in place, I was able to quickly launch 4 invocations of the (same) Step Function - each independently executing, in parallel:
The Step Function status page shows 4, concurrently-running executions:
This confirms the documentation (that multiple instances of a given Step Function can be instantiated and executed concurrently / in parallel). Obviously, if a given Step (e.g. Lambda which executes a StackSet update, etc) obtains a 'lock' on a given resource (Stackset, bucket policy, etc) - this will block a similar execution.
Additional, more detailed testing would be required to fully drain the possible conflicts - but this provides a solid baseline understanding of the capabilities and implications of concurrent execution.
Relevant AWS Documentation References:
Top-level Step Functions documentation:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/index.html
Developer Guide:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/welcome.html
Executions (concurrency) reference:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions/latest/dg/concepts-state-machine-executions.html
Subsequent Stories:
Potential:  Insert story for queueing Step Function executions / processing.

--- Page: SCP Cloudformation Asset Tagging Enforcement ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=61079932
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Pros
SCP Asset Tagging Policies are much simpler to write and maintain for Cloudformation templates than it would be to enforce upon individual AWS resources and services.
For the example below, please note that each resource/service would require its own individual policy block and for each tag.
Since tags must be added manually during the deployment of the Cloudformation template, the tags automatically propagate to the AWS resources/services defined in the template(with the exception of any resources/services that are not able to be tagged such as EBS volumes).  This means the developer does not need to add tags for each resource within the template.
Developer is prevented from deploying the template if the appropriate tags are not input during the deployment process.
Cons
Tags must be manually input during deployment of Cloudformation template, otherwise, the developer will be unable to deploy the template.
If any required tags are not present, the developer will encounter an explicit deny error that is void of any useful information (See image below).  Furthermore, the developer won't be able to view the SCP policy within the Master Account.
Not all tags can be propagated to AWS resources as not all AWS resources can be tagged(ie: EBS volumes).
If the developer manually creates resources without the use of a Cloudformation template, the Asset Tagging policy will not be enforced.
Realistically, this can only resolve the enforcement of asset tagging for QA and Production environments.

--- Page: Tagging Enforcement Plan 2019-Q1 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51061646
Labels: ccoe-tagging, ccoe-landing-zone, ccoe-operational-integration
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview of Tagging Enforcement Plan
A new capability was released by AWS yesterday:
remediation actions in AWS Config Rules
. This is powerful.
After brainstorming, the team decided on this course of action.
We will evaluate resources in each AWS Account. When AppID values are known to be invalid (empty, *UNKNOWN* or a known bad pattern), the default AppID for that AWS Account will be used to populate the AppID tag. The default AppID is a new value stored in the AWS Account's Parameter Store that is created at the time of account provisioning.
This new capability will allow resources to be compliant while offering the most flexibility to our customers. FOr AWS Accounts with many workloads, we will rely on two things: the dominant workload is the default AppID and that people are entering valid AppIDs when launching new resources.
Soon after this initial deployment, the team will add a validation of AppID feature that will check all AppIDs against the AMPS database.
Givens / Assumptions
In the Landing Zone environment there is a new Account-level AWS Parameter Store. In that parameter store are things like default APPID, owner, etc.
Account-Level-Parameter-Store
The Account-Level-Parameter-Store has an accurate APPID value
Account-Level-AppID
The Account-Level-Parameter-Store has an accurate Owner Email address
Account-Level-Owner-Email
The scope of this is EC2, ECS, EKS, RDS and S3
Basic regular expressions (Regex) can be performed in the Remediation process so the APPID can be checked for easy-to-spot variations, such as XXXX, 0000 or 9999.
Ability to use Regex To be validated
Not in Scope for MVP
Validation of the APPID against values in AMPS (using the AMPS API) is out of scope for MVP.
Reporting of the efforts using QuickSight or similar BI / Visualization tools is out of scope for MVP
AWS Config Remediation Logic (MVP)
Remediation Logic
Evaluate the AppID tag.
If AppID tag is malformed, blank, or matches a known bad value, then:
Look up the
Account-Level-AppID
from the account's
Account-Level-Parameter-Store
Rewrite the AppID tag for the resource
Trigger a notification event
Notification event
SNS Topic => MS-Teams Channel zTagging-Alerts (email:
2e913bd6.PGE.onmicrosoft.com
@
amer.teams.ms
)
SNS Topic => Account-Level-Owner-Email
Deployment Plan
Get APPIDs for existing accounts
Make sure
Account-Level-Parameter-Store
is in place for all accounts
Populate
Account-Level-AppID
in each account's
Account-Level-Parameter-Store
.
Refactor Account Creation code as needed
Write a cross-account Lambda that fires the AWS Config Remediation process on a regular basis (start with every 5 minutes)
Whiteboard Sketch

chunks.page_title: AWS Assessment: Infrastructure as Code Strategy
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Assessment: Infrastructure as Code Strategy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51151130
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Note: All assessments/analysis are based on the current understanding of the PG&E environment.
1. Infrastructure as Code (IAC) Strategy
1.1. Current State
Currently, most AWS resources are instantiated using CloudFormation StackSets. This requires a human to be tasked with each provisioning, decommissioning, or updating task. The human manually interacts with the console or CloudFormation.
1.2. Guiding Principles and Constraints
1.2.1. Use an IAC (Infrastructure As Code) pipeline to manage provisioning, updating, and decommissioning infrastructure.
An IAC pipeline is a much more stable, extensible, and manageable approach than manually updating infrastructure via the console or even a disparate set of CloudFormation templates. Treating infrastructure as code and automating it via a scripting language is critical to being able to reliably deploy and change infrastructure across many accounts and many environments.
Using a pipeline allows multiple workers to share a common set of infrastructure code with accountability and manageability. It also allows the workers to work independently while updating the same set of infrastructure. Finally, it allows workers for some software development best practices to be implemented in the infrastructure space (code reviews, pull requests, version management, etc.)
1.2.2. Building code through a pipeline is more secure.
Building code through a pipeline allows PG&E to apply the principle of least privilege. The developer only needs to have rights to commit and merge code into the source code repository. The Pipeline’s service role is the only role that needs the permissions to create or tear down infrastructure.
1.2.3. CloudFormation is the PG&E standard for IAC.
CloudFormation is the cornerstone of the functionality the CCOE has delivered. It is the defacto IAC standard and an AWS native service.
1.2.4. GIT is the PG&E code repository standard
As a temporary measure, a Landing Zone repository is being hosted on
github.com
. However, PG&E has a mult-repository strategy that will most likely require the Landing Zone repo contents to be re-distributed at some point. The multi-repository strategy is not widely known and not being enforced, so an effort should be undertaken to finalize this standard, enforce it going forward, and clean up old repositories to be in compliance.
1.2.5. Use AWS CodeBuild as a managed Jenkins service..
The AWS CodeBuild service is essentially a managed Jenkins service. It runs Jenkins under the hood and is pay-as-you-go. This means that at midnight, when no one is building anything, PG&E will not be spending money on idle Jenkins servers. Further, the service will scale to any capacity PG&E will require (elastically).
1.2.6. Use AWS CodePipeline to manage pipelines.
Using AWS CodePipeline will allow PG&E IAC developers to automate the deployment and execution of their IAC code in an automatic fashion. From the moment code is committed to the GIT repository the pipeline will manage the execution of the code in CloudFormation and provide visibility to pipeline health and error logging (in AWS CloudWatch).
1.3. Recommended Strategy
PG&E is developing an IAC strategy that is aligned with AWS recommendations.
1.4. Automation
The building of this IAC infrastructure will, itself, be done through IAC. This will allow PG&E to rapidly add additional environments, pipelines, etc. as required.
1.5. Recommended Growth Path
1.5.1. Consider standardizing the IAC solution within the organization
The Locate & Mark and MRAD teams have been utilizing open source Terraform for environment provisioning, whereas the CCOE has been using CloudFormation. Ideally PG&E would choose a single standard, so the teams don’t have to maintain two separate skillsets for something that could be accomplished with one.
1.5.2. Consider building PG&E-specific templates for each AWS service
The central Infrastructure team focuses on creating and maintaining example templates for the application teams to use in order to provide “Self Service” usage. These templates will be crafted to comply with all PG&E requirements and standards for that service. This allows development teams to manage their application’s own IAC code repository alongside any application code, removing the central CCOE team as a blocker from the development cycle.
1.5.3. Consider using branching and pull requests as a mechanism for enforcing PG&E standards on infrastructure
In order to maintain control of key elements (eg IAM permissions) the development teams must issue pull requests from their branches into the master branch for each environment. The central infrastructure team can then review the changes (e.g. in a CloudFormation template) and decide whether the proposed code is within standards. If, for example, a developer writes IAC code which grant IAM permissions that are too permissive according to the standards, the reviewer acts as a gatekeeper and declines the pull request.

chunks.page_title: CloudFormation Change Management & Other Tools? (Terraform/ Salt?)
chunks.content:
--- Page: CloudFormation Change Management & Other Tools? (Terraform/ Salt?) ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=50863843
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

I wanted to make a comparison of some change management issues and best practices with CloudFormation alongside other alternatives that are available.
One big flaw that I have noticed with CloudFormation is that the tool does not care about the "state" of the environment (initially).
An example that I wrote up for another CloudFormation user:
So what does this look like? I am going to show an example of this with Security Groups on Cloudformation.
I have a CFT template that deploys a set of SecurityGroups (can be viewed here as the JSON file).
https://github.com/PGEDigitalCatalyst/aws_cfn_rds_securitygroups/blob/master/cftrdstemplate.json
The template is deployed successfully and I can see my Security groups under the EC2 configuration.
You can see the resources are created. What happens when I modify one of these resources?
As you can see I modified the resource (that the CFT initially configured). Now going back to Cloudformation, note the GUI interface below..
Nothing indicates any change was made to infrastructure resources, so Cloudformation doesn't care about state.
This is an issue, it means you have to make sure unauthorized or improper changes don't happen to begin with. But what if they do happen?And what if it's done for a valid reason that you were simply not aware of?
With CloudFormation it seems you may be out of luck if this type of change happens because there is no ability to DIFF the active configuration against the Cloudformation template (unless someone knows of a way?).
One tool that allows you to diff against an active configuration (in an environment) is terraform.
I will deploy the same set of security groups using the terraform automation tool and demonstrate. The Config Template can be found here:
https://github.com/PGEDigitalCatalyst/aws_cfn_rds_securitygroups/blob/master/sg_rdstftemplate.tf
Running the tool we prep and deploy the Security groups.
As you can see the resource are created and "in-sync."
Now I make a change to the Security Groups I just created.
Notice what happens when we run the config template against the infrastructure.
So we can see the Security Group was modified, and the DIFF indicates that our template should be changed back to it's "original state" unless we modify/update the template or pull the configuration state from the Security Group.
So with this being said, it's an interesting topic, and I am sure some folks would rather use tools that have the ability to Diff between old/new configurations. Maybe amazon will add this type of feature in the future to cloudformation? Regardless one has the ability to use both tools as Terraform can even Diff against cloudformation templates
Example code on Github:
https://github.com/PGEDigitalCatalyst/aws_cfn_rds_securitygroups/blob/master/rdssg.tf
Working Example ->
Running the Terraform template (with embedded CFT file).
The problem remains though as CFT wont care if a user modifies the created resources unfortunately. I tried to apply a IAM Policy to my resources as well directly through cloudformation and I was still able to modify them directly within EC2. I am not sure if this is an IAM issue or if the modification just needs to be done by a different user (that didint originally deploy the template).
Example ->

--- Page: CloudFormation Templates ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=49950049
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Infrastructure-as-Code
Landing Zone Repo
There are many ways to automate the provisioning of resources within commercial cloud environments:  Some offer multi-cloud capabilities (e.g. Hashicorp Terraform) while others may be superset / subset of capabilities, and have various sweet spots / value propositions.  One service which cannot be ignored is the AWS native
CloudFormation
, with it's deep and rich integration across the AWS service landscape - as well as a rich ecosystem of
examples
and
Quick Starts
provided not just by AWS, built the entire community.
PG&E-developed CloudFormation templates are stored in a variety of locations, depending on project need, access requirements and more.  Below is a list of publically-available templates hosted in the open, on-prem Bitbucket repository "
Innovation Hub
":
aws-waf-pge-sourceip
This Cloudformation template automates the creation of AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) ruleset and ACL in one of two contexts:
Global
or
Regional.
Global
:  IPsets, String Match, etc created with global context are then available for use in WAF rules associated with CloudFront (AWS' Content Distribution Network)
Regional
:  IPsets, String Match, etc created with regional context are then available for use in WAF rules associated with Application Load Balancers within AWS Regions / AZs / VPCs.
PG&E has outbound PAT pools at each of our Internet egress points - and are reflected in the table at the bottom of the
page.  AWS currently only supports 'common' CIDR boundaries for expression in WAF rules - so some massaging was required to translate all the ranges into /24 CIDR notation.  Rather than repeating them here, you'll find them in the template.
Running this template in an AWS account, as selecting Global scope, will result in the creation of an IP Filter Set containing all of PG&E's outbound PAT pools (e.g. the sourceIP address for all outbound connections to be used for restricting access to just connections originating from the corporate network (excludes carrier-connected mobile devices, without special / additional treatment).  The template also instantiates a WebAZL consisting of the IPSet Rule - so that it's ready to be applied to any Cloudfront distribution (see Regional rulesets for use with Application Load Balancers within a VPC).

--- Page: AWS Assessment: IAM/Federation Strategy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51151133
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Note: All assessments/analysis are based on the current understanding of the PG&E environment.
1. IAM/Federation Strategy
1.1. Current State
Federation is configured in the Payee account, which was the master aka billing aka organizations account. Base user roles and base service roles are deployed to each account through a CloudFormation StackSets.
1.2. Guiding Principles and Constraints
1.2.1. Centralize roles for deployment
Put all required user roles into the Base User Roles CFT, and put all required service roles in the Base Service Roles CFT, to enable easy management and deployment.
1.2.2. Federate once, then assume role
Configure AD Federation in a single account, then have users assume roles to get into other accounts. This simplifies the federation strategy, and limits the amount of external dependencies when creating new accounts.
1.3. Recommended Strategy
PG&E is generally operating in compliance with AWS recommendations. The current strategy of federating to a single account and having users Assume Role to other roles/accounts is a good way to centralize authentication and expedite new account creation, as no additional federation is required for deploying new accounts.
1.4. Automation
Base user and service roles are deployed as part of the new account creation automation. There is no automation around the Master account or Federation.
1.5. Recommended Growth Path
1.5.1. Move Federation to new account
Now that Payee is being decommissioned, the team has an opportunity to move Federation out of the Master account, which helps to limit blast radius in the event of a misconfiguration. It is recommended to either move this to it’s own “Authentication” account or to Shared Services. The benefit of the Authentication account is that it truly limits blast radius is keeps it separate from all other functionality. The benefit of the Shared Services account is that users will be authenticated right into the account where most of the manual administration is happening, so it removes the extra step of having to assume role into Shared Services.
1.5.2. Configure target roles to limit who can assume
The current implementation has IAM policies in roles in Payee that limit which roles those users can assume. The target roles (eg CloudAdmin in Logging) are configured to simply trust Payee. This should be tuned, so the target role specifies which roles in Payee can assume it, therefore having two layers of controls (Payee IAM Policy and target role Policy) on target role authentication.

chunks.page_title: AWS Landing Zone - EBS Volume Encryption
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - EBS Volume Encryption ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=61095197
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Summary: Encryption of Elastic Block Storage volumes is critical to the security of data in AWS. EBS volume encryption can be enabled by default at an account level. This will enable both IT and customer accounts to encrypt all EBS volumes rather than relying on inconsistent manual implementations of EBS volume encryption. The process includes creation of a Customer Master Key (CMK) in AWS Key Management Service (KMS), with proper permissions, and then using that CMK to enable encryption at an account level within the EC2 Dashboard.
1) Under AWS Services select
KMS
and select
Create Key
. Be sure to be in the desired region.
2) Select
Symmetric
and
Next
3) Create
Alias
and
Description
for the key and add appropriate tags.
4) Define
Key Administrators
. These users are allowed to manage the CMK and change permissions on it and delete the key.
5) Define
key usage permissions
. Define the IAM Principals that are allowed to use the CMK, both users and roles. If a user or role isn't defined here then access to the CMK will not be allowed. This includes any IAM roles that are used in Cloud Formation to create EBS Volumes.
Example of a
Role
being added for key permissions-
Example of a
User
being added for key permissions-
6) After reviewing the key policy select
Finish
7) After the key is created navigate to Key Rotation and select "
Automatically rotate the CMK every year
" and select
Save
8) Navigate to EC2 under AWS Services. Be sure to use the "New EC2 Experience" in order to view the EC2 Dashboard. Select EBS encryption under Account attributes
Settings
and select the option
EBS encryption
.
9) On the EBS encryption Settings select
Manage
.
10) Search for the encryption key you just created. Select
Enable
and select
Update EBS Encryption
NOTE: Do not use the Default encryption key as this can cause issues later on.
https://blog.skeddly.com/2019/06/use-caution-when-enabling-default-encryption-of-new-ebs-volumes.html
11) Once these steps are completed and the account level encryption setting is enabled, all EBS volumes created in this local account will be encrypted by default. Key rotation will be automatic after 365 days.
12) A
CloudWatch Rule
should be set up in the account to detect a
schedule key deletion
along with an SNS Topic to subscribe to for alerts.
Event Pattern:
{
"source": [
"aws.kms"
],
"detail-type": [ "AWS API Call via CloudTrail"
],
"detail": {
"eventSource":
[
"
kms.amazonaws.com
"
],
"eventName": [
"ScheduleKeyDeletion"
]
}
}

chunks.page_title: AWS Landing Zone - EC2 Auto lifecycle
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - EC2 Auto lifecycle ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=81955454
Labels: aws, shutdown, tag, schedule
Chunk 1/1
---

With the emphasis on saving & cost reduction, COE team has developed a CFT template to automatically stop & start the EC2 instances during off peak hours using AWS Lambda & Scheduler.
AWS customers/partners can deploy the template in their respective DEV & TEST accounts as below:
Q&A
Q. How this EC2 auto lifecycle is implemented ?
A. The CFT creates two Lambda functions ( ccoe-scheduled-ec2-stop & ccoe-scheduled-ec2-start) and uses AWS Scheduler to trigger these function at scheduled time to start/stop the EC2s that meets the condition.
Q. Does the logic also stops the EC2 launched by AWS autoscale launch configurations?
A. Yes. Enable it through the above ssm parameter stop_ec2 flag
Q. How to I change the Stop time of standalone EC2?
A. You have to change Stop time in UTC which by
Default
:
cron(0 2 ? * TUE-SAT *) in CFT parameter pScheduledStop (Which is Monday to Friday 7pm PDT)
Q. How to I change the Start time of standalone EC2?
A. You have to change Start time in UTC which by
Default
:
cron(0 15 ? * MON-FRI *)
in CFT parameter pScheduledStart (Which is Monday to Friday 8am PDT)
Q. Can I stop running standalone EC2 before the off-peak hours?
A. Yes. This CFT will only stop if the running EC2 meets the condition that need to be stopped.
Q. What If I need to access my application running inside my standalone EC2  that is already stopped by this CFT during off-peak hours?
A. Simply start the EC2 and access your application.  Don't forget to stop it manually after your are done for the day. EC2 will be auto cycled from following day.
Q. How can I skip one of existing running standalone EC2 Instances to be stopped during non-peak hours?
A. Create a EC2 Tag "
OffHourStop" and set to  "false" (
case-sensitive
) for that particular instance
Q. How do I make sure some of standalone EC2s that I launch in the future not be auto stop/started ?
A. Create a EC2 Tag "
OffHourStop" and set to "false" (
case-sensitive
) as part of EC2 creation.
Q. Is there is any logging to get some details on which EC2s are managed by this Lambda functions?
A.  You can look into the Lambda function logs under following  Cloud Watch Log groups:.
/aws/lambda/ccoe-scheduled-ec2-stop
/aws/lambda/ccoe-scheduled-ec2-start

chunks.page_title: AWS Landing Zone - Future Press Release 2020 Q4
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Landing Zone - Future Press Release 2020 Q4 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=67306332
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Press release
Cloud COE Announces Dynamic Asset Tagging and Enhanced Monitoring with PagerDuty
AWS Resources with Focus on PG&E Asset Management and Monitoring
Cloud COE offers a multi pronged approach to asset management by way of dynamic asset tagging during Cloudformation template deployments. We are taking advantage of the fact that CloudFormation deployments propagate tags to every resource deployed if the resource can be tagged. This aligns with CI/CD best practices by having infrastructure as code. It also simplifies the enforcement process by checking only the CloudFormation Stacks or StackSets with required tags instead of creating custom controls for every resource. Service Control Policies (SCP) provide central control over all accounts within an organization. We are utilizing an SCP to act as a guardrail to prevent Cloudformation deployment without the minimum mandatory tags.
Since some of the mandatory tags are found within the System of Record, the AMPS Database, the Cloud COE developed an automated process to populate the tags with this information; System Owner, Data Classification, Compliance, and Cyber Risk Impact Score.  Additionally, we created a mechanism to ensure the tags related to the AMPs information will be in sync with the AMPS Database.  These mandatory tags provide Cyber the ability to better analyze the severity of vulnerabilities and it is important that the tags are up to date. A developer can leverage these benefits simply by deploying CloudFormation Stacks or StackSets.
The Landing Zone accounts now support multi-region which allows creation of
fault-tolerant applications with easy failover to a backup region. There is an added cost involved when running multi-region, but it is available if you have a business requirement.
The Cloud COE continues to move towards enabling our business partners with powerful monitoring tools.  AWS Limit Monitor was introduced during Q3 as a means to automatically provision the services necessary to proactively track resource usage and send notifications as you approach service limits.  We will add the Limit Monitor as part of the Account Creation bundle to help amplify the visibility on your service quotas.
PagerDuty is an incident management platform that provides reliable notification, automatic escalations, on-call scheduling, and other functionality to help teams detect and fix infrastructure problems quickly.  We will integrate PagerDuty as a solution to detecting changes in transit connectivity (internet / intranet).   The initial rollout will be a proof of concept using the ManagedAD account.  Our tactful approach to diagnosing changes in connectivity will put emphasis on how we triage these incidents so notifications sent to partners are meaningful.  Stay informed when connectivity outages occur outside and within your VPN and when they have been resolved.
Multi pronged approach to asset management by way of dynamic asset tagging:
Implement SCP for mandatory tags with predeifned values.
Implement functionality to dynamically update SCP policy.
Update amps related tags in Cloudformation templates when they are created or updated.
Remediate Non-Compliance based upon predetermined Config rules.
When amps data is updated, update CF stacks and stacksets.
Newly added Multi Region capability for high availability.
Streamlining Account Creation by automating IAM role creation.
Deploying Limit Monitor as part of account creation to amplify your visibility on your service quotas.
PagerDuty integration to detect changes in transit connectivity.

chunks.page_title: AWS Assessment: Account and VPC Strategy
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Assessment: Account and VPC Strategy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51151126
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Note: All assessments/analysis are based on the current understanding of the PG&E environment. Please also see:
as this contains our current guiding principles.  Please note that we will revisit strategy and modify if needed as we further mature in our cloud journey.
1. Account Strategy
1.1. Guiding Principles and Constraints
1.1.1.
Applications/LOBs  Accounts
When applications are onboarded, typically they will need a stack of four accounts: Dev, Test, QA, and Prod. The Dev environment is for development, the Test environment is for testing deployment of infrastructure/environment CloudFormation, the QA environment is for testing with production data (and due to the presence of production data, this is classified as a production-level environment), and the Prod environment is for running the production application. Applications may decide to just have one non-prod and one prod environment depending on their use case (so 2 environments  instead of 4).
1.1.2.
Create as few accounts as possible to achieve required business and technical outcome
s
This PG&E principal is driven by a desire to reduce complexity and overhead, as the current Landing Zone requires noteworthy time and effort to create and maintain new accounts.
PGE requires at least 2 AWS accounts (non-prod and prod) as minimum requirement if decided to have new accounts for an application.
We will have LoB specific DEV/TEST/QA/PROD accounts and will be shared by multiple application(s) (VPC per application) regardless of BIA Tier.
If LoB already has multiple AWS account(s) already in place, We recommend grouping lower Tier application(s) together in one account (Tier 3/4/5) vs higher Tier such as Tier 1/Tier 2 for further isolation (Example: Customer Care has PSPS as Tier 1 in its own account and SOMAH/AOP shared in another account for lower Tier App). It's Optional.
If LoB has large no# of applications which are EXTERNAL facing (i.e. public), We recommend grouping and isolating all of them separately with its own set(s) DEV/TEST/QA/PROD AWS account due to:
DIFFERENT NETWORK/SECURITY ISOLATION (i.e. DDOS protection)
DIFFERENT MONITORING/ALERTING/LOGGING Needs
DIFFERENT SLA/URGENCY
KEEPING THEM ISOLATED FROM INTERNAL facing APP(s)
If application consists of external & internal facing needs; It will be treated as EXTERNAL facing.
We will not mix different LoB workload into same account to limit cross LoB sharing complexity, need for extremely large CIDR range and to limit blast radius.
These apps would all be managed by the same team (not multiple teams in one account)
You will rely on VPC Security Groups for micro-segmenting applications from a network security perspective, rather than multiple VPCs. You can still connect the applications via port/SG referencing if they need to communicate.
Resource cost could be split up by resource tags, thus tagging is critical to get right. But networking cost would need to be shared or evenly divided by the app owners
You accept that there is a potential for applications to be simultaneously impacted or impact each other since they're sharing the same account. The examples we discussed were: reaching account limits, throttling / contention, IAM credential exposure (although we do not recommend using long-term IAM keys).
You do not expect these applications to be subject to M&A activity in the near future.
Grouping multiple applications under same LOB (with different AMPS-ID)  in the same AWS account
Multiple Apps (strong dependency & chattiness) belong to the same LOB can be grouped/deployed in the same AWS account only if following criteria is met.
Grouped Apps fall under the same application Tier or lower Tier (but not higher).
None of the Apps have public/internet facing endpoints exposed outside of PG&E intranet.  Any internet facing Apps owned by the same LOB will be treated as standalone App and need have its own AWS account & VPC.
Tagging will be critical for show/chargeback cost(s) per application along with least privilege permissions to ensure that application resource access can be allowed or not.
Team could opt-in to create App Specific AD group to access AWS Console even though it's shared account and further lock down access to application specific resources ONLY with use of tag(s) (say by APP_ID)
Accounting model aligns with DevSecOps operating model where app team(s) build it; manage it; secure it and maintain it (i.e. own it). If CloudOps/AppOps structure is required to help LoB(s) manage/maintain application(s), It can still be supported with different AD Role such as CloudAdmin
NOTE:
There could be an application/program such as PSPS and CCSP where we may run into AWS account limit issues and we may consider having its own set of accounts if necessary.
1.2. Recommended Strategy
The current account strategy is aligned with AWS recommendations. Ideally, as the CCOE develops additional automation around account creation and management, the guiding principal of “as few accounts as possible” would be reviewed and revised. Automation can significantly reduce the overhead and maintenance currently associated with new accounts, which would alter the factors included in the cost-benefit analysis.
1.3. Automation
Step Functions and Lambdas are used to deploy new accounts, with much of the account functionality being provisioned through triggering Stacksets. The process is documented in the wiki (INSERT LINK). It started as a manual deployment process, and has continually been refined and shortened as automation is being built to handle more of the process.
1.4. Recommended Growth Path
1.4.1. Consider Account Repurposing Automation
The CCOE is continuing to create accounts, but sometimes projects aren’t run to completion and some accounts are left partially provisioned and/or unused. Consider creating a variation on the new account provisioning automation to repurpose these accounts to be used for other projects/applications.
1.4.2. Account Segregation by Compliance Regime
Some applications in the portfolio require SOX compliance. It is AWS best practice to segregate compliance regimes at the account level, ensuring that there are no “shared” resources between various compliance regimes. For this reason, a set of SOX compliance accounts is recommended.
2. VPC Strategy
2.1. Current State
The current strategy is to deploy one VPC per account, with it only being a single tier of private subnets (across three availability zones) and management subnets (across two availability zones). All VPC ingress and egress traffic flows through the Transit environment, which acts as the public tier.
2.2. Guiding Principles and Constraints
2.2.1. All non-local traffic must be inspected by Palo Alto Networks firewalls
All traffic that is not destined for the same VPC where it originated (eg inter-VPC, internet traffic, traffic to/from on-prem) must traverse the Transit environment in order to be analyzed and filtered by Palo Alto firewalls. This also implies that direct VPC peering is prohibited, as any traffic flowing through a peered connection would circumvent Palo Alto inspection.
2.2.2. Network Protection Services owns and operates Transit environment
The Network Protection Services team owns and operates the Transit environment where all traffic traverses. This environment was designed and built without input from the CCOE, which introduces additional risk into the Landing Zone.
2.2.3. VPCs architected with single-tier subnetting
Considering all traffic not local to the VPC must go through the Transit environment and Palo Altos, there is no reason for each VPC to have public subnets. This led the team to design VPCs as single-tier, with three private subnets and two management subnets.
2.3. Recommended Strategy
With the recent release of Transit Gateway and Resource Access Manager, the options for connectivity are much broader than ever before. Leveraging Transit Gateway and/or sharing VPCs across multiple accounts (via Resource Access Manager) would produce a cheaper, more stable environment compared to what currently exists.
2.4. Automation
VPC creation is an automated part of initial account provisioning. It is deployed via StackSets, and tags all network resources with values pulled from Parameter Store. The template includes creation of a Virtual Gateway and VPN connection to enable VPN connectivity with the Palo Altos in the Transit environment.
