chunks.page_title: AWS IAM Identity Center Onboarding Wiki
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS IAM Identity Center Onboarding Wiki ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=326965523
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

This wiki explains how to onboard to AWS IAM Identity Center using this repository. The process involves creating an Active Directory group and submitting a pull request with your role definition.
Overview
The
aws-idc-policy-sets
repository manages AWS IAM Identity Center permission sets and account assignments through two distinct approaches:
Global Roles
: Organization-wide permissions based on Organizational Units (OUs)
Line of Business (LOB) Roles
: Specific permissions for individual business units based on account name patterns
Onboarding Process
Step 1: Create an Active Directory Group in Microsoft Entra ID (MEA)
Request a new AWS group in Microsoft Entra ID following the standard process:
Follow the AWS group naming convention:
AWS-APPid-Environment-Role
Example:
AWS-A2102-All-Audit
or
AWS-A2781-Test-Secbydesign_Ops
Include appropriate approvals for the access level you're requesting
Make note of the exact group name, as you'll need it for your role definition
Step 2: Clone the Repository
Step 3: Create a New Role Definition
For Line of Business (LOB) Roles (Most Common)
Create a feature branch:
Determine your LOB folder:
For production:
lob-roles/roles/<lob-name>/
Create a new YAML file based on the template:
Edit the YAML file with your role definition:
For Global Roles (Less Common)
Create a feature branch:
Determine your target folder:
For production:
global-roles/roles/
Create a new YAML file based on the template:
Edit the YAML file with your role definition:
Step 5: Submit a Pull Request
they should review the plan results in the PR.
Commit your changes:
Create a pull request on GitHub
Wait for approval from the repository maintainers
Once approved, your role will be deployed based on the repository's CI/CD pipeline
Key Considerations
Account Targeting
LOB Roles
: Target accounts using name patterns:
Global Roles
: Target accounts using OUs:
Environment Targeting for LOB Roles
Use the
environment
parameter in account assignments to target specific environments by matching patterns in account names:
Permission Levels
Follow the principle of least privilege when defining permission sets:
Dev/Test environments:
AdministratorAccess
: Full access with caution - acceptable for development/testing environments
PowerUserAccess
: Everything except IAM user/role management
ReadOnlyAccess
: View-only access for developers needing to inspect resources
QA/Prod environments:
For LOB roles:
Strict read-only access is required
(ViewOnlyAccess or ReadOnlyAccess)
ViewOnlyAccess
: Restricted read-only for monitoring
For specific operational needs: Use inline policies to extend read-only access with specific granular permissions
Example of extending read-only access with specific inline permissions:
Example of service-specific policy with strict restrictions:
Security Guardrails
The system supports optional security guardrails that can be enabled per permission set:
The guardrails provide baseline security by denying access to:
Dangerous services (Lightsail, GameLift, DeviceFarm, etc.)
Dangerous actions (deleting CloudTrail, creating internet gateways)
IAM user/group management
Troubleshooting Common Issues
If your role deployment is not working as expected:
AD Group Sync Issues:
Verify the AD group exists and the exact name matches your configuration
AD group changes can take up to 4 hours to synchronize with IAM Identity Center
Account Pattern Matching Problems:
Test your patterns with actual account names
For regex patterns, validate using a regex testing tool
Remember that global roles use "path" to match OU names, not the full OU path
For LOB roles, the environment parameter is matched against account names
Permission Set Conflicts:
Ensure no conflicting permission sets exist for the same principals in the same accounts
Check the IAM Identity Center console for assignment status
Getting Help
If you need assistance with role creation:
Review the examples in the
examples/
directory
Consult the README files in each directory for detailed documentation
Open an issue in the repository with your specific question
Contact the cloud security team:
Email: cloud-security@example.com
Slack: #aws-idc-support
Office hours: Every Tuesday at 2pm in Teams

chunks.page_title: AWS Identity Center - MEA Sync Issue
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Identity Center - MEA Sync Issue ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=342299143
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

⚠️ Known Issue: AWS Provisioning Failure Due to Phone Attribute Sync
Overview
We have identified a new issue impacting AWS user provisioning through
MEA (My Electronic Access)
and
Identity Center (EntraID)
. The problem occurs when the
phone attribute
is synchronized for users who have multiple phone numbers. This causes the provisioning process to fail.
Who is Affected?
Users with more than one phone number in their profile.
Any new AWS user being provisioned via MEA and Identity Center.
Current Status
There is
no workaround
for this issue at this time.
The
SSO team is actively working on a permanent fix
.
To mitigate the problem, we will
remove the phone attribute from synchronization
, as it is not required for AWS access.
Action Plan
Attribute removal is in progress and will resolve the issue for all affected users.
No user action is required at this time.
Getting Help
For questions or updates, please reach out via:
Teams Channel:
Cloud Product - AWS Identity Center
Historical Note
The previous timing issue related to MEA nightly sync and AWS group assignment has been resolved. The workaround steps for that issue have been removed from this page.

chunks.page_title: AWS Identity Center SCIM Group Provisioning Automation
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Identity Center SCIM Group Provisioning Automation ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=350696535
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
This automation tool automatically assigns security groups to Entra ID enterprise applications that use SCIM provisioning and SSO. Once a group is assigned to the enterprise app in Entra ID, Entra ID's built-in SCIM provisioning automatically syncs it to the target system (like AWS Identity Center, Terraform Cloud, Snowflake, etc.).
What this tool does:
Automates the assignment of groups to enterprise apps in Entra ID
What Entra ID does:
SCIM provisioning and SSO to the target system (fully automatic once assigned)
Repository:
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-identity-center-scim-sync
Current Use Case:
AWS Identity Center, but
easily adaptable
to any enterprise app using Entra ID SCIM provisioning (Azure DevOps, Terraform Cloud, Snowflake, etc.)
What Problem Does This Solve?
Manual Problem:
New security groups created in MEA (e.g.,
AWS-A1234-Prod-Developers
)
Entra ID Admin has to manually open Azure Portal and assign each group to the enterprise application
Until assigned, Entra ID doesn't know to provision the group via SCIM
Users can't access the application until manual assignment is done
Repetitive manual work
for every new group
Automated Solution:
Script detects new groups matching your prefix pattern (e.g.,
AWS-*
,
AZR-*
)
Automatically assigns them to the enterprise application in Entra ID
Entra ID's SCIM provisioning takes over from there
- automatically syncing to AWS Identity Center (or other target system)
Users get access immediately after group assignment
Zero manual work
in Azure Portal or target system
How Teams Can Use This
For IT Administrators (Full Access)
Run the script to automatically provision new groups:
./quick_start.sh
# Shows what needs to be added, asks for confirmation, then applies changes
For Team Members (Read-Only Access)
Even without provisioning permissions, you can see what groups need attention:
python3 automate_scim_sync.py --compare
# Shows the gap analysis without making any changes
This helps you:
See which groups need to be provisioned
Request help from someone with appropriate access
Understand the current sync status
For Future Automation
This foundation is
designed to be reusable
for any enterprise application in Entra ID that uses SCIM provisioning and SSO:
Azure Functions deployment
- Runs automatically on a schedule (recommended for Microsoft ecosystem)
AWS Lambda deployment
- Alternative for AWS-centric environments
Adapt for other apps
- Just change the enterprise app ID and group prefix (see "Extending to Other Applications" below)
Examples of other apps this works with:
Azure DevOps (use
AZR-*
prefix)
Databricks workspaces
Snowflake environments
Any SaaS app with Entra ID SCIM provisioning enabled
The Azure Functions deployment guide is already included in the repository (
AZURE_FUNCTIONS_DEPLOYMENT.md
).
Key Features
Automatic Group Discovery
- Finds all AWS-* security groups in Entra ID
Gap Analysis
- Compares Entra ID vs currently provisioned groups
Safe Add-Only Policy
- Never removes groups automatically (manual review required)
Ignore List
- Exclude specific groups from automation (shared team config)
Multi-Prefix Support
- Can sync AWS-, AZR-, or any prefix pattern
Azure Functions Ready
- Deploy for full hands-off automation
Complete Audit Trail
- All operations logged for compliance
Repository Structure
aws-identity-center-scim-sync/
├── quick_start.sh                 # Main entry point (interactive)
├── automate_scim_sync.py          # Core automation logic
├── .env.example                   # Configuration template
├── .scim_ignore                   # Team-shared ignore list
├── README.md                      # Full documentation
├── SETUP.md                       # Setup instructions
├── AZURE_FUNCTIONS_DEPLOYMENT.md  # Automation deployment guide
└── scripts/                       # Helper scripts
├── azure_cli_groups.py        # Query Entra ID groups
├── get_all_provisioned_groups.py  # Query SCIM groups
├── compare_groups_final.py    # Gap analysis
└── filter_aws_security_groups.py  # Filter security groups
Example Output
📊 Running analysis to check for differences...
📊 Entra ID Security Groups: 523
📊 Currently Provisioned:    524
✅ Groups in both systems:    522
➕ Missing from SCIM:         1
📊 Coverage:                  99.8% ✅
➕ Groups to add (1):
• AWS-A3612-All-XSOARBG
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
⚠️  Found 1 group(s) that need to be added to SCIM
Would you like to apply these changes? (yes/no): yes
🔄 Applying changes...
✅ Added: AWS-A3612-All-XSOARBG
✅ Changes applied successfully!
Safety Features
Add-Only Policy
The automation will
never
automatically remove groups
Groups that exist in SCIM but not in Entra ID are flagged for manual review only
Interactive Confirmation
When run manually, the tool always asks for confirmation before making any changes
You see exactly what will be added before it happens
Ignore List
Groups in
.scim_ignore
are excluded from comparison and won't trigger alerts
Perfect for distribution lists or intentionally out-of-sync groups
Troubleshooting
Authentication Errors
If you see "grant has expired" errors, this happens after password changes or after 90 days.
Solution:
az logout
az login
Permission Errors
Verify you have:
Group.Read.All
- Read Entra ID groups
Application.ReadWrite.All
- Manage enterprise app assignments
Script Errors
Check the logs in the
logs/
directory for detailed error messages.
Prerequisites
Azure CLI
- Installed and authenticated (
az login
)
Python 3
- Python 3.x installed on your system
Entra ID Permissions
- Read groups (Group.Read.All)
Application Permissions
- Manage enterprise app assignments (Application.ReadWrite.All)
Configuration
Required Settings (.env file)
ENTERPRISE_APP_ID=<your-aws-identity-center-app-id>
APP_ROLE_ID=<your-app-role-id>
GROUP_PREFIXES=AWS-
Finding Your IDs
Enterprise App ID:
az ad sp list --filter "startswith(displayName,'AWS')" \
--query "[].{displayName:displayName, id:id}" -o table
Copy the
id
(Object ID) value to your .env file
App Role ID:
az ad sp show --id <ENTERPRISE_APP_ID> \
--query "appRoles[].{displayName:displayName, id:id}" -o table
# Look for the "User" role
Copy the role
id
to your .env file
See
SETUP.md
in repository for detailed step-by-step instructions.
Ignore List (Team Shared)
The
.scim_ignore
file is
committed to the repository
and shared across the team.
Add groups that should NOT be automatically provisioned:
# Distribution groups / Non-security groups
AWS-Data_Labelers
# Break glass accounts / Special access groups
AWS-Prod-SOMAH_BreakGlass
When you add a group to this file and commit,
everyone on the team benefits
- no need for each person to configure their own ignore list.
How It Works
The automation runs in 4 steps:
Query Entra ID for all AWS-* security groups
Query SCIM to get currently provisioned groups
Compare the two lists to find gaps
Add missing groups to SCIM provisioning
Extending to Other Applications
This tool is
designed to be reusable
for any enterprise application in Entra ID that uses SCIM provisioning.
How Entra ID SCIM Works:
You assign a security group to an enterprise application in Entra ID
Entra ID automatically provisions that group to the target system via SCIM
This tool automates step #1 - the assignment to the enterprise app
To adapt for another application (e.g., Databricks, Azure DevOps, Snowflake):
Update
ENTERPRISE_APP_ID
to your target application's Object ID
Update
GROUP_PREFIXES
to match your naming pattern (e.g.,
AZR-
,
GCP-
,
DBX-
)
Update
.scim_ignore
with application-specific exclusions
Run the same automation
Real-world examples:
AWS Identity Center
-
AWS-*
groups (current implementation)
Azure DevOps
-
AZR-*
groups
Databricks
-
DBX-*
groups
Snowflake
-
SNW-*
groups
Deployment options:
Azure Functions
- Recommended for Microsoft ecosystem (deployment guide included)
AWS Lambda
- Alternative for AWS-centric environments
Manual runs
- On-demand execution when needed
Support and Contribution
Repository:
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-identity-center-scim-sync
Documentation:
README.md
- Complete user guide
SETUP.md
- First-time setup
AZURE_FUNCTIONS_DEPLOYMENT.md
- Automation deployment
Issues or Questions:
Create an issue in the GitHub repository
Contact the Identity & Access Management team
Production Status
This tool is currently in production use for AWS Identity Center SCIM provisioning. It has successfully provisioned
500+ groups
and maintains
99.8% synchronization coverage
.
Last Updated: October 2025
Maintained by: CSCOE

--- Page: Ansible vs SCCM ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=79566104
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Background
:
Ansible was selected as a tool to perform auto remediation of STMP findings.  The overall goal would be to automate finding resolution where applicable to simplify the effort required from the IT Admins and the Tool SMEs to maintain the health and coverage of the various security tools.  The work began as a proof of concept where Ansible would use STMP data to target remediation of QRadar agent findings.
The tools teams already have the capability of working with the SCCM team to target remediation efforts for STMP findings, but due to workload and resource constraints this process is not performed in any normal fashion.  Instead, tools teams resolve systemic issues on the server and admin side and rely on the IT Admins to install the tool on the asset and to maintain the health of the asset itself.  STMP cases are assigned to the IT Admins and they are expected to resolve the issues as appropriate.
Options
:
Use SCCM to fix issues with Windows assets – QRadar WinCollect Agent (as an example) could be installed by SCCM or can kick off repair scripts as necessary.
Pros –
Software is already packaged
SCCM already is configured on assets
Logic could be created in SCCM to target any assets that are missing WinCollect agent
Process already exists for packaging/deploying software/CRQs
SCCM can be used to remediate other STMP finding types with manual work from the tools teams
Cons –
Tool teams would be responsible for reviewing STMP reports regularly and would provide list of assets to SCCM team to target for remediation
SCCM currently has no way to pull finding data from STMP, targeting remediation efforts based on finding definition would be manual process by tool teams
Tool Admins would need to pull reports from STMP and work with SCCM admins to perform cleanup.
Logic targeting assets missing WinCollect agent would need maintenance to ensure new asset types are targeted as new assets are onboarded into STMP
Logic would need to be developed to automate finding remediation
either on the SCCM side (pull findings from STMP) or
STMP end (STMP tool would push commands to tell SCCM which package to deploy to the specific target assets)
Only supports Windows assets
Use Ansible to automate finding fixes
Pros –
Ansible can be used to automate fixes on any OS type, only one “tool” to use for remediation solutions
Ansible can use existing SCCM packages to deploy software
Ansible can automate remediations outside of the SCCM client (uses WinRM)
Works on assets where SCCM client is broken, could possibly fix SCCM client issues
Ansible can pull data via the STMP API, which allows for automation of remediation depending on finding type
Cons –
Not “plug and play” – we are designing a solution as tool is not currently used for this function at PG&E (Balaji supporting currently)
Still in build process, will not have a complete working POC until EOY
Solution must be thoroughly tested before releasing to production
No process exists for automating finding remediation via Ansible, multiple business constraints must be identified and resolved
Determine when Ansible is allowed to perform remediation actions (after business hours, etc)
Determine which assets are allowed to be remediated automatically (UDN non-nerc may be free reign, ODN would need to be clearly defined)
Determine what level of approval is needed to perform remediation actions (Ansible can execute whenever vs submit CRQ and get approval)
No process exists for determining “who” will own Ansible and the various aspects of auto remediation
Cloud Automation Team
Maintain Ansible server
Work with SMEs to design and update remediation scripts
Work with CMR team to design remediation scripts for new finding types
CMR Team
Work with tool SMEs to identify findings to remediate based on STMP trend data
work with Ansible team to provide trigger for auto remediation (hostname list, finding type, etc)
Tool SME
Work with CMR and Ansible teams as necessary to support remediation actions

chunks.page_title: PG&E AWS Identity Center Access Guide
chunks.content:
--- Page: PG&E AWS Identity Center Access Guide ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=350698843
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
This guide provides step-by-step instructions for accessing AWS resources through PG&E's AWS Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO). The process is straightforward and involves requesting the appropriate access group through MEA (My Electronic Access) and then using the Identity Center portal.
Prerequisites
Valid PG&E employee credentials
Access to MEA (My Electronic Access) system
Knowledge of your team's designated AWS access group
Step 1: Request Access Through MEA
Identify the correct access group
: Work with your team to determine the specific AWS access group that has been set up for your team's needs
Submit MEA request
: Request the identified AWS access group through the My Electronic Access (MEA) system
Wait for approval
: Once approved, you'll be granted access to the appropriate AWS resources
Step 2: Access AWS Identity Center
Navigate to the Identity Center
: Go to
https://pge.awsapps.com/start
Login
: Use your PG&E credentials to authenticate
Step 3: Select Your AWS Account
Once logged in, you'll see the AWS access portal with:
Accounts tab
: Displays all AWS accounts you have access to (typically your team's specific accounts)
Each account shows:
Account name and ID
Associated email address
Available access roles (varies by team and access requirements)
Step 4: Access AWS Resources
Choose your account
: Click on the AWS account you want to access
Select access method
: You have several options for accessing AWS resources:
Access Keys Option
Click "Access keys" next to your desired role
This provides you with temporary AWS credentials
Direct Console Access
Click directly on the role name to access the AWS Management Console with that role's permissions
Available Access Roles
Based on your team's setup, you may see roles such as:
ReadOnly
: View-only access to AWS resources and configurations
[TeamName]ReadOnlyAccess
: Team-specific read-only permissions
PowerUser
: Elevated permissions for development and deployment tasks
AdminAccess
: Full administrative access to team resources
BreakGlass
: Emergency access for critical situations (restricted use)
Note
: Most teams have access to their individual accounts with role-based permissions tailored to their specific needs and responsibilities.
Getting Credentials for Programmatic Access
If you need to use AWS CLI or SDKs, click on "Access keys" for your role to get:
AWS Access Key ID
AWS Secret Access Key
AWS Session Token
The portal provides multiple options for using these credentials:
Environment variables
: Export commands for your terminal
AWS credentials file
: Text to add to your ~/.aws/credentials file
Individual values
: Copy specific credential values for use in your applications
Troubleshooting
Can't see expected accounts
: Verify your MEA access group request was approved
No access roles available
: Contact your team lead to confirm the correct access group
Authentication issues
: Ensure you're using valid PG&E credentials
Important Notes
AWS Identity Center credentials are temporary and will expire
Always use least-privilege access principles - only request the minimum access needed
Contact your team's AWS administrator if you need additional permissions or have questions about access groups
Support
For technical support or questions about AWS Identity Center access:
Primary Support Channel
:
Cloud Product - AWS Identity Center Teams Channel
Use this Teams channel for:
AWS Identity Center access issues
Questions about access groups and permissions
Technical troubleshooting

chunks.page_title: AWS Access Architecture — Layered Security Model
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Access Architecture — Layered Security Model ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364973961
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

How MEA, Identity Federation, and Sonrai work together
MEA remains the front door for all access requests and governance. Sonrai adds a
security enforcement layer inside AWS
that MEA was never designed to provide.
How a User Gets Access — End to End
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              USER                        │
│  "I need access to AWS."                 │
└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
│
▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
MEA  │  My Electronic Access                    │
│                                          │
│  ● User requests an AWS entitlement      │
│  ● Manager approves                      │
│  ● MEA assigns "AWS-*" AD group          │
└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
│
AD group syncs
│
▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
Federation   │  AD  ──►  Entra ID  ──►  Identity Center│
│                                          │
│  Group membership becomes a              │
│  Permission Set in AWS                   │
└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
│
User signs in via SSO
│
▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        AWS ENVIRONMENT                           │
│                                                                  │
│   User lands with their IAM role & permissions.                  │
│   They can access services, call APIs, manage resources.         │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ┐ │
│  │          SONRAI — Active Inside AWS                  NEW   │ │
│  │                                                            │ │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────┐    │ │
│  │  │ PERMISSIONS FIREWALL │  │ JUST-IN-TIME ACCESS     │    │ │
│  │  │                      │  │                         │    │ │
│  │  │ Blocks high-risk     │  │ User checks out a       │    │ │
│  │  │ actions in real time │  │ break-glass role for     │    │ │
│  │  │ (e.g. disable        │  │ emergency access —      │    │ │
│  │  │ CloudTrail, delete   │  │ time-limited, logged,   │    │ │
│  │  │ S3 buckets)          │  │ auto-revoked            │    │ │
│  │  └──────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────────┘    │ │
│  │                                                            │ │
│  └ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ┘ │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Read this diagram bottom-up to understand why both systems are needed:
MEA gets you
into
AWS. Sonrai governs what happens
after you're in
.
The Layers
Layer 1 — My Electronic Access (MEA) |
Identity Governance
MEA is the system of record for
who is allowed to access what
.
Function
What It Does
Access Requests
Users request entitlements through the self-service portal
Manager Approvals
Approval workflows route to the right people
User Certification
Periodic review and recertification of access
Deprovisioning
Timely removal of access on role change or termination
AD Group Assignment
Assigns "AWS-*" prefixed security groups in Active Directory
⬇ AD groups sync to Entra ID
Layer 2 — Identity Federation |
Directory & SSO
The sync pipeline that
gets users from on-prem AD into AWS
.
Component
Role
Active Directory
"AWS-*" security groups define role membership
Entra ID (Azure AD)
Cloud directory synced from AD
AWS IAM Identity Center
Permission Sets mapped to AWS accounts and roles
⬇ SSO & Permission Sets grant access to AWS accounts
Layer 3 — AWS Environment |
Cloud Resources
Where the actual workloads live — hundreds of services, thousands of permissions.
What's Here
Scale
IAM Roles & Policies
Hundreds of granular permissions across services
Multi-Account Structure
Workloads distributed across many AWS accounts
AWS Services
S3, EC2, Lambda, RDS, IAM, and many more
⬇ Sonrai enforces at the permission level
Layer 4 — Sonrai Security |
Cloud Permission Security
NEW
Sonrai operates
inside AWS
to control what users can actually do — even after they've been granted access.
Capability
What It Does
Permissions Firewall
Blocks high-risk permissions in real time — even if IAM policy allows them
Just-in-Time (JIT) Access
Temporary checkout of break-glass roles and emergency access
POND Analytics
Visibility into blocked and approved permission-on-demand requests
Why Both Are Needed
MEA
Sonrai
Controls
Who gets access to AWS
What they can do once inside AWS
When
Before access is granted
While access is being used
Scope
AD group membership and entitlements
Individual AWS permissions (thousands)
Enforcement
Provisioning / deprovisioning users
Real-time permission blocking in the cloud
Examples
"Grant Jane the Developer role"
"Block anyone from disabling CloudTrail"
MEA still handles:
Who gets access to AWS (entitlement requests)
Manager approvals and access workflows
Periodic certification and recertification
Deprovisioning when people leave or change roles
AD / Entra ID group membership lifecycle
Sonrai adds on top:
Blocks dangerous permissions that MEA can't see
Real-time enforcement inside AWS (not just at login)
JIT checkout for break-glass and emergency roles
Granular control over thousands of AWS permissions
Analytics on what's being blocked and why
FAQ: "Does Sonrai replace MEA?"
No.
They solve fundamentally different problems. One cannot substitute for the other.
MEA is a system of record for identity governance.
It answers:
Who is this person? What are they entitled to? Did their manager approve it? Has it been recertified? When do we revoke it?
Without MEA, there is no structured way to request access, no approval workflow, no certification cycle, and no automated deprovisioning. None of that is in scope for Sonrai — or for any cloud permissions tool.
Sonrai is a security enforcement layer inside AWS.
It answers:
Should this specific API call be allowed right now?
MEA has no visibility into the thousands of individual AWS permissions behind a single role. It assigns you a group — it doesn't know or control whether that group lets you delete an S3 bucket, disable CloudTrail, or modify an IAM policy. That's the gap Sonrai fills.
Capability
MEA
Sonrai
Access request workflows
Yes
—
Manager approval routing
Yes
—
Periodic access certification
Yes
—
Automated deprovisioning
Yes
—
AD / Entra ID group lifecycle
Yes
—
Fine-grained AWS permission control
—
Yes
Real-time permission blocking
—
Yes
Just-in-Time / break-glass access
—
Yes
Permission-on-Demand (POD)
—
Yes
AWS permission analytics
—
Yes
MEA doesn't have JIT, POD, or real-time permission enforcement — and it was never built to. Sonrai doesn't manage identities, approvals, certifications, or deprovisioning — and it was never built to.
These are complementary layers, not competing products.
The bottom line:
MEA controls
who walks through the door
. Sonrai controls
what they can touch once inside
.

chunks.page_title: Mitigating Controls for Powershell and WinRM
chunks.content:
--- Page: Mitigating Controls for Powershell and WinRM ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=72648843
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Ansible executes powershell scripts remotely on Windows assets, and the "Allow Remote Shell Access" GPO must be enabled.  We must implement mitigating controls to ensure remote shell access is not exploited.
Note:
Risk and Strategy must approve exception to standard for GPO.  STMP team met with Gordon Mak on 12/14 to discuss exception to standard and he is going to help get the process moving forward.
Identified controls:
Submit request to SRB for approval -
No longer required.  Ray Fong confirmed that Ansible does not need SRB approval since it's a part of the RHEL distro we use.
Ensure the ansible server is locked down and any related service accounts are vaulted
Work with Jon's team to configure brokered access on Ansible test and production servers
Set a winrm authorized host list on every local server
Need to schedule meeting with Windows Admins to set up host list
Will need to identify the Ansible servers for the whitelist
Use our PAN solution to limit 5986 traffic - Work with Will's team to verify
PANFW may already block this - app id, explicit source and port
Can we block by User ID?
Use certs for WinRM, turn off listener on 5985
Use Endgame to whitelist known scripts and alert for anything else
What data is logged in Endgame?  Will this satisfy the need to implement logging for Powershell scripting?
Implement code signing for PowerShell
Code signing would need to be implemented for any scripting on the asset
Implement logging for Powershell scripts
How do we prevent passwords and other critical info from appearing in logs in cleartext?
Useful Links:
Security Considerations for PowerShell Remoting using WinRM
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/scripting/learn/remoting/winrmsecurity?view=powershell-7.1
Lateral Movement Using WinRM and WMI
https://redcanary.com/blog/lateral-movement-winrm-wmi/
How to configure WINRM for HTTPS
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-client/system-management-components/configure-winrm-for-https
About Signing
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/microsoft.powershell.core/about/about_signing?view=powershell-7.1
Policy CSP - RemoteShell
https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/client-management/mdm/policy-csp-remoteshell
Tasks to follow up on:
Check with SRB to see if Ansible has been approved
Work with Endgame Admins to whitelist known benign activity
Work with Windows Admins to implement winrm whitelist and create process for updating list
Update - 1/4/2021 -
Documentation provided by MSFT for securely implementing WinRM and Powershell.
https://www.fireeye.com/content/dam/fireeye-www/global/en/solutions/pdfs/wp-lazanciyan-investigating-powershell-attacks.pdf
jasonmcboyd/psTrustedHosts (github.com)
PowerShell Gallery | psTrustedHosts 1.1.0
Windows Remote Management - Win32 apps | Microsoft Docs
Remote Services: Windows Remote Management, Sub-technique T1021.006 - Enterprise | MITRE ATT&CK®

chunks.content:
--- Page: ⚡ Just-in-Time (JIT) Access with Sonrai - Temporary Privilege Elevation ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=338470998
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

⚡ Just-in-Time (JIT) Access - Temporary Admin Privileges
📘 What is JIT Access?
Just-in-Time (JIT) Access provides temporary administrator privileges when you need them. Instead of having permanent admin access, you request it when needed and it automatically expires.
🔄 JIT Workflow
🛠️ How to Request JIT Access
Current Method: AWS Identity Center
1. Log into AWS Identity Center
2. Select your AWS account
3. Click on an admin role (e.g., "Administrator")
4. If JIT-protected, you'll see an approval request
5. Wait for account owner approval (if required)
6. Access granted for limited time (default: 4 hours)
Future Method: Microsoft Teams
👥 Account Owner Approval
Account owners have *
delegated authority
* to approve JIT requests for their accounts:
Account Type
Approval Process
Test/Dev
Auto-approve with notification
QA/Prod
Manual approval required
⏰ Access Duration
*
Default Duration:
* 4 hours
*
Automatic Expiration:
* Yes - no manual cleanup needed
*
Extension:
* Submit a new request if more time is needed
🔍 What Gets Monitored
All commands and API calls during your JIT session
Complete audit trail for compliance
Real-time session monitoring
Automatic logging to Sonrai platform
🆘 Support
For JIT access issues:
*
Teams Channel:
*
Cloud Product - Sonrai
- Questions and discussion

chunks.page_title: 🛡️ Approving Permissions on Demand (PoD) Requests in AWS via Sonrai
chunks.content:
--- Page: 🛡️ Approving Permissions on Demand (PoD) Requests in AWS via Sonrai ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=338467919
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

📘 Overview
To streamline access control across our ~150 AWS accounts, we are implementing a decentralized approval model using Sonrai's Cloud Permissions Firewall. Each AWS account owner will be delegated as a Permissions on Demand (PoD) Approver. This ensures that access decisions are made by those closest to the users and workloads.
🔁 Workflow Summary
Step-by-step process:
User attempts a restricted action in AWS (e.g., deleting a WAF ACL)
Permissions Firewall blocks the action and triggers a PoD request
User receives a ChatOps message (currently via email; Teams integration coming soon)
User justifies the request (if human identity)
Account owner (PoD Approver) receives notification and approves or denies the request
Access is granted or denied, and the action is logged for audit
🧩 Key Components
🔐 Permissions Firewall
Enforces least privilege by blocking sensitive permissions
Triggers PoD requests when blocked actions are attempted
🧑‍💼 PoD Approvers
Assigned at the account or OU level
Do not need AWS permissions themselves—approval is handled via Sonrai
💬 ChatOps Integration
Current:
Email-based notifications
Future:
Microsoft Teams integration with interactive approval workflows
🛠️ Setup Instructions
1. Assigning PoD Approvers
Platform Admins assign approvers per account or OU in the Sonrai UI
Approvers can be account owners or designated delegates
2. Configuring Notifications
Email is the current notification method
Teams integration is being tested and will support:
Permission requests
Policy violations
Emergency access
Escalation workflows
3. Identity Mapping (Optional)
For users with different AWS and Teams identities, configure identity mapping using Sonrai's GraphQL API.
✅ Approval Process
Step
Action
1
User attempts a sensitive action in AWS
2
Permissions Firewall blocks the action
3
PoD request is generated
4
User (if human) receives a justification prompt via email
5
Approver receives notification
6
Approver approves or denies the request
7
Access is granted or denied and logged
⏱️ Escalation & Expiry
🔍 Auditing & Reporting
All PoD requests, approvals, and denials are logged in the audit trail.
Reports include:
Requester
Approver
Service
Scope
Action taken
🧪 Testing & Validation
Before full deployment, validate the following:
Email and Teams notification routing
Approval delegation chains
Multi-approver scenarios
Emergency access workflows
SLA response time validation
🚧 Known Limitations

chunks.content:
--- Page: Twistlock to Wiz CLI Migration Guide ---

Important Note:
After the migration date or the end of our contract with Prisma Cloud on March 18th, 2025, Twist CLI will no longer function. However, this will not be an issue if the pipeline and scanner are not run. If a pipeline is not scheduled to run, there is no need to update it immediately. Updates should be made the next time the pipeline runs or a pull request is made. Teams should be prepared before going into production by testing and ensuring readiness to use the new Wiz CLI. It is crucial to understand that not everything needs to be updated by the cutover date; updates are only necessary when the scanner runs. Essentially, you do not need to update everything immediately; just ensure it is updated the next time it runs.
Overview
This document outlines the process for migrating from Twistlock/Prisma CLI to Wiz CLI scanning in your buildspec.yaml configurations.
Important: Wiz Authentication
The WIZ_CLIENT_ID and WIZ_CLIENT_SECRET are available in AWS Secrets Manager as a shared secret across all AWS accounts. You
must
use the shared secret name "shared-wiz-access" as shown below:
Note:
Do NOT use custom secret paths (like /pge/wiz/wiz-secret). Always use the shared secret.
Migration Methods
1. GitHub Copilot-Assisted Migration
Use this prompt in VS Code with GitHub Copilot:
2. Manual Migration
Remove Legacy Components
Environment Variables to Remove:
PRISMA_CONSOLE
PRISMA_SCAN_VULNERABILITY_THRESHOLD
PRISMA_SCAN_COMPLIANCE_THRESHOLD
Secrets to Remove:
PRISMA_KEY_ID
PRISMA_SECRET
Add Wiz Components
1. Add Secrets Configuration:
2. Add Installation Commands:
3. Update Scanning Commands:
Migration Checklist
Ensure Wiz scan is placed after Docker image build but before pushing to registry
Verify complete removal of all Prisma/Twistlock configurations
Test the scanning process after migration
Update related documentation
Verify correct usage of shared-wiz-access secret
Reference
Also see:

Check Access for Wiz:
https://app.wiz.io/idp-login?clientId=9l20kmtiepaj0i4gq3qdohfcm&idp=PGE-Wiz-SAML-SSO
Unable to access then follow the below steps.
Requesting Wiz Access
1) Roles
Use the existing role:
Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_GlobalProject_Reader
This role provides global read-only access across Wiz projects and is suitable for most users who need visibility into Wiz dashboards and data.
2) Requesting MEA (Entitlement)
To formally request access via MEA:
Go to
MEA
and select the entitlement value should reflect the full group name:
Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_GlobalProject_Reader
This MEA group will provide Global Project reader role access to Wiz Dashboard. If the team hasn't created the project in Wiz, please follow the next steps.
3) Project Creation in GitHub
Important:
Not everyone needs to create a project.
Only team leads should do this if the project does not already exist.
Coordinate with your team lead before proceeding.
Each team is responsible for creating and managing their own Wiz project configuration in the centralized GitHub repository. Follow these steps:
Navigate to the repository:
https://github.com/pgetech/cscoe-wiz-kiosk/tree/main
From a feature branch select the config file
Use the existing templates and examples in the repository to define your project configuration. This includes specifying the
mea_group_name
,
wiz_role_id
, and other metadata relevant to your team’s access needs.
Add a configuration in config.yaml. Some examples
mea_group_name
- It is the AD group name that has to get created outside of this request.
wiz_role_id
: Pre-defined roles created by CSCOE......  Select appropriate role from below
search
: A string that is used to search for projects in Wiz. For example, a case insensitive string 'mrad' matches all the
AWS accounts names
that has 'mrad' string in it.
description
:
Description for the mapping
Commit changes and raise a PR request. Upon approval Terraform Cloud pipeline runs automatically and the role mapping gets created. Paste the PR in this channel
Cloud COE | Wiz Defend Question and Answer | Microsoft Teams
or reach to out the individuals who can approve.
4) Final Steps
After the PR is approved and MEA is processed, access will be provisioned automatically via the Terraform Cloud pipeline.
Wait a few minutes, then log in to the Wiz dashboard to verify access:
https://app.wiz.io/idp-login?clientId=9l20kmtiepaj0i4gq3qdohfcm&idp=PGE-Wiz-SAML-SSO
Use your email address and select the
SSO
option to sign in.
5)
Following are the roles that can be requested
Request
Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_Developer

Wiz CLI is a command-line interface tool designed to streamline and automate various tasks in your CI/CD pipeline. It provides a set of commands to manage builds, deployments, and other CI/CD processes efficiently.

--- Page: Wiz Cloud Configuration Rules ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=326969961
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Workflow Overview
The
pge-wiz-iac
repository manages custom Cloud Configuration Rules (CCRs) for Wiz, a cloud security platform. This repository follows an Infrastructure as Code (IaC) approach using Terraform to define, deploy, and maintain security compliance rules across various AWS resource types.
Repository Structure
The repository is organized as follows:
cloud-configuration-rules/
- Main directory containing all rule definitions
Resource-specific directories (e.g., iam, neptune, route53, redshift)
main.tf
- Defines Terraform modules for each resource type
providers.tf
- Contains provider configurations
versions.tf
- Specifies required Terraform versions
Each resource type directory follows a standardized structure:
resourceType/
├── rule1/
│   ├── metadata/
│   │   ├── metadata.json
│   │   ├── description.md
│   │   └── remediation.md
│   └── terraform/
│       └── query.rego
└── rule2/
└── ...
Development Process
Rule Identification
Identify a security compliance requirement for a specific AWS resource type
Determine the appropriate rule checks needed to enforce compliance
Rule Development
Create appropriate directory structure for new rule
Develop Rego query logic in
query.rego
file
Document rule details in metadata files
Local Testing
Test rule functionality using Terraform's local plan capabilities
Validate that rule correctly identifies compliant and non-compliant resources
Integration Process
Code Review
Submit changes via pull request
Peer review of rule logic and documentation
Ensure adherence to standardized structure
Rule Deployment
Merge approved pull requests to main branch
Execute Terraform workflow to deploy rules to Wiz platform
The Terraform module uses Wiz's provided custom CCR module:
module "resource_type" {
source = "https://wizio-public.s3.us-east-2.amazonaws.com/deployment-v2/terraform/terraform-wiz-custom-ccr.zip//terraform-wiz-custom-ccr"
base_dir = "./resource_type"
}
Validation
Verify rules are active in Wiz console
Confirm rules are detecting issues correctly
Maintenance Process
Rule Updates
Update rule logic or metadata as needed
Follow the same development and integration process
Version Control
Maintain history of rule changes through Git
Tag significant versions for reference
Adding New Rules
Step 1: Create a new resourceType directory
Create a new directory for the resource type in the
cloud-configuration-rules/
directory if it doesn't exist already. The directory name should be the resource type name in lowercase (e.g., ec2, s3).
Step 2: Create a new rule directory
Create a new directory for each new rule within that resource type directory. The directory name should be the rule name in lowercase with underscores (e.g., ensure
kms
encryption
is
enabled, avoid
public
access).
Step 3: Add metadata directory and files
Create a metadata directory with the required files:
metadata.json
: Contains rule metadata including severity, name, and other attributes
description.md
: Provides a detailed description of the rule's purpose
remediation.md
: Outlines steps to remediate non-compliant resources
Step 4: Add rule logic
Create a terraform or cloud_formation directory (based on the IaC tool) and add the
query.rego
file containing the Open Policy Agent (OPA) logic to evaluate resources.
Step 5: Update
main.tf
If adding a new resource type, add a new module block in the
main.tf
file to include your new rules in the deployment.
Deployment Process
Initialize Terraform workspace:
terraform init
Review planned changes:
terraform plan
Apply changes to Wiz platform:
terraform apply
Verify rules are active in the Wiz console and functioning correctly.
Wiz Console settings:
Ensure that the deployed cloud config rules are added to the framework
Ensure that the policy has the right framework attached to it
Ensure that the run task deployment has the policy attached to it.
Ensure that the terraform workspace see the cloud config rule being detected in the workspace attached to the Wiz-CSCOE runtask

What Is This
This wiki covers the
SIOC + Cloud Security partnership
on Wiz threat monitoring. Everything you need to know about monitoring Wiz alerts is in these pages.
Wiz monitors our AWS and Azure environments for active threats — credential theft, privilege escalation, malicious activity, data exposure, and more. When it finds something, it creates an alert with all the evidence already collected.
What SIOC Is Responsible For
SIOC monitors and triages every Wiz alert rated Critical or High. All of them.
What SIOC Does
What SIOC Does NOT Do
Read every Critical and High alert
Write or tune detection rules
Decide: close (with reason) or escalate to IR
Analyze raw CloudTrail or cloud logs
Document the decision in the alert and ticket
Decide which rules should be enabled
Contact app owners when activity is unclear
Perform containment (that's IR)
Export evidence (JSON) and attach to ticket
Fix the underlying issue (that's the app owner)
There is no subset. There is no "we only monitor these rules." Wiz has 367 detection rules at Critical and High. If one of them fires, it's in SIOC's queue.
How This Works
That's the whole job. Read, decide, document.

Responsibility

--- Page: Wiz Frequently Asked Questions ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=326966005
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Please see the Wiz FAQ below. For detailed information about each section, click on the corresponding header.
EKS FAQ:
What are the prerequisites for deploying Wiz controllers in a Kubernetes cluster?
Before deploying Wiz controllers, ensure your Kubernetes cluster allows outbound HTTPS connectivity to Wiz, Helm version 3.10 or later is installed locally, and you are using a supported operating system/platform. For Wiz Admission Controller, Kubernetes version 1.19 or later is required.
How do I create the necessary secrets for deploying Wiz controllers?
Use AWS Secret Manager to create secrets named "shared-eks-wiz-creds" in respective accounts. Sensor credentials are available in the same secret as username and password. You can also get pull keys from Wiz Tenant Info.
Code Scanning FAQ:
How do I request access to scan my local code with Wiz?
If you don't already have access to Wiz, you need to request access to the Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_Developer role. This role grants you the necessary permissions to scan your local code using the Wiz CLI and IDE.
How do I authenticate using the Wiz CLI?
Once you have access, authenticate using the Wiz CLI by running the command to use device code authentication. You will be prompted to authenticate your device via browser, and successful authentication will be confirmed in the command line.
How do I scan my local code with Wiz?
After authentication, navigate to the directory containing your code and run the commands to scan directories, Infrastructure as Code (IaC) scripts, and Docker images. Optionally, you can specify the path and project ID to organize your scans better. To get the project ID, visit the Wiz Project Settings page and copy the project ID.
Migrating from Twistlock to Wiz CLI FAQ:
What should I do if my pipeline is not scheduled to run before the migration date?
If your pipeline is not scheduled to run before the migration date or the end of the contract with Prisma Cloud, there is no immediate need to update it. Updates should be made the next time the pipeline runs or a pull request is made. Ensure readiness by testing and preparing to use the new Wiz CLI before going into production.
How do I authenticate Wiz CLI using AWS Secrets Manager?
The WIZ_CLIENT_ID and WIZ_CLIENT_SECRET are available in AWS Secrets Manager as a shared secret across all AWS accounts. Use the shared secret name "shared-wiz-access" in your buildspec.yaml configuration. Do not use custom secret paths; always use the shared secret.
What are the steps for migrating from Twistlock/Prisma CLI to Wiz CLI?
T
o migrate, start by removing all legacy components related to Prisma/Twistlock, including environment variables, secrets, and commands. Next, add the necessary Wiz components by configuring secrets using the shared secret name "shared-wiz-access" for WIZ_CLIENT_ID and WIZ_CLIENT_SECRET. Include installation commands to download and set up Wiz CLI, and update scanning commands to use Wiz CLI for Docker image scanning. Finally, ensure the Wiz scan is placed after the Docker image build but before pushing to the registry, verify the complete removal of all Prisma/Twistlock configurations, and test the scanning process after migration.
Requesting Wiz Access FAQ:
How do I request access to Wiz?
To request access to Wiz, use a config file from GitHub. Ensure you have write access to the GitHub Wiz team. You can request access to the Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_<app_name>
Reader role by going to MEA and clicking on 'Entitlement Management Create' to create an entitlement. Fill out the necessary options, including Application (AD UDN), Environment (Prod), AMPS Application (A3539), Group Name Suffix (Wiz
{app_name}_{type}), and provide a description of the role.
What roles can be requested for Wiz access?
The roles that can be requested include:
Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_Developer: Perform code scanning using Wiz Code IDE extension and Wiz CLI.
Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_DocumentationReader: View Wiz documents.
Can I request multiple roles for a single AD group?
Yes, you can request multiple roles for a single AD group. However, when logging in, users must ensure they are using the role they intend to.
Wiz CLI FAQ:
What are the prerequisites for using Wiz CLI?
To use Wiz CLI, you need access to the Apr-A3539-Prod-Wiz_DocumentationReader role in MEA to view Wiz documents. You also need a Linux, Mac, or Windows host (either local or in the cloud) to download Wiz CLI. Additionally, Wiz CLI must be allowed to create a local directory to store authentication data.
How do I authenticate to the Wiz API using Wiz CLI?
The WIZ_CLIENT_ID and WIZ_CLIENT_SECRET environment variables are available in AWS Secrets Manager under the name "shared-wiz-access." Set these environment variables to the Client ID and Client Secret in Wiz UI. Use the command
wizcli auth --id <CLIENT_ID> --secret <CLIENT_SECRET>
to authenticate.
How can Wiz CLI be integrated with CI/CD tools?
Wiz CLI can be integrated with popular CI/CD tools such as Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, AWS CodeBuild, Azure DevOps, and more. For example, in AWS CodeBuild, you can utilize the buildspec.yaml file to download and authenticate Wiz CLI, scan directories, IaC scripts, and Docker images, and perform other CI/CD tasks.
Using Wiz with Terraform Cloud FAQ:
What are the steps to manage Terraform Cloud Run tasks in Dev and Test accounts?
A:
To manage Terraform Cloud Run tasks in Dev and Test accounts, follow these steps:
Run Terraform Plan to see the proposed changes.
Review the Post-Plan section, even if it indicates passed, by navigating to the "All" tab.
Expand each task to review detailed information.
Identify and remediate any critical or high findings.
Why is the Post-Plan section important in Terraform Cloud Run tasks?
A:
The Post-Plan section is crucial because it provides detailed scan results with Wiz, identifying any configurations and infrastructure code issues. Reviewing this section helps ensure that any issues are addressed early, maintaining the integrity and reliability of the QA and Prod environments.
How does following these steps help in maintaining quality and security in production environments?
A:
By identifying and resolving issues in Dev and Test accounts, you ensure that only well-tested and secure configurations move to QA and Prod environments. This approach helps maintain the highest standards of quality and security in production, where stricter standards are applied.

How Wiz Runtime Sensors Work
Wiz Runtime Sensors are
lightweight, eBPF-based agents
designed for
real-time threat detection and response
in cloud workloads. They operate at the
Linux kernel level
, monitoring system calls, processes, network activity, and file changes without significant performance impact.
Key capabilities:
Real-time threat detection
: Identifies malicious behaviors like image drift, log tampering, network scanning, and file integrity changes.
Runtime vulnerability validation
: Confirms which vulnerabilities are actively exploitable in memory.
Cloud-native compatibility
: Works across Kubernetes, VMs, containers, and serverless environments.
Agentless + runtime sensor
: Combines Wiz’s agentless scanning with runtime telemetry for full visibility.
Custom detection rules
: Supports user-defined rules for alerting or blocking malicious activity.
Wiz Runtime Sensor Overview
[1]
Wiz Runtime Sensor for Linux
[2]
When to Use Wiz Runtime Sensors
Use Wiz Runtime Sensors when you need:
Use Case
Description
Deep runtime visibility
Monitor live workload behavior to detect threats that static scans miss.
Real-time response
Automatically block or alert on suspicious activity like remote shells or ransomware.
Cloud threat hunting
Correlate runtime signals with cloud activity for forensic investigations.
CI/CD pipeline security
Embed sensors to monitor runtime behavior during deployment stages.
Hybrid/multi-cloud protection
Extend consistent runtime security across AWS, Azure, GCP, and on-prem.

Introduction
This document outlines the steps and best practices for managing Terraform Cloud Run tasks in Dev and Test accounts. The primary goal is to ensure that any issues are identified and resolved before moving to QA or Prod environments.
Steps to Follow
1.
Run Terraform Plan
: Execute the Terraform plan to see the proposed changes.
2.
Review Post-Plan section
: Even if the post plan indicates passed, navigate to the "All" tab.
3.
Expand Tasks
: Expand each item to review detailed information.
4.
Identify Issues
: Look for any critical or high findings and be sure to remediate whatever possible.
Conclusion
By following these steps, you can ensure that Terraform Cloud Run tasks are executed smoothly in Dev and Test accounts. The post-plan section is particularly important as it provides detailed scan results with Wiz, identifying any configurations and infrastructure code issues. Addressing these issues early helps maintain the integrity and reliability of the QA and Prod environments. While developers are given more flexibility in Dev and Test, the standards are much stricter for production to ensure the highest quality and security.

--- Page: 2.0 F5 Silverline WAF SaaS - Blocking Policies ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=59738956
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
The Blocking policy will be enabled for the June of the PSPS Emergency Web accounts.  This Excel file can be used as template to track blocking progress,
F5SiliverlineWAF_BlockingPhases.xlsx
.
WAF Setup: Blocking Phases
What is Phased Blocking?
What blocking modules are tuned in each phase?
What attack types are mitigated in each phase?
Note: This is just a guide. Modules can move between phases at the discretion of the SOC and the customer.
What is Phased Blocking?
The SOC recommends tuning policies in phases.
3. SOC moves modules into Blocking mode so the modules can protect the application.
1. In each phase, the listed modules are observed in Transparent mode for a limited period of time.
2. Then the customer approves the SOC moving the modules into Blocking mode.
Open ticket with the SOC referencing the Phase # you wish to move to.
For more information on this process, see:
Q&A: What is "Tuning" a WAF Policy? What is SOC vs. my responsibilities in this process?
WAF Onboarding Video 4: WAF Policy Tuning Overview
Phases
PRE-REQUISITES
Silverline WAF has been examining incoming traffic in transparent mode.
Silverline WAF has satisfied user requirements pertaining to the site flow patterns.
Sufficient learning and tuning has taken place to ensure a low false positive rate prior to blocking enforcement.
PHASE 1
In this initial phase, we tune attack signatures and other “known bads.” These blocking modules can be very finely tuned by cookies and parameters. This phase provides great security from the beginning.
Blocking Modules
Blocking Modules tuned in this phase:
Attack Signature Detected
Illegal HTTP Status in Response
Illegal Method
Illegal URL (if disallowed URLs configured)
Attack Types
Attack Type categories mitigated in this phase include:
Command Execution Signatures
Cross Site Scripting Signatures
Directory Indexing Signatures
HTTP Response Splitting Signatures
Information Leakage Signatures
OS Command Injection Signatures
Path Traversal Signatures
Remote File Include Signatures
SQL Injection Signatures
Server-Side Code Injection Signatures
XPath Injection Signatures
Information Leakage
Forceful Browsing
PHASE 2
In this phase, we tune RFC-compliance modules, look for bad format patterns that don’t comply with current RFC standards. This is often indicative of an attacker.
Blocking Modules
Blocking Modules tuned in this phase:
Cookie not RFC-Compliant
Disallowed File Upload Content Detected
Evasion Technique Detected
Failed to Convert Character
HTTP Protocol Compliance Failed
Request Length Exceeds Defined Buffer Size
Mandatory HTTP Header is Missing
Attack Type
Attack Type categories mitigated in this phase include:
Detection Evasion
HTTP Parser Attack
Abuse of Functionality
Parameter Tampering
PHASE 3
In this phase, we whitelist “known goods,” such as cookies, parameters (user name and password), and URIs. This helps us mark known good users so they will always be let through.
Blocking Modules
Blocking Modules tuned in this phase:
Parameter related violations (when configured)
Illegal File Type
Illegal Redirection Attempt
Modified Domain Cookies
Illegal URL (for allowed URLs, when appropriate for the application)
Malformed JSON data
Malformed XML data
Attack Types
Attack Type categories mitigated in this phase include:
Abuse of Functionality
Cross Site Scripting (XSS)
Cross-site Request Forgery
Forceful Browsing
Illegal Redirection
Parameter Tampering
Path Traversal
Predictable Resource Location
Remote File Include
Server-Side Code Injection
Session Hijacking
SQL-Injection
Trojan/Backdoor/Spyware
Vulnerability Scan
Web Scraping
XML Parser Attack
Other Modules (optional)
These modules are not enabled by default but are typically enabled in consultation with the SOC and/or in response to specific identified application protection requirements.  Such modules are transitioned to blocking as agreed between the SOC and the Customer, and they are not considered part of any specific Phase.
Blocking Modules
Common additional modules:
Geolocation Enforcement
Parameter/URL length or format violations
Illegal Meta Character of a Parameter name or value or in the URI
Web Services violations
CSRF protection
APPENDIX
Attack signature logic
Follow this section to understand how to distinguish between false positive requests that triggered an attack signature violation and malicious requests.
1. High Occurrences of an Attack Signature
a. Classic False Positive – The attack signature does not comply with the application logic. Options:
• Disable attack signature (either globally or on a parameter level)
• Create a user defined attack signature (could be complex)
• Open a case with F5 SOC requesting to tune the attack signature
b. Malicious Attack – Could be a malicious worm or script that is endlessly hitting the web application. How could this be determined?
• Are there any additional attack signatures that are triggered with these requests?
• Check the user-agent on the request. Is it a valid browser?
2. SQL Injections/XSS Attacks – SQL/XSS based attack signatures look for a combination of words and Meta characters, an efficient malicious SQL/XSS based injection attack will most probably trigger a number of different attack signatures in WAF. When encountering false positives where legitimate users are getting blocked on valid texts follow the below procedures:
a. Disable the specific attack signature
• Disable the attack signature on the specific parameter
• Disable the attack signature globally
b. Open a support ticket and request to have the attack signature less generic and more specific.
Article Updated: 2020-04-15 22:57:42 UTC

chunks.page_title: How To Configure Your On-Premises Setup To Route Traffic To Silverline Proxies
chunks.content:
--- Page: How To Configure Your On-Premises Setup To Route Traffic To Silverline Proxies ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=81953449
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Description
Before you can start routing traffic through Silverline Proxies, you must configure your on-premises setup with these steps.
Assumes you have already set-up proxies:
INITIAL Application Proxy Configuration for HTTP / HTTPS / WAF Proxy Service Type
or
INITIAL DDoS Proxy Configuration for TCP / UDP / DNS Cache Service Type
Assumes you have already tested and verified proxies
How to Test & Verify New Proxies
Environment
Silverline DDoS Protection
Silverline WAF
Proxy/Proxies
Procedure
How to Configure Your On-Premises Setup
IMPORTANT: Before you can start routing traffic through Silverline proxies, you must complete these steps.
Allow F5 Silverline IP Space
(
107.162.0.0/21
and
107.162.48.0/20
)
unrestricted through any firewall or router
. Ideally, you want to
only
allow the Silverline subnets, exclusively.  This will ensure that only Silverline-routed traffic can reach your network, and thus prevent attackers from being able to circumnavigate the Silverline infrastructure.
Regional PoPs customers
must also allow these IP ranges:
https://snat.f5silverline.com/
Q&A: What are the Silverline Subnets (SNAT) / IP Address ranges?
Disable any type of rate limiting or mitigation gear
as all connecting IPs will be F5 Silverline’s proxy IPs.
Lower TTLs
on all applicable DNS records
Turn on HTTP keep alive
in your webserver. This is important, as it reduces latency and server load. -- jump to
Turn on HTTP KeepAlive
for a step-by-step guide.
Change your webserver logging to use X-Forwarded-For
. This ensures that your web logs track who is attempting to access your website, as opposed to just tracking Silverline's proxy IP -- jump to
Web Logging: Configure to Use X-Forwarded-For
for a step-by-step guide.
Any application-based rules based off Source IPs should also be based off of X-Forwarded-For values.
Provision and test
VIPs,
then
cut over DNS to your newly assigned VIPs.
How to Set up DNS for use with Silverline
WAF
Prevent access from outside internet to your application, while still allowing Silverline traffic.
Webserver Optimization
Turn on HTTP KeepAlive
Turning on
HTTP KeepAlive
is important to both reduce latency and reduce webserver load.  Turning this on can yield load-reductions of 1000:1 ratio in relation to connections to your webserver as well as greatly improve the customer experience through reduced latency.
Apache
(httpd.conf)
<IfModule prefork.c>
StartServers 20
MinSpareServers 5
MaxSpareServers 20
ServerLimit 10000
MaxClients 5000
MaxRequestsPerChild 0
</IfModule>
Nginx
(nginx.conf)
# Timeout for keep-alive connections. Server will close connections after this time.
keepalive_timeout 300;
# Number of requests a client can make over the keep-alive connection. This is set high for testing.
keepalive_requests 100000;
IIS 6
Right click on web site and click on Properties
In the web site properties box check “Enable HTTP Keep-Alives” and set the timeout to at least 300 seconds.
IIS 7 / 7.5
Click on the web site
In the Features View, double click “HTTP Response Headers”
Click on Set Common Headers… in Actions panel
You should see HTTP keep-alive already checked.  If not, check it
Web Logging: Configure to Use
X-Forwarded-For
Note: XFF Header insertion is only available with the HTTP, SSL HTTP or SSL HTTP OFFLOAD proxy service types.
Logging X-Forwarded-For (XFF)
When placing your webserver behind a reverse proxy such as F5 Silverline’s, it’s important to remember that the IP addresses that connect to you will no longer be the client on the internet but IP addresses of F5 Silverline’s proxy cluster. To ensure that visibility of who’s viewing your website is not lost in the web logs,
it is important to configure logging on your webserver to use the
X-Forwarded-For
header as the source IP.
Apache
(httpd.conf)
LogFormat "%{X-Forwarded-For}i %l %u %t \"%r\" %>s %b \"%{Referer}i\" \"%{User-Agent}i\" %V %D " proxy
LogFormat "%h %l %u %t \"%r\" %>s %b \"%{Referer}i\" \"%{User-agent}i\" %V %D " direct
SetEnvIf X-Forwarded-For "^.*\..*\..*\..*" forwarded
CustomLog logs/direct_access_log direct env=!forwarded
CustomLog logs/proxy_access_log proxy env=forwarded
Nginx
(nginx.conf)
# Timeout for keep-alive connections. Server will close connections after this time.
keepalive_timeout 300;
# Number of requests a client can make over the keep-alive connection. This is set high for testing.
keepalive_requests 100000;
IIS 6
Right click on web site and click on Properties
In the web site properties box check “Enable HTTP Keep-Alives” and set the timeout to at least 300 seconds.
IIS 7 / 7.5
IIS 7 & later
Download (from the link below) and install IIS Advanced Logging.  Once installed, follow the steps below to add the X-Forwarded-For log field to IIS.
http://www.microsoft.com/en-gb/download/details.aspx?id=7211
1. From your Windows Server 2008 or Windows Server 2008 R2 device, open IIS Manager
2. From the Connections navigation pane, click the appropriate server, web site, or directory on which you are configuring Advanced Logging. The Home page appears in the main panel
3. From the Home page, under IIS, double-click Advanced Logging
4. From the Actions pane on the right, click Edit Logging Fields
5. From the Edit Logging Fields dialog box, click the Add Field button, and then complete the following:
-in the Field ID box, type X-Forwarded-For
-from the Category list, select Default
-from the Source Type list, select Request Header
-in the Source Name box, type X-Forwarded-For
-click the OK button in the Add Logging Field box, and then click the OK button in the Edit Logging Fields box
6. Click a Log Definition to select it. By default, there is only one: %COMPUTERNAME%-Server. The log definition you select must have a status of Enabled
7. From the Actions pane on the right, click Edit Log Definition
8. Click the Select Fields button, and then check the box for the X-Forwarded-For logging field
9. Click the OK button
10. From the Actions pane, click Apply
11. Click Return To Advanced Logging
12. In the Actions pane, click Enable Advanced Logging
Related Content
INITIAL Application Proxy Configuration for HTTP / HTTPS / WAF Proxy Service Type
INITIAL DDoS Proxy Configuration for TCP / UDP / DNS Cache Service Type
How to Test & Verify New Application / DDoS Proxies
Last Updated: 2020-10-20 00:02:02 UTC / Created: 2019-04-12 20:35:50 UTC

Maintained by
Overview
This finding
identifies API Gateway Stages that do not have the required tags per the PGE tagging standard.  To find out more about the current PG&E tagging standard, please refer to the following wiki page:
Criteria
Enforced at the stage level.  Can be configured on the resource; however, the detection is performed on the stage.  This would apply uniquely to each stage you deploy.
"AppID" tag must exist on the stage
"Environment"  tag must exist on the stage
"Owner" tag must exist on the stage
"Compliance" tag must exist on the stage
"Notify" tag must exist on the stage
"DataClassification" tag must exist on the stage
"CRIS" tag must exist on the stage

Existing Resource: Remediation Method
The following video provides an overview of how to properly tag API Gateway resources.
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
Changing your API to have the proper tags will not impact downstream consumers.  However, you must re-deploy the API to the stage in order for these changes to be applied.
While the videos above demonstrate console level configuration, it is assumed that your APIs are probably created using a Cloud Formation Template (CFT) or possibly Terraform.  You will need to update these templates in order for the remediations to be reproducible and reliable.

Maintained by
Overview
This finding identifies API stages that do not have logging enabled.
As seen in the console image shown above, this finding very simply checks to confirm that
Enable CloudWatch Logs
is checked and the Log Level is set to a value of
ERROR
or

.
Criteria
Enforced at the stage level.  Cannot be configured on the resource, only at the stage level.  This would apply uniquely to each stage you deploy.
Logging must be enabled
Logging level must be set to ERROR or INFO
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
The following video provides an overview of the this finding:
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
Changing your API to have logging enabled should not impact downstream consumers.  However, you must re-deploy the API to the stage in order for these changes to be applied.  Of course, testing should be done accordingly to ensure no negative impacts to your production environment before these changes are deployed.
While the videos above demonstrate console level configuration, it is assumed that your APIs are probably created using a Cloud Formation Template (CFT) or possibly Terraform.  You will need to update these templates in order for the remediations to be reproducible and reliable.
Cloud Formation
Type
: AWS::ApiGateway::Stage

:

: dev
TracingEnabled
: true
RestApiId
: !Ref rApiGateway
DeploymentId
: !Ref ApiDeployment

:
-

'/*'

'*'

chunks.page_title: AWS API Gateways without API Keys and Authorization set to NONE
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS API Gateways without API Keys and Authorization set to NONE ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=61089207
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Maintained By
Overview
This finding identifies API endpoints that have not been secured.  In order to remediate this finding you must either require the API to use an API key or you must use a
Lambda Authorizer
.
For the API Key method,
API Gateway reads the key and compares it against the keys in the usage plan. If there is a match, API Gateway throttles the requests according to the plan's request limit and quota thereby protecting the service from being overloaded.
As for the latter resolution,
a
Lambda Authorizer
is a peculiar type of Lambda function. It accepts an object containing a token and returns a JSON policy to allow or block an API execution.  At PG&E the authorizer will take the token and validate access against our IdP (PingFederate) which is also integrated with Active Directory and MyElectronic Access.  This token validation in the
Lambda Authorizer
provides both authentication and authorization.
Criteria
Enforced on API Methods
Enforced on resource definitions and
not
on deployed stage.  It is possible to receive vulnerability tickets on resources that have never been deployed.
OPTIONS method is
not
included in enforcement
MOCK integrations are
not
included in enforcement
Rule triggered when apiKeyRequired is false and authorizationType equals NONE
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
The following video provides an overview of the two remediation methods:
Note that in this video you will see a console level overview of how to create an API key and the associate usage plan and then link the key/usage plan to your API deployment stage.  In addition, this video will walk you through how to assign a Lambda Authorizer.  This instructional aid only shows you how to configure your API to use an Authorizer; it does not show you how to make a
Lambda Authorizer
.  It should also be noted that you can use a Lambda Authorizer
and
an API Key.  The Lambda Authorizer can return the API key as part of the response and the API can then use that key instead of passing the
X-API-Key header directly.  This hybrid approach is not covered in the video above.
If you do not have an available Lambda Authorizer in your account and need help creating one, the video below is a step-by-step how-to guide for making a Lambda Authorizer that will integrate with PG&E Active Directory using groups managed by MyElectronicAccess:
Please note that more detailed information regarding creating the Lambda Authorizer can be found at the following GitHub Repository:
GitHub: Lambda Authorizer with AD Group support
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
Changing your API to use an API key and/or a Lambda Authorizer will most likely impact any downstream users of your APIs.
If you select the API Key method, consumers will need to add the
X-API-Key header to their requests and they will need to manage the API key that you provide them.  This will result in code changes on their end.
If you select the Lambda Authorizer Method, consumers will need to provide the access token for their authenticated user.  This also will require a code change on their end in all probability.  As a result, any changes to the Authorization or API Key configuration of your API should be coordinated with downstream consumers and proper change management and clearances should be performed.
While the videos above demonstrate console level configuration, it is assumed that your APIs are probably created using a Cloud Formation Template (CFT) or possibly Terraform.  You will need to update these templates in order for the remediations to be reproducible and reliable.
Cloud Formation
UsagePlan
:
Type
:
'AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlan'
DependsOn
: [ rApiGateway, Stage ]
Properties
:
ApiStages
:
-
ApiId
: !Ref rApiGateway
Stage
: !Ref Stage
Description
: Usage Plan for TestAPI Key for Reference Implementation of REST API in AWS API Gateway
Quota
:
Limit
: 5000
Period
: MONTH
Throttle
:
BurstLimit
: 200
RateLimit
: 100
UsagePlanName
: Plan_ReferenceImplementationApiKey
ApiKey
:
Type
:
'AWS::ApiGateway::ApiKey'
DependsOn
: rApiGateway
Properties
:
Name
: ReferenceImplementationApiKey
Description
: CloudFormation API Key V1
Enabled
:
'true'
UsagePlanKey
:
Type
:
'AWS::ApiGateway::UsagePlanKey'
DependsOn
: [ rApiGateway, UsagePlan, ApiKey ]
Properties
:
KeyId
: !Ref ApiKey
KeyType
: API_KEY
UsagePlanId
: !Ref UsagePlan
rApiKeyGatewayMethod
:
Type
: AWS::ApiGateway::Method
Properties
:
ApiKeyRequired
: true
AuthorizationType
: NONE
HttpMethod
: GET
Useful Links
Set up API keys using the API Gateway console
Use API Gateway Lambda authorizers
