chunks.page_title: AWS API gateway not private and not using Silverline
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS API gateway not private and not using Silverline ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=61089281
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Maintained By
Overview
Regional and Edge Optimized endpoint types are public endpoints.  Unless an endpoint is deemed to be a public endpoint
and
it is configured to user Silverline, the endpoint type must be configured as Private.  This will help secure PG&E data and make APIs that are designed only for internal use to be available only within the PG&E network.
Criteria
Enforced on resource definitions and
not
on deployed stage.  It is possible to receive vulnerability tickets on resources that have never been deployed.
If configured to use Silverline in an approved use case, your resource policy must contain 107.162.0.0/16
If not using Silverline, Endpoint Type must equal Private
If Private, Resource Policy must include proper VPCe
Private API Accessible from AWS Accounts
If you're only accessing the API from other AWS Accounts
Private API Accessible from AWS Accounts and On Prem
If you need access from AWS Accounts and On Prem
Public API
If you need access from outside of the PG&E network
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
The following video provides an overview of how to configure a Private endpoint including the resource policy needed for the implementation at PG&E:
There are two different approaches for private endpoints:
PG&E Internal only services.  This use case is for browsers, apps, etc. in PG&E User land on the UDN that need to be able to access REST endpoints that are for internal use only.  In this use case, no external F5 configuration is required.  This configuration is 100% self service.
PG&E services that need to be exposed publicly.  In this use case browsers, apps, etc. on the internet need access to REST endpoints.  In order to expose these services, Cybersecurity must be engaged and changes to F5 are required.  External REST endpoints are only allowed via F5 or Silverline.
To publish an API as Private, following steps have to be done:
1. Publish the API on AWS Gateway as Private
2. Add a resource policy in your API to allow traffic from Transit VPC Endpoint only.
3. After you have updated the Endpoint Type to Private (and Save Changes) and you have updated the Resource Policy (and Save) do not forget to go to Resources and Re-Deploy your API.  The changes will not take effect until you do.
Additional Steps if you need to expose the API to public clients
Reach out to the F5 team at
f5admins@pge.com
or Picou Jr., Perry <PMPA@
pge.com
>) to configure the mapping of your API and provide the F5 URL
Provide the F5 URL for your API to end users/apps to consume
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
Please note that this change will be disruptive as this method requires
a URL change.  For example, your REST endpoint as shown in API Gateway may look like this:
https://1aiu2ekyd4.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/dev/cert/statistics
..
This Url will need to change to the following:
https://1aiu2ekyd4.us-west-2.atcapi.pge.com/dev/cert/statistics
.
The latter is routable from user land on the UDN.
All downstream consumers of your API will no longer be able to call the current URL.  Once the updates have been made, your endpoint will have a new URL that is only accessible from within the PG&E network.  You will need to coordinate with all of your API’s consumers in order to not impact operations.  Please ensure that proper change management and clearances are performed.
Cloud Formation
rApiGateway
:
Type
:
"AWS::ApiGateway::RestApi"
Properties
:
Name
: !Ref pApiName
Description
:
"This is a reference implementation of API Gateway using CloudFormation that is fully compliant with PG&E Cybersecurity policies"
Tags
:
-
Key
: AppID
Value
: !Ref pAppID
-
Key
: Owner
Value
: !Ref pOwner
EndpointConfiguration
:
Types
:
- PRIVATE
Policy
: !Sub |
{
"Version": "2012-10-17",
"Statement": [
{
"Effect": "Allow",
"Principal": "*",
"Action": [
"execute-api:GET",
"execute-api:OPTIONS",
"execute-api:POST",
"execute-api:DELETE",
"execute-api:PUT",
"execute-api:Invoke"
],
"Resource": [
"arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*/*/GET/*",
"arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*/*/OPTIONS/*",
"arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*/*/POST/*",
"arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*/*/DELETE/*",
"arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*/*/PUT/*"
]
},
{
"Effect": "Deny",
"Principal": "*",
"Action": "execute-api:*",
"Resource": "arn:aws:execute-api:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:*/*",
"Condition": {
"StringNotEquals": {
"aws:sourceVpce": "vpce-09e783836bb2de47b"
}
}
}
]
}

chunks.page_title: AWS EBS Snapshot is not encrypted
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS EBS Snapshot is not encrypted ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=121635525
Labels: saf20, aws, ec2, ebs, kb-how-to-article
Chunk 1/1
---

It is not possible to update an existing snapshot to use encryption. Therefore it will be necessary to create a copy of an existing unencrypted snapshot and encrypt the copy. Existing EBS snapshots that are not encrypted will need to be converted to encrypted snapshots.
You will need to delete the old snapshot
after the new snapshots have been created in order for the ticket to close.
Via Console
To copy a snapshot using the console
Open the Amazon EC2 console at
https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/
.
In the navigation pane, choose
Snapshots
.
Select the snapshot to copy, and then choose
Actions
,
Copy snapshot
.
For
Description
, enter a brief description for the snapshot copy.
By default, the description includes information about the source snapshot so that you can identify a copy from the original. You can change this description as needed.
For
Destination Region
, select the Region in which to create the snapshot copy.
Specify the encryption status for the snapshot copy.
To encrypt the snapshot copy, for
Encryption
, select
Encrypt this snapshot
. Then, for
KMS key
, select the KMS key to use to encrypt the snapshot in the destination Region.
Choose
Copy snapshot
.
CLI Example
aws ec2 copy-snapshot \
--region us-west-2 \
--source-region us-west-2 \
--source-snapshot-id snap-066877671789bd71b \
--description "This is my copied snapshot." \
--
kms-key-id arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:012345678910:key/1234abcd-12ab-34cd-56ef-1234567890ab \
--encrypted
More information regarding copying snapshots using encryption can be found here
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/ec2/copy-snapshot.html
Related articles
Related articles appear here based on the labels you select. Click to edit the macro and add or change labels.

--- Page: AWS ECR Image Based Vulnerabilities ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=180126077
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

ECR is the AWS service for storing container images. Images are made of several packages, each with it's own version number. As time passes, vulnerabilities are identified by the cyber security bodies and are documented via
CVE Findings
. This article will walk you the steps to view ECR image tickets that belong to your team and how to remediate them.
Step-by-step guide for viewing the Brinqa ECR Image Vulnerabilities report
Log into Brinqa
Copy and paste the link to the brinqa report in your web browser:
https://brinqavmp.comp.pge.com/tvm/cloudTickets?ticketType=Prisma%20Image%20Vulnerability&status=New&appId=
YourAppIdHere
Replace the above underlined string with your own appid (e.g. &appid=2102)
Hit enter on the browser and the report will provide you with all ECR vulnerability tickets for your team
The result should look like below:
Download the report by clicking on the download icon (down facing arrow with line below)
Once the report is ready you will receive an email with a download link
Download the report
Step-by-step guide for Remediating ECR Images that are not currently in use (e.g. older versions of your image)
Export the image
Delete the image
Step-by-step guide for Remediating ECR Images that are currently in use
Create a new version of the image with the latest package updates to ensure that the image is on the latest patched version
Deploy the new version of the image to your lower level environments for testing
Once validated, deploy the new image to your production environment
Export the old image
Delete the old image

chunks.page_title: How to identify which AWS Lambda functions are using same IAM Role
chunks.content:
--- Page: How to identify which AWS Lambda functions are using same IAM Role ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=300752117
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Prerequisites:
Brinqa Access
Prisma access
Overview
:
This policy identifies AWS Lambda functions that are using same IAM role.
Multiple Lambda functions using same IAM role violate the Principle of Least Privilege. Lambda function privileges are controlled by IAM roles. So Lambda functions execution role should be attached with appropriate IAM roles instead of generic or shared roles.
Policy as found in Prisma Cloud
Remediation Steps
:
Copy the AlertID from the assigned Brinqa ticket under finding overview (This is what connect our infrastructure backend Prisma Cloud to our front end ticketing tool Brinqa)
2.  After copying the AlertID you will log into
Prisma Cloud
and Ensure you select
Cloud security tab on the left corner
Alerts Tab on the top bar
and add filter AlertID and paste the copied AlertID from Brinqa
select the AWS Lambda using same IAM Role
3. Select the Asset name and navigate to the relationships tab and select the IAM role (this is the assigned IAM role to the function)
4. after selecting the IAM role another pop up will come and navigate to the Identity tag to view which IAM roles are assigned to what Lambda functions. (This shows what lambda functions share the same IAM role)
5. For remediation change alter the IAM execution role for one or more lambda functions to ensure each functions does not have a shared permissions to ensure the principle of Least Privilege.

chunks.page_title: AWS KMS Customer Managed Key does not have Key Rotation enabled
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS KMS Customer Managed Key does not have Key Rotation enabled ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=189564228
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

If you are experiencing a false positive, whereby rotation is enabled, but you are still getting ticketed, please ensure the following:
If you have deployed a KMS Resource using the pge-terraform-modules aws/kms module, please ensure you are using versions >= 0.0.7.
If you have deployed a KMS resource manually, or using CFT, please ensure you have added an EXPLICIT ALLOW BLOCK Statement in the KMS resource-based policy:
{
"Sid"
:
"CSCOE Prisma Cloud Access"
,
"Effect"
:
"Allow"
,
"Principal"
: {
"AWS"
:
"arn:aws:iam::${account_num}:role/PrismaCloudReadWriteMasterMemberRole-member"
},
"Action"
:
"kms:*"
,
"Resource"
:
"*"
}
If neither of these was the issue, please ensure you have proiperly enabled rotation for the KMS Key. From the console view, when you first create a KMS key, you will not have the ability to enable rotation. First, create the key, then, go to the resource and you will see Key Roation Configuration. Please ensure the Key rotation checkbox has been checked:
If you are using CloudFormation for the generation of your KMS key, please ensure you have
EnableKeyRotation
:
set to True

:
AWS::KMS::Key
Properties
:
Description
:
String
Enabled
:
Boolean
EnableKeyRotation
:
Boolean
KeyPolicy
:
Json
KeySpec
:
String
KeyUsage
:
String
MultiRegion
:
Boolean
PendingWindowInDays
:
Integer
Tags
:
-
Tag
Additional Notes:
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-kms-key.html

chunks.page_title: AWS Lambda Environment Variables not encrypted at-rest using CMK
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Lambda Environment Variables not encrypted at-rest using CMK ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=61089287
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Maintained By
Overview
When you create or update Lambda functions that use environment variables, AWS Lambda encrypts them using the AWS Key Management Service. When your Lambda function is invoked, those values are decrypted and made available to the Lambda code. This policy verifies that Lambda function uses the AMS Key Management Service to encrypt variables at-rest with CMK.
Criteria
Function has at least 1 environment variable
Environment variables must be encrypted at rest using a CMK
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
1. Go to the AWS console Lambda dashboard.
2. Click on the function name.
3. Under 'Environment variables', expand 'Encryption configuration.'
4. Under 'KMS key to encrypt at rest', choose 'Use a customer master key.'
5. Change the value of the dropdown menu to use your own CMK.
6. Select Save on upper right side of the page.
Additional Information:
To protect your environment variables, Prisma Cloud advises that you should select the 'Enable encryption helpers' checkbox when creating (or updating) your function. By selecting this option, your environment variables will be individually encrypted using a CMK of your choice. When supplying your own CMK key, you'll need to add kms:Decrypt to your execution role. In addition, the user that will be creating and updating the Lambda function must have permissions to use the KMS key.
The following video is available to illustrate how to make these changes top your function in the console:
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
Changes to the encryption of environment variables will be transparent to the execution of you Lambda; however, any changes to your application should be properly tested before deployment.
While the videos above demonstrate console level configuration, it is assumed that your Lambda Functions are probably created using a Cloud Formation Template (CFT) or possibly Terraform.  You will need to update these templates in order for the remediations to be reproducible and reliable.
CloudFormation Example:
Key Creation
rCmkKeyForLambdaEnvVars
:
Type
: AWS::KMS::Key
Properties
:
Description
: !Join [
''
, [
"CMK Key used for encrypting Environment Variables for the "
, !Ref pAudience,
" Authorizer"
,!Ref pAuthorizerName,
" Lambda Authorizer Function"
]]
Enabled
: true
EnableKeyRotation
: true
KeyPolicy
:
Version
:
"2012-10-17"
Id
: !Join [
''
, [!Ref pAudience,
" Authorizer"
,!Ref pAuthorizerName,
"Policy"
]]
Statement
:
-
Sid
: Allows root access the key
Effect
: Allow
Principal
:
AWS
:
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:root
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/CloudAdmin
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/SuperAdmin
Action
:
- kms:*
Resource
:
"*"
-
Sid
: Allow use of the key
Effect
: Allow
Principal
:
AWS
:
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/CloudAdmin
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/SuperAdmin
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/ServiceCatalogLaunchRole
Action
:
-
'kms:Encrypt'
-
'kms:Decrypt'
-
'kms:ReEncrypt*'
-
'kms:GenerateDataKey*'
-
'kms:DescribeKey'
Resource
:
'*'
-
Sid
: Allow attachment of persistent resources
Effect
: Allow
Principal
:
AWS
:
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/CloudAdmin
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/SuperAdmin
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/ServiceCatalogLaunchRole
Action
:
-
'kms:CreateGrant'
-
'kms:ListGrants'
-
'kms:RevokeGrant'
Resource
:
'*'
Condition
:
Bool
:
'kms:GrantIsForAWSResource'
:
'true'
rCmkKeyForLambdaEnvVarsAlias
:
Type
: AWS::KMS::Alias
DependsOn
: rCmkKeyForLambdaEnvVars
Properties
:
AliasName
: !Sub alias/lambda-authorizer-encryption-key-${pAuthorizerName}
TargetKeyId
: !Ref rCmkKeyForLambdaEnvVars
Key Usage
rLambdaAuthorizer
:
Type
:
"AWS::Lambda::Function"
Properties
:
Handler
:
"index.handler"
Role
: !GetAtt [ rAuthorizerExecutionRole, Arn ]
Code
:
S3Bucket
:
"ccoe-template-repo"
S3Key
:
"LambdaAuthorizers/LambdaAuthorizerDeployment.zip"
Runtime
:
"nodejs12.x"
Timeout
: 3
KmsKeyArn
: !GetAtt [ rCmkKeyForLambdaEnvVars, Arn ]
Environment
:
Variables
:
AUDIENCE
: !Ref pAudience
You may opt to do a service level Customer Managed CMK in the account.  In this case, you would have a reusable CMK with an Alias of
servicekey/lambda
.  In addition, Parameter Store would also have a parameter called
/servicekey/lambda
that would contain the ARN of the key to be used by all Lambdas in that account.  If you opt to use this approach, please use the following CloudFormation:
For the Parameters Section:
# Specify KMS Key of Lambda Service to Encrypt Environment Variables
pLambdaKMSArn
:
Description
:
"ARN of Lambda Service CMK"
Type
:
'AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<String>'
Default
: /servicekey/lambda
And in your Lambda Function:
KmsKeyArn
: !Ref pLambdaKMSArn

Using AWS Lambda environment variables

chunks.page_title: AWS Lambda Using Same IAM role
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Lambda Using Same IAM role ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=68321816
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Maintained By
Overview
When you create or update Lambda functions, it must have a unique named IAM role for that function.  When your Lambda function is invoked, this specific IAM role will be used.
Criteria
Each Lambda function must have a uniquely named and created IAM role.
IAM roles cannot be used by more than one Lambda function.
Existing Resource: Remediation Method:
1. Go to the AWS console Lambda dashboard.
2. Click on the function name.
3. Under 'Permissions", review the current role name.  If non-unique, proceed to the next stop.  If the role name is unique/specific, then your Lambda does not need to be remediated.
4. Go to "IAM" from main AWS console screen.
5. Create a new role, name it very similar to your Lambda function name and attach any and all required AWS IAM policies to the newly created role.
6. While creating the new role, please assign tags per the video below.
7, Save your newly created IAM role while keeping a track of it's name.
8. Return back to the Lambda dashboard in the AWS console & click on "Permissions".
9.  Click "Edit" under "Execution Role", find the name of your newly created role, and then click save.
Additional Information:   When creating a new, unique IAM role for your Lambda function, if your function code is currently in Node.js 6.x, you will need to modify and upgrade to Node.js 12.x.
New Resource: Creating New Role for a New Lambda Function:
1. Go to the AWS console Lambda dashboard.
2. Click on "Create Functions"
3. Create this new function, give it a clearly defined, unique name.
4. Under "Permissions", click on "Change default execution role" and then under "Execution role", click on "Create a new role with basic Lambda permissions.
5. Click on "Create functions".
6. Once the new Lambda function has been created, review and confirm a new IAM role with it's specific name has been created.
The following video is available to illustrate how to make these changes to your function in the console:
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
While the video above demonstrates console level configuration, it is assumed that your Lambda Functions are probably created using a Cloud Formation Template (CFT) or possibly Terraform.  You will need to update these templates in order for the remediations to be reproducible and reliable.
CloudFormation Example:
Unique Role with managed policy and an inline policy:
# This inline policy is required in order to enable tracing in the Lambda function (as per Cyberpolicy policy)
rTracerPolicy
:
Type
: AWS::IAM::ManagedPolicy
Properties
:
Description
: Policy that allows xray tracing
ManagedPolicyName
: !Join [
''
, [!Ref pAuthorizerName,
"TracerPolicy"
]]
PolicyDocument
:
Version
:
'2012-10-17'
Statement
:
-
Effect
: Allow
Action
:
- xray:PutTraceSegments
- xray:PutTelemetryRecords
Resource
:
"*"
# Unique (as per Cybersecurity policy) role for Lambda execution and Private VPC attachment
rAuthorizerExecutionRole
:
Type
: AWS::IAM::Role
Properties
:
RoleName
: !Join [
''
, [!Ref pAuthorizerName,
"Role"
]]
AssumeRolePolicyDocument
:
Version
:
'2012-10-17'
Statement
:
-
Effect
: Allow
Principal
:
Service
: lambda.amazonaws.com
Action
: sts:AssumeRole
ManagedPolicyArns
:
- arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaVPCAccessExecutionRole
- arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
- !Ref rTracerPolicy
Applying the role:
rLambdaAuthorizer
:
Type
:
"AWS::Lambda::Function"
Properties
:
Handler
:
"index.handler"
Role
: !GetAtt [ rAuthorizerExecutionRole, Arn ]
As stated in the documentation and video, if your Lambda code is in Node.js 6.x, you will need to convert your code to Node.js 12.x.
This has the potential for impacting the functionality of your Lambda and may require updating other components of the code such as libraries, etc.
Please be sure to thoroughly test your Lambda functionality after converting to Node.js 12.x.

chunks.page_title: AWS Lambda Functions Not Assigned To Access Within VPC
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Lambda Functions Not Assigned To Access Within VPC ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=70327960
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Maintained By
Overview
This policy identifies the AWS Lambda functions which do not have access within the VPC. Amazon Lambda functions should have access to VPC-only resources such as AWS Redshift data warehouses, AWS ElastiCache clusters, AWS RDS database instances, and service endpoints that should be only accessible from within a particular Virtual Private Cloud (VPC).
Criteria
All Lambda functions that access certain resources should only be accessible through a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC).
Existing Resource-Remediation Method:
1. Sign in to the AWS Console
2. Go to the Lambda main page
3. In the navigation pane, select 'Functions'
4. Choose the reported Lambda function
5. Click on 'Actions' and select 'View details'
6. Under 'Network', select the VPC that you want to assign
7. Assign 2 Subnets and 1 Security group
8. Click 'Save'
New Resource:
1. Sign in to the AWS Console
2. Go to Lambda
3. In the navigation pane, select 'Create Function'
4. Give the function a unique name and click "Create Function"
5. Click on 'Actions' and select 'View details'
6. Under 'Network', select the VPC that you want to assign
7. Assign 2 Subnets and 1 Security group
8. Click 'Save'
The following video is available to illustrate how to make these changes to your function in the console:
CloudFormation Sample Code
Parameters
Parameters
:

:
Type
: AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<String>
Description
:
"SSM Parameter that holds the VPC"
Default
:
"/vpc/id"

: 1

:
Type
: AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<String>
Description
:
"SSM Parameter that holds the Subnet for Availability Zone A."

:
"/vpc/privatesubnet1/id"

: 1
pSubnetIdaZb
:
Type
: AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<String>
Description
:
"SSM Parameter that holds the Subnet for Availability Zone B."
Default
:
"/vpc/privatesubnet2/id"
MinLength
: 1
pSubnetIdaZc
:
Type
: AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<String>
Description
:
"SSM Parameter that holds the Subnet for Availability Zone C."
Default
:
"/vpc/privatesubnet3/id"
MinLength
: 1
Security Group
rSecurityGroup
:
Type
: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup
Properties
:
GroupName
: !Join [
""
, [
"SecurityGroup"
, !Select [
"0"
, !Split [
"-"
, !Ref pVpcId ] ], !Select [
"1"
, !Split [
"-"
, !Ref pVpcId ] ] ] ]
GroupDescription
:
"Account Creation Security Group"
VpcId
: !Ref pVpcId
SecurityGroupEgress
:
# Needs to be wide open to support system calls to SSM to get Parameter Store variables
-
IpProtocol
: tcp
FromPort
: 443
ToPort
: 443
CidrIp
:
"0.0.0.0/0"
-

: tcp
FromPort
: 443
ToPort
: 443
CidrIp
:
"10.210.0.32/32"
-

: tcp
FromPort

ToPort
: 443
CidrIp
:
"130.19.244.11/32"
-
IpProtocol
: tcp
FromPort
: 53
ToPort
: 53
CidrIp

"10.89.56.2/32"
-

FromPort

CidrIp
:
"10.89.56.2/32"
Lambda Execution Role
The Lambda Execution Role needs to be updated to include these permissions under Statement of Policies
-
Effect
:
"Allow"
Action
:
-
"ec2:CreateNetworkInterface"
-
"ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces"
-
"ec2:DeleteNetworkInterface"
Resource
:
"*"
Lambda
The Lambda resource needs to be updated to include the VPC and Subnet configurations
VpcConfig
:
SecurityGroupIds

- !GetAtt rSecurityGroup.GroupId

- !Ref pSubnetIdaZa
- !Ref pSubnetIdaZb
- !Ref pSubnetIdaZc

chunks.page_title: AWS Lambda function is missing required tags
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Lambda function is missing required tags ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=79565852
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Maintained By
Overview
This finding
identifies Lambda Functions that do not have the required tags per the PGE tagging standard.  To find out more about the current PG&E tagging standard, please refer to the following wiki page:
Cloud Security Tagging Standard
Criteria
Lambda function must have the following tags:
"AppID" tag must exist
"Environment"  tag must exist
"Owner" tag must exist
"Compliance" tag must exist
"Notify" tag must exist
"DataClassification" tag must exist
"CRIS" tag must exist
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
Go to the AWS console Lambda dashboard
Click on the function name
Click the configuration tab
Click 'tags'. from the left side bar
Input all proper key/value pairs for your tags
The following video provides an overview of how to properly tag API Gateway resources
CloudFormation Example:
rLambdaFunction
:
Type
:
"AWS::Lambda::Function"
Properties
:
Tags
:
-
Key
: AppID
Value
: !Ref pAppID
-
Key
: Owner
Value
: !Ref pOwner

chunks.page_title: AWS Aurora PostgreSQL DB Cluster snapshot is encrypted using default KMS key instead of Customer Managed CMK
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Aurora PostgreSQL DB Cluster snapshot is encrypted using default KMS key instead of Customer Managed CMK ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=180126056
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Explanation
It is currently required that all RDS clusters be configured with KMS CMK (via SAF 2.0). If you are not using CMKs for encryption of your databases you will see tickets with the summary titles above. Currently it is not possible to modify an existing RDS cluster to use a different encryption key. You will need to take a snapshot of the existing cluster and redeploy the instance with a CMK.
Steps for Remediation
Modifying encryption for your RDS clusters is currently not supported. You will need to redeploy your clusters with a snapshot of your cluster using CMK.
Create a snapshot of the existing cluster
Deploy a new cluster using the CMK information

chunks.page_title: AWS S3 Bucket is not Blocking Public Access
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS S3 Bucket is not Blocking Public Access ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=61096553
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

scroll to the bottom for programmatic remediation of AWS S3 bucket public access
!! Important !!
If S3 bucket is serving web content ( Static Web Hosting is enabled ) follow the
Cloud Tagging Notes
optional non-security tags for remediation.
Overview
Issue
Resolved
Criteria
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
CloudFormation Example
Add PublicAccessBlockConfiguration: under Properties
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
Useful Links
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Programmatic remediation of AWS S3 bucket public access not blocked
You should have some familiarity with AWS Lambda, GitHub and Cloud Formation.
Cloudformation template: s3_block_public_access.yaml
It creates Lambda function with an associated schedule to run everyday. The function will check each S3 bucket. If not already blocking public access and is not for web hosting, public access blocking will be enabled unless the bucket is tagged with {"BucketType": "StaticWeb"} or {aws:cloudformation:stack-name}. Buckets with web hosting configuration will be skipped because it is not compatible with public access blocking.
Github Repo
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-s3-security-remediation
Download and save the s3_block_public_access.yaml file locally to your computer
Log into targeted AWS account and navigate to AWS cloud-formation service enter the required information.
AWS cloud formation will deploy the associated resources.  If the IAM role already exists then the account has already deployed this script and is running.
IAM
s3-block-public-access-role
Json
permissions
To verify the script has deployed check AWS lambda function called s3-block-public-access

chunks.page_title: AWS Access logging not enabled on S3 buckets
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS Access logging not enabled on S3 buckets ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=157484265
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Intro:
You should have some familiarity with AWS Lambda, GitHub and Cloud Formation.
For partner accounts Scroll to the bottom regarding the repo and associated permissions to deploy the auto remediation lambda function into your accounts
To check S3 server access logging is enabled go to the suspect S3 bucket > properties and scroll down to server access logging.
Verify the S3 account has the target logging bucket. Target logging bucket follows the following naming convention
ccoe-s3-accesslogs-spoke-${AWS::Region}-${AWS::AccountId}
2.Check the S3 bucket > properties resource Tags.
If Bucket Tag key contains "cloud formation" then you must update and deploy the template to enable S3 access logging to future proof any template change and undoing any manual alteration to enable server access logging.
CF( cloud formation )
Template
implementation of enabling access logging
To programmatically enable S3 access logging. You must update the associated GitHub repository. From the troubled bucket cloud formation stack name tag. You will be able to trace the deployment method and associated GitHub Repo.
Once you have identified the GitHub Repository input the following configuration in the S3 bucket resource under properties.
Official doc
***Note make sure to substitute
pBucketName to the S3 bucket resource name***
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
LoggingConfiguration:
DestinationBucketName:
!Sub ccoe-s3-accesslogs-spoke-${AWS::Region}-${AWS::AccountId}
LogFilePrefix: !Sub ${pBucketName}/
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
b.
If Bucket Tag key does not contain a "cloud formation". Then you can enable S3 access logging via a lambda function.
For auto remediation
Enter into shared services Prod account and search for lambda function  "
s3-access-logging"
Move the test tab and run the Lambda function remember to input the account that the S3 is in for remediation
example input
{
"AccountId": "686137062481"
}
3. Run the lambda function and S3 access logging should be enabled for all buckets within the targeted account.
PARTNERS
Please find the
GitHub Repository Cloud Formation Template
for access logs.
****
note
the function without proper permissions will generate an error due to the fact that the input parameter takes an account number that could be different in which the function is deployed. Thus you must add an sts assume role to the lambda function. ****
Once deployed to your selected account. open the lambda function and browse to selected execution role.
Browse to create an inline policy amd select JASON TAb
Enter the following configuration and you can begin executing the code
{
"Version": "2012-10-17",
"Statement": [
{
"Action": [
"lambda:InvokeFunction",
"s3:ListAllMyBuckets",
"s3:GetBucketWebsite",
"s3:GetBucketTagging",
"s3:PutBucketLogging",
"s3:GetBucketLocation"
],
"Resource": "*",
"Effect": "Allow"
},
{
"Action": [
"logs:CreateLogGroup",
"logs:CreateLogStream",
"logs:PutLogEvents"
],
"Resource": "arn:aws:logs:*:*:*",
"Effect": "Allow"
},
{
"Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
"Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/AWSCloudFormationStackSetExecutionRole",
"Effect": "Allow"
}
]
}
once the Lambda Permissions have been updated you can move back to the lambda function
Move the test tab and run the Lambda function remember to input the account that the S3 is in for remediation
example input
{
"AccountId": "686137062481"
}

--- Page: AWS S3 Missing Required Tags ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=79562150
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Cloud COE has developed an automated programmatic remediation of 'S3 buckets missing required tags' through the use of a Lambda function that is scheduled to run daily.
This Lambda function will check all S3 buckets in the account which were not created by ClouFormation or Terraform.
Note:
Buckets created by CloudFormation or Terraform will be skipped by this Lambda function, and should be remediated through changes in the stack tags. This Function will not over write existing mandatory tags and will only add the mandatory tags that are missing.
Cloud Tagging Standard
Mandatory tags:
AppID
Environment
DataClassification
CRIS
Notify
Owner
Compliance
Git Repo CF template
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-s3-security-remediation/blob/master/s3_add_missing_tags.yaml
Steps:
1) Download or store the CF template locally
- If using git you can clone the file/repo using the following command
git clone
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-s3-security-remediation.git
example shown below
2) Deploy template
Log in the AWS account with sufficient permissions (admin or above to be able to deploy resources) and navigate to AWS cloud formation service in the search bar
In cloud formation head to stacks and create stack new resources
Upload CF stack and complete Required tags that will be added to non compliant resources.
Navigate to Lambda function and configure the required test event.
The lambda function has a time out of 3 minutes
.

chunks.page_title: AWS S3 bucket is not using PGE managed KMS key
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS S3 bucket is not using PGE managed KMS key ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=76586242
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Remediation
Per PG&E CS-TECH-020 Standard, customer managed keys (CMKs) must be used with 'Confidential' or 'Restricted' data. AWS managed keys (KMS) are approved for general encryption at rest requirements.
In Cloud COE accounts, we use this
Cloudformation template
to create a CMK with alias name servicekey/s3 and store its ARN in parameter store /servicekey/s3.  You can use the Cloudformation template as a reference to create CMK in partner's account, but you have to modify the key policy to include your IAM roles.
If you have other AWS resources (e.g. IAM role, Lambda, EC2 etc) access the CMK encrypted buckets, you have to add the following permissions in their resource role policy:
for S3 read access: kms:Decrypt
for S3 write access: kms:Encrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey
Note for S3 buckets accessed by Storage Gateway
If your S3 bucket is configured for Storage Gateway file share access, please update CMK key policy to allow use of the key by principal arn:aws:iam::972333128287:root
Sid: Allow use of the key by Storage Gateway account
Effect: Allow
Principal:
AWS:
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/SuperAdmin
- !Sub arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/CloudAdmin
- "arn:aws:iam::972333128287:root"
Action:
- 'kms:Encrypt'
- 'kms:Decrypt'
- 'kms:ReEncrypt*'
- 'kms:GenerateDataKey*'
- 'kms:DescribeKey'
Resource: '*'
Using S3 console
In the bucket's Properties tab, edit the Default Encryption.
Using Cloudformation
Parameters:
pCmkArn:
Description: Parameter store of the S3 CMK ARN for bucket encryption
Type: 'AWS::SSM::Parameter::Value<String>'
Default: '/servicekey/landingzone'
Resources:
rS3bucket:
Type: AWS::S3::Bucket
Properties:
BucketEncryption:
ServerSideEncryptionConfiguration:
- ServerSideEncryptionByDefault:
SSEAlgorithm: "aws:kms"
KMSMasterKeyID: !Ref pCmkArn
BucketKeyEnabled: true
Using SDK boto3 API - Python
def encrypt_bucket_with_cmk( bucket_name, cmk_arn ):
try:
response = s3_client.put_bucket_encryption(
Bucket=bucket_name,
ServerSideEncryptionConfiguration={
'Rules': [
{
'ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault': {
'SSEAlgorithm': 'aws:kms',
'KMSMasterKeyID': cmk_arn
},
'BucketKeyEnabled': True
},
]
}
)

When
that S3 bucket access log is configured with centralized logging bucket and label
Then

Step-by-step guide
Add the steps involved:

Navigate to the Bucket/Properties Tab
Scroll down to
Server access logging
press Edit.
Enable Server access logging
Populate Target bucket with the s3 address of the bucket, ccoe-s3-accesslogs-spoke-region-account#/ .
Specify a subfolder, aka label, by appending this bucket's name to the Target bucket field.
Push Save Changes.
Related articles
Related articles appear here based on the labels you select. Click to edit the macro and add or change labels.

chunks.page_title: AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=61096551
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
When you receive a Brinqa ticket with the summary "
AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy". This ticket indicates that your bucket policy does not have a
Deny
for traffic that is not using
SecureTransport
. To remediate this ticket please ensure that you update your bucket policy. Examples of how to remediate this via JSON (directly from the S3 console) or from CF template can be found below.
How To Remediate the Bucket Policy JSON
Ensure that you have the below JSON block added to your Bucket Policy to
Deny
access to your buckets when
SecureTransport
is not being used. This policy can be updated directly from the S3 web console or CLI but we suggest that you use infrastructure as code (TF, CFT) as a best practice. More information on how to use the S3 console can be found here
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/example-policies-s3.html
Sample Bucket Policy to remediate your Brinqa ticket
{
"Version"
:
"2012-10-17"
,
"Statement"
: [
{
"Effect"
:
"Deny"
,
"Principal"
:
"*"
,
"Action"
:
"s3:*"
,
"Resource"
: [
"arn:aws:s3:::examplebucket/*"
,
"arn:aws:s3:::examplebucket"
],
"Condition"
: {
"Bool"
: {
"aws:SecureTransport"
:
"false"
}
}
}
]
}
How to Remediate the Bucket Policy Via CloudFormation Template
The example YAML CF template block shows how to create and attach a bucket policy which has a
Deny
for traffic that is not using
SecureTranport
for a bucket with the resource name rExampleBucket.
rExampleBucketPolicy:
Type:
'AWS::S3::BucketPolicy'
Properties:
Bucket: !Ref rExampleBucket
PolicyDocument:
Version:
'2012-10-17'
Statement:
- Sid: AWSSecureTransport
Effect: Deny
Principal:
'*'
Action:
's3:*'
Resource:
- !Sub
'arn:aws:s3:::${rExampleBucket}'
- !Sub
'arn:aws:s3:::${rExampleBucket}/*'
Condition:
Bool:
aws:SecureTransport:
false
Resolved
Criteria

chunks.content:

URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=192349205
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

This details below involve instructions on how to set up either a SAML or OAuth application to use MFA.
SAML MFA Setup
Enable MFA for a SAML application
Add SAML Connection to the MFA SAML App Selector (may be called just MFAApp in some environments)
Remove SAML Connection from the associated Selector - most likely Kerberos & Form - PGE
Replicate changes
OAuth MFA Setup
Enable MFA for an OAuth/OIDC application
Add OAuth client to the MFA OAuth App Selector (aka MFAOAuth)
Remove OAuth Client from the associated Selector - most likely Kerberos & Form - PGE OAuth
Replicate changes

chunks.page_title: PG&E Entra ID Client Credential OAuth Flow (System to System Authentication)
chunks.content:
--- Page: PG&E Entra ID Client Credential OAuth Flow (System to System Authentication) ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=334373272
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

PG&E
Entra ID
Client Credential OAuth Flow
This document
provides
steps
to perform
certificate-based
client authentication
for
OAuth flow.
Certificate based authentication is
a
secure
authentication than the traditional
password/
secret-based
authentication.
Since
Entra ID (formerly known as Azure AD)
will be PG&E’s new Identity provider aka
OAuth authorization server, this document will focus on
integration with
Entra ID
.
Client authentication is
important in the OAuth flow
. It
becomes
more
important in Client Credentials grant flow
because of
it being the only authentication
requirement
to obtain bearer token.
Steps to
obtain Bearer token in Client credentials OAuth flow
-
1.Generate a
certificate:
OAuth
clients
will need to
generate
a certificate and share the
public
cert with
Azure
SSO team.
SSO
team will upload the public cert in the
Entra ID
for the OAuth connection. OAuth client in this case
is
the application trying to obtain bearer token from
Entra ID
.
Certificate for non-
prod
connection can be self-signed. For prod certificate should be CA
signed (
can be internal PG&E CA
signed
)
. CN
and DNS
value of the
certificate
should be in th
e
format -
{
A
pp
ID
}
-{
Application
Name
}
.
EntraID.AuthN.pge.com
Tip:
U
se tools like OpenSSL to generate the certificate.
openssl req -x509 -sha256 -nodes -days
365
-newkey rsa:
4096
-keyout
private
.key -out mycert.crt
2.O
btain the x
5t header parameter
for the JWT
:
X5t is Base64url-encoded SHA-1 thumbprint of the X.509 certificate's DER
encoding.
Tip: OpenSSL has commands to generate
x5t. one such command is
- echo $(
openssl
x509 -in mycert.
crt
-fingerprint -
noout
) | sed 's/SHA1 Fingerprint=//g' | sed 's/://g' |
xxd
-r -
ps
| base64
3.Construct JWT
:
JWT
has 3 parts
–
a.
Header
: It
contains
the information
about the
key and the algorithm used to generate the signature.
Sample header-
{
"
alg
": "RS256",
"
typ
": "JWT",
"x5t": "Py3f8vKDUjR3bqfUhzMIyOb8XuM="
}
Parameter
Remark
alg
Should be RS256
typ
Should be JWT
X5t
Explained in step#
2 ,
Base64url-encoded SHA-1 thumbprint of the X.509 certificate's DER encoding.
b.
Pay load
:
C
ontains
set of claims
.
Sample payload
-
{
"
aud
": "
https://login.microsoftonline.com/e84bb675-81b1-4f49-985b-3e2d77c6c94d/oauth2/v2.0/token
",
"exp": 1689303330,
"
iss
": "c1127ab5-330a-46ed-b6a9-039795748df9",
"
jti
": "22b3bb26-e046-42df-9c96-65dbd72c1c81",
"
nbf
": 1484592741,
"sub": "c1127ab5-330a-46ed-b6a9-039795748df9"
,
"
iat
": 1484592741,
}
Claim
Sample value
Description
aud
https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenantId}/oauth2/v2.0/token
The "
aud
" (audience) claim
identifies
the recipients that the
JWT is intended for (Azure AD)
exp
The "exp" (
expiration
time) claim
identifies
the
expiration
time on or after which the JWT MUST NOT be accepted for processing. See
RFC 7519, Section 4.1.4
. This allows the assertion to be used until then, so keep it short - 5-10 minutes after
nbf
at most. Azure AD does
not place
restrictions on the
exp
time currently
.
Iss
ClientID
The "
iss
" (issuer) claim
identifies
the principal that issued the JWT, in this case your
client
application. Use the GUID application ID.
jti
The "
jti
" (JWT ID) claim provides a unique identifier for the JWT. The identifier value MUST be assigned in a manner that ensures that there is a negligible probability that the same value will be accidentally assigned to a different data object; if the application uses multiple issuers, collisions MUST be prevented among values produced by different issuers as well. The "
jti
" value is a case-sensitive
string.
RFC 7519, Section 4.1.7
nbf
The "
nbf
" (not before) claim
identifies
the time before which the JWT MUST NOT be accepted for processing.
RFC 7519, Section 4.1.5
. Using the current time is
appropriate
.
sub
ClientID
The "sub" (subject) claim
identifies
the subject of the JWT, in this case also your application. Use the same value as
iss
.
iat
The "
iat
" (issued at) claim
identifies
the time at which the JWT was issued. This claim can be used to
determine
the age of the JWT.
RFC 7519, Section 4.1.5
.
c.
Signature
:
S
ignature allows a JWT to be validated against modifications and
it
confirms the authenticity of the
JWT
.
4. Call Entra ID to obtain bearer token:
Make a
POST
call to
Entra ID
token end point -
https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant ID}/oauth2/v2.0/token
with following OAuth parameters in the body
.
client_id
–
T
his is the client identifier.
The value
for this parameter will be provided by
Azure
SSO team.
grant_type
=
client_credentials
scope
-
This defines the access granted to the bearer token.
The value
for this will be provided
by
Azure
SSO team.
client_assertion_type
=
urn:ietf
:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-bearer
client_assertion
–
B
ase 64 encoded JWT
constructed in step#3 above.
If OAuth parameters in the request are correct and authentication is successful
,
Entra ID
will provide
access
token in response.
Sample response:
{"token_type":"Bearer","expires_in":3599,"ext_expires_in":3599,"access_token":"
yJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsIng1dCI6IlhSdmtvOFA3QTNVYVdTblU3Yk05blQwTWpoQSIsImtpZCI6IlhSdmtvOFA3QTNVYVdTblU3Yk05blQwTWpoQSJ9..sJyB8_9YimSP9YArshSZf9yaGsUATBSm7KAuq1cJ8fPcIfzdLHGfvkDXlry6echT1PganBYQhy3kgN24ysGtKwqbbhroytbcbA_5Zoki3o-7CrNPmMoJR2AjVPLDgkid76bvYgPEg_hCb9xLWCVqOXubA7gwVI-vCHtPBpT8nT6r3CnyEtMy0yea2MorCryyZUyatOe4ptYq_28rnVx7CKxbhGmvC5TG3AxzMdI3I1FdRie_AIvp
2Mm
Gf7autpWvQknj47PdgS4mw3V47qovKFnOLpgstQ-VI3bCYyhe5qokpNWffBmoA17q4mR5e60sr9JzAQxyP_nT39RlwOCV7
"}
Decoded token value -
{
"
aud
": "
api
://
69019a41-8eab-4961-a8b7-0fed123eebc0.lab.pge.com
",
"
iss
": "
https://sts.windows.net/e84bb675-81b1-4f49-985b-3e2d77c6c94d/
",
"
iat
": 1710263293,
"
nbf
": 1710263293,
"exp": 1710267193,
"
aio
": "E2NgYBBTsP6z+/xWf95XXdMuv7/
mBAA
=",
"
appid
": "69019a41-8eab-4961-a8b7-0fed123eebc0",
"
appidacr
": "1",
"
idp
": "
https://sts.windows.net/e84bb675-81b1-4f49-985b-3e2d77c6c94d/
",
"
oid
": "ed131581-28cb-4112-9833-a138e0fefda5",

chunks.page_title: Intake a new SAML or OAuth Application
chunks.content:
--- Page: Intake a new SAML or OAuth Application ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=196347123
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

These notes are for the Ping or Azure SSO Admin Team, not for the Application Teams,,,
How to intake a new SAML or Auth Application (the process for PingAccess is slightly different).
Most application teams should first work with the MEA team to get their AD groups created.  They likely have filled out the Rapid Onboarding form, so you may be able to get some information from
https://rapidonboarding.cloud.pge.com
.
Ask the application team to fill out the SSO Intake questionnaire at:
SSO Questionnaire.xlsx
Ask the application team to create a Work Order using:
CyberSecurity Services (pge.com)
-  Service = Authentication
Add an entry to the CommSheet spreadsheet on the appropriate tab (New Integration) at:
CommSheet.xlsx
.
Confirm that AD groups have been created (for PROD Tenant) and tenant-only groups have been added to AAD Dev for lower environments, where required.
For Ping deployments, create a Standard CRQ for lower environments using the PingFederate -  Lower Environment Changes template.  For PROD deployment, create a Standard CRQ using the New PingFederate SSO Integration template.  Add Anton or Ping Operations group for Approvals before the Scheduled stage.  Call ENOC to start and end CRQs.
For Azure deployments, no CRQ is required for changes to the PGEDev Tenant.  For PROD changes, create a Standard CRQ using the Azure AD SSO - Configure new application template.  Add Anton or Ping Operations group for Approvals before the Scheduled stage.  Call ENOC to start and end CRQ.

chunks.page_title: Move an application - Post move follow up steps
chunks.content:
--- Page: Move an application - Post move follow up steps ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=195036793
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

After migrating an application to a new location, here are two recommended steps to ensure the application is found during reboots and other automated procedures
Create a Link from the old location to the new
Create a link to ensure the application can be found in the old location.
If the application used to live at /opt/appname and you moved it to /app/appname, run this command after moving /opt/appname:
ln -s /app/appname /opt/appname
The first directory listed in the command is the new location and the second directory is the old location.
Create a WO for the Linux Ops team to update INIT scripts
Locate the associated INIT scripts in /etc/init.d.
Copy any impacted scripts to a temporary location
Edit the temporary location scripts and update the PATH or location of the binaries
Create a Work Order (WO) for the UNIX/Linux team at:
https://iis10t2prd.cloud.pge.com/myitservices/forms/intake/453
Follow up with an email to "IT I&O IO SNO Unix Linux Ops NEW" Distribution List (DL) if not completed prior to the next patch or planned reboot of the server(s) in question.

chunks.page_title: Move location of PingFederate Session Store
chunks.content:
--- Page: Move location of PingFederate Session Store ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=195037155
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

If you need to change where PingFederate stores the Session Store (used for Persistent Grants), you need to follow these steps.
Add new Data Store to PingFederate using Admin UI
Copy the System ID for the new Data Store from the PF Data Store page
Backup and copy the AccessGrantManagerLDAPOracleImpl.xml file
As pingfed user:
Archive
/opt/pingfederate/admin/current/pingfederate/server/default/data/config-store/org.sourceid.oauth20.token.AccessGrantManagerLDAPOracleImpl.xml
vi
/opt/pingfederate/admin/current/pingfederate/server/default/data/config-store/org.sourceid.oauth20.token.AccessGrantManagerLDAPOracleImpl.xml
Change these two entries:
<c:item name="PingFederateDSJNDIName">LDAP-E956C06CFB589519E9E89E2958778EE55A8B0491</c:item>
^ Enter the new Data Store System ID
<c:item name="SearchBase">ou=AccessGrantsDev,dc=utility,dc=pge,dc=com</c:item>
^ LDAP Search Base
Restart PingFederate Admin instance
Replicate using Cluster Management in Admin UI
Restart PingFederate Engines

Force an attribute CaSe
Example:  Teamsite
Pge.com
(PGEDev Tenant)
Settings under Manage Claim:
Source:  Transformation

Parameter 1:  [Attribute to be edited]
Note:  In this example, we are also extracting the Mail Prefix, to send only the user's Corp ID, not full email addresss.
PGEDev Tenant - samaccountname attribute

Source:  Transformation

Parameter 1:  user.userprincipalname
Send hard-coded text in a claim

Source:  Attribute
Source Attribute:  Enter the desired text in the dropdown where you usually choose the Attribute Name
Translate AD Group Names to other text (e.g. role names)
Examples:  MyPreferences PossibleNow (PGEDev Tenant), Apptio (PROD Tenant)

Claims Conditions
Choose appropriate Source Type (Microsoft Entra Guests for PGEDEV and Members for PROD)

Choose Source - Attribute
Enter desired new value for Value
Allow Connections from AWS to Bypass Device Compliance (possibly TEMP solution):
Examples:
PGE GIS Portal Arcgis.com* and Geomartcloud Enterprise*

Attribute Set:  ApplicationsAndServices

Value:  Allow

Attribute Assignment Administrator.

--- Page: Certificates and PKI ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=189563590
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

As of March 2023, we are going to assist with approving SSL certificate requests outside of business hours.
Instructions
To administer the PKI Certificates, you will need access to KeyFactor Portal
https://pgecerts.cloud.pge.com/Keyfactorportal/CertificateRequests
Job aid:
PKI Certificates - iPRiME Wiki - PGE Wiki
Login to the KeyFactor Portal
Open an Incognito session in Chrome
Login with your Admin Credentials
Click on Workflow tab the drop-down menu and select Certificate Requests
Select the certificate and click details to review the metadata
Make sure the spelling is accurate, PG&E needs to be spelled out like (Pacific Gas and Electric Company)- Needs to be spelled out as in parenthesis
-Always should be San Francisco or Oakland location
-State should be California
-Country should be US
-Organization unit (OU) should list the environment (Development, Production, Test, or QA) make sure they spell each thing out as listed in parenthesis
Common name needs to be listed within three fields: Common Name, distinguished name field, and in the subject alternative names.
Subject Alt Name: one person listed, make sure they are not a contractor, look in JAT, (JAT will show Account expire date for a contractor).
-Common Name: FQDN of the server
The order could be different but as long as all of the values are included.
After you review approve or deny the request. Supply a comment if appropriate. The system will notify the user of the approval or rejection.

--- Page: Patching Validation ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=195035471
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

How to validate Ping and ODSEE/OUD applications after patching.
PingFederate
Login to PingFederate Admin console and confirm that all patched servers are showing up under System → Cluster Management.  In PROD, there should be a total of 10 objects listed, if all servers are up and running.
Here is an example of all PingFederate PROD servers up and running:
Also, use Splunk or go to /logs/pf_engine on all patched PF Engines and check that all instances are running and there are no unusual errors.
Here are two errors to check for.  If you see them, see if one PingFederate engine IP address shows up more often than the others in the first error, and restart PingFederate on that server.
index=ping "Unable to retrieve artifact from"
index=ping "Unexpected problem looking up authorization code"
PingFederate PROD servers
pritappgenlc023
pritappgenlc024
pritappgenlc025
pritappgenlc026
pritappgenlc027
pritappgenlc028
pritappgenlc029
pritappgenlc030
OUD Session Store
PingFederate relies on OUD for a Sesson Store.  At this time (April 2023), OUD runs on the PingFederate Admin instances (as part of Application Health Initiative, OUD will be moved to its own servers).  Validate that OUD is running with:
ps -ef | grep oud
The above should return a single process that looks like this:
pingfed  44183     1  0 Mar31 ?        00:35:15 /usr/lib/jvm/jdk1.8.0_112/jre/bin/java -Xms2056m -Xmx2056m -d64 -XX:+UseCompressedOops -server -Xmn512m -XX:MaxTenuringThreshold=1 -XX:+UseConcMarkSweepGC -XX:CMSInitiatingOccupancyFraction=55 -Dorg.opends.server.scriptName=start-ds org.opends.server.core.DirectoryServer --configClass org.opends.server.extensions.ConfigFileHandler --configFile /app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/config/config.ldif
Also check the OUD logs at /app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/logs/errors for any unusual errors.
If OUD didn't start or needs to be restarted, it can be stopped and started with these commands (run as pingfed user):
/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin/stop-ds
/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin/start-ds
OUD PROD Servers
pritappgenlc023 (primary)
pritappgenlc027 (secondary)
PingAccess
Login to PingAccess Admin console and confirm that all patched servers are showing up under System → Clustering.  In PROD, there should be a total of 6 Engines listed, if all servers are up and running.
Here is an example of all PingAccess PROD servers up and running:
Also, use Splunk or go to /logs/pa_engine on all patched PA Engines and check that all instances are running and there are no unusual errors.
PingAccess PROD servers
PRITAPPGENLC043 (Primary Admin)
PRITAPPGENLC044
PRITAPPGENLC046
PRITAPPGENLC047
PRITAPPGENLC048 (Secondary Admin)
PRITAPPGENLC051
PRITAPPGENLC052
PRITAPPGENLC053
ODSEE
ODSEE is used to store external user authentication data.  It must be up and running for external-facing applications using PingFederate to authenticate.
Validate that ODSEE is running with:
ps -ef | grep slapd
The above should return a single process that looks like this:
slapd     2640     1  0 Feb10 ?        03:51:49 /app/dsee7/lib/64/ns-slapd -D /app/dsee7/slapd-user -i /app/dsee7/slapd-user/logs/pid
Also check for unusual errors in /logs/slapd/user/errors.  You can also use Apache Directory Studio to login to auth.comp.pge.com and run an LDAP query.
ODSEE PROD Servers
itauthprd01 (Primary)
itauthprd02
itauthprd11
itauthprd12

Azure App Service is a platform that lets you run web applications, mobile back ends, and RESTful APIs without worrying about managing the underlying infrastructure. Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) is a managed container orchestration service based on Kubernetes. It simplifies deploying, managing, and scaling containerized applications using Kubernetes. AKS provides integrated CI/CD, monitoring, and security features, and supports seamless integration with Azure services such as Microsoft Entra ID, Azure Key Vault, and Azure Monitor.

Control Definition
In Progress
Control Development
In Progress
Azure Key Vault is a cloud service for securely storing and accessing secrets. A secret is anything that you want to tightly control access to, such as API keys, passwords, certificates, or cryptographic keys. Key Vault service supports two types of containers: vaults and managed hardware security module (HSM) pools. Vaults support storing software and HSM-backed keys, secrets, and certificates. Managed HSM pools only support HSM-backed keys.

--- Page: Azure SQL Database ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=342299132
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Azure SQL Database is always running on the latest stable version of the SQL Server database engine and patched OS with
99.99% availability
. PaaS capabilities built into Azure SQL Database enable you to focus on the domain-specific database administration and optimization activities that are critical for your business. With Azure SQL Database, you can create a highly available and high-performance data storage layer for the applications and solutions in Azure.
SQL Databse Policies:
Control Type
Category
Severity
Controls
Description
Compliance Mapping
Wiz Policy Enforced
Azure Policy Enforced
Access Control and Identity Mgmt
Security
HIGH
SQL Database vulnerability assessment should send email notifications to admins and subscription owners
This rule checks whether the SQL Database Vulnerability Assessment (VA) periodic recurring scans are being sent to admins and subscription owners. This rule fails if `DatabaseSecurityAlertPolicy.properties.state` is `enabled`, indicating that VA periodic scans are on, and `DatabaseSecurityAlertPolicy.properties.emailAccountAdmins` is `false`. SQL Vulnerability Assessment is a feature of Microsoft Azure Defender for SQL. It is an easy-to-configure service that can discover, track, and help you remediate potential database vulnerabilities. It is recommended to use it to proactively improve your database security and enable the `Also send email notifications to admins and subscription owners` setting.
Logging and Monitoring
Security
HIGH
SQL Database Threat Detection log retention should be set to 365 or greater
This rule checks whether the SQL database is enabled and has a retention period of 365 days or more for threat detection events. Azure SQL Database Threat Detection is a security intelligence feature that identifies potential threats to the database. This feature detects potential vulnerabilities, SQL injection attacks, and anomalous database access patterns. The retention period defines the number of days to retain threat detection events.
Encryption and Key Management
Security
HIGH
SQL Database Ledger should be enabled
This rule checks whether Ledger is enabled on Azure SQL databases. Enabling the Ledger on Azure SQL databases provides an additional layer of security by providing a tamper-evident ledger for tracking and auditing changes over time. It is recommended to enable this feature to enhance the immutability and auditability of the data within the database.
Encryption and Key Management
Security
HIGH
SQL Database transparent data encryption should be enabled
This rule checks whether transparent data encryption (TDE) is enabled on the SQL Database. This rule fails if the SQL Database is not a master database and if `TransparentDataEncryption.properties.state` is set to `Disabled`. Azure SQL Database transparent data encryption helps protect against malicious activity by performing real-time encryption and decryption of the database, associated backups, and transaction log files at rest without requiring changes to the application.
System Management Configuration
Security
HIGH
SQL Database should be zone redundant
This rule checks whether the Azure SQL Database is configured to be zone redundant. Zone redundancy enables high availability and protects your Azure SQL Database from datacenter-level failures by automatically replicating your data across multiple availability zones.
Logging and Monitoring
Security
HIGH
SQL Database should have Threat Detection enabled
This rule checks whether the SQL database has Threat Detection enabled. Azure SQL Database Threat Detection is a security intelligence feature that identifies potential threats to the database. This feature detects potential vulnerabilities, SQL injection attacks, and anomalous database access patterns.
Infrastructure Security
Security
HIGH
SQL Database should not use 'Basic' SKU
This rule checks if the SQL Database is using the Basic or Consumption SKU pricing tier. The Basic SKU is a legacy SKU and has feature limitations such as disabled Service Level Agreements (SLAs). It is recommended to upgrade the SKU tier to maintain a higher level of availability and performance.
Access Control and Identity Mgmt
Security
HIGH
SQL Database should be configured to send Threat Detection email alerts
This rule checks whether the threat detection policy of the SQL database is configured to send email alerts. This rule fails if 'emailAddresses' is empty. Azure SQL Database Threat Detection is a security intelligence feature that identifies potential threats to the database. Ensure the SQL database sends threat detection alerts to the relevant email addresses.

Azure Storage Accounts provide scalable, durable, and secure cloud storage for a variety of data objects including blobs, files, queues, tables, and disks. They support multiple redundancy options and integrate with Azure services for secure access, encryption, and monitoring. This service is critical for storing application data, logs, backups, and more.

Setup in AWS Account
Secrets manager
This is required for domain joining only. A secret with name cscoe-aad-win-appproxy-papm-pwd with key value <username>_papm_pwd where username is a variable in TF Vars and respective password.
Required for
registering the connector
. A secret with name adproxy-service-account-pwd with key <username-for-app-registration> where username is a variable in
TF Vars
and respective password.
Role
Terraform variable
instance_profile_name
will be set to
windows-ec2-golden-role,
which would have already been created with account setup. Make sure this role is present. You can also setup a role of your own by giving the policy arns in TF Vars
Terraform variable values in Parameter Store:
These are mostly set in every account but ensure these terraform variables have respective keys from AWS Parameter store
For ex.
vpc_id
terraform variable in
TF Vars
should carry a value of /vpc/id
subnet_id1
: like vpc_id above
golden_ami
: should be set to the custom AMI for App Proxy which holds the App Proxy connector software that can be registered.
Security Groups
additional_security_groups
: These security groups are created in every account, make sure these
security group ids (NOT security group names)
are passed in TF variable and are set in AWS account.
Verification
Once tfvars are setup in dev/prod branches, committing changes in those respective branches initiates a Terraform Cloud Pipeline (
dev
) with completed Terraform plan phase. Apply the changes by clicking the 'Apply' in TFC pipeline after verifying changes in plan phase.
Logon to Systems manager in EC2(by selecting the EC2 and clicking on 'Connect' on top right in AWS UI) that is just spun up and get a host name
Logon to App Proxy in Entra and verify the instance

--- Page: Enterprise (App Proxy) Application Setup ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=305070751
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

App Proxy Architecture Diagram
High level view
Implementation at PGE
CSCOE creates Enterprise App Proxy application in Entra, following information is required from the application team...
Internal URL that is used to access the application
(Optional) List of users that require access to the application (Not Recommended check C.)
(Recommended) Project Manager/Owner fills the
SSO questionnaire
and attaches it to a
Front door
request, this would place a work order for Abhishek Chauhan to set up SSO. Result will be an Entitlement created in MEA , respective AD group in Entra. Examples...
Entitlement
Group in Entra
First, application will be created with <app-name>-<tenant>.<domain>.  For example, if application name is 'workviz' and domain as -pge.msappproxy.net, it would be workviz-pge.msappproxy.net where PGE is the tenant.
Once the application is created, either individual users are added to this application or MEA mapped AD group is attached for access to users.
Application team will try to access the application. incase of any issues they can reach out to CSCOE. Some of the issues that other applications have faced are given below with solutions.
After successfully completing above steps, Step 2, 3 and 4 will be repeated with custom domain.
Issues
Sorry, but we are having trouble signing you in
. User doesn't have access, reach out to CSCOE for access or SSO is configured, user needs to request for respective entitlement in MEA
403 Error
. Ensure CIDR for app proxy connector is added to WAF firewall allow list. You can see the steps
here
.
400 Invalid_redirect_uri
: When customer lands on main page after authentication, any re-routings from that page are errored. Entra has no settings related to re-routing, corresponding application team needs to check their settings.
