chunks.page_title: Entra as Identity Provider for applications
chunks.content:
--- Page: Entra as Identity Provider for applications ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=279021283
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

for example if App Proxy Connector is running in a network which has access to application.
We create an Enterprise application in Entra that represents on-prem/cloud application, map this application with App Proxy connector and manager user assignment to this Enterprise application. Homepage URL given by Enterprise application can be used to authenticate the users and access the application.
Setting up Azure Shell
Login into portal.azure.com and sign-in to it. After successful sign-in, click on top right to open new Azure shell session.
Open the editor from Shell session where files can be managed.
You can clone the repo or simply copy this
file
into the editor.
Run 'connect-azuread' and 'connect-mggraph' commands and complete any verifications required.
Creating Enterprise Application or Service Principal
Prepare to execute the PowerShell script...
Example: .
/application-management.ps1 -application_name appToBeDeleted05 -external_domain pgedev.msappproxy.net -internal_url https://mediawiki2.nonprod.pge.com/ -connector_group_name ToBeDeletedGroup
<filename>.ps1
-application_name
- Name of the application
-external_domain
- Domain where we are creating this application for ex.
pgedev.msappproxy.net
-internal_url
- Actual application URL (should be access to App Proxy connector either public or in same network where App Proxy connector is )  ex.
https://mediawiki2.nonprod.pge.com/
-connector_group_name
- App Proxy Connector group name
Accept any installations and execute the PowerShell script.
Go to portal.azure.com. → Search for Enterprise Applications → All Applications → Search for the application you created.    Look for 'users and groups' on the left menu with in the application and click on 'Add New User'
Add User/s or Group/s who requires access to the application and click on save.
User accessing the application
User can go to
https://myapps.microsoft.com
and sign-in with his credentials (once user is added to application it may take up to 5 mins for app to be visible in myapps).
User can click on the app and continue signing-in . After successful signing-in, he will be redirected to the application

--- Page: Get Custom Attributes - Graph API ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=279020307
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Using Graph Explorer
Open Graph Explorer and sign-in to it.
overwrite the following URLs in any API and make sure you have selected 'GET'
To fetch all Custom Attributes -
https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/identity/userFlowAttributes?$filter=userFlowAttributeType
eq 'custom'
To fetch a specific attribute for an user, find the app-id of the application that is provided by MS for extensions (named as add-extensions-app) or simply make the call mentioned above to find the app-id and the attribute names.
https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/<upn
or object-id of user>?$select=mail,extension_<app-id-of-aad-extensions-app>_<attribute-name>
Make sure you grant the required permissions in 'Modify Permissions' tab

chunks.page_title: External Identity - Onboard external users via Self service
chunks.content:
--- Page: External Identity - Onboard external users via Self service ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=262374749
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Dynamically Assigning users to Group
We can assign external users who sign up using self-service sign-in flow into a group by selecting 'Dynamic User' option in a group. We can write the query based on built-in attribute or extension(custom) attribute
Password Reset
We can let the user perform self service password reset (
SSPR
) by setting this. Check for 'password reset' in the main search bar and go to properties.
Enable self-service sign-up for your tenant
Before you can add a self-service sign-up user flow to your applications, you need to enable the feature for your tenant.
Go to '
Microsoft Entra id → External Identities → External Collaboration Settings
'
Create an application that will be linked with user flow in later steps
To link this user flow to application, register an app first '
Microsoft Entra id → App Registrations → New Registration' .
Git it a name and select Register
With in the just created application, select 'Quickstart' and the select the Angular as shown below
Select on 'Make these chagnes for me' in Step 1. This adds redirect URI
http://localhost:4200
into the app which we left blank while registering.
Click on 'Download' in step2 which downloads the sample application which has to be run on the system where 'portal.azure.com' is open.
To perform steps in Step 4, make sure you have Node js then Go to Downloads , uninstall the package and CD to location where you can find package.json
To start the project click on npm start
Now, come back here after you go to  'Create the user flow for self service sign-up'  section and perform all the steps.
You can open the application in browser '
http://localhost:4200'
and click on 'Login
You will now get a login screen. Click on 'Create One' as shown below
Since we have provided with 'Sign in with one time pass code' as identity provider it sends code to the personal emailid given
You are then take to a confirmation page
It then asks for attributes we have chosen
Move through by accepting. It shows what we can do and asks for acceptance
Now, we can see the login is successful. We can logout if we wish
We can now verify that the user is added and 'creation type' is shown as 'Self service'
Create the user flow for self-service sign-up
A self-service sign-up user flow creates a sign-up experience for the application you're providing to external users. You can configure user flow settings to control how the user signs up for the application:
Account types used for sign-in, such as social accounts like Facebook, or Microsoft Entra accounts
Attributes to be collected from the user signing up, such as first name, postal code, or country/region of residency
The user can sign in to your application, via the web, mobile, desktop, or single-page application (SPA). The application initiates an authorization request to the user flow-provided endpoint. The user flow defines and controls the user's experience. When the user completes the sign-up user flow, Microsoft Entra ID generates a token and redirects the user back to your application. Upon completion of sign-up, an account is provisioned for the user in the directory. Multiple applications can use the same user flow.
Go to '
Microsoft Entra id → External Identities → User Flow' and click on 'New User Flow
'
You can provide a name of the user flow which is prefixed with 'B2X_1_' (Labelled 1) ,  select the identity provider (Labelled 2) and select the attributes(Labelled 3) that are shown to customer while signing up
Once after the user flow is created, you can edit customer attributes, page layout, identity providers etc by selecting that user flow.
Go to 'Applications' from the menu with in the user flow page , add the application as shown

--- Page: Get Public Facing PKI ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=323290677
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

You can also crosscheck the generated CSR using
3) Once the req file is created ensure the respective private key is generated in
Submit to Sectigo(Entrust earlier)
1) We follow the same steps either to generate a new cert or to renew. Renewing here is to generating new certs. There is an 'Auto Renewal' option in Sectigo,but it may not be effective really
From this
sharepoint
, select Sectigo portal on the left hand side menu
2) Enter your email and wait for an authentication link
3) Once a link from the email is opened, we see a screen like this where you can enter days and upload CSR from previous step in 'Generate CSR'
4) Enter primary contact information in Certificate requester
5) Approver is optional , we can provide secondary contact here with pge emailid.
6) Proceed with submission
Get PFX certs
Following are the steps for Windows server scenario
Download PKCS#7 from Sectigo or from the email that Sectigo has sent
Open
certlm.msc as administrator ,
go to personal → certificates → All tasks (right click and select this) → import and select the PKCS#7 file
Ensure you select the Automatic option , this will place  Trusted certificates, Intermediate Certs in those respective folders automatically
Double click on the certificate and check that you have private key. You can also go to certificate path and check the path

--- Page: Networking Checklist ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=310973127
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

To Successfully access your application ensure these checks are done
WAF Firewall
If you have any resource like Load Balancer, CloudFront, API Gateway or similar that is using WAF, ensure that this CIDR(192.80.244.0/24) is whitelisted. For example, below are the steps to verify whitelisting of a cloud fronted application...
CIDR should not be added manually but added via TF while setting up S3 web.
Work is in progress to get a CIDR that is specific to Application Proxy Connectors which will be replace the current CIDR (192.80.244.0/24) that has wider range
To view ACL, go to CloudFront page in AWS UI and select the respective application related entry form the list (if the firewall associated with the application is known, we can switch to step 5 directly)
Switch to 'Security' tab in CloudFront entry to identify the firewall associated with it
Click on that firewall, select 'Rules' tab and click on the rule that has 'Allow' as action
Expected CIDR needs to be part of this list, if not application team needs to get the CIDR added to this list using Terraform Automation
Application hosted on Private Network
App Proxy needs to have visibility to the application. For example if the intended application that needs to be proxied has a destination url '
https://engage-queries-dev.nonprod.pge.com'
then this URL should be visible to App Proxy.
Public Hosted application:
If the application is hosted pubilcly(url can he accessed publicly) then no additional setup is required.
Private Hosted application:
If the application is hosted privately, we need to connect the VPC that the application is hosted on with the VPC app proxy is on. Following are details of App Proxy VPC
Region:
West 2
VPC
:
vpc-096aba9a97a08eb5d
AWS Account
: 381492164201
Custom domain - DNS
If the application URL (ex workviz-dev.msappproxy.net) has a domain msappproxy.net which is a default one, no DNS check is required in that case
If there is any other custom domain like nonprod.pge.com given for the application like https://engage-dev.nonprod.pge.com, ensure that DNS entry is added
Public/Private DNS CName entry
: Select public/Private depending on what hosted zone is configured for your application
TF Code
: Sample
TF code
for achieving the same
Example
:  For nonprod.pge.com, Add a CName entry in AWS Account (
514712703977- PGE-LZCommonVPC-Dev) for Hosted Zone Name(
nonprod.pge.com). Below screenshot shows mapping of engage-dev.nonprod.pge.com to engage-dev-pge.msappproxy.net
For prod hosted zones check 686137062481 (PGE-LZSharedServices-Prod)
Gateway Timeout
For Entra Team
Once after a new application is created and FER is approved , if you get an error like this it could be Network issue. Ensure Firewall is implemented, test connectivity to application from all EC2 instances.
Cross check if the App Proxy group is assigned to correct Connector group in Entra App Registrations.

chunks.page_title: Local Setup: How to Add and Expose a Static WebSite on AWS EC2 for Remote Access through App Proxy in Microsoft Entra ID
chunks.content:
--- Page: Local Setup: How to Add and Expose a Static WebSite on AWS EC2 for Remote Access through App Proxy in Microsoft Entra ID ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=229312935
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Pre-requisites:
Get yourself an Entra ID Administrator identity, ie tahvadmin@lab.pge.com, you will fetch your password every 5 days from
Connectivity to/from Azure AD Domain, lab.pge.com, Domain controllers (10.94.0.36 & 10.94.0.37) from AWS Subnet(s)
nslookup 10.94.0.36; nslookup lab.pge.com
Deploy an EC2 with PGE's Golden Windows AMI using terraform.  See
https://github.com/pgetech/tf-module-test/tree/s7aw/ec2/pge_windows
Join that EC2 Windows Server with Entra Domain Services managed domain, lab.pge.com. See
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory-domain-services/join-windows-vm
Find or Create your Entra ID Application Proxy Connector Group.  See the Azure/Home/Application proxy console.
Configure an Must we do any configuration (PAPM) to permit RDP?
Part One(1) Installing and Registering the App Proxy Connector:
Sign onto PAPM to fetch/create your temporary Entra ID adminstrator's password.
Browse to the AWS EC2 Console and copy the Private IP address of the target EC2.
Install an RDP Client onto your workstation and Add PC; populate PC name: with IP address; push Add
Double-click the target PC connection and enter your admin credentials.
Launch IE and Install Microsoft Edge.
Add a tab and open the edge settings then set Edge as your default  Browser,
Using Edge, navigate to
https://portal.azure.com
and sign in using your administrator lanid @ domain.
Push Continue
Sign on with administrator's lanid only.
From the Home page, choose "App proxy",
Choose "Download connector service"
Push "Accept terms & Download", open the downloaded file
Open File Explorer, navigate to your Downloads folder, run the AADApplicationProxyConnectorInstaller.exe as administrator.
Pushing yes...
Push install to complete the installation wizard...
Enter your Administrator credentials to complete the registration process.
Now you should see the target host's connector added to your connector group.
And flip-flop, confirm that your target EC2's App Proxy is healthy.
Open the Windows Services Manager, services.msc.  Check to see if the status for the following two services is
Running
.
Microsoft Entra application proxy Connector
enables connectivity.
Microsoft Entra application proxy Connector Updater
is an automated update service.
If they are NOT running, right-click and try to start them. You are DONE installing and registering App Proxy Connector.
Part Two(2) Installing the windows web server, IIS:
Given PGE's Golden AMI is based on Windows Server 2019, Internet Information Service is NOT installed by default.  So, we have to install it.
Command-line installation?
Or Via the console?  Start Windows Server Manager; navigate to Manage/Add Roles and Features.
Choose Installation Type/Role-based...; push Next.
Your local, target server should be selected by default; press Next.
Check "Web Server (IIS)"
Push Add Feature.
Push Next(s)... accept the default "Role Services", next; Push Install.
Push Close.
This is what DONE looks like; confirm that your IIS is a healthy GREEN.
Optional, Part Three(3) Configuring a custom web-site:
Command line? in line 2, replace the types with parameter values.
Or console? Start Windows Server Manager; navigate to Manage/Add Roles and Features.
In the
Connections
pane, right-click the
Sites
node in the tree, and then click
Add Website
.
Fill out the form; I entered a name, path, port 8000 and accepted the defaults.
Need a homepage?  try this...
https://www.tutorialstonight.com/sample-html-code-for-homepage#google_vignette
In
Features View
of IIS Manager, double-click
Authentication
.
Choose to Edit "Anonymous Authentication"; select "Application pool identity"; push OK.
Confirm IIS sees your homepage, my file is named index.html.  In
Features View
of IIS Manager, double-click
Default Document
.
Test it with a browser.  Enter
http://locahost
to see the default windows site.
Part Four(4) Configuring DNS, HTTPS/TLS:
To be SAF2 compliant(encryption of data in-transit),  we configure IIS with HTTPS/TLS Certificate.
Contact Active Directory team and request your internal domain name, ie
pgec2site.lab.pge.com
, and IP address be added to AADDS.
Use this form,
SampleINF.txt
to Request a TLS/HTTPS certificate for your endpoint.
How to submit-retrieve a CSR - Windows computer - command line.txt
My example request text file, pgec2site.txt, looks like this...
[Version]
Signature="$Windows NT$"
[NewRequest]
Subject = "CN=
pgec2site.lab.pge.com
,OU=Development,O=Pacific Gas and Electric Company,L=San Francisco,ST=California,C=US" ; Remove to use an empty Subject name.
;Because SSL/TLS does not require a Subject name when a SAN extension is included, the certificate Subject name can be empty.
;If you are using another protocol, verify the certificate requirements.
;EncipherOnly = FALSE ; Only for Windows Server 2003 and Windows XP. Remove for all other client operating system versions.
Exportable = TRUE   ; TRUE = Private key is exportable
KeyLength = 2048     ; Valid key sizes: 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384
KeySpec = 1          ; Key Exchange ? Required for encryption
KeyUsage = 0xA0      ; Digital Signature, Key Encipherment
MachineKeySet = True
ProviderName = "Microsoft RSA SChannel Cryptographic Provider"
RequestType = PKCS10 ; or CMC.
[EnhancedKeyUsageExtension]
; If you are using an enterprise CA the EnhancedKeyUsageExtension section can be omitted
OID=1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.1 ; Server Authentication
OID=1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.2 ; Client Authentication
[Extensions]
; If your client operating system is Windows Server 2008, Windows Server 2008 R2, Windows Vista, or Windows 7
; SANs can be included in the Extensions section by using the following text format. Note 2.5.29.17 is the OID for a SAN extension.
2.5.29.17 = "{text}"
_continue_ = "dns=
pgec2site.lab.pge.com
&"
_continue_ = "email=tahv@
pge.com
&"
_continue_ = "email=CYBERSECURITYServicesCloudSecurityEmployees@
pge.com
&"
; Notes:
; a. remove DNS entries that are not needed or add entries if more names are needed
; b. the common name is required and should be the first dns= entry
; c. supply at least two PG&E email contacts, an Exchange distribution list is a good option.
; d. update the OU= entry above with: Development, QA, Production, Sandbox, Test
I open a terminal as an Admin in my EC2; issue this command to generate the certificate request file.
certreq -new pgec2site.txt pgec2site.req which looks like this.
-----BEGIN NEW CERTIFICATE REQUEST----- MIIFETCCA/kCAQAwgZsxCzAJBgNVBAYTAlVTMRMwEQYDVQQIDApDYWxpZm9ybmlh MRYwFAYDVQQHDA1TYW4gRnJhbmNpc2NvMSkwJwYDVQQKDCBQYWNpZmljIEdhcyBh bmQgRWxlY3RyaWMgQ29tcGFueTEUMBIGA1UECwwLRGV2ZWxvcG1lbnQxHjAcBgNV BAMMFXBnZWMyc2l0ZS5sYWIucGdlLmNvbTCCASIwDQYJKoZIhvcNAQEBBQADggEP ADCCAQoCggEBAOw2sliCLFIOB3ddpELAStsJeLjx8jwPTlcKnyFA+qPW79Kp1tMQ 8EYaA6Rc7iZIdTIQmSPxogKb5sTS289vFQAL5yTZGdKGCoVuNjR+4iQB4mCDrJFw j+ROZv+0DUYMICM6oAoVR4VF6N9Q+3GXnDOinCk7KzTqLP7FcQDV15ofUVUPmUTG mDz7sU5kk+VxLN4lPwqu9Oo9mpeSu4VtJj+w9fanyoDshf8D+AGNWUq5RdGWb8KP 1ihelnHjJBJ2bv8pux8cbMq/d4O1+2PB96yeblvsTa23UcyRxsojRDFoEoUvOv3s 7HKCOqB/sc5qF0g/3k60CAYxt0IBAyEsBX0CAwEAAaCCAi4wHAYKKwYBBAGCNw0C AzEOFgwxMC4wLjE3NzYzLjIwVgYJKwYBBAGCNxUUMUkwRwIBCQwbRUMyQU1BWi1G Q01HRlBHLmxhYi5wZ2UuY29tDBhFQzJBTUFaLUZDTUdGUEdcc3NtLXVzZXIMC2Nl cnRyZXEuZXhlMHIGCisGAQQBgjcNAgIxZDBiAgEBHloATQBpAGMAcgBvAHMAbwBm AHQAIABSAFMAQQAgAFMAQwBoAGEAbgBuAGUAbAAgAEMAcgB5AHAAdABvAGcAcgBh AHAAaABpAGMAIABQAHIAbwB2AGkAZABlAHIDAQAwggFABgkqhkiG9w0BCQ4xggEx MIIBLTAOBgNVHQ8BAf8EBAMCBaAwHQYDVR0lBBYwFAYIKwYBBQUHAwEGCCsGAQUF BwMCMGMGA1UdEQRcMFqCFXBnZWMyc2l0ZS5sYWIucGdlLmNvbYEMdGFodkBwZ2Uu Y29tgTNDWUJFUlNFQ1VSSVRZU2VydmljZXNDbG91ZFNlY3VyaXR5RW1wbG95ZWVz QHBnZS5jb20weAYJKoZIhvcNAQkPBGswaTAOBggqhkiG9w0DAgICAIAwDgYIKoZI hvcNAwQCAgCAMAsGCWCGSAFlAwQBKjALBglghkgBZQMEAS0wCwYJYIZIAWUDBAEC MAsGCWCGSAFlAwQBBTAHBgUrDgMCBzAKBggqhkiG9w0DBzAdBgNVHQ4EFgQUPTbl 1G6tY+fbjhm0nwpczyC34/QwDQYJKoZIhvcNAQEFBQADggEBAL+UaC7DjbGMmR87 0s7nxoxQq9otCyUC8sWDLwisPkLnnyvx6qDdZCwZeIfHZKWzw03+dQsdKzjmU6W1 5UBJjPbzzomAD5mAhE3Rx4jJEHLr+YqobyqXbT0IToT/Dh9B9040OHYXBElYcsa8 VE57sM84t+B0j7DojDr0Im8O9YuuZD9p5svPJV5Bm5rtH3o2zLNH8P3ZFom9o7pq CC9yTwtTCclMw2QSvlRrRBmX/aLUnvQOsAPmTHZMga7t/oxCkxUDsJ+QT2iHxYOx HgC13wVGxy/B5id8sCefuS/qCrmfU7wEAbiFzPVDb2AekpiL2NI4xVBQtqYFh5Pf hocSy9I=-----END NEW CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----
Given my EC2 has no connectivity to the on-premise certificate request endpoint, "
rcdesecapxx0007.lab.pge.com
\PGE Test Issuing CA 1", I navigate my desktop browser to
https://pgetestca.pge.com/certsrv/certrqxt.asp
and manually enter my request.
Wait a couple of days and check to see if your pending certificate request has been issued. Visit
https://pgetestca.pge.com/certsrv/certckpn.asp
Push the Download CA certificate and figure out a way to copy it to the IIS host.
Options include S3 bucket; copy/paste file to/from clipboard with RDP; share your workstation folder via RDP.
Install the TLS Certificate into IIS.  Open IIS Manager, navigate to Server Certificates.
Choose Complete Certificate Request
Specify the certificate file you downloaded in step 6. Use the Personal certificate store.
Enable the installed certificate by adding an HTTPS binding to your website. Navigate to website/bindings
Part Five(5) Exposing the web-site:
Now that we've prepared our environment and installed a connector, we're ready to add on-premises applications to Microsoft Entra ID.
Sign in to the
Microsoft Entra admin center
as at least a
Application Administrator
.
Browse to
Identity
>
Applications
>
Enterprise applications
.
Select

Threat-1
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Authenticate, Spoof User Identity, Spoof AWS SFTP Service Endpoint, Spoof Notification(s), Spoof Email Sender, Spoof GeoLocation, CRUD User
Threat agent or source
External threats
includes but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Goals
Reliable Identity
Trustworthy Authentication
Accurate Access Management
Exploit details
A spoofing attack is a situation in which a person or program successfully identifies as another by falsifying data, to gain an illegitimate advantage.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
SPOOFING
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches as threat responses
SFTP encrypts authentication
being transferred over the SSH data stream.
Require clients store private-key(s) in a secure vault
.  This option may protect the theft of private-key(s) should the 3rd party system be compromised.
Use IP blacklisting and whitelisting.
It may be necessary to deploy SFTP Server in private VPC to block access then manage a white-list of our approved 3rd parties ip addresses. This can be implemented with AWS Web Application Firewall (WAF) and/or VPC hosted endpoints and VPC SecurityGroups.
Issue certificates to external users, 3rd parties.
This option will ensure that anyone accessing your system has a PGE-security certificate to verify who they are. This can be implemented with MTLS protocol and AWS Certificate Manager service.
SFTP doesn’t stop the unauthorized transfer of data to third parties.
This can lead to non-compliant disclosures of data, which may break regulatory rules on confidentiality and privacy.
Threat-2
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Tamper with Data-In-Transit, Tamper with File(s)Data-at-Rest, Tamper with Credentials?, Tamper with Data-In-Use,  Tamper with Notifications, Tamper with Logs, Encrypt by DataClassification?  Crack Encryption(s) AES, KMS, TLS?
Threat agent or source
Those working inside an organization
may be a significant cause of elevated privileges.
External threats
includes but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Goals
Data Integrity
Notification Integrity
Protect by Data Classification = Restricted, Confidential, Internal, Public
Exploit details
Those working inside an organization
may be a significant cause of tampering.
External threats
includes but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
TAMPERING
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Unique Customer Managed Key(s) per Workflow Instance;  Managed by AWS KMS; used by AWS S3.
SFTP encrypts everything being transferred over the SSH data stream; from the authentication of the users to the actual files being transferred, if any part of the data is intercepted, it will be unreadable because of the encryption.
Configure SSH with AES-256 cipher-algorithms
SSH uses a hashing algorithm, usually SHA-2, to determine data integrity.
AWS Hardened SFTP Service
Use Digital Signatures (MD5) for Files & Notifications.
Threat-3
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Report on Authentication(s), Report on Workflow Transaction(s), Report by User-Identity, Transaction-Identity, Workflow-Instance, Sign File-Transfer, Sign Notification
Threat agent or source
Both legitimate users and criminal perpetrators.
Goals
The inability to deny responsibility for performing a specific act.
Assurance that the sender of information is provided with proof of delivery and the recipient is provided with proof of the sender’s identity, so neither can later deny having processed the information.
Protection against an individual falsely denying having performed a particular action. Provides the capability to determine whether a given individual took a particular action such as creating information, sending a message, approving information, and receiving a message.
Exploit details
Nonrepudiation is achieved through cryptography, like digital signatures,
and includes other services for authentication, auditing and logging. In online transactions, digital signatures ensure that a party cannot later deny sending information or deny the authenticity of its signature.
SFTP does not centralize audits , logs or documentation.
Most compliance frameworks require some documentation to demonstrate compliance. SFTP can include audit logs, but without a centralized SFTP server documenting access across multiple systems can make documentation hard and raise red flags for assessors. Likewise, documentation must also adhere to privacy laws, which becomes exponentially more difficult over multiple SFTP servers.
Include documentation and auditing.
Document things like compliance and file access. Monitor file access as well as document things like user consent and other requests.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
REPUDIATION
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Use digital signature(s) to support a determination of whether a message was actually signed by a given entity.
To mitigate the risk of people repudiating their own signatures, the standard approach is to involve a trusted third party.
Use CCOE's Centralized Logging: AWS CloudTrail & CloudWatch Logs
Each Workflow Instance has an identity; searchable in CloudTrail logs.
Each Transaction has an identity; searchable/traceable in CloudTrail logs.
Each File has identity; searchable/traceable in CloudTrail logs.
CloudTrail logs searchable by User identity.
Threat-4
Threat attributes
Use Case Name
Assign File PGE DataClassification, Configure Users, Folders and Roles per Workflow Instance
Threat agent or source
Those working inside an organization
are a significant cause of data breaches.
External threats
includes but are not limited to hackers, cybercriminal organizations and state-sponsored actors.
Goals
Trust & Privacy of Data, ie Personally Identifiable Information
Data Loss Prevention
Enforcement of Data Classification Policies
Reasonable Storage Costs
Exploit details
This may also be known as
data privacy
or
data breach
.  Data privacy is challenging since it attempts to use data while protecting an individual's privacy preferences and personally identifiable information.  A data breach is a security violation, in which sensitive, protected or confidential data is copied, transmitted, viewed, stolen or used by an individual unauthorized to do so.
Threat Category (STRIDE)
INFORMATION DISCLOSURE
Potential threat countermeasures or architecture approaches
Use folder security for internal access.
Access controls can be a pain to set up because somebody has to do it manually on individual folders. Business users typically don’t have the skills or permission to do this, so organizations often resort to these users writing help desk tickets for IT to undertake access management tasks.
Implement trust boundaries,
aka multi-tenant isolation, using AWS S3 Bucket Policy + S3-Labels(aka Folders) + KMS Key Policy.
Practice the principle of least privilege
when configuring AWS DataTransferUser + ssh-key + IAM RBAC.
Require clients store private-key(s) and signatures in a secure vault
.  This option may protect the theft of private-key(s) should the 3rd party system be compromised.
Use CCOEs

Use PGE

Business defined data storage policies; use S3 Storage Class Analysis.

Criminal perpetrators of DoS attacks often target sites or services hosted on high-profile web servers such as banks or credit card payment gateways. Revenge, blackmail and hacktivism can motivate these attacks.

crash or flooding of services.

A
denial-of-service attack (DoS attack)
is a cyber-attack in which the perpetrator seeks to make a machine or network resource unavailable to its intended users by temporarily or indefinitely disrupting services of a host connected to a network. Denial of service is typically accomplished by flooding the targeted machine or resource with superfluous requests in an attempt to overload systems and prevent some or all legitimate requests from being fulfilled. In a
distributed denial-of-service attack (DDoS attack)
, the incoming traffic flooding the victim originates from many different sources.

DENIAL OF SERVICE

Protect the AWS Application Load Balancers and SFTP servers behind a firewall

Maximize application availability and responsiveness with managed DDoS protection.  Configure AWS Shield.
Use AWS Shield to configure static thresholds to protect services.
To integrate logging with PGE's SIOC so they
can detect and mitigate attacks,  use CCOE's Centralized Logging infrastructure.

--- Page: Deploy SPA for PingOne (Implicit) Demo ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=50856963
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Implicit Flow
The Open ID Connect Implicit flow is recommended for native apps and websites that have no dynamic web server (such as a single-page-application deployed via S3/Cloudfront). In such scenarios, everything that is passed between the app or site and the IdP can be viewed using browser development tools.
In the Implicit flow, a public/private key (JSON Web Key or JWK) scheme is used to encrypt or sign user details. When you register your client app with the IdP (PingOne), you will receive a client ID and you will register valid redirect uri's with the PingOne authorization server. In an Implicit flow, registration of the redirect_uri is necessary to ensure the users will not be tricked into visiting a malicious client site. The authorization server will never redirect the user to an invalid redirect_uri.
In Step 1, the user attempts to start a session with your client app and is redirected to the OpenID Provider (PingOne), passing in the client ID, which is unique for that application.
In Step 2, the OpenID Provider authenticates and authorizes the user for a particular application instance.
In Step 3, user details are encoded by the OpenID Provider into an id_token (JWT) that contains user information (scopes and claims, in OAuth/OIDC terms) and signature (using RS256), which is passed to a preconfigured redirect_uri that exists in the application (callback)
In Step 4, the client app confirms the JWT id_token and confirms the signature using the public key. If everything is fine, a session is established for the user.
In the Implicit flow, the transaction is secure despite the fact that the client app cannot be authenticated as it exists only in the browser or mobile device. Authentication can be trusted because the IdP sends tokens encrypted using a public/private key scheme and will only send tokens to the preconfigured Redirect URI. In other words, someone could steal the public key and client id, but that doesn’t matter, because only the IdP has the proper information (the redirect URI for the intended client app and the private key) to use the public key and client ID correctly.
PingOne configuration
Create a OIDC client called React-App1-Implicit
Use Grant type:
Implicit
In the Authentication Flow tab, provide the following URL values
Start SSO URL:
https://d2z8b22s4ylwto.cloudfront.net/login
Redirect (Callback) URI:
https://d2z8b22s4ylwto.cloudfront.net/authentication/callback
Add the following attributes to be returned by the User Info endpoint
Choose the below allowed Scopes for this client
Map the attributes for the User Info endpoint

Source code can be found at
https://github.com/sunshiva-pge/pingone-oidc-spa.git
Demo URLs

--- Page: PingFederate Improvements for L&M ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51055208
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Summary
Several changes are planned in an effort to improve the availability of PG&E's PingFederate SSO service.  Although the existing architecture satisfies the requirements for PG&E Tier 1 applications (<4 Hour RTO), there have been several incidents in the past year which have impacted the ability for users to authenticate using PingFederate.
Our proposal for changes in the near-term (Q1 2019) include the following:
Simplify the traffic path to the PingFederate servers, removing components which have historically caused downtime
Expand monitoring to identify a wider range of potential problems
Improve observability by integrating with Splunk and AppDynamics
In the long term, we are planning to move infrastructure to AWS.  Additional monitoring will provide us with service availability metrics so that we can measure our progress.
Simplify the traffic path
Prior to February 2019, a complex DNS and load balancing configuration was in place in order to support MobileIron Device Posture checking.  This component has been moved to a separate load balancer VIP.
Expand monitoring
We will implement a synthetic transaction health check.  This check will simulate a user logging in every minute, both from the Internet and from within the PG&E network, and against each of the 8 PingFederate servers.  This health check will help quickly detect when one server appears healthy, but is experiencing an issue.
The health check will be run in AWS and monitored with CloudWatch sending alerts directly to ENOC.  CloudWatch will provide historical service availability metrics.
Improve observability
By the end of February, logs will be sent to Splunk and the PingFederate App and Dashboards will be installed.  Approved users will be able to view detailed metrics and logs for PingFederate.  For example, number of successful and failed logins with error codes.
AppDynamics (pending approval) will provide even more visibility into the health of PingFederate, including mapping network connections between PingFederate and its upstream and downstream dependencies.
Architecture Diagram

--- Page: Deploy Webapp for PingOne (Auth Code) Demo ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=50856965
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Authorization Code Flow
The Authorization Code flow is an option for apps that have web-server logic that enables back-end communication with the IdP (PingOne). It functions like a traditional three-legged OAuth2 and results in an OAuth access token being returned to the web application via hidden, encrypted calls made entirely on the back end. In this flow, the end-user authorizes the client, and the IdP sends a special, one-time-use code that can be exchanged by the back-end server for an OAuth access token. This exchange needs to include the client id and client secret in addition to the code, just like a traditional OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Grant. It is more secure than the Implicit flow, because tokens are not visible through the browser and the client app can also be authenticated.
In Step 1, the user attempts to start a session with your client app and is redirected to the OpenID Provider (OneLogin), passing in the client ID, which is unique for that application.
In Step 2, the OpenID Provider authenticates the user, and the user authorizes permissions for the client application to access the user-authorized resources.
In Step 3, a one-time-use code is passed back to the web server using a registered redirect_uri.
In Step 4, the web server passes the code, client ID, and client secret to the OpenID Provider’s token endpoint, and the OpenID Provider validates the code and returns a one-hour access token.
In Step 5, the web server uses the access token to get further details about the user (if necessary) and establishes a session for the user.
PingOne configuration
Create a OIDC client called Nodejs-App1-Code
Use Grant type:
Authorization Code
In the Authentication Flow, provide the following urls
Start SSO URL :
https://pingone-oidc-dev.7ypz2h2yyg.us-west-1.elasticbeanstalk.com/auth
Redirect (Callback) URI:
https://pingone-oidc-dev.7ypz2h2yyg.us-west-1.elasticbeanstalk.com/auth/callback
Add the following attributes to be returned by the User Info endpoint
Choose the below allowed Scopes for this client

chunks.page_title: Register a Relying Party Application in PingOne
chunks.content:
--- Page: Register a Relying Party Application in PingOne ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=51056301
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Log in to the PingOne admin console
https://admin.pingone.com
.Your login credentials will be unique to PingOne, using your PGE lanid email as the username (e.g. abcd@pge.com).
Click the "Applications" tab, followed by the "OIDC" tab, followed by the "Add Application" button
Add details for the application name, description, business purpose, etc. Step 2 involves selection of "Authorization Code" or "Implicit" for the OAuth2 grant type. If you are unsure of your needs in this regard, please refer to the
Ping OAuth2 Developer Guide
. Step 3 involves registration of the "Start SSO URL" and the "Redirect URIs" - the Start SSO URL can be thought of as your login page. If your application has a /login endpoint which will trigger the use of the angular-oauth2-oidc login process, place that full URL here. Otherwise, you could use "
https://localhost
:{portnumber}" for now. The redirect URIs are very important to register correctly. This is the page to which users will be redirected after successful authentication. If you do not have a /callback endpoint established, then register the home page of your application (i.e.
https://localhost
:{portnumber}). When you deploy your application to other environments beyond your local dev server, you will need to register the redirect URI's of those environments in this PingOne configuration. Continue selecting the options that you would like for your application, but note that default settings will allow successful creation of the PingOne client.
When you have saved the configuration settings, the application will show up in your list of OIDC applications and you will be able to see configuration details. You will need to configure your application with some of these details, including the Client ID, Client Secret (if applicable), Issuer, and possibly other values.
So long as your callback endpoint is running and receiving traffic, you can test that your configuration works successfully by clicking the "Authentication Flow" tab and visiting the "Initiate Single-Sign On URL" - this will take you to the PGE login form hosted by Ping, and upon successful authentication you will be redirected to your newly registered application. Note that if you have an active session with Ping (i.e. if you've logged in to a federated application with your lanid and password) you may not notice the redirect, and you will just see the rendering of your application's callback endpoint.

Troubleshooting
Some users see a 'User does not exist' error after logging into Ariba via SSO
This is a known issue on Ariba. Ariba accounts are created with uppercase usernames and if the SAML token contains lowercase characters, then a matching user is not found during SSO. In order to fix this, the attribute contract in the PingFederate/PingOne bridge was changed to pass all user IDs as uppercase

--- Page: SSO for Sumo Logic ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=50875954
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Link to initiate SSO to Sumo Logic for PGE users with the SMARTworksDevelopers AD Role:
https://sso.connect.pingidentity.com/sso/sp/initsso?saasid=c95a0423-a5f2-4aa6-87a6-d359edffcda0&idpid=a6405f3b-818d-4474-b224-f91beda1fca9
This page is a step-by-step guide for setup of Sumo Logic with PingOne to enable SSO to other applications in the PGE ecosystem which are registered with PingOne (i.e. GitHub, Slack, AWS, etc).
Topics covered:
Steps for SSO Integration, Role-based Access Provisioning, SP-Initiated Login, and Provisioning New Users in Sumo Logic
Steps for Simple SSO Integration with Sumo Logic
Troubleshooting
Note: Only One of the SSO Integration Types can be set up for a given Service Provider (specifically, per unique Entity ID). If you choose one procedure below for setup, you will need to delete it entirely in order to proceed with the other setup option.
Steps for SSO Integration, Role-based Access Provisioning, SP-Initiated Login, Provisioning New Users in Sumo Logic
In order to set up Sumo Logic with the ability to provision new users and to have users automatically assume specific roles in SumoLogic upon SSO login, you must set up Sumo Logic as a SAML integration
without using PingOne's pre-configured settings for Sumo Logic
. If you don't need the ability to provision new users and to have users automatically assume specific roles in Sumo Logic, you can skip to the easier setup procedure
below, linked here.
Log in to the
PingOne admin console
with your PGE email (not LanID) and password. You must be a PingOne admin in order to set up a new PingOne SAML integration with Sumo Logic.
Navigate to the "My Applications" tab, and the "SAML" sub-tab. Click the button for "Add Application" and click "New SAML Application"
Give the application a name, description, category, and icon graphic if desired, then click "Continue to Next Step"
You should be on page with the headline "2. Application Configuration" (see image below)
Download the PingOne Account Origination Certificate. Now head to Sumo Logic and configure a new SAML integration from the Sumo Logic end. The
official Sumo Logic documentation for SAML integrations can be found here.
After you have added the new IdP SAML integration in Sumo Logic, select the new configuration from the Sumo Logic Configurations list. You will see a url called the "Assertion Consumer" - this will be entered in the PingOne configuration. Back in the PingOne admin console, you should still be on Step 1 - SSO Instructions. Click "Continue to Next Step" - this should bring you to Step 2 - Configure Your Connection. Paste the "Assertion Consumer" url received from Sumo Logic in the "ACS" field. For the "Entity ID" field, use
https://service.us2.sumologic.com/ui/
. Click "Continue to Next Step"
You should be on step "3. SSO Attribute Mapping" in PingOne. Create four attribute maps according to the values in the screenshot below:
Application Attribute
Identity Bridge Attribute
SAML_SUBJECT
email
GIVEN_NAME
fname
LAST_NAME
lname
SUMO_ROLE
* requires "Advanced" mapping
* SUMO_ROLE is intended to send relevant Active Directory user roles to Sumo Logic so that users can be provisioned into accurate user groups at Sumo Logic. The current role being mapped is the "SMARTworksDevelopers" role. To extract this role from authorized users, click the "Advanced" button in the SUMO_ROLE attribute row. In the "IdP Attribute Name or Literal Value", use the "memberOf" attribute. In the drop-down Function selector, choose "ExtractByRegularExpression" and enter .*(SMARTworksDevelopers).* in the expression field. See the screenshot below for reference
Save the settings and click "Save and Publish" to complete the SSO Integration setup between Sumo Logic and PingOne. Once the configuration is saved, PingOne will provide a link to use for SSO to Sumo Logic. Click this link and use the LanId and password of an authorized SMARTworksDevelopers user to access Sumo Logic. If the SSO is unsuccessful, please see the
Troubleshooting
section below.
Steps for SSO Integration with Sumo Logic
Log in to the
PingOne admin console
with your PGE email and password. You must be a PingOne admin in order to set up a new PingOne connection with Sumo Logic.
Navigate to the "My Applications" tab, and the "SAML" sub-tab. Click the button for "Add Application" and click "Search Application Catalog"
Search for "Sumo Logic" and select the "Sumo Logic" search result, then click the "Setup" button
Download the PingOne Account Origination Certificate and make note of the issuer parameter shown on this screen. Provide these to your Sumo Logic admin for integration configuration on the Sumo Logic side of the integration.
Refer to the
Sumo Logic SAML Integration documentation
for reference about where to find the integration settings for using SAML with a new IdP. Complete the steps through Step 9. In Step 8, select "Use SAML Subject". Do not configure additional optional steps beyond Step 9.
After you have added the new IdP SAML integration in Sumo Logic, select the new configuration from the Sumo Logic Configurations list. You will see a url called the "Assertion Consumer" - this will be entered in the PingOne configuration. Back in the PingOne admin console, you should still be on Step 1 - SSO Instructions (see image in step 4). Click "Continue to Next Step" - this should bring you to Step 2 - Configure Your Connection. Paste the "Assertion Consumer" url received from Sumo Logic in the "ACS" field. Paste "
https://service.us2.sumologic.com/ui/
" in the "Entity ID" field. Click "Continue to Next Step"
In the Step 3 - Attribute Mapping section, use "email" in the field form input for "Identity Bridge Attribute or Literal Value. Click "Continue to Next Step"
Click "Save & Publish". The integration is complete. Now, copy the "Initiate SSO URL" and paste it in a new browser window
The new URL will either prompt you for a login at a Ping Federate - hosted HTML form (
https://itiampingdev....)
or it will initiate SSO if you already have a valid session open with a different PingOne-registered service provider
Troubleshooting
If you receive an error page response from Sumo Logic that looks like the one below, it means that your user is not registered as a Sumo Logic user. Users must be registered in Sumo Logic with their PGE email, as this is the attribute that is mapped from PingOne to Sumo Logic for user login. In the case of the error below, the user
csco@pge.com
successfully logged in via the Ping Federate login screen to initiate SSO, but that email was not associated with a user in Sumo Logic.

Transit.Next PAN Firewall Sizing Considerations
The Transit.Next design pattern Introduces new AWS services including Transit Gateway and Gateway Load Balancer, greatly simplifying and streamlining the architecture and traffic flows.  It also collapses the three discrete "pairs" of PAN firewalls currently deployed in TransitV2 (PROD, CORE, and INT) into a single, combined, load-balanced fleet spread across 3 Availability Zones.
TransitV2 Resources And Utilization
TransitV2 is comprised of the following PAN Firewall Instances (NPS to confirm / validate function, sizing, historic performance metrics).  Note that while there is a 'pair' for each of these functions, only a single firewall is active and passing traffic at any given time.
CORE:  These firewalls terminate the VPN tunnels from On-Prem (Remote Access Firewalls) and then route to the PROD firewalls

VM-500
EC2 Instance Size:
m4.2xlarge
EC2 vCPU:
8
EC2 Memory:
32GB

(1Gb/s)

818Mb/s
Peak CPU Utilization, past 7 days:
44%
(unique spike: next highest peak is 24%)
PROD:  These firewalls connect to CORE, and provide the dual 1.2Gb/s VPN connections to 120+ Workload accounts

, and provide Internet Ingress/Egress (in combination with f5 ADCs) to Workload accounts

--- Page: AWS IAM Identity Center - Troubleshooting ---

This is mainly for CCOE and Cyber as most of the steps involve looking in Identity Center
Terraform Plan Errors
Invalid Index - The given key does not identify an element in this collection value
Solution
Ensure the account assignment permission set is spelled correctly. Notice that the permission set in the account assignment is missing an "s" after Operation.
Error: reading IdentityStore Group - ResourceNotFoundException: GROUP not found
Solution
Ensure the MEA Group is spelled correctly and the case matches. If it does, check Identity Center if the group is in there.
Log into the Cyber Prod account,
553067951317
Go to Identity Center → Groups
Search for the MEA Group
You will need to type in the full name or at least the beginning of it
Ensure the MEA Group shows up in Identity Center
If the groups do not show up, check back the next day
It takes about 1 day to show up
You will need to close your original pull request and create a new one to trigger another Terraform plan
Unable to see Ops role in Access Portal
Check that the person has the MEA Group
Go to
https://sailpointui.utility.pge.com/
Go to Manage Access → Manage User Access
Search for the user and select it
Click on Remove Access
I'm sure there's better ways to check if someone is part of an MEA Group, but this is the way I do it
Verify the person has the AWS MEA Group
If the MEA Group does not show up, make sure they received an email that their MEA request was approved
It could take a few hours for the MEA Group to show up after approval
If the MEA Group is there, follow
Check in Identity Center that the person is a user in the associated MEA Group
Check in Identity Center that the person is a user in the associated MEA Group
Log into the Cyber Prod account,
553067951317
Go to Identity Center → Groups
Search for the MEA Group
You will need to type in the full name or at least the beginning of it
Click on the Group Name
Check that the user is listed
If the user is not listed, you may need to wait up to 24 hours from the time your MEA Request was approved for it to show in Identity Center
If it's been more than 24 hours, please contact Cyber. See
You most likely do not have the MEA Group
Apr-A2102-All-AWS-IDC-SCIM-Users
Check in Identity Center that the Account has the MEA Group listed under Users and Groups
This sometimes occurs if the MEA Group isn't fully provisioned before creating the group in Identity Center
Log into the Cyber Prod account,
553067951317
Go to Identity Center → Multi-account permissions
Search for the account
Click on the Account Name
Verify the MEA Group shows under Users and groups
If there's a Group not found uner the Username / group name, re-run the Terraform workspace

--- Page: Alert Response by Type ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364978956
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Quick reference for SIOC M&R — what to do for each major category of Wiz alert.
Credential Misuse
What it looks like:
"EC2 Instance Credentials Used Outside Of AWS"
"Lambda Function Credentials Used Outside Of AWS"
"ECS Task Credentials Used Outside Of AWS"
AttackSequence:IAM/CompromisedCredentials
Why it matters:
Cloud credentials should only be used from within the cloud environment they belong to. If EC2 instance creds show up in an API call from an external IP, someone likely stole them.
What to do:
Check the principal and source IP in the alert
Is this a known CI/CD system or approved external tool? → Contact owner to confirm
If not →
Escalate to IR immediately.
This is likely credential theft.
Root / Privileged Identity Activity
What it looks like:
"Failed AWS console login by root user"
"Password recovery requested by root user"
"Root Activity From Unusual Country"
"Root Federation Token Generation"
Why it matters:
Root is the god-mode account. It should almost never be used. Any root activity is suspicious by default.
What to do:
Was this pre-approved? Check for change records or prior notification.
Contact the account owner immediately.
If no one claims it →
Escalate to IR.
If it's from an unusual country →
Escalate to IR.
Malicious IP / Tor Activity
What it looks like:
"AWS API Call Was Made From A Very Malicious IP Address"
"Access from a Tor exit node"
"SSH Communication With Malicious IP"
Any GuardDuty alert with "TorIPCaller" or "MaliciousIPCaller"
Why it matters:
Wiz cross-references source IPs against threat intelligence feeds. Hits on known-bad IPs are high confidence.
What to do:
Check the alert for the source IP and the action taken
Was this a successful action or just an attempt?
If successful →
Escalate to IR.
If attempt only → Document, monitor for follow-up activity
Public Exposure
What it looks like:
"S3 object configured as publicly exposed via ACL"
"RDS DB snapshot configured as publicly exposed"
"EBS snapshot was configured to be public"
"OpenSearch domain endpoint configured as publicly exposed"
"Storage Account Made Publicly Accessible"
Why it matters:
Making a cloud resource public that contains data is an immediate data exposure risk.
What to do:
Check: does this resource contain sensitive data? (Wiz often tells you in the description)
Contact the owner — was this intentional?
If it contains sensitive data and wasn't approved →
Escalate to IR
If it doesn't contain sensitive data but still wasn't approved → Close with a note, recommend the owner fix it
Crypto Mining
What it looks like:
"CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B"
"CryptoCurrency:Runtime/BitcoinTool.B"
"Impact:Runtime/CryptoMinerExecuted"
"Digital currency mining related behavior detected"
"CloudFormation Stack created for aws-ethereum-miner"
Why it matters:
Crypto mining in your cloud = someone compromised your environment and is spending your money.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
This is always malicious.
The underlying resource is compromised and needs to be isolated.
Known Threat Actors
What it looks like:
"Call to CreateUser API with parameters associated with threat actor JavaGhost"
"Call to CreateUser API with parameters associated with threat actor EMERALDWHALE"
"Call to CreateUser API with parameters associated with threat actor Legion"
Any alert mentioning a named threat actor
Why it matters:
Wiz's threat intelligence team tracks specific attack campaigns. If your environment matches their signatures, it's not a guess — it's a confirmed pattern.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
Named threat actor matches are high confidence.
Include the threat actor name in your escalation — IR may have additional context.
Privilege Escalation
What it looks like:
"Unusual Attempt By Principal To Edit Their IAM Policy"
"IAM Policy Attachment Bruteforce Attempt"
"PrivilegeEscalation:Runtime/CGroupsReleaseAgentModified"
"PrivilegeEscalation:Runtime/RuncContainerEscape"
"Microsoft Entra ID (AAD) principal escalated itself to Global Administrator"
Why it matters:
An identity trying to give itself more permissions is a classic attack pattern.
What to do:
Check who/what is doing the escalation
Is this an admin performing approved work? → Contact to confirm
If it's a service account or unknown identity →
Escalate to IR
Container escapes (CGroups, Runc) →
Always escalate
Data Exfiltration / Destruction
What it looks like:
"Exfiltration:S3/AnomalousBehavior"
"Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Delete"
"Unusual DB Export Task To External Bucket Started"
"Multiple Storage Accounts With Sensitive Data Deleted Within A Short Timeframe"
Why it matters:
Data leaving the environment or being destroyed at scale is one of the worst outcomes.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
Check scope — is it one bucket/account or multiple?
Do not wait for owner confirmation if the behavior is clearly anomalous.
Kubernetes / Container Threats
What it looks like:
"Pentesting Pod Created in Cluster"
"Command Executed in kube-system Pod"
"Anonymous workload was created"
"Known Offensive Tool Namespace Used"
Anything with "EKS", "AKS", or "Kubernetes" in the name
Why it matters:
Kubernetes clusters run production workloads. Suspicious activity in a cluster can mean an attacker has a foothold.
What to do:
Check: which cluster? Prod or Dev?
Pentesting tools, offensive tool namespaces, or kube-system access →
Escalate to IR
Anonymous access granted →
Escalate to IR
If unsure about Kubernetes context, loop in Cloud Security for help
Supply Chain / Wiz Threat Intelligence
What it looks like:
"Wiz-Identified Compromised Secret Usage"
"Shai-Hulud leaked secrets used in the last day"
"Publicly exposed AWS access keys found on public Github repository"
Wiz IR emergency email notifications
Why it matters:
These are Wiz's own threat intelligence team telling you something is actively compromised.
What to do:
Escalate to IR immediately.
This is not theoretical — it's active.
Include all details from the Wiz alert.
For Wiz email notifications: engage SIOC leads + Cloud Security + IR on-call simultaneously.
Azure Identity (Entra ID) Threats
What it looks like:
"Impossible travel: successful Microsoft Entra ID user login from multiple countries"
"Successful Signin Using a User Agent Associated With Credential Stuffing Attacks"
"Admin consent given to a risky third-party application"
"Microsoft Entra ID principal escalated itself to Global Administrator"
"User Authenticated To Malicious Application"
Why it matters:
Entra ID is the identity backbone for Azure. Compromised identities here can affect everything.
What to do:
Impossible travel / credential stuffing → Contact the user. Were they traveling? Did they log in from both locations?
Admin consent to risky apps / Global Admin escalation →
Escalate to IR
Malicious application authentication →
Escalate to IR

--- Page: Incident Response with Wiz ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364978958
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

When Does IR Get Involved
IR gets involved when SIOC M&R escalates a Wiz alert. The escalation criteria are simple:
Situation
Action
Activity is clearly malicious
Escalate immediately
Account/app owner cannot be reached
Escalate
Owner says "that wasn't us"
Escalate
Critical severity + can't quickly confirm benign
Escalate
Wiz IR team sends an emergency notification
Escalate immediately
When in doubt, escalate.
A false escalation costs a conversation. A missed incident costs a lot more.
What Wiz Gives IR
When IR receives an escalation, all of the evidence is already packaged in the Wiz alert. No one needs to go pull raw CloudTrail logs or stitch together a timeline manually.
Already Available in Every Alert
Evidence
Where in Wiz
What happened
Alert description + AI analysis
Who did it
Principal / identity section — IAM user, role, service account, IP address
What was affected
Resource details — account, region, resource type, ARN
When
Event timeline — full chronological list of cloud events with timestamps
Attack chain
Investigation graph — visual diagram of the full activity sequence
MITRE mapping
MITRE ATT&CK tactic and technique (e.g., Execution > Command and Scripting Interpreter)
Cloud account context
Subscription tags — AppID, Environment (Prod/Dev), LOB, owner
AI assessment
Blue Agent / Mika analysis — malicious vs. benign, confidence, narrative
Related events
All correlated cloud events across the timeline
How To Export
JSON Export
— Three-dot menu (top right) > Export as JSON. This is the full machine-readable record.
Print View
— Printer icon (top right) > generates a clean printable summary.
Direct Link
— Copy the alert URL and include it in the IR ticket. Anyone with Wiz access can see the full investigation.
IR Workflow for Wiz Alerts
Phase 1: Triage (SIOC M&R — already done before escalation)
Alert reviewed
Context gathered (account, owner, environment)
Intent checked with owner (or owner unreachable)
Evidence exported (JSON)
Decision to escalate documented in Wiz comment
Phase 2: Investigation (IR)
Using the Wiz alert as the starting point:
Review the investigation graph
— understand the full attack chain
Check the event timeline
— look for events before and after the triggering alert
Assess scope
— is this isolated to one account or is the same principal active elsewhere?
Check for related alerts
— search Wiz for the same principal, IP, or resource across other alerts
Determine blast radius
— what data/systems were accessible from the compromised identity?
Wiz can answer most of these questions without leaving the platform:
Threats
— filter by principal or account to see related alerts
Cloud Events
— full event log searchable by identity, action, resource
Security Graph
— visualize what the compromised identity has access to
Phase 3: Containment
Depending on the threat type:
Threat
Containment Action
Who
Compromised IAM credentials
Disable access keys, rotate credentials
App Owner (directed by IR)
Compromised EC2/Lambda credentials
Rotate instance role, investigate instance
App Owner + Cloud Security
Unauthorized root login
Reset root password, enable MFA, check for persistence
Cloud Security + App Owner
Public exposure (S3, RDS, etc.)
Remove public access immediately
App Owner (directed by IR)
Crypto miner / malware
Isolate instance, preserve forensics
IR + Cloud Security
Tor/malicious IP access
Block IP, investigate identity
IR + Cloud Security
Phase 4: Recovery & Documentation
Confirm threat is contained
Document the full timeline (Wiz JSON export + IR notes)
Identify root cause
Recommend preventive measures
Close the Wiz alert with resolution notes
Wiz Emergency Notifications
Wiz's own IR team occasionally sends
direct email notifications
when they detect an active threat campaign targeting our environment. This has happened before and was accurate.
Item
Details
What
Email from Wiz IR about an active threat targeting our tenant
Frequency
Rare — maybe once or twice a year
Accuracy
High — previous notifications were confirmed and actionable
Distribution List
(Cloud Security to provide)
Action
Treat as Critical. Immediately engage SIOC M&R, Cloud Security, and IR.
Action Required:
Cloud Security must provide the emergency email DL.
Wiz IR Readiness
Wiz tracks our
Incident Response Readiness
based on log source coverage. The more logs we feed Wiz, the better our ability to detect and investigate.
Where to find it:
Wiz >
Threat Center
>
Response Readiness
This readiness score accounts for:
Are cloud management logs flowing? (CloudTrail, Activity Logs, Audit Logs)
Are threat detection sources connected? (GuardDuty, Defender for Cloud)
Are identity logs ingested? (Entra ID, Okta)
Are network flow logs enabled? (VPC Flow Logs)
Are data access logs enabled? (S3 Data Events, Storage Account logs)
Is the Wiz Runtime Sensor deployed?
Is the EDR integration active? (CrowdStrike)
Gaps in coverage = blind spots in detection. This is a metric we should track and report.
Key Contacts
Role
Responsibility
Contact
SIOC M&R (on shift)
Alert triage, initial investigation, escalation
(standard SIOC contact)
Cloud Security
Wiz platform, rule configuration, cloud expertise
(TBD)
IR On-Call
Incident containment and response
(standard IR contact)
Wiz Emergency
Active campaign notifications
(email DL — Cloud Security to provide)

--- Page: Wiz Monitoring & Response Runbook ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364977556
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

For SIOC M&R Engineers
Audience
SIOC Monitoring & Response Engineers
Scope
All Wiz Critical and High severity threat alerts
Last Updated
February 26, 2026
Owner
Cloud Security Team
Contents:
What Is This
What You Monitor
What a Wiz Alert Looks Like
What To Do When You Get an Alert
How To Collect Evidence
When To Escalate
RACI
Common Questions
1. What Is This
Wiz is a cloud security platform used by major enterprises and government agencies worldwide. It monitors our AWS and Azure cloud environments and fires alerts when it detects threats — things like credential theft, privilege escalation, malicious IP activity, crypto mining, and more.
Wiz's threat detection rules are written by a
professional security research team
. When Wiz rates something as Critical or High, it means their researchers have determined it represents a real security risk. These are not guesses — they are backed by threat intelligence, MITRE ATT&CK mappings, and correlation across cloud events.
Your job:
When Wiz fires a Critical or High alert, you investigate it and decide whether to close it or escalate it.
That's it. Wiz does the hard part. You do the response part.
2. What SIOC Monitors — Exactly
Every Wiz alert rated Critical or High. All of them. No subset, no filtering, no exceptions.
This is not negotiable. Critical and High are the only two severity levels that reach SIOC. Wiz has five severity levels — SIOC owns two of them:
Wiz Severity
Who Owns It
What SIOC Does
Critical
SIOC
Investigate immediately. Active exploitation, exposed credentials, confirmed attack sequences.
High
SIOC
Investigate promptly. Malicious IP activity, privilege abuse, suspicious behavior, public exposure.
Medium
Cloud Security
Nothing. Not yours.
Low
Cloud Security
Nothing. Not yours.
Informational
Cloud Security
Nothing. Not yours.
There is no "Major" severity in Wiz. Only the five levels above.
What "investigate" means — specifically
For every Critical or High alert, you do three things:
Read the alert
in Wiz (description, AI analysis, investigation graph)
Decide
: close it (with documented reason) or escalate to IR
Document
your decision in the alert and your ticket
That's it. You are not building detections. You are not tuning rules. You are not analyzing raw cloud logs. Wiz already did the detection, correlation, and analysis. You are reading the result and making a triage decision.
How many alerts are we talking about?
Right now:
46 open threats
at Critical or High across our entire environment. This is not a flood.
Wiz has
367 detection rules
at Critical and High severity (232 AWS, 135 Azure). Not every rule fires every day — these are the rules that
can
fire. The actual alert volume is low.
The full rule list is in the
Threat Detection Rules Reference
.
What SIOC does NOT need to do
Not Your Job
Who Does It
Write or tune detection rules
Cloud Security / Wiz
Query CloudTrail or raw cloud logs
Wiz does this automatically
Decide which rules to enable
Cloud Security / Wiz
Monitor Medium, Low, or Informational alerts
Cloud Security
Perform containment actions
Incident Response
Fix the underlying issue
App / Account Owner
3. What a Wiz Alert Looks Like
Wiz gives you
everything you need
right in the alert. You do not need to go dig through CloudTrail logs or hunt through multiple consoles. Here's what you'll see:
The Alert Summary
Every alert shows you at a glance:
Field
What It Tells You
Threat name
What happened (e.g., "CreateAssociation API execution targeting all instances")
Severity
Critical or High
Status
Open, In Progress, or Resolved
Description
Plain-English explanation of what this rule detects and why it matters
Principal
Who did it — the IAM user, role, or service account
Resource
What was affected
Subscription
Which AWS account or Azure subscription
Event Origin
Where the event came from (e.g., AWS CloudTrail Events)
MITRE ATT&CK
What attack tactic this maps to (e.g., Execution, Persistence, Exfiltration)
Context Tags
Automated enrichment — things like "Automation User Agent", "Unseen Principal Type Rule"
Created At / Last Updated
When it happened
The AI Analysis (Blue Agent / Mika)
Wiz includes an
AI-powered analysis
right in the alert. It will tell you:
Whether it thinks the activity is malicious or benign
A confidence level
A plain-English
story
of what happened, step by step
The
potential impact
if it were malicious
In many cases, the AI will straight-up tell you:
"This threat was classified as Not Malicious"
with an explanation like
"This represents legitimate AWS Systems Manager automation for inventory collection."
You can use this to help your decision, but always verify.
The Investigation Graph
Wiz shows a
visual graph
of the activity chain:
Who initiated the action (IP, service, user)
What identity was used (IAM role, service account)
What action was taken
What resource was affected
The full timeline of events (with timestamps)
This is your investigation — Wiz already did it for you. You're reviewing it, not building it from scratch.
The Event Timeline
Below the graph, Wiz shows every related cloud event in chronological order:
AttachRolePolicy
CreateRole
CreateAssociation (the triggering event)
PutBucketEncryption
Each event shows
who
did it,
what
they did, and
when
— pulled directly from cloud logs.
4. What To Do When You Get an Alert
Step 1: Read the alert
Open the Wiz alert. Read the description. Look at:
Severity
— Critical or High?
Subscription
— which account? Prod or Dev?
Principal
— who or what did this?
AI Analysis
— what does Wiz think?
Step 2: Make a call
What You See
What You Do
AI says "Not Malicious" + activity is from a known service account / automation
Close it.
Document why (e.g., "Legitimate SSM automation per Wiz AI analysis").
Activity looks expected but you're not 100% sure
Contact the account/app owner.
Ask if this was planned. Their contact info is in the subscription tags (AppID, LOB) or CMDB.
Owner confirms it was expected
Close it.
Document the owner confirmation.
Owner doesn't respond or says it wasn't them
Escalate to IR.
Activity is clearly suspicious (malicious IP, Tor exit node, crypto miner, exfiltration)
Escalate to IR immediately.
Don't wait for owner response.
Step 3: Document your decision
Change the alert status in Wiz and add a comment explaining your decision. That's your audit trail.
5. How To Collect Evidence
All of the evidence is already in the Wiz alert.
You do not need to go to the AWS console, pull CloudTrail logs, or build a timeline manually. Wiz already did this.
To export the full alert details:
Open the alert in Wiz
Click the
three-dot menu
(top right corner of the alert)
Select
Export as JSON
Save this JSON to your ticket / system of record
The JSON export contains
everything
: the alert details, the triggering rule, MITRE mappings, the affected subscription, the principal, cloud account info, timestamps, and all related events.
Here's what a real export looks like (abbreviated):
{
"id": "823ce26a-dad9-5fe6-8b8f-a4416cc62c23",
"type": "THREAT_DETECTION",
"severity": "HIGH",
"status": "OPEN",
"description": "This rule detects a CreateAssociation execution targeting all instances...",
"sourceRule": {
"name": "CreateAssociation API execution targeting all instances",
"ruleSeverity": "HIGH",
"origins": ["AWS_CLOUDTRAIL"],
"securitySubCategories": [
{ "title": "Command and Scripting Interpreter", "category": "Execution" }
]
},
"cloudAccounts": [
{ "name": "PGE-AcademyReporting-Dev", "externalId": "171870468977", "cloudProvider": "AWS" }
],
"entitySnapshot": {
"name": "PGE-AcademyReporting-Dev",
"cloudPlatform": "AWS",
"tags": { "AppID": "APP-3006", "Environment": "Dev", "LOB": "IT" }
}
}
That's it.
Drop the JSON into your ticket. Done.
Screenshots
If your process requires screenshots, Wiz also has a
print
button (printer icon, top right) that generates a clean printable view of the alert.
6. When To Escalate
Escalate to Incident Response when
any
of these are true:
The activity is clearly malicious (Wiz AI agrees, or the rule name makes it obvious)
The account/app owner cannot be reached
The owner says "that wasn't us"
The alert involves production systems and you can't confirm it's expected
The alert is
Critical
severity and you can't quickly confirm it's benign
When in doubt, escalate. It is always better to escalate something that turns out to be benign than to close something that turns out to be an incident.
7. RACI
Activity
SIOC (M&R)
Cloud Security
App Owner
IR
Monitor and triage alerts
A
I
-
-
Investigate and validate
R
I
C
-
Escalate when needed
R
I
I
A
Contain and respond to incidents
I
C
R
A
Fix the issue
-
C
A
I
Maintain Wiz rules & config
-
A
-
-
R
= does the work /
A
= owns it /
C
= consulted /
I
= informed
In plain English:
You own the alerts. Cloud Security keeps the platform running and is kept in the loop. App owners answer your questions and fix things. IR handles confirmed incidents.
8. Common Questions
Q: Do I need cloud expertise to do this?
No. Wiz translates cloud events into plain-English alerts with AI-powered analysis. You read the alert, check if the activity was expected, and make a call. If something is over your head, that's what Cloud Security is here for — reach out.
Q: What if the AI says it's "Not Malicious" — can I just close it?
Use the AI as a strong signal, but apply your own judgment. If the AI says "Not Malicious" and the principal is a known automation account in a Dev environment, close it with confidence. If the AI says "Not Malicious" but the activity looks weird to you, dig a little more or ask Cloud Security.
Q: What if I don't understand a specific alert type?
Ask Cloud Security. That's literally what they're here for. Over time you'll learn the common patterns. Most alerts fall into a handful of categories (root login, credential misuse, public exposure, suspicious API calls).
Q: Didn't we used to use GuardDuty for this?
Yes. Wiz ingests GuardDuty findings AND adds its own detection rules on top. It also correlates across multiple sources (CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, Entra ID, etc.) and adds AI analysis. It's a strict upgrade. You should be using Wiz, not looking at raw GuardDuty.
Q: Where do I find Wiz?
Navigate to
Threats
in the left sidebar of Wiz. Filter by
Severity equals Critical or High
and
Status equals Open
. That's your queue.
Q: How do I export evidence?
Three-dot menu (top right of any alert) > Export as JSON. Drop it in your ticket.

--- Page: Wiz for SIOC Engineers ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364978963
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What Is Wiz
Wiz is a cloud security platform. It connects to our AWS and Azure environments and monitors everything — who's logging in, what API calls are being made, what's exposed to the internet, what's communicating with known malicious IPs, and a lot more.
When Wiz sees something bad, it creates an alert. That alert lands in your queue.
Where To Find Wiz
Log in to Wiz at your organization's Wiz URL
Click
Threats
in the left sidebar
Filter:
Severity = Critical, High
and
Status = Open
That's your queue.
What You'll See
Every alert has:
Section
What It Shows
Summary bar
Severity, status, rule name, timestamps
Description
Plain-English explanation of what happened and why it matters
AI Analysis
Wiz's AI (Blue Agent / Mika) tells you if it thinks this is malicious or benign, with a confidence level and a story
Investigation Graph
Visual diagram: who did what, to which resource, in what order
Event Timeline
Every related cloud event, in order, with full details
Context
Cloud account, subscription tags (AppID, LOB, Environment), resource details
Wiz Severity Levels
Severity
What It Means
Your Job
Critical
Active exploitation, exposed creds, confirmed attack sequences
Investigate immediately
High
Malicious IP, privilege abuse, suspicious behavior, public exposure
Investigate promptly
Medium
Moderate risk, may be expected
Not your job
Low
Low risk
Not your job
Informational
Baseline telemetry
Not your job
How Wiz Compares to GuardDuty
If you've used GuardDuty before:
GuardDuty
Wiz
Scope
AWS only
AWS + Azure + GCP + OCI + Kubernetes
Detection
AWS-managed rules
Wiz security research team rules + ingests GuardDuty
Context
Alert with basic details
Alert with AI analysis, investigation graph, identity context, MITRE mapping
Evidence
You have to go dig in CloudTrail
Everything is right in the alert
Cross-cloud
No
Yes — correlates across cloud providers
Wiz ingests GuardDuty findings AND adds its own rules on top. It's a strict upgrade.
Your First Alert — What To Do
Open the alert
— read the description
Check the AI analysis
— does Wiz think it's malicious?
Look at the account
— Prod or Dev? Check the tags (Environment, AppID)
Make a call:
Clearly benign / expected automation → Close it, add a comment explaining why
Not sure → Contact the app/account owner (check tags for AppID)
Suspicious or owner says "wasn't us" → Escalate to IR
Export evidence
— three-dot menu (top right) → Export as JSON → attach to your ticket

Purchasing additional license(s) base on FQDN/URL of the application is now done through AWS Market Place Private Offer so billing goes to the AWS account and charged to the business owner accordingly.  Refer to the following steps to get started:
Have the project to get pre-approval with this approximate cost:
$205.55/mo or $1644.43/yr co-term with existing contract for each FQDN/URL.
Email to F5 (Nicole Powers <n.powers@
f5.com

A.Miyazono@F5.com
