chunks.page_title: AWS S3 Bucket is not Blocking Public Access - cf-templates
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS S3 Bucket is not Blocking Public Access - cf-templates ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=74450910
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
Issue
When deploying CloudFormation stacks in an account and you upload a template file, AWS will automatically create an S3 bucket named cf-templates-[unique id]-[region] if the bucket does not already exist. The default settings for this bucket is set to be public.
Resolved
Criteria
Existing Resource: Remediation Method
Automatic Remediation
A scheduled job named s3-block-public-access-cf-templates-schedule will run at 5:00 pm every day to update the public settings for the cf-templates buckets to block all public access.
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
None
Useful Links

chunks.page_title: AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy - cf-templates
chunks.content:
--- Page: AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy - cf-templates ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=74450916
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Overview
Manual Remediation
IaC remediation via CF
Automated programatic remediation
Issue
When deploying CloudFormation stacks in an account and you upload a template file, AWS will automatically create an S3 bucket named cf-templates-[unique id]-[region] if the bucket does not already exist. The default settings for this bucket does not require to use secure transport.
Manual Remediation
How To Remediate the Bucket Policy JSON
Ensure that you have the below JSON block added to your Bucket Policy to
Deny
access to your buckets when
SecureTransport
is not being used. This policy can be updated directly from the S3 web console or CLI but we suggest that you use infrastructure as code (TF, CFT) as a best practice.
CF Stack Remediation
How to Remediate the Bucket Policy Via CloudFormation Template
The example YAML CF template block shows how to create and attach a bucket policy which has a
Deny
for traffic that is not using
SecureTranport
for a bucket with the resource name rExampleBucket.
Resolved
Criteria
Automated programatic remediation
Overview
:
Cloud COE has developed an automated programmatic remediation of 'AWS S3 bucket without SecureTransport policy' through the use of a Lambda function that is scheduled to run daily. This Lambda function will check all S3 buckets in the account which were not created by ClouFormation or Terraform and update the security policy to attach a bucket policy which has a
Deny
for traffic that is not using
SecureTranport.
Note:
Buckets created by CloudFormation or Terraform will be skipped by this Lambda function, and should be remediated through changes in the stack. This Function will not over write existing bucket policies instead checks if the secure transport is denied.
Git Repo CF template
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-s3-security-remediation/blob/master/s3_secure_transport.yaml
Steps:
1) Download or store the CF template locally
If using git you can clone the file/repo using the following command
git clone
https://github.com/pgetech/aws-s3-security-remediation.git
example shown below
2) Deploy CF Template
Log in the AWS account with sufficient permissions (admin or above to be able to deploy resources) and navigate to AWS cloud formation service in the search bar
In cloud formation head to stacks and create stack new resources
Navigate to the named Lambda function and configure the test event
The lambda function has a time out of 3 minutes
inputs for the test event are shown below
Existing Resource: Remediation Impacts
None

--- Page: Bentley SAML SSO - 26 Jun 2023 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=212304382
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Summary
Bentley application reported SAML SSO issues after
itiampingrc.cloud.pge.com
was removed from the
itiamping.cloud.pge.com
GTM, due to Rancho private cloud/network issues.  Issues are with a thick client on Citrix network only.  App did not fail on VPN.
Temporary solution:
F5 team (Chiraag Patel) re-enabled the itiampingrc address in the itiamping GTM pool at 4:21 PM.  This appears to have resolved the issue.
Long-term fix:
Not sure - why is Citrix seems to be dependent on itiampingrc being part of the itiamping GTM pool.
Root Cause:
Timeline (06/26/2023):
Errors began around 1 PM.  Initial errors may have been due to intermittent problems with Rancho private cloud.  Errors after 1:41 PM were due to itiampingrc being "disabled" in the itiamping pool.  The VIP itself was never disabled.
I got called into bridge at 4 PM.  Enabled itiampingrc in itiamping pool at 4:21 PM.  No reports after 4:21 PM.
Helpful Splunk search terms:
index=ping "connectionid=
https://ims.bentley.com
"
Does indicate WSFED as the protocol, not sure if that is related?
Details
Bentley thick client failed on Citrix when itiampingrc was taken out of the
itiamping.cloud.pge.com
GTM pool.

--- Page: PF OUD Patching August 2023 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=219483093
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Background
Recent OUD crashes have Oracle Support recommending we apply the latest patch bundle to PingFederate OUD Session Store.  Currently, it is version 11.1.2.3.0 11.1.2.3.0 (Build 20150414142803Z).  However, currently, the opatch utility used to apply patches to OUD is throwing error 73 and doesn't work, as it think OUD is still installed in /opt.  So, Oracle Support provided steps to fix this discrepancy and opatch.  Below are the instructions to fix opatch and to apply the latest patch bundle to OUD.
Also included are the steps to change OUD to use JDK 1.7 (aka JDK 7).
OPatch Fix
Edit /opt/oud/oraInventory/ContentsXML/inventory.xml
DEV:
Replace the line:
<HOME NAME="OH371002176" LOC="/opt/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
with:
<HOME NAME="OH371002176" LOC="/
app
/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
PROD:
Replaces the lines:
<HOME NAME="OH152105084" LOC="/opt/oud/Middleware/OAUTH" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
<HOME NAME="OH1175260011" LOC="/opt/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="2"/>
with:
<HOME NAME="OH1175260011" LOC="/app/oud/Middleware/OUD" TYPE="O" IDX="1"/>
Restart OUD.
OPatch Validation
Run opatch lsinventory to confirm it fixed the issue.
Apply Latest Patch Bundle
Download p31541461_111230_Generic.zip to /home/pingfed/src.
Unzip to a temporary location (such as /logs/oud_latest_bundle).
export ORACLE_HOME=app/oud/Middleware/OUD .
export PATH=$PATH:/app/oud/Middleware/OUD/OPatch:/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin
cd to temporary location/31541461
Run:
stop-ds
opatch apply
start-ds
Change OUD Java
Install Oracle JDK 1.7 (aka JDK 7)
Download p32464070_170301_Linux-x86-64.zip to /home/pingfed/src.
mkdir /app/oracle
cd /app/src
unzip ~/src/p32464070_170301_Linux-x86-64.zip
cd /app/oracle
tar zxvf /app/src/jdk-7u301-linux-x64.tar.gz
mv jdk1.7.0_301 java
ln -s java jdk1.7.0_301
Update OUD Java
export PATH=/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin:$PATH
cp -p app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/config/java.properties ~/archive/java.properties.[DATE, e.g 08-02-23]
cd /app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/bin
./stop-ds
vi /app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/config/java.properties
Edit default.java-home and change value to /app/oracle/java/jre
Edit overwrite-env-java-home and change value to true
Save
./dsjavaproperties
./start-ds
Confirm the version of Java that OUD is now running with ps -ef |grep OUD.

Temporary solution:

--- Page: PingFederate OUD Issues - 6 and 7 Jul 2023 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=213714823
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Summary
MRAD apps, SOMAH and Agency Pledge reported issues with PingFederate.  Checked the PF logs and saw some errors related to OUD on pritappgenlc023.  Noticed email alerts from SiteScope indicating OUD was unavailable at the same time as the errors.  Checked OUD error logs, which were empty.  Checked access logs and saw a gap in activity during the same time period.  No root cause at this time.
Temporary solution:
None.  No changes were made.  Issue cleared after 1 hour.
Current Status:  No issues have been reported since issue cleared.
Long-term fix:  TBD
Root Cause:  TBD
Timeline (07/06/2023):
Errors began at 4:51 PM per Angus Tsoi (MRAD).
Errors stopped at 5:45 PM per Angus Tsoi (MRAD).
OUD gap in access log activity from 16:53:43 to 17:45:47.
From 16:51:21 to 16:53:43, no actual searches happen, just CONNECT and DISCONNECT entries.
CONNECT/DISCONNECT coming from PingFederate Engines/Admin, and F5s.
During this same period, I see entries in the PF Admin request logs implying that the Admin instance and server were still receiving traffic, just issues with OUD and/or port 1389.
Timeline (07/07/2023):
5:13 PM - noticed email alert sent by SiteScope at 5:11 PM
Checked logs and found similar behavior to day before (CONNECT/DISCONNECTS and then no activity).
5:15 PM - stopped OUD on 023 with stop-ds as pingfed user
5:16 PM - started OUD on 023 with start-ds as pingfed user
Activity immediately appeared in the OUD access logs.  Directory started successfully in error logs.  Messages during shutdown about DB corruption, but that may be "normal" during shutdown.
Helpful Splunk search terms (SPLUNK doesn't seem to have all the entries, local logs saved off to /logs/pf_engine/tmp):
index=ping status=failure source="/opt/pingfederate/engine/current/pingfederate/log/splunk-audit.log" description="description=\"Access Grant Management Exception\""
"Unable to establish a connection to server
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
"
Follow-up Items
ELG6 created Outlook rule to FW'd SiteScope OUD pritappgenlc023 failure errors to ENOC-SHIFT until better alerting can be set up (right now, just an email to Ping Operations, seemingly).
Need to work with ENOC Monitoring team to set up better alerts - either integrate with PagerDuty or get alerts on ENOC Dashboard for callouts.
Need to get F5 VIP in front of OUD in PROD, as PingFederate doesn't appear to failover to 027 in this situation.  May need to test whether it does better with F5 VIP in lower environments.
Open Oracle Support case to get a root cause or to determine how to get a root cause in the future.
Look into OUD and Java version to see if upgrade(s) might resolve this.  Check App Health version and timeline for replacing PingFederate OUD.

--- Page: PingFederate OUD Issues 16 Mar 2023 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=190942565
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Summary
One of the PROD OUD instances (pritappgenlc027) experienced some issues around 4:10 PM.  This caused invalid_grant errors in PingFederate and for some OAuth clients.  Mostly Mobile apps appear to be impacted.  It should be only those using Persistent Grants or Access Grants.
Temporary solution:
Removed pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com:1389 from the "
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389 (cn=Directory Manager)" Data Store Hostnames field in PingFederate Admin console.  Originally just stopped OUD on 027, but the errors persisted, along with some additional reported slowness.
Current Status:
PingFederate is currently only configured to connect to pritappgenlc023.  OUD is running on pritappgenlc027, but PingFederate doesn't have it listed in the Data Store Hostname list.  However, the access logs on pritappgenlc027 do show some activity.
Bridge call team decided they wanted to leave pritappgenlc027 out of the mix for now, to see if we can determine a root cause (unlikely, since there are no errors in the OUD logs).  They want to bring it back into the mix tomorrow (Friday 3/17) at 4 PM PST).  They will need a Ping Resource to make the change and monitor if there are any errors in PingFederate or OUD after the change.  If the issue occurs again, we may need to remove 027 from PingFederate Data Store again.  They may want us to create a CRQ for the Ping change.  If so, you can use the PIngFederate M&O Activity Standard CRQ.
Instructions:
Login to PROD PingFederate Admin console
Navigate to System → External Systems → Data Stores
Click on third Data Store in the list named "
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389 (cn=Directory Manager)"
Click on LDAP Configuration
Click on Edit to the right of
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389 in the Hostnames section
Add "
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389" to the end of the value (final valued should be "
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389"
Click on Save
Replicate Changes
If there are issues later reported for the mobile apps and there are indications of issues again, remove
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389 from the list again - follow the above instructions, just change step 6 to remove "
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
:1389" from the end of the line.  Final value would be just
pritappgenlc023.comp.pge.com
:1389.
Long-term fix:  TBD
Root Cause:  TBD
Timeline (03/16/2023):
Errors began at 4:08 PM per app teams.
First email report at 4:25 PM.  PagerDuty incident notified ELG6 at 4:41 PM.
Joined bridge at 4:45 PM.
OUD stopped on pritappgenlc027 at 5:08 PM.
pritappgenlc027 removed from PingFederate Admin Data Store Hostname list around 5:12.
Apps reported working by 5:15 PM.
OUD restarted on pritappgenlc027 at 5:14 PM, but left out of PF Data Store list.
Helpful Splunk search terms:
invalid_grant
"Unable to establish a connection to server
pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com
"
Details
Noted the following errors in PingFederate logs, but it was not repeating continuously and thus made it harder to find:
PingFederate Error (/logs/pf_engine/server.log)

Network connectivity issues
Tried using telnet to connect to pritappgenlc027.comp.pge.com on port 1389 from PF Engines in both data centers.  Engines in both data centers had slow connections (it would hang for several seconds).  Speedy responses from 023.  This is why I shut down 027 and removed it from the config.  Network team did not see any issues during this time.  Cloud team did not see any issues during this time.
OUD Error (/app/oud/Middleware/PINGPRD/OUD/logs/errors and access)
There were no errors in the OUD logs on pritappgenlc027  at the time of the issue.  Access logs started writing at 4:10 PM.  Previous error had been from 8:27 PM the night before and was unrelated.
Other Helpful Notes
Not sure why 027 even had activity, but it had activity in the day or so leading up to this.  Maybe due to OUD-related work performed the previous evening?
Follow Up
Recommend we follow up with Ping to better understand the load-balancing algorithm on the data stores.  We keep seeing unexpected activity in "secondary" server (027).
Consider putting OUD behind a load-balancer, so we have better control over where PingFederate is connecting for OUD.  Consider seeing if we can keep connections within each data center?

--- Page: PingFederate Patching Validation Errors 01 June 2023 ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=206800343
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

Summary
After automated patching of PROD FXIOC PingFederate Engines, ELG6 was performing validation and noticed that Splunk was showing an unusual error starting at 6:25 PM.  Initially suspected it was an issue with OUD, as the timing was similar and we have been having OUD issues recently.  However, it appeared to be issues related to a specific IP address, 10.30.4.183 -
pritappgenlc028.comp.pge.com
.  So, to fix things as quickly as possible, I did a quick stop and start of PingFederate on pritappgenlc028.  This seemed to fix the errors.  Below are the various details I found in the log files that might be a clue as to the root cause.
Long-term fix:
Restart PingFederate on pritappgenlc028 resolved the issue.
Root Cause:
PingFederate did not start properly on pritappgenlc028 after RHEL patching reboot.  Root cause of restart problem unknown.
Timeline:
06/01/2023 from 6:25 PM to 7:12 PM
6:24 PM PinFederate started on pritappgenlc028 after patching
6:25 PM PingFederate Engine all clear email for pritappgenlc028
6:25 PM PingFederate errors began: Unexpected problem looking up authorization code
7:12 PM PingFederate shutdown on pritappgenlc028
7:12 PM PingFederate errors end
7:14 PM PingFederate started on pritappgenlc028
Helpful Splunk Queries
index=ping "Unable to retrieve artifact from"
index=ping "Unexpected problem looking up authorization code"
So, the first error above indicates, in the long Java error this:
Unable to retrieve artifact from 10.30.4.183:7600
This is the error that made me decide to restart PingFederate on pritappgenlc028.
Details
Most of the errors indicated issued with pritappgenlc028, so ELG6 did a stop and start on that one server and confirmed the errors stopped.
Copied off server.log on 028 during the time to /logs/pf_engine/tmp on 028.
LOTS of these errors for several minutes:
2023-06-01 18:27:36,250  WARN  [org.eclipse.jetty.server.HttpChannel] /idp/userinfo.openid
javax.servlet.ServletException: javax.servlet.ServletException: java.lang.RuntimeException: All nodes have failed to return a valid response from RPC 'get'. Failing call.
...
Caused by: javax.servlet.ServletException: java.lang.RuntimeException: All nodes have failed to return a valid response from RPC 'get'. Failing call.
I don't know what the above error means and it stops at 6:29, when the Unable to retrieve artifact errors started.
And in the
028
log files, it says it is
Unable to retrieve artifact from 10.31.5.22/10.31.5.23/10.31.5.24
(RCIOC Engines - pritappgenlc024, pritappgenlc025, pritappgenlc026).
Note - The PingFederate Admin console Cluster Management did not indicate an issue with pritappgenlc028 (it was listed in the cluster) and pritappgenlc028 was responding to HeartBeat requests starting at 6:25 PM, so the alerting scripts did not indicate an issue with pritappgenlc028.

Summary
Issues were seen today for both PingAccess and PingFederate.  Basically, lots of connection time out errors in both PingAccess (PA) and PingFederate (PF) logs seen starting on Saturday (6/24), some on Sunday (6/25) and then again this morning on and off.
Temporary solution:
Issues may have resolved on their own.  Network or spanning tree changes may have been removed that were put in place on Friday.
Long-term fix:
Not sure if one was applied or not.  Need to watch out for the errors.
Root Cause:
Timeline (06/26/2023):
Ping errors were seen starting primarily on Saturday, again on Sunday, but the first large spike was at 7 AM this morning.  The Saturday and Sunday errors MAY have been unrelated.  MyAccount reported issues on Saturday morning, around the same time.

Service Overview
Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed service that offers a choice of high-performing foundation models (FMs) from leading AI companies like AI21 Labs, Anthropic, Cohere, Meta, Mistral AI, Stability AI, and Amazon via a single API, along with a broad set of capabilities you need to build generative AI applications with security, privacy, and responsible AI. Since Amazon Bedrock is serverless, you don't have to manage any infrastructure, and you can securely integrate and deploy generative AI capabilities into your applications using the AWS services you are already familiar with.

--- Page: AWS Bedrock AgentCore ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=354847903

Chunk 1/1

Status
Control Definition
DEFINED
Control Development
DEFINED
Service Overview
Amazon Bedrock AgentCore extends the approved Amazon Bedrock service to enable deployment and operation of highly effective AI agents securely at scale. AgentCore builds upon Bedrock's enterprise-grade security foundation, adding agent-specific capabilities for production deployments.
AgentCore is composed of 7 distinct services. SAF approval status:
✅ APPROVED SERVICES (Top 3)
AgentCore Runtime
- Serverless runtime for deploying containerized AI agents with session isolation and extended execution support
AgentCore Memory
- Persistent storage for conversation history and agent context with encryption at rest
AgentCore Code Interpreter
- Secure sandboxed environment for Python code execution with network isolation
⚠️ RESTRICTED SERVICES (Require Special Approval)
AgentCore Gateway
- API and tool connectivity layer (REQUIRES SECURITY REVIEW - external service integration)
AgentCore Browser
- Web interaction capabilities (REQUIRES SECURITY REVIEW - public internet access)
AgentCore Observability
- OpenTelemetry tracing and monitoring (REQUIRES SECURITY REVIEW - data collection scope)
🚫 NOT APPROVED SERVICE
AgentCore Identity
- OAuth token vault and credential management (NOT APPROVED - high risk third-party data access, see separate security analysis)
Note:
Amazon Bedrock Guardrails are
REQUIRED
for all PG&E generative AI applications using AgentCore. Guardrails must be implemented for all applications, particularly those dealing with Personally Identifiable Information (PII) and other systems that require stringent controls. AgentCore agents should integrate with Bedrock Guardrails to ensure applications are developed with the highest standards of security, privacy, and responsible AI practices. For AI inferences, we will continue to use only the foundational models provided by Amazon Bedrock.
Encryption Requirements:
Per PG&E CS-TECH-020 Standard, customer managed keys (CMKs) must be used with 'Confidential' or 'Restricted' data. AWS managed keys (KMS) are approved for general encryption at rest requirements where Data Classification is 'Internal' or 'Public'.

Managed Service Security Coverage
As a fully managed AWS service, Amazon Bedrock AgentCore
automatically handles
the following security controls without customer configuration:
AWS-Managed Controls (No Customer Action Required):
✅ Encryption at rest for all AgentCore data using AWS managed keys
✅ Infrastructure patching and vulnerability management
✅ DDoS protection via AWS Shield
✅ Service-to-service authentication between AgentCore components
✅ Container runtime security for AgentCore Runtime
✅ Network isolation for Code Interpreter sandboxes
Customer Responsibility:
The policies below focus on
customer-configurable settings
that must be enforced.
Bedrock AgentCore Policies (APPROVED Services)
The following policies apply to
APPROVED services only
(Runtime, Memory, Code Interpreter). These are
enforceable, checkable configurations
that teams must implement.
Runtime Configuration Policies
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Network Security
AgentCore Runtime must deploy in VPC mode (NOT Public)
Critical
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore Runtime must use IAM authentication (NOT JWT tokens)
High
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore Runtime must use HTTP protocol (unless A2A/MCP justified)
Medium
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore agents must be associated with Bedrock Guardrails
Critical
Preventive
Access Control and Identity Management
AgentCore Runtime must use IAM execution roles with least privilege permissions
High
Detective
Logging and Monitoring
AgentCore Runtime must enable CloudWatch logging
High
Rationale for Runtime Policies:
VPC Deployment (NOT Public):
Prevents internet exposure of agent endpoints. Public mode allows unrestricted access and is not appropriate for enterprise use.
IAM Authentication:
Uses AWS-native authentication instead of managing JWT tokens. Simpler and more secure than external token management.
HTTP Protocol:
Standard REST API protocol. MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) are specialized protocols that should be used only with security justification.
Memory Encryption Policies
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Encryption and Key Management
AgentCore Memory must use customer managed keys (CMK) for Confidential or Restricted data per CS-TECH-020
High
Detective
Logging and Monitoring
AgentCore Memory operations must be logged to CloudWatch or S3
Medium
Rationale for Memory Policies:
CMK Requirement:
PG&E CS-TECH-020 mandates customer managed keys for Confidential/Restricted data. AWS managed keys are acceptable for Internal/Public data.
Logging:
Required for audit trails and debugging agent behavior.
Code Interpreter Security Policies
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Network Security
AgentCore Code Interpreter must use SANDBOX network mode (NOT PUBLIC)
Critical
Rationale for Code Interpreter Policies:
SANDBOX Network Mode:
Isolates code execution from internet access. PUBLIC mode allows executed code to make outbound connections, creating data exfiltration risk.
General Governance
Control Type
Category
Policy Name
Severity
Preventive
Infrastructure Security
AgentCore resources must be tagged for governance and cost allocation
Medium
RESTRICTED SERVICES: Gateway, Browser, Observability
Status:
⚠️ Requires individual security review and CSCOE approval before use.
These services enable capabilities that introduce additional risk:
Gateway:
API integration with external services
Browser:
Web scraping and interaction with public internet
Observability:
Enhanced telemetry collection beyond CloudWatch
Approval Process:
Submit request to Security Architecture Forum with use case justification
Security team will assess risk and define additional controls
CSCOE approval required for production use
Note:
Each service will require custom security requirements based on specific use case.
NOT APPROVED SERVICE: Identity
Status:
🚫
NOT APPROVED
Risk Level:
CRITICAL - Third-party OAuth token storage and external data access
Reason for Denial:
Stores OAuth tokens for 25+ external services (Google, Slack, Salesforce, GitHub, etc.)
Enables AI agents to access user data outside PG&E infrastructure
High risk of data exfiltration, compliance violations, and credential theft
Conflicts with NERC CIP, SOX, and data residency requirements
Alternative Approaches:
Use AgentCore Gateway with IAM roles instead of OAuth
Store credentials in approved vault (EntraID, AWS Secrets Manager)
Direct AWS SDK integration without external services
Exception Process:
If Identity use is deemed business-critical:
Submit exception request to Security Architecture Forum
Provide detailed security controls implementation plan
Demonstrate no alternative approaches exist
Limit to Internal/Public data classification only
Requires CSCOE approval
Note:
The "Bedrock Agent without guardrails" Wiz policy applies to both Bedrock Agents and AgentCore agents. This policy is currently enforced in the PG&E environment and will automatically detect AgentCore agents deployed without Guardrails.

Service Overview
Amazon Comprehend uses natural language processing (NLP) to extract insights about the content of documents without the need to build, train, and deploy machine learning models. It can analyze text documents to identify entities, key phrases, language, sentiments, and other common elements with high accuracy. The Entity Recognizer feature allows you to create custom entity recognition models that can identify specific business-related entity types not covered by the standard entity types. These custom models help organizations extract industry-specific information from unstructured text across various document formats, enabling more targeted data analysis and automated document processing workflows.

Service Overview
AWS Lambda is a serverless compute service that lets you run code without provisioning or managing servers, creating workload-aware cluster scaling logic, maintaining event integrations, or managing runtimes. With Lambda, you can run code for virtually any type of application or backend service with zero administration. Lambda automatically scales your application by running code in response to each event, from a few requests per day to thousands per second. You only pay for the compute time you consume—there's no charge when your code isn't running. Lambda supports multiple programming languages and provides seamless integration with other AWS services, enabling you to build sophisticated event-driven applications that automatically respond to changes in data, shifts in system state, or actions by users.

Service Overview
Amazon DynamoDB is a key-value and document database that delivers single-digit millisecond performance at any scale. It's a fully managed, multi-region, multi-active, durable database with built-in security, backup and restore, and in-memory caching for internet-scale applications. DynamoDB can handle more than 10 trillion requests per day and can support peaks of more than 20 million requests per second. Many of the world's fastest growing businesses such as Lyft, Airbnb, and Redfin as well as enterprises such as Samsung, Toyota, and Capital One depend on the scale and performance of DynamoDB to support their mission-critical workloads. DynamoDB offers encryption at rest, point-in-time recovery, and on-demand backup capabilities to help protect your data and provide robust data management.

Status
- Control Definition: DEFINED
- Control Development: DEFINED
- Approval Recommendation: APPROVED
Service Overview
AWS Managed Services (AMS) is a fully managed service that takes over operational management of AWS accounts, including provisioning, monitoring, patching, backup, security, and compliance. It supports two modes:
- AMS Accelerate: Lightweight operational support.
- AMS Advanced: Full lifecycle management including landing zones, change management, access control, and endpoint security.
AMS enforces security baselines (CIS, NIST, PCI), automates operations, and integrates with AWS native security tools (GuardDuty, Macie, Security Hub). It operates under a shared responsibility model.

Service Overview:
Amazon API Gateway is a fully managed service that makes it easy for developers to create, publish, maintain, monitor, and secure APIs at any scale. APIs act as the "front door" for applications to access data, business logic, or functionality from your backend services. Using API Gateway, you can create RESTful APIs and WebSocket APIs that enable real-time two-way communication applications. API Gateway supports containerized and serverless workloads, as well as web applications.
API Gateway handles all the tasks involved in accepting and processing up to hundreds of thousands of concurrent API calls, including traffic management, CORS support, authorization and access control, throttling, monitoring, and API version management. API Gateway has no minimum fees or startup costs. You pay for the API calls you receive and the amount of data transferred out and, with the API Gateway tiered pricing model, you can reduce your cost as your API usage scales.

Service Overview:
AWS Backup enables you to centralize and automate data protection across AWS services. AWS Backup offers a cost-effective, fully managed, policy-based service that further simplifies data protection at scale. AWS Backup also helps you support your regulatory compliance obligations and meets your business continuity goals. Together with AWS Organizations, AWS Backup enables you to centrally deploy data protection (backup) policies to configure, manage, and govern your backup activity across your organization’s AWS accounts and resources, including Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instances, Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes, Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) databases (including Amazon Aurora clusters), Amazon DynamoDB tables, Amazon Elastic File System (EFS), Amazon FSx for Lustre, Amazon FSx for Windows File Server, and AWS Storage Gateway volumes.

Service Overview:
Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) is an object storage service that offers industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance. This means customers of all sizes and industries can use it to store and protect any amount of data for a range of use cases, such as websites, mobile applications, backup and restore, archive, enterprise applications, IoT devices, and big data analytics. Amazon S3 provides easy-to-use management features so you can organize your data and configure finely-tuned access controls to meet your specific business, organizational, and compliance requirements. Amazon S3 is designed for 99.999999999% (11 9's) of durability, and stores data for millions of applications for companies all around the world.  S3 and it's sub-capabilities such as intelligent tiering options are covered and approved.

Powershell Steps to join a Windows EC2 instance to
lab.pge.com
, AWS account 514712703977
Use admin credentials for SignOn to
Microsoft Azure
.
Run Powershell as Administrator
Enter these commands to configure DNS for the local host's ethernet interface…
$Domain
=
'
lab.pge.com
'
$IfIndex = (Get-NetRoute | % { Process { If (!$_.RouteMetric) { $_.ifIndex } } }) Set-DNSClientServerAddress –interfaceIndex $IfIndex –ServerAddresses ("10.94.0.36","10.94.0.37")
Set-DnsClient
-ConnectionSpecificSuffix
$Domain
-InterfaceIndex $IfIndex
add-computer –domainname $Domain -restart
See also,
Join Computer to Domain Using PowerShell
Steps to Find EC2 Joined to AADDS
Pre-conditions:
Use admin credentials for SignOn to
Microsoft Azure
.
Create a “Bastion Host” VM in Azure to manage ADDS
Navigate Home/Virtual Machines
Create/Azure Virtual Machine
Select same resource-group used for ADDS,
rg-cyber-cscoe-sapns2-dev01
Select an Image of type Windows Server 2019 Data Center
Architecture: Intel , Size: Standard_DS1_v2
Configure Network to allow RDP connectivity
Add an inbound network security group rule with destination port 3389.
Download an RDP file
Navigate to Home/Virtual Machines/Bastion-ADDS-01/Connect
Select Private IP address and port 3389
Push Download
Steps:
Use admin credentials for SignOn to
Microsoft Azure
.
Navigate to Home/Azure Active Directory/Devices
Filter on the hostname of your Virtual Machine, ie Bastion-ADDS-01
Post-condition:
Confirm or deny host joined domain.
Windows GUI Steps to join a Windows EC2 instance to
lab.pge.com
, AWS account 514712703977
Launch RDP Client
Import RDP file
Use admin credentials for SignOn.
Navigate to Control Panel/Network and Internet/Network Connections
Right-click Ethernet/Properties/Internet Protocol
Ensure the DNS Settings 10.94.0.36, 10.94.0.37
Navigate to Control Panel/System
Select Change Settings within the Computer name, domain and workgroup settings
Select System Properties/Change
Enter
lab.pge.com
in the “Member Of/Domain” text field.
Windows responds with username/password dialog box.
See Zac for credentials
AWS Systems Manager Steps to join a Windows EC2 instance to
lab.pge.com
, AWS account 514712703977
Deploy an EC2 Instance using PGE Golden Windows AMI.
Windows role must have these policies…
arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/
AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore
arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/
AmazonSSMDirectoryServiceAccess
Navigate to the Systems Manager Console/Fleet Manager
Confirm that the instance is managed by SSM; given you used CCOE automation which uses PGE’s Golden AMI; it should.
Navigate to the Systems Manager Console/Run Command
Search for Command document,
AWS-JoinDirectoryServiceDomain
Populate the form…
Directory Id = d-9267475ab4
Directory Name =
lab.pge.com
DNS Ip Addresses = 10.94.0.37, 10.94.0.36
Choose your instance however you want; I do it manually with the ec2’s instance id.
Given that I don’t have S3 bucket, key, policies that grant access, I use cloudwatch logs for output, accept defaults.
Push Run
See also,
https://youtu.be/qaj9Titw0MM

Here we will create an RDS Custom Engine Version(CEV) leveraging an AWS published WindowsServer2019 AMI with SQL Server 2019 pre-installed, licenses included.
Steps:
AWS CLI
Required AWS Resources
Open the context (right-click) menu for the link
custom-sqlserver-onboard.zip
and choose
Save Link As
.
Save and extract the file to your computer.
Although this template creates all of the required resources, you won't need them all, most of them are already provided by the CCOE.
For example, account 514712703977 only required these resources.
Another example for a plain'ole unchanged free-tier account.
Deploy the resource using commands similar to this.  AWS requires us to upload large templates (>5k?) to an S3 bucket.
Launch an EC2 using an AWS published Windows AMI, licenses included.

chunks.page_title: Create an RDS Database using a SQL Server 2019 Custom Engine Version (CEV)
chunks.content:
--- Page: Create an RDS Database using a SQL Server 2019 Custom Engine Version (CEV) ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=246417457
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

So, a CEV is a pre-requisite.  Either reuse an existing CEV or
make your own
.
To see if an existing CEV exists, navigate to RDS Console/Custom engine versions
Choose each entry to see its description.  You are looking for something like "Custom SQL Server 15.00.4322.2" which is "Microsoft SQL Server 2019 (RTM-CU22) (KB5027702) - 15.0.4322.2 (X64)"
Note the matching engine "version name", ie 15.00.4322.2.cev-13.
Navigate to RDS Console/Create Database
Choose "Standard Create" and "Microsoft SQL Server"
Choose management type, "Amazon RDS Custom"
Choose edition, "SQL Server Standard Edition"
Check engine version to be "Use custom engine version"
Then select "15.00.4322.2.cev-13"
Choose Templates, "Production"
Give your database a unique name, choose a username and password.
Set the storage options that you want, we beleive that 100MB is a minimum, 3000 iops.
Set your options similar to above, however, your VPC & DB Subnet Group will be unique to your stacks.
Deny public access to prevent exposure to attacks.
Take note of the security groups; there are a couple from the CCOE and one from my stack.
Choose a pre-made instance profile and kms key. I used the ones from my CloudFormation stack.
Choose "Full Automation"; I don't require backups for my experiments.
Check I'm all set and push Create database.

--- Page: Playbook: Computing Resource Compromise ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364979030
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What This Covers
A compute resource (EC2 instance, Lambda function, container, VM) has been compromised — malware is running on it, it's communicating with malicious infrastructure, or it's being used for unauthorized activity (crypto mining, C2, lateral movement).
What It Looks Like in Wiz
Alert Examples
Severity
CryptoCurrency:EC2/BitcoinTool.B
High
CryptoCurrency:Runtime/BitcoinTool.B
High
Impact:Runtime/CryptoMinerExecuted
High
Execution:Runtime/MaliciousFileExecuted
High
Execution:Runtime/ReverseShell
High
Backdoor:EC2/C&CActivity.B
High
Backdoor:Runtime/C&CActivity.B
High
Trojan:EC2/DNSDataExfiltration
High
DefenseEvasion:Runtime/ProcessInjection.Proc
High
High severity malware discovered by GD
High
Mass Uplink Communication With a Malicious IP
High
SSH Communication With Malicious IP
High
Instance Involved in SSH Brute Force Attempt
High
Suspicious EDR and cloud defense evasion activities originated from an EC2 instance
Critical
Enumeration and RCE on instance(s) using EC2 UserData
Critical
Network scanning and attempt to change RDS master password originated from an EC2 instance
Critical
MITRE ATT&CK:
Execution, Persistence, Command and Control, Impact, Lateral Movement
Investigation Steps
Step 1: Identify the compromised resource
From the Wiz alert, capture:
Field
Where in Wiz
Resource ID
Instance ID, Lambda ARN, container/pod name
Resource type
EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS pod, Azure VM, AKS pod
AWS account / Azure subscription
Cloud account section
Region
Resource details
Public IP (if any)
Network properties
Instance role / identity
What IAM role is attached
Step 2: Understand what the resource is doing
From the Wiz alert:
Investigation graph
— what actions has this resource taken?
Event timeline
— full sequence of events
AI analysis
— is this a known malware family? What's the behavior pattern?
Key questions:
Is it communicating with known malicious IPs? (C2)
Is it mining cryptocurrency?
Is it scanning the network? (lateral movement)
Is it exfiltrating data?
Did it create new IAM users/roles/keys? (pivoting to IAM compromise)
Step 3: Check for lateral movement
In Wiz:
Did the instance role make API calls it shouldn't have?
Are there alerts on other resources in the same account?
Did the attacker move from this resource to other accounts or services?
Evidence Collection
From Wiz (primary)
[ ] Alert JSON export (three-dot menu > Export as JSON)
[ ] Investigation graph screenshot
[ ] Event timeline
[ ] AI analysis
[ ] Resource details (instance ID, role, region, public IP)
[ ] Network connections (malicious IPs communicated with)
[ ] MITRE classification
From Wiz Runtime Sensor (if deployed)
If the Wiz sensor is on the compromised host, you may also see:
Process execution details (what binary ran, parent process, command line)
File system changes
Network connections at the process level
Manual Fallback
AWS — EC2:
# Get instance details
aws ec2 describe-instances --instance-ids <instance-id>
# Check security groups (network access)
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <sg-id>
# Get console output (may show boot-level compromise)
aws ec2 get-console-output --instance-id <instance-id>
# Check instance metadata for role credentials
# (from within the instance if accessible)
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role-name>
# Check VPC Flow Logs for network activity
aws logs filter-log-events \
--log-group-name <flow-log-group> \
--filter-pattern "<instance-private-ip>"
AWS — Lambda:
# Get function details
aws lambda get-function --function-name <function-name>
# Check recent invocations
aws logs filter-log-events \
--log-group-name /aws/lambda/<function-name> \
--start-time <timestamp>
Azure — VM:
# Get VM details
az vm show --resource-group <rg> --name <vm-name>
# Check NSG rules
az network nsg list --resource-group <rg>
# Get boot diagnostics
az vm boot-diagnostics get-boot-log --resource-group <rg> --name <vm-name>
Containment
Priority: Isolate the compromised resource to stop lateral movement and C2 communication.
AWS — EC2 Instance
Step
Action
Who
1. Isolate network
Replace all security groups with an empty "isolation" SG (no inbound or outbound rules)
IR / Cloud Security
2. Preserve evidence
Create an EBS snapshot of all attached volumes
before
termination
IR
3. Revoke instance role sessions
Add deny-all inline policy to the instance role
IR
4. Capture memory (if possible)
SSM RunCommand to dump memory, or use Wiz sensor forensics
IR / Cloud Security
5. Stop (don't terminate)
aws ec2 stop-instances --instance-ids <id>
— preserves the disk for forensics
IR
Do NOT terminate the instance immediately
— you need the disk for forensics.
AWS — Lambda
Step
Action
Who
1. Disable function
Set reserved concurrency to 0:
aws lambda put-function-concurrency --function-name <name> --reserved-concurrent-executions 0
IR / Cloud Security
2. Preserve code
Download the deployment package for analysis
IR
3. Check environment variables
aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name <name>
— look for injected secrets/C2 URLs
IR
AWS — EKS / Container
Step
Action
Who
1. Cordon the node
kubectl cordon <node>
— prevent new pods from scheduling
IR / Cloud Security
2. Capture pod details
kubectl describe pod <pod> -n <namespace>
IR
3. Delete the compromised pod
kubectl delete pod <pod> -n <namespace>
IR
4. Investigate the node
If the node itself is compromised, isolate via security group
IR
Azure — VM
Step
Action
Who
1. Isolate network
Apply an NSG with deny-all rules
IR / Cloud Security
2. Snapshot disk
az snapshot create --resource-group <rg> --source <disk-id> --name <snapshot-name>
IR
3. Revoke managed identity sessions
Disable the managed identity if compromised
IR
4. Stop VM
az vm stop --resource-group <rg> --name <vm-name>
IR
Post-Containment
[ ] Analyze disk snapshots / memory captures for IOCs
[ ] Identify how the resource was compromised (vulnerable app, SSRF, exposed management port, supply chain)
[ ] Check if the instance role credentials were used elsewhere (pivot to
IAM Playbook
)
[ ] Scan for persistence mechanisms (cron jobs, systemd services, modified AMIs, Lambda layers)
[ ] Rebuild the resource from a known-good image — do not simply "clean" a compromised instance
[ ] Document the full timeline in the IR ticket
[ ] Update Wiz alert status to Resolved with notes

--- Page: Playbook: Data Exfiltration ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364979032
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What This Covers
Data is leaving the environment or being destroyed — S3 buckets being copied to external accounts, database snapshots shared publicly, storage accounts deleted at scale, or anomalous data transfer patterns.
What It Looks Like in Wiz
Alert Examples
Severity
Exfiltration:S3/AnomalousBehavior
High
Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Delete
High
Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Permission
High
Impact:S3/AnomalousBehavior.Write
High
Unusual DB Export Task To External Bucket Started
High
S3 object configured as publicly exposed via ACL
High
RDS DB snapshot configured as publicly exposed
High
EBS snapshot was configured to be public
High
OpenSearch domain endpoint configured as publicly exposed
High
Storage Account Made Publicly Accessible
High
Multiple Storage Accounts With Sensitive Data Deleted Within A Short Timeframe
Critical
S3 bucket policy changed to allow public access on a bucket with sensitive data
Critical
Sensitive data publicly exposed following S3 bucket policy change
Critical
Mass data download from sensitive S3 buckets
Critical
MITRE ATT&CK:
Exfiltration, Impact, Collection
Investigation Steps
Step 1: Identify what data is involved
From the Wiz alert, capture:
Field
Where in Wiz
Resource name
S3 bucket, RDS instance, storage account, EBS snapshot
Resource type
Storage, database, snapshot
AWS account / Azure subscription
Cloud account section
Data classification
Does Wiz flag this resource as containing sensitive data?
Who did it
Principal — IAM user, role, source IP
What happened
Specific API calls (GetObject, PutBucketPolicy, ModifySnapshotAttribute, etc.)
Step 2: Assess scope and severity
From the Wiz alert:
Investigation graph
— what sequence of actions led to this?
Event timeline
— how much data was accessed? Over what time period?
AI analysis
— is this a known exfiltration pattern?
Key questions:
Is this a single resource or multiple resources?
Was data copied to an external account or made public?
How much data was transferred? (check byte counts in VPC Flow Logs if available)
Does the resource contain sensitive data (PII, credentials, financial data)?
Is this still actively happening?
Step 3: Identify the actor
In Wiz:
Who performed the action? (IAM user, role, service account)
What IP did the action originate from? (internal vs. external)
Is this a legitimate identity that was compromised, or an attacker-created identity?
Did this identity also appear in other alerts? (pivot to
IAM Playbook
)
Evidence Collection
From Wiz (primary)
[ ] Alert JSON export (three-dot menu > Export as JSON)
[ ] Investigation graph screenshot
[ ] Event timeline (all related cloud events with timestamps)
[ ] AI analysis assessment
[ ] Resource details (bucket name, database ID, storage account)
[ ] Data classification (does Wiz flag sensitive data on this resource?)
[ ] Principal details (who did it, from where)
[ ] MITRE ATT&CK classification
Manual Fallback
AWS — S3 Exfiltration:
# Check bucket policy (was it changed to allow public/external access?)
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <bucket-name>
# Check bucket ACL
aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket <bucket-name>
# Check S3 access logs (if enabled) for bulk downloads
aws s3api get-bucket-logging --bucket <bucket-name>
# Check CloudTrail for S3 data events
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=ResourceType,AttributeValue=AWS::S3::Bucket \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--max-results 50
# Check for bucket replication to external accounts
aws s3api get-bucket-replication --bucket <bucket-name>
AWS — Database Exfiltration:
# Check if RDS snapshots were shared
aws rds describe-db-snapshot-attributes --db-snapshot-identifier <snapshot-id>
# Check for recent snapshot exports
aws rds describe-export-tasks --filters Name=source-arn,Values=<db-arn>
# Check if EBS snapshots were made public
aws ec2 describe-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id <snapshot-id> --attribute createVolumePermission
AWS — General:
# Check CloudTrail for the principal's recent activity
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=<username> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--max-results 50
# Check VPC Flow Logs for large data transfers
aws logs filter-log-events \
--log-group-name <flow-log-group> \
--filter-pattern "<instance-private-ip>"
Azure — Storage Exfiltration:
# Check storage account access level
az storage account show --name <account-name> --resource-group <rg> \
--query "allowBlobPublicAccess"
# Check storage account network rules
az storage account show --name <account-name> --resource-group <rg> \
--query "networkRuleSet"
# Check activity log for storage modifications
az monitor activity-log list \
--resource-id <storage-account-resource-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
# List storage account keys (check if keys were accessed)
az storage account keys list --account-name <account-name> --resource-group <rg>
Azure — Database Exfiltration:
# Check SQL database export operations
az monitor activity-log list \
--resource-id <sql-server-resource-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
# Check Key Vault access logs for secret/key retrieval
az monitor activity-log list \
--resource-id <keyvault-resource-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
Containment
Priority: Stop data from leaving and preserve evidence of what was accessed.
AWS — S3 Public Exposure / Exfiltration
Step
Action
Who
1. Block public access
aws s3api put-public-access-block --bucket <bucket> --public-access-block-configuration BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true
IR / Cloud Security
2. Revert bucket policy
Restore the previous bucket policy (from CloudTrail event history)
IR / App Owner
3. Revoke external sharing
Remove any cross-account grants from bucket ACL or policy
IR
4. Disable replication
If replication was configured to an external account, delete the replication rule
IR
5. Contain the identity
If the acting principal is compromised, pivot to
IAM Playbook
IR
AWS — Database / Snapshot Exposure
Step
Action
Who
1. Make snapshot private
aws rds modify-db-snapshot-attribute --db-snapshot-identifier <id> --attribute-name restore --values-to-remove all
IR / App Owner
2. Make EBS snapshot private
aws ec2 modify-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id <id> --attribute createVolumePermission --operation-type remove --user-ids all
IR / App Owner
3. Cancel export tasks
aws rds cancel-export-task --export-task-identifier <task-id>
IR
4. Contain the identity
Pivot to
IAM Playbook
if identity is compromised
IR
AWS — Active Bulk Download
Step
Action
Who
1. Restrict bucket access
Add a deny-all bucket policy (or restrict to known IPs)
IR / Cloud Security
2. Revoke the principal's access
Deactivate access keys or add deny-all inline policy
IR
3. Assess what was taken
Review S3 data events in Wiz or CloudTrail for all GetObject calls
IR
Azure — Storage Account Exposure
Step
Action
Who
1. Disable public access
az storage account update --name <account> --resource-group <rg> --allow-blob-public-access false
IR / Cloud Security
2. Restrict network access
az storage account update --name <account> --resource-group <rg> --default-action Deny
IR / Cloud Security
3. Rotate storage keys
az storage account keys renew --account-name <account> --resource-group <rg> --key key1
App Owner (directed by IR)
4. Contain the identity
Pivot to
IAM Playbook
if identity is compromised
IR
Azure — Mass Deletion
Step
Action
Who
1. Contain the identity
Immediately disable the acting principal
IR
2. Check soft-delete
Verify if deleted resources can be recovered via soft-delete policies
IR / App Owner
3. Check backups
Identify available backups for affected resources
App Owner
4. Lock remaining resources
Apply Azure Resource Locks (CanNotDelete) on remaining sensitive storage accounts
Cloud Security
Post-Containment
[ ] Determine exactly what data was accessed or exfiltrated
[ ] Assess whether the data was sensitive (PII, credentials, financial, regulated)
[ ] If sensitive data was exposed, determine notification requirements (privacy, legal, compliance)
[ ] Identify how the exfiltration happened (compromised credentials, misconfiguration, insider)
[ ] If credentials were involved, complete the
IAM Compromise Playbook
[ ] Review and harden data access policies for affected resources
[ ] Document the full timeline in the IR ticket
[ ] Update Wiz alert status to Resolved with notes

--- Page: Playbook: IAM Compromise ---
URL: https://wiki.comp.pge.com/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=364979034
Labels: none
Chunk 1/1
---

What This Covers
An IAM identity (user, role, access key, or service account) has been compromised — either the credentials were stolen, or an attacker is using them to perform unauthorized actions.
What It Looks Like in Wiz
Alert Examples
Severity
EC2 Instance Credentials Used Outside Of AWS
High
Lambda Function Credentials Used Outside Of AWS
High
ECS Task Credentials Used Outside Of AWS
High
AssumeRole from a malicious AWS account
High
Publicly exposed AWS access keys found on public Github repository
High
Wiz-Identified Compromised Secret Usage
High
AttackSequence:IAM/CompromisedCredentials
High
Impossible travel: same user/role login from multiple countries
High
Virtual Machine Credential Used Outside Azure
High
Credential theft activity following cloud activity associated with long-term AWS keys
Critical
Publicly exposed highly privileged AWS access keys found on public Github repository
Critical
MITRE ATT&CK:
Credential Access, Initial Access
Investigation Steps
Step 1: Identify the compromised identity
From the Wiz alert, capture:
Field
Where in Wiz
Identity name
Principal section (IAM user ARN, role ARN, or access key ID)
Identity type
User vs. role vs. service account vs. access key
Source IP
Event details — where did the API call originate?
AWS account / Azure subscription
Cloud account section
Timeframe
Alert created/first event timestamps
Step 2: Assess scope of compromise
In the Wiz alert:
Review the
investigation graph
— what actions did this identity take?
Review the
event timeline
— how far back does the suspicious activity go?
Check: did this identity create new users, roles, or access keys? (persistence)
Check: did this identity access data stores, modify security groups, or disable logging? (lateral movement / defense evasion)
Step 3: Check for related activity
In Wiz Threats, search for:
Same principal across other alerts
Same source IP across other alerts
Same AWS account / Azure subscription
Evidence Collection
From Wiz (primary)
All of this is in the alert:
[ ] Alert JSON export (three-dot menu > Export as JSON)
[ ] Investigation graph screenshot
[ ] Event timeline (all related cloud events with timestamps)
[ ] AI analysis assessment
[ ] Principal details (ARN, access key ID, source IP)
[ ] MITRE ATT&CK classification
Manual Fallback (if Wiz is unavailable)
AWS:
# List recent API calls by the compromised identity
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=<username> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--max-results 50
# Check if new access keys were created
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name <username>
# Check if new users were created recently
aws iam list-users --query "Users[?CreateDate>=\`<timestamp>\`]"
# Check recent role assumptions
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
--lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=AssumeRole \
--start-time <timestamp>
Azure:
# Check recent sign-in activity
az monitor activity-log list \
--caller <principal-id> \
--start-time <timestamp> \
--output table
# Check Entra ID sign-in logs (requires Azure AD access)
az ad audit-logs sign-ins list \
--filter "userPrincipalName eq '<user>'" \
--top 50
Containment
Priority: Stop the attacker from using the stolen credentials.
AWS — IAM User Compromise
Step
Command / Action
Who
1. Deactivate access keys
aws iam update-access-key --access-key-id <key> --status Inactive --user-name <user>
App Owner (directed by IR)
2. Invalidate sessions
Attach an inline deny-all policy with a condition on
aws:TokenIssueTime
before the compromise
IR / Cloud Security
3. Reset console password
aws iam update-login-profile --user-name <user> --password-reset-required
App Owner
4. Review and revoke new persistence
Delete any access keys, users, roles, or policies created by the attacker
IR
AWS — Role / Instance Credential Compromise
Step
Action
Who
1. Revoke active sessions
Update the role's trust policy or add a deny-all inline policy
IR / Cloud Security
2. Investigate the source
If EC2 instance creds were stolen, the instance itself may be compromised — see
Compute Playbook
IR
3. Rotate credentials
Stop/start the EC2 instance to rotate instance role credentials
App Owner
Azure — Entra ID Compromise
Step
Action
Who
1. Revoke user sessions
az ad user update --id <user-id> --account-enabled false
IR / Cloud Security
2. Reset password
az ad user update --id <user-id> --force-change-password-next-sign-in true
App Owner
3. Revoke OAuth tokens
Azure Portal > Entra ID > Users > Revoke sessions
IR
4. Review app consents
Check for newly granted app permissions that should be revoked
IR
Post-Containment
[ ] Confirm attacker access is fully revoked
[ ] Identify how credentials were compromised (leaked on GitHub, phishing, SSRF, etc.)
[ ] Check for any data accessed during the compromise window
[ ] Generate new credentials for the affected identity
[ ] Document the full timeline in the IR ticket
[ ] Update Wiz alert status to Resolved with notes
